🎭 Full Mock Interview #4 — Java Mid/Senior Engineer

Company: VietBank Digital (Digital Banking Arm, 300 engineers)

Context: VietBank Digital is the technology subsidiary of a major bank.

They're rebuilding legacy banking systems into microservices. The team

handles 2M+ transactions/day. Regulatory compliance is critical.

They need senior Java engineers for the Transaction Processing team.

📞 ROUND 1 — HR SCREENING (Video Call, 25 min)

👩 Linh (HR Business Partner): Hi Khanh! I'm Linh from VietBank Digital.

Thanks for your interest in us. Quick disclaimer — since we're in banking,

our interview process is a bit more structured than startups. Four rounds

total: HR, behavioral, technical, then a final culture-fit with the VP

of Engineering. Today is round one. Shall we begin?

👨 Khanh: Absolutely. And I appreciate the transparency about the

process — I like knowing what to expect upfront.

👩 Linh: Good! First question — tell me about a **project you're

most proud of** in your career. Not just what you built, but why

it makes you proud.

👨 Khanh: The one I keep coming back to happened about 18 months ago.

Our middleware was experiencing intermittent 401 errors — about 5% of

requests were failing randomly. It had been happening for months,

and previous investigations hadn't found the root cause. Everyone assumed

it was "the identity provider being flaky."

I volunteered to deep-dive into it — nobody asked me to, it wasn't

assigned to me. I spent a week adding detailed correlation logging

across the entire token lifecycle: when tokens were issued, cached,

refreshed, and used. I discovered that our token cache was using

in-memory storage without synchronization. Under concurrent requests,

two threads would occasionally try to refresh the same token simultaneously.

One would succeed, the other would overwrite it with a stale value.

The fix was straightforward — I switched to a thread-safe cache with

atomic refresh using computeIfAbsent(). The 5% error rate dropped

to zero. Literally zero — we monitored for 3 months afterward.

Why I'm proud: it wasn't a flashy feature. Nobody would see it on a

product page. But it fixed something the team had accepted as "normal"

for months. It taught me that sometimes the most impactful work is

questioning the things everyone else has given up on.

**[📝 Shows initiative (volunteered), deep technical skill, persistence,

and a thoughtful reflection on what "impact" means.]**

👩 Linh: That's a great story. Very relevant for banking too —

we can't accept random failures. Now — how do you **stay current

with technology**? Java evolves fast, new frameworks appear

constantly. What's your learning routine?

👨 Khanh: I have three channels:

Daily (10 min): I follow a curated list of tech blogs and newsletters —

Baeldung for Java/Spring, InfoQ for architecture, and the Java weekly

digest. I skim titles during coffee and deep-read 1-2 articles

that are relevant to my current work.

Weekly (2-3 hours): I practice algorithm problems on LeetCode —

not because I love grinding, but because it keeps my problem-solving

sharp. I also read source code of libraries I use. Last month I

read how Resilience4j implements circuit breaker internally —

it helped me configure it much better.

Quarterly (project-based): I pick one technology to learn properly

by building something. Last quarter it was Kafka — I set up a full

producer-consumer pipeline locally and intentionally tested failure

scenarios. This quarter I'm exploring Java 21's virtual threads.

I also believe in learning FROM work, not just outside work. Every

production incident I investigate teaches me more than any course.

I keep a personal "lessons learned" doc — it's at about 40 entries now.

**[📝 Structured answer: daily/weekly/quarterly. Specific examples.

The "lessons learned doc" is a standout detail that shows maturity.]**

👩 Linh: I love the lessons-learned document — that's very disciplined.

Banking question now: how do you feel about working with **strict

processes**? Code reviews, security scans, change approval boards,

compliance requirements. Some engineers find it frustrating.

👨 Khanh: I actually welcome it. My healthcare experience taught me

that process exists for a reason — when you're dealing with patient data

or financial transactions, a shortcut that saves 2 hours could cause

a problem that takes 2 months to clean up.

My approach: I don't fight the process, I try to make it efficient.

For example, at my current company, code reviews were a bottleneck —

PRs sat for 2-3 days. I proposed a "review SLA" — reviewers respond

within 4 business hours, and authors keep PRs small (under 400 lines).

The team adopted it, and our average review turnaround dropped to

under 6 hours.

So I'd do the same at VietBank: respect the process, but look for

ways to make it less painful without compromising the controls.

**[📝 Directly addresses the concern. Shows adaptability AND initiative.

The review SLA example proves they don't just "tolerate" process —

they optimize it.]**

👩 Linh: Perfect mindset for banking. Last HR question — is there

anything about your background or experience that might be a concern

for us, and how would you address it?

👨 Khanh: Yes, I think the obvious one is: I don't have financial

services experience. My domains are healthcare and general middleware.

Here's why I don't think it's a blocker: the core engineering skills

transfer — API design, concurrency, data integrity, security.

Healthcare actually shares a lot with banking: both are regulated,

both require audit trails, both have zero tolerance for data loss.

What I'd need to learn: specific banking concepts like settlement

cycles, transaction reconciliation, and compliance frameworks like

PCI-DSS. I'm a fast learner — I went from zero Kafka knowledge to

building production consumers in 3 weeks. I'd invest the same

intensity into banking domain knowledge.

I'd also be proactive: I'd ask the team to recommend reading materials,

shadow experienced engineers during my first month, and pair-program

on my first feature with someone who knows the domain well.

**[📝 Addresses the elephant in the room proactively. Draws parallels

(healthcare ↔ banking). Shows a concrete learning plan.]**

🎭 ROUND 2 — BEHAVIORAL (Video Call, 45 min)

Setting: Panel: Engineering Manager + Team Lead from Transaction team.

👨‍💼 Tuan (EM, Transaction Team): Hey Khanh, I'm Tuan.

This is Minh — Team Lead. We'll dive into how you handle real

situations. No right or wrong answers — just be authentic.

👨‍💼 Tuan: First — tell me about a time you had to **say NO

to a stakeholder** — a PM, a client, a manager — about something

they wanted. How did you handle it?

👨 Khanh: About a year ago, our client's product manager wanted us

to add a "remember me" feature that would keep users logged in for

30 days by storing their session token in a browser cookie with no

expiration.

[Situation] This was for a financial application — the middleware I

work on handles authentication for banking-adjacent services.

[Task] I needed to push back on this without damaging the relationship.

The PM was under pressure from their business team — users were

complaining about re-logging in every day.

[Action] I didn't just say "no, that's insecure." That would feel

like I'm blocking them. Instead, I did three things:

First, I acknowledged their pain: "I understand users are frustrated

with daily logins. That's a real UX problem worth solving."

Second, I explained the risk in business terms, not technical terms:

"If a user's device is stolen or compromised, a 30-day persistent

session means the attacker has 30 days of unrestricted access to their

account. If a fraud happens during that window, the liability and

reputational damage to the company is significant."

Third, I proposed alternatives:

  • Option A: "Remember me" with 7-day refresh token (not 30) +

re-authentication for sensitive operations (transfers, password change)

  • Option B: Biometric authentication (fingerprint/Face ID) so users

don't need to type passwords but still authenticate each session

  • Option C: Push notification login — tap to approve instead of

entering credentials

[Result] The PM chose Option A — 7-day refresh token with

step-up authentication for sensitive actions. The business team

was happy because logins were less frequent. Security was happy

because sensitive operations still required fresh auth.

The PM actually thanked me afterward and said "I appreciate that you

didn't just say no — you gave me something I could bring back to

the business team."

👩‍💻 Minh (Team Lead): Good. Now — tell me about a time

requirements were vague or ambiguous and you had to figure

out what to build. How did you navigate the uncertainty?

👨 Khanh: This happens more often than it should, honestly.

[Situation] About 8 months ago, we received a requirement from the

client: "We need the middleware to support multi-tenancy." That was

the entire requirement. One sentence. No details about what multi-tenancy

meant for their system — data isolation level, tenant provisioning

process, performance expectations — nothing.

[Task] I needed to turn this vague sentence into something buildable

without spending 3 weeks just gathering requirements.

[Action] My approach was to make the ambiguity visible rather

than assume or wait for clarity.

Step 1: I wrote a one-page RFC (Request for Comments) with 12

specific questions:

  • Does each tenant get a separate database, separate schema, or shared

tables with a tenant_id column?

  • How many tenants initially? 5? 500? 5000?
  • Can tenant A's data ever be visible to tenant B under any circumstance?
  • Who provisions new tenants — an admin UI or our team manually?

I sent this to the client PM and our tech lead with the note:

"Before I write any code, I need answers to these. I've included

my recommended answer for each one based on my understanding."

Step 2: We had a 1-hour alignment call. Out of 12 questions, the PM

had clear answers for 5, my recommendations were accepted for 4,

and 3 needed further discussion with their architect.

Step 3: I didn't wait for the remaining 3 answers. I designed the

system to be flexible — I implemented shared tables with tenant_id

column but abstracted the data access layer behind an interface.

If they later needed separate schemas, I could swap the implementation

without changing the service layer.

[Result] We delivered multi-tenancy in 4 weeks. The client was

impressed that we started before all questions were answered

without building the wrong thing. The abstraction layer proved

valuable 6 months later when one high-volume tenant needed

dedicated tables for performance — I swapped the implementation

in 2 days.

👨‍💼 Tuan: Excellent. Turning ambiguity into structured questions —

that's exactly the kind of thinking we need. Next one: tell me about

a time you improved a process or practice on your team —

something that wasn't your job to fix, but you fixed anyway.

👨 Khanh: Yes — this was about our deployment process.

[Situation] When I joined my current project, deployments were manual.

The process was: build the JAR locally, SSH into the server, stop the

service, upload the JAR, start the service. It took about 20 minutes

and required someone senior because one wrong step could break the

service. Deployments happened once every 2 weeks because they were

stressful.

[Task] Nobody asked me to fix this. But after my third manual

deployment at 11 PM on a Friday, I decided enough was enough.

[Action] Over 2 weeks (using slack time between feature work),

I set up a basic CI/CD pipeline:

  • GitLab CI to build, test, and package on every merge to main
  • Docker containerization of the service
  • A simple deployment script that pulls the new image, does a

rolling restart (start new → health check → stop old), and

notifies the team on Slack

I didn't ask for permission first — I built a proof of concept,

demonstrated it to the team, and said: "This took 20 minutes to

deploy before. Now it takes 3 minutes with one click. No SSH,

no human error risk."

[Result] The team adopted it within a week. Deployment frequency

went from every 2 weeks to 2-3 times per week. Bugs got fixed

faster because we weren't batching changes. My tech lead said this

was the single biggest productivity improvement the team had that year.

Six months later, we extended it to all 4 services the team owns.

👨‍💼 Tuan: That's initiative. You saw friction, fixed it

without waiting for someone to assign it. I respect that.

Last behavioral question. We deal with **production incidents

that have financial impact** — a bug in transaction processing

could cost real money. How do you approach testing and quality

to prevent these issues?

👨 Khanh: Testing in financial systems needs layers — no single

test type catches everything.

My approach is a testing pyramid:

Layer 1 — Unit tests (fast, many): Every public method has unit tests.

I test the happy path AND edge cases: null inputs, boundary values,

concurrent access. I aim for 80%+ line coverage but focus on

meaningful coverage — testing business logic, not getters/setters.

Layer 2 — Integration tests (slower, fewer): These test real

interactions: actual database queries (using Testcontainers with

a real PostgreSQL), actual Kafka message consumption, actual Redis

operations. I've been burned by mocks that hide real bugs.

Layer 3 — Contract tests: When my service depends on another

team's API, I write contract tests (using Spring Cloud Contract or Pact).

If they change their response format, my build breaks before

production does.

Layer 4 — End-to-end smoke tests: After deployment, automated

tests hit the real endpoints with test data. Verify the critical

path works: create transaction → process → verify balance.

Beyond code tests:

  • Code review with a checklist: thread safety, SQL injection,

input validation, error handling

  • Security scanning (SonarQube, Snyk) in the CI pipeline
  • Feature flags — deploy dark, enable gradually, monitor metrics
  • Canary deployments — new version serves 5% traffic first.

If error rate spikes, auto-rollback.

For financial transactions specifically, I'd add idempotency keys

to prevent duplicate processing and reconciliation jobs that

compare our records with the bank's records nightly.

👨‍💼 Tuan: That's the most comprehensive testing answer I've heard

in interviews. The reconciliation point especially — that shows you

understand banking operations. Great session.

Any questions for us?

👨 Khanh: Yes — what's the current test coverage on the

Transaction team? And are you happy with it, or is improving

test quality one of the goals for the new hire?

👨‍💼 Tuan: Honest answer — coverage is around 60%. We want to

get to 80% for critical paths. So yes, testing culture improvement

is definitely part of the role.

👨 Khanh: That excites me. Building a testing culture is something

I find really rewarding. Second question — how does VietBank handle

incident post-mortems? Blameless or...?

👩‍💻 Minh: Blameless. We have a formal template: timeline,

root cause, contributing factors, action items. Written within

48 hours of resolution. Published to the entire engineering org.

👨 Khanh: That's the right approach. Thanks both!

🎭 Full Mock Interview #4 — Part 2: Technical Round

Company: VietBank Digital | Role: Senior Java Engineer, Transaction Team

💻 ROUND 3 — TECHNICAL (Video Call, 65 min)

Setting: Monday, 2 PM. Google Meet + CoderPad.

👨‍💻 Duc (Staff Engineer): Hey Khanh! I'm Duc, Staff Engineer

on the Platform Architecture team. Today's plan:

  1. Java memory & GC (10 min)
  2. Java modern features (10 min)
  3. Spring Security (10 min)
  4. System design: notification system (15 min)
  5. Live coding (20 min)

Let's go!

PART A — Java Memory & Garbage Collection (10 min)

👨‍💻 Duc: Walk me through JVM memory areas and how

garbage collection works. Then tell me: how would you

diagnose a memory leak in production?

👨 Khanh: JVM memory is split into:

Heap (where objects live):

  • Young Generation — newly created objects land here

- Eden space: objects are born here

- Survivor spaces (S0, S1): objects that survive one GC cycle

move here

  • Old Generation (Tenured) — objects that survive many GC cycles

get promoted here. Long-lived objects like caches, singletons.

Non-Heap:

  • Metaspace (Java 8+): stores class metadata, method bytecode.

Replaced the old PermGen. Grows dynamically, limited by native memory.

  • Thread stacks: each thread has its own stack for method frames,

local variables. Default 512KB-1MB per thread.

  • Code cache: JIT-compiled native code.

Garbage Collection flow:

  1. Minor GC — triggered when Eden fills up. Copies live objects

from Eden to a Survivor space. Dead objects (no references) are

freed. Fast — typically < 50ms.

  1. Major GC / Full GC — cleans Old Generation. Slower — can take

hundreds of milliseconds. Can cause "stop-the-world" pauses.

Modern collectors:

  • G1 (default since Java 9) — divides heap into regions, collects

regions with the most garbage first. Targets configurable pause times.

  • ZGC / Shenandoah — ultra-low latency collectors. Pauses < 10ms

even with terabyte heaps. Great for latency-sensitive banking

applications.

For VietBank's transaction processing, I'd recommend ZGC on Java 21 —

consistent sub-10ms pauses means transactions never stall due to GC.

Diagnosing a memory leak:

  1. Symptom: Old Generation keeps growing, Full GC frequency

increases, eventually OutOfMemoryError.

  1. Step 1 — Monitor: Use JMX metrics (heap usage over time) via

Grafana. If heap grows linearly and never comes down after GC →

likely leak.

  1. Step 2 — Heap dump: jmap -dump:live,format=b,file=heap.hprof <pid>

or configure -XX:+HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError to auto-capture.

  1. Step 3 — Analyze: Open dump in Eclipse MAT (Memory Analyzer Tool).

Look at "Dominator Tree" — which objects hold the most memory.

Look at "Leak Suspects Report" — MAT auto-detects likely leaks.

  1. Common causes I've seen:

- Unbounded caches (HashMap that grows forever, no eviction)

- Event listeners never deregistered

- ThreadLocal variables not cleaned up

- Large collections held in static fields

- Connection/stream objects not closed (no try-with-resources)

In my middleware project, we had a leak from a HashMap storing

request correlation data. We put entries in on each request but

only removed them on successful responses. Failed requests left

orphan entries. Fix: switched to Caffeine cache with TTL-based

expiry. Problem solved.

PART B — Java Modern Features (10 min)

👨‍💻 Duc: You mentioned Java 21. Tell me about Stream API —

when do you use it, and when do you avoid it?

👨 Khanh: I use Streams when:

Good use cases:

// Filtering + transforming + collecting — clean and readable
List<String> activeUserEmails = users.stream()
    .filter(u -> u.isActive())
    .map(User::getEmail)
    .collect(Collectors.toList());

// Aggregation
double avgAge = users.stream()
    .mapToInt(User::getAge)
    .average()
    .orElse(0.0);

// Grouping
Map<String, List<User>> byCity = users.stream()
    .collect(Collectors.groupingBy(User::getCity));

When I avoid Streams:

  1. Simple loops — if it's a basic for-each with side effects

(logging, DB saves), a traditional loop is clearer:

// Don't do this — Streams shouldn't have side effects
users.stream().forEach(u -> userRepository.save(u)); // BAD

// Just use a for loop
for (User u : users) { userRepository.save(u); } // BETTER
  1. Performance-critical hot paths — Streams have overhead

(lambda creation, object wrapping). In a tight inner loop

called millions of times, a for-loop is measurably faster.

  1. Complex stateful operations — when the logic requires mutable

state across iterations, Streams become contorted. A for-loop

with clear variables is more readable.

  1. Debugging — Stream chains are harder to debug step-by-step.

Modern IDEs help, but breakpoints inside lambdas are still awkward.

👨‍💻 Duc: How about Optional? How do you use it correctly?

👨 Khanh: Optional was designed for one thing: **method return

types** where absence is a valid outcome.

Good usage:

// Repository method — user might not exist
public Optional<User> findByEmail(String email) { ... }

// Calling code
User user = userRepo.findByEmail(email)
    .orElseThrow(() -> new UserNotFoundException(email));

// Safe transformation chain
String city = userRepo.findByEmail(email)
    .map(User::getAddress)
    .map(Address::getCity)
    .orElse("Unknown");

Anti-patterns I avoid:

// DON'T use Optional as method parameter
void process(Optional<String> name) // BAD — just use @Nullable or overload

// DON'T use Optional in fields
class User { Optional<String> middleName; } // BAD — use null

// DON'T use Optional.get() without checking
optional.get(); // BAD — defeats the purpose, may throw NoSuchElementException

// DON'T use Optional for collections
Optional<List<User>> // BAD — return empty list instead

The rule I follow: Optional is for return types only. Never as

fields, parameters, or in collections. And always use orElse(),

orElseThrow(), map(), flatMap() — never .get().

PART C — Spring Security (10 min)

👨‍💻 Duc: How would you implement JWT-based authentication

in a Spring Boot microservice? Walk me through the architecture.

👨 Khanh: Here's how I'd set it up:

Architecture:

Client → Login → Auth Service → returns JWT (access + refresh token)
Client → API calls with "Authorization: Bearer <JWT>" header
API Service → JWT Filter validates token → allow/reject

Implementation:

  1. JWT Filter — a custom OncePerRequestFilter that runs before

every request:

@Component
public class JwtAuthFilter extends OncePerRequestFilter {
    @Override
    protected void doFilterInternal(HttpServletRequest request,
            HttpServletResponse response, FilterChain chain)
            throws ServletException, IOException {

        String header = request.getHeader("Authorization");
        if (header == null || !header.startsWith("Bearer ")) {
            chain.doFilter(request, response); // no token, let it through
            return;                            // (security config will block if needed)
        }

        String token = header.substring(7);
        try {
            Claims claims = jwtUtil.validateAndParse(token);
            String userId = claims.getSubject();
            List<String> roles = claims.get("roles", List.class);

            // Set authentication in SecurityContext
            UsernamePasswordAuthenticationToken auth =
                new UsernamePasswordAuthenticationToken(
                    userId, null,
                    roles.stream().map(SimpleGrantedAuthority::new).toList()
                );
            SecurityContextHolder.getContext().setAuthentication(auth);
        } catch (JwtException e) {
            response.setStatus(401);
            return;
        }
        chain.doFilter(request, response);
    }
}
  1. Security Config:
@Bean
public SecurityFilterChain filterChain(HttpSecurity http) throws Exception {
    return http
        .csrf(csrf -> csrf.disable()) // stateless, no CSRF needed
        .sessionManagement(sm -> sm.sessionCreationPolicy(STATELESS))
        .authorizeHttpRequests(auth -> auth
            .requestMatchers("/api/auth/**").permitAll()
            .requestMatchers("/api/admin/**").hasRole("ADMIN")
            .anyRequest().authenticated()
        )
        .addFilterBefore(jwtFilter, UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter.class)
        .build();
}
  1. Token design:
  • Access token — short-lived (15 min), contains userId + roles.

Stateless validation (just verify signature + expiry).

  • Refresh token — long-lived (7 days), stored in DB. Used to

get new access tokens without re-login. Can be revoked.

  • Secret key — stored in environment variable or vault (never

in code). Use RS256 (asymmetric) for multi-service architectures

so services can verify without knowing the signing key.

Security considerations for banking:

  • Token rotation: issue new refresh token on each refresh call

(invalidate old one — prevents replay attacks)

  • Rate limiting on /auth/login to prevent brute force
  • Blacklist for revoked access tokens (Redis set with TTL)
  • Never put sensitive data (account numbers) in JWT payload —

it's base64-encoded, not encrypted

PART D — System Design: Notification System (15 min)

👨‍💻 Duc: Design a bank notification system. When financial

events happen (transaction completed, low balance, suspicious

activity), users should receive notifications via their preferred

channels: push notification, SMS, and email. Some notifications

are urgent (fraud alert), some are informational (monthly statement).

Scale: 2M users, ~5M notifications/day.

👨 Khanh: [Thinks for 60 seconds]

Requirements:

  • Multi-channel: push, SMS, email
  • User preference: each user chooses which channels they want
  • Priority levels: URGENT (fraud) = deliver within 30 seconds;

NORMAL = deliver within 5 minutes; LOW = batch, deliver within 1 hour

  • Reliability: urgent notifications must never be lost
  • Scale: ~5M/day ≈ ~60 notifications/second average, bursts up to 500/s

Architecture:

Financial Event (e.g., transaction completed)
        │
        ▼
┌──────────────────┐
│ Notification API  │ ← receives event, creates notification request
│ (Spring Boot)     │
└────────┬─────────┘
         │
         ▼
┌──────────────────┐
│     Kafka         │ 3 topics by priority:
│                   │ - notifications.urgent (partition=8)
│                   │ - notifications.normal (partition=4)
│                   │ - notifications.low    (partition=2)
└────┬────┬────┬───┘
     │    │    │
     ▼    ▼    ▼
┌──────────────────┐
│ Dispatcher        │ Consumer group per priority
│ Service           │ Urgent: more consumers, faster processing
│                   │
│ 1. Fetch user     │
│    preferences    │ (Redis cache — channel prefs)
│ 2. Fan out to     │
│    channel queues │
└──┬─────┬─────┬───┘
   │     │     │
   ▼     ▼     ▼
┌─────┐┌─────┐┌─────┐
│Push ││SMS  ││Email│  Channel-specific workers
│Svc  ││Svc  ││Svc  │
│     ││     ││     │
│FCM/ ││Twilio││SES/ │
│APNs ││     ││SMTP │
└─────┘└─────┘└─────┘

Key Design Decisions:

1. Priority-based Kafka topics

Rather than one topic with priority field, I use separate topics.

Why? Because Kafka consumers poll in order — a flood of LOW priority

notifications would delay URGENT ones in a shared topic. Separate

topics allow independent consumer scaling: 8 consumers for urgent,

2 for low.

2. User preferences in Redis

// user:preferences:{userId}
{
  "channels": ["push", "email"],
  "quietHours": {"start": "22:00", "end": "07:00"},
  "language": "vi"
}

Cached in Redis with 1-hour TTL. Cache miss → query PostgreSQL →

populate cache. User updates preferences → invalidate cache.

Quiet hours: LOW and NORMAL notifications held until quiet hours end.

URGENT notifications (fraud) bypass quiet hours.

3. Idempotency

Each notification has a unique notification_id. Before sending,

the channel service checks Redis: SETNX sent:{notification_id} 1 EX 86400.

If key already exists → skip (duplicate). This prevents sending the

same SMS twice if Kafka consumer retries.

4. Delivery tracking

CREATE TABLE notification_log (
  id BIGINT PRIMARY KEY,
  user_id BIGINT,
  type VARCHAR(50),
  channel VARCHAR(20),
  status VARCHAR(20),  -- PENDING, SENT, DELIVERED, FAILED
  created_at TIMESTAMP,
  sent_at TIMESTAMP,
  provider_response TEXT
);

Every send attempt is logged. Failed notifications go to a

retry queue (DLQ) with exponential backoff: retry at 1min, 5min, 30min.

After 3 failures → mark as FAILED, alert operations team.

5. Template engine

Notifications use templates stored in a config service:

"transaction_completed": {
  "push": { "vi": "Giao dịch {amount} VND thành công", "en": "..." },
  "email": { "vi": "...", "en": "..." }
}

This allows non-engineers to update notification text without

code deployments. Templates support variables: {amount}, {merchant}.

Monitoring:

  • Notification delivery rate per channel (should match send rate)
  • Average delivery latency by priority (URGENT < 30s SLA)
  • Failure rate by channel (if SMS failure > 5%, alert)
  • Queue depth per priority topic (lag)
  • Cost tracking: SMS is expensive — alert if daily SMS count

exceeds budget threshold

PART E — Live Coding (20 min)

👨‍💻 Duc: Last section. Here's the problem:

*"Given an array of intervals [start, end], merge all

overlapping intervals."*

Input:  [[1,3],[2,6],[8,10],[15,18]]
Output: [[1,6],[8,10],[15,18]]
(because [1,3] and [2,6] overlap → merge into [1,6])

👨 Khanh: OK, let me think through this.

Overlapping intervals merge when the start of one is ≤ end of the previous.

But this only works if intervals are sorted by start time.

Approach:

  1. Sort intervals by start time
  2. Iterate through: compare current interval with last merged one
  3. If overlap (current.start ≤ lastMerged.end) → extend lastMerged.end
  4. If no overlap → add current as new merged interval
public int[][] merge(int[][] intervals) {
    // Step 1: Sort by start time
    Arrays.sort(intervals, (a, b) -> a[0] - b[0]);

    // Step 2: Merge
    List<int[]> merged = new ArrayList<>();
    merged.add(intervals[0]); // start with first interval

    for (int i = 1; i < intervals.length; i++) {
        int[] last = merged.get(merged.size() - 1); // last merged
        int[] curr = intervals[i];                    // current

        if (curr[0] <= last[1]) {
            // Overlapping: extend the end of last merged
            // Use max because curr could be inside last entirely
            // e.g., last=[1,10], curr=[2,5] → still [1,10]
            last[1] = Math.max(last[1], curr[1]);
        } else {
            // No overlap: add as new interval
            merged.add(curr);
        }
    }

    return merged.toArray(new int[merged.size()][]);
}

Trace through:

Sorted: [[1,3],[2,6],[8,10],[15,18]]

i=0: merged = [[1,3]]
i=1: curr=[2,6], last=[1,3] → 2 <= 3 → overlap!
     last[1] = max(3,6) = 6 → merged = [[1,6]]
i=2: curr=[8,10], last=[1,6] → 8 > 6 → no overlap
     merged = [[1,6],[8,10]]
i=3: curr=[15,18], last=[8,10] → 15 > 10 → no overlap
     merged = [[1,6],[8,10],[15,18]] ✓

Edge cases:

  • Single interval → return as-is
  • All intervals overlap → merge into one
  • Already sorted → still works
  • Nested intervals: [[1,10],[2,5]] → max(10,5)=10 → [[1,10]] ✓
  • Empty input → I'd add a check: if (intervals.length <= 1) return intervals

Complexity:

  • Time: O(n log n) for sorting + O(n) for merging = O(n log n)
  • Space: O(n) for the result list (O(log n) for sort stack)

👨‍💻 Duc: Perfect solution. Clean code, good trace, good edge

case analysis. I notice you used max(last[1], curr[1]) —

why is the max important?

👨 Khanh: Because of nested intervals. If last = [1,10] and

curr = [3,5], a naive approach of just setting last[1] = curr[1]

would shrink it to [1,5] — losing the fact that the original

interval extended to 10. max() ensures we always keep the

farthest endpoint.

👨‍💻 Duc: Excellent. Really strong session Khanh.

The VP of Engineering will be in touch for the final culture

round. Any questions?

👨 Khanh: Thank you Duc! Two questions:

First — does VietBank use event sourcing for the transaction

ledger? I've read that some banks use it for auditability —

storing every state change as an immutable event rather than

overwriting records.

👨‍💻 Duc: Not yet, but it's on our 2027 roadmap. The current

system uses traditional CRUD with audit log tables. Moving to

event sourcing is one of the bigger architectural initiatives.

👨 Khanh: That's exciting — I'd love to be part of that migration.

Last question: how does the team handle backward compatibility

when evolving APIs that other teams depend on?

👨‍💻 Duc: API versioning via URL (/v1/, /v2/). Old versions

stay alive for 6 months after deprecation. We use OpenAPI specs

and run contract tests across teams. Breaking changes require

an architecture review.

👨 Khanh: Solid process. Thanks for everything!

📊 FULL SCORECARD — Interview #4

RoundCategoryScore
HRProudest project10/10
Staying current10/10
Strict process comfort9/10
Addressing own gaps9/10
BehavioralSaying NO to stakeholder10/10
Handling ambiguity (RFC approach)10/10
Improving team process (CI/CD)10/10
Testing & quality strategy10/10
TechnicalJVM memory & GC10/10
Stream API + Optional9/10
Spring Security / JWT10/10
System Design: Notifications10/10
Coding: Merge Intervals10/10

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

📊 All 4 Interviews — Complete Coverage Map

Category#1 FinCore#2 LogiTech#3 CloudMart#4 VietBank
CompanyFintechLogisticsE-CommerceDigital Banking

BehavioralConflict, mentorIncident, feedbackMistake, estimationSay NO, ambiguity, process
JavaJVM, volatileSpring lifecycleCompletableFutureGC, Streams, Optional
Spring@TransactionalBeans, annotations—Security, JWT
DesignURL ShortenerGPS TrackingFlash SaleNotification System
CodingTop K FrequentGroup AnagramsLRU CacheMerge Intervals
Unique topicCDN strategyWebSocketRedis LuaIdempotency, priority queues

🎭 Full Mock Interview #5 — Java Mid/Senior Engineer

Company: TravelNow (Online Travel Agency, Series C, 180 engineers)

Context: TravelNow is an OTA (like Agoda/Booking.com) operating in

Southeast Asia. They handle hotel/flight bookings — 300K bookings/day

at peak (holiday season). Engineering team is scaling from 180 to 250.

They need senior Java engineers for the Booking Engine team.

📞 ROUND 1 — HR SCREENING (Video Call, 20 min)

👩 Amy (Head of Talent): Khanh! Hi, I'm Amy. I actually run talent

for all of engineering here, so I do fewer interviews but deeper ones.

Let's make this count. No small talk — let's get into it.

👩 Amy: First — I'm going to be direct. We have 15 candidates

for 3 senior Java positions. Why should we pick you over the

other 14?

👨 Khanh: Fair question. Three things differentiate me:

First — I've worked in regulated, zero-tolerance environments.

Healthcare and financial middleware taught me that "it works on my

machine" isn't good enough. I think about failure modes, edge cases,

and data integrity before I write the first line. For a booking

platform where a double-booking or lost reservation costs real money

and customer trust, that mindset matters.

Second — I bridge the gap between "just coding" and "owning a system."

I don't just implement features. In my current role I introduced CI/CD

where there was none, built monitoring dashboards, wrote operational

runbooks. I leave systems better than I found them. You're scaling

fast — you need engineers who build sustainable systems, not just

ship features.

Third — I learn fast and adapt to new domains. I went from zero

Kafka knowledge to building production consumers in 3 weeks. I've

worked across healthcare, fintech, and middleware. Travel will be my

fourth domain — and I've already started reading about booking system

patterns: overbooking strategies, inventory hold mechanisms,

payment reconciliation. I don't wait to be taught.

**[📝 Direct, structured, backed with evidence. Connects each point

to what TravelNow specifically needs. The "already researching booking

patterns" is a killer detail.]**

👩 Amy: Bold answer — I like it. Now the flip side.

Tell me about a time you failed at something. Not a typo

that caused a bug — a real failure where the outcome was bad

and it was meaningfully your fault.

👨 Khanh: About 2 years ago, I was asked to estimate a feature —

integrating a new identity provider into our middleware. I estimated

2 weeks. It took 6 weeks.

[What happened] I underestimated badly because I only looked at

the happy path. The provider's API documentation was clean, the

integration seemed simple. But I didn't account for:

  • Their sandbox environment was different from production (different

error codes, different timeout behavior)

  • We needed to support both the old and new provider simultaneously

during migration (dual-auth path)

  • The token format was incompatible with our existing cache structure

After week 2, I was only 30% done. I had to go to my manager and say

"I got this wrong — here's the revised timeline and here's why."

[What I learned]

  1. Never estimate from the docs alone. I now always do a 2-day

spike — build a minimal working connection — before giving a

timeline. The spike reveals the real complexity.

  1. Estimate in ranges, not single numbers. "2-4 weeks" with

assumptions listed is more honest than "2 weeks guaranteed."

  1. Flag early. I used to wait until the deadline to admit delays.

Now I have a personal rule: if I'm 30% through the timeline

and less than 30% done, I raise a flag immediately.

The feature was delivered — it works great today. But those 4 extra

weeks delayed another team's work and damaged trust temporarily.

I had to earn it back.

**[📝 Real failure, not a humble-brag. Takes full responsibility.

Shows specific lessons with actionable changes. The "30% rule" is

memorable and practical.]**

👩 Amy: That's a mature answer. Most people deflect with "I'm

too much of a perfectionist." You actually failed and learned.

Now — our Booking Engine team has a lot of legacy code (some

parts are 5 years old, original authors are gone). How do you

feel about working in a legacy codebase?

👨 Khanh: I don't love legacy code — nobody does — but I respect

it. That code has been running in production for 5 years, handling

millions of bookings. It works. That deserves respect before

I start changing things.

My approach to legacy code:

Week 1-2: Understand before changing. I read the code like a

detective story — follow the main request paths, understand the

data model, find the "why" behind weird-looking code. That strange

null check on line 47? It's probably there because of a production

bug in 2022. I look at git blame and old PRs before removing anything.

Month 1: Add tests before refactoring. The biggest risk with

legacy code is changing behavior unintentionally. Before I refactor

anything, I write characterization tests — tests that capture the

current behavior, even if I think it's wrong. Now I have a safety net.

Ongoing: Strangler fig pattern. I don't rewrite legacy systems.

I gradually replace pieces — build a new service next to the old one,

route traffic gradually, verify behavior matches, then retire the

old code. Small, reversible changes.

I've done this before. When I joined my current project, the

authentication flow was a 2,000-line monolithic class. Over 3 months,

I extracted it into 5 focused classes with proper separation.

No big bang rewrite — just steady improvement while continuing

to ship features.

👩 Amy: Perfect philosophy. We've had engineers come in and

try to rewrite everything in month one — it never ends well.

You get it. I'll pass you to the tech team. Great conversation!

🎭 ROUND 2 — BEHAVIORAL (Video Call, 40 min)

Setting: Panel: Tech Lead + Senior Engineer from Booking Engine.

👨‍💼 Kevin (Tech Lead): Hey Khanh! I'm Kevin, Tech Lead on

the Booking Engine. And this is Lan — Senior Engineer on the team.

👨‍💼 Kevin: Jump right in. Tell me about a time a PM or manager

gave you an unrealistic deadline and you had to push back.

How did you handle it without damaging the relationship?

👨 Khanh: This happened about 6 months ago. Our client's PM

wanted us to implement a new tenant provisioning flow — creating

isolated configurations, credentials, and routing rules for new

client organizations joining the platform.

The PM said: "We promised the sales team this would be ready in 1 week.

Can you make it happen?"

[Assessment] I spent 2 hours scoping it mentally. My honest assessment:

3 weeks minimum. The provisioning flow touched 4 services, required

a new admin API, database migrations, and security review.

[How I pushed back]

I didn't say "impossible." I said: "Let me show you what 1 week

buys us vs 3 weeks."

I created a simple table:

Scope1 week2 weeks3 weeks
Manual provisioning via script✅✅✅
Admin API for self-service❌✅✅
Automated security validation❌❌✅
Monitoring & alerting❌❌✅

I said: "In 1 week, I can give you a manual script that I run.

You tell me 'provision client X', I execute a script, it's done

in 30 minutes. This unblocks sales immediately. Then in weeks 2-3,

I build the proper API and automation."

[Result] The PM loved it. Sales was unblocked in 1 week. The full

solution was delivered in 3 weeks. The PM later told me: "This

was the first time an engineer gave me a clear tradeoff instead

of just saying 'not possible.'"

👨‍💼 Kevin: That scope table is gold. I'm stealing that

technique for my own conversations with PMs.

👩‍💻 Lan: Tell me about a time you joined a project and found

code or architecture that you strongly disagreed with. Maybe

you thought it was poorly designed. What did you do?

👨 Khanh: When I joined my current middleware project, I found

that all business logic was inside the controller layer. Controllers

were 500-800 lines long. No service layer, no separation of concerns.

Validation, business rules, database calls — all in the controller.

My gut reaction was: "This is terrible, I need to fix it immediately."

But I paused.

[What I did]

First, I asked why it was this way. I talked to the original

developer (who was still on the team). He said: "When we started,

it was a simple CRUD app with 3 endpoints. We never expected it

to grow to 30 endpoints. By the time it got big, we were shipping

too fast to restructure."

That context changed my attitude. This wasn't bad engineering — it

was reasonable decisions under different constraints that became

tech debt as the system grew. Totally normal.

Second, I didn't propose a big rewrite. Instead, I adopted a rule:

**"every new feature follows the clean architecture, and every

bug fix in old code includes a small refactor."**

When I built new endpoints, I used proper layering: Controller →

Service → Repository. When I fixed bugs in existing endpoints,

I extracted one piece of logic into a service method. Over 6 months,

about 60% of the codebase was restructured — without any dedicated

"refactoring sprint."

Third, I documented the target architecture in a wiki page with

a simple diagram. New team members understood immediately where to

put new code. Before that, everyone just copied the nearest controller

and repeated the pattern.

[Result] Code review time decreased because PRs were smaller and

more focused. New feature development got faster because services

were reusable. And nobody was blamed for the old code — we just

gradually improved it as a team.

👨‍💼 Kevin: That's the right approach — respect what exists,

improve incrementally. I've seen too many senior engineers come in

and alienate teams by criticizing everything on day one.

Next question. At TravelNow, peak booking season (holidays, festivals)

means 3-5x normal traffic for weeks at a time. Tell me how you

would prepare a system for a known traffic spike — like

Vietnamese New Year, when everyone books travel at the same time.

👨 Khanh: Great question. I'd approach it in three phases:

Phase 1 — Baseline (4 weeks before)

  • Run load tests simulating 3x and 5x current traffic. Use tools

like Gatling or k6. Find the first bottleneck — it's usually the

database, not the application servers.

  • Profile the hot paths — the booking creation flow, payment

processing, inventory check. These are the ones that matter under load.

  • Set up dashboards with clear capacity indicators: CPU/memory per

pod, DB connection pool utilization, request latency percentiles,

error rates.

Phase 2 — Harden (2 weeks before)

  • Scale horizontally: increase pod replicas for booking service.

Increase DB read replicas. Pre-warm Redis caches with popular

hotel data.

  • Add circuit breakers on non-critical dependencies. If the

"recommended hotels" service is slow, don't let it block the

booking flow — degrade gracefully.

  • Rate limiting: per-user and per-IP. Prevent one user (or bot)

from consuming disproportionate resources.

  • Queue non-urgent work: confirmation emails, loyalty point

calculations, analytics events → push to Kafka, process async.

Only the payment and inventory reservation must be synchronous.

Phase 3 — Monitor & React (during peak)

  • War room: dedicated Slack channel, key engineers on standby,

PagerDuty escalation rules adjusted.

  • Feature flags ready: ability to disable non-critical features

(reviews, recommendations) to free up resources if needed.

  • Runbooks: "If DB connection pool > 80%, do X. If booking

latency > 2s, do Y." Pre-written, pre-tested.

  • Auto-scaling policies: trigger new pods when CPU > 70%

sustained for 2 minutes.

After peak season: retrospective. What broke? What almost broke?

What do we invest in before the next peak?

👨‍💼 Kevin: Comprehensive. The 3-phase structure is very

practical. I especially like the "pre-warm caches" and

"feature flags to shed non-critical load" ideas.

Any questions for us?

👨 Khanh: Two! First — what's the **biggest booking-related

incident** you've had in the past year? Not for gossip — I want to

understand what failure modes your system is susceptible to.

👨‍💼 Kevin: Fair question. Last Tet holiday, our payment

provider had a 40-minute outage. We didn't have a fallback provider,

so all bookings with that payment method failed. We've since

integrated a secondary provider with automatic failover.

👨 Khanh: That's a great recovery. My second question — how

does the Booking Engine team do on-call? Is it shared with

other teams, and how often do engineers get woken up?

👩‍💻 Lan: Weekly rotation among 5 engineers. We get paged about

once a week on average — mostly during business hours. True

middle-of-night pages happen maybe once every 2 months.

👨 Khanh: Very reasonable. Thanks both!

🎭 Full Mock Interview #5 — Part 2: Technical Round

Company: TravelNow | Role: Senior Java Engineer, Booking Engine

💻 ROUND 3 — TECHNICAL (Video Call, 65 min)

Setting: Thursday, 3 PM. Google Meet + CoderPad.

👨‍💻 Huy (Principal Engineer): Khanh! I'm Huy, I own the

architecture for the Booking Engine. Today:

  1. Design patterns & SOLID (15 min)
  2. Spring Boot internals (10 min)
  3. System Design: hotel booking (20 min)
  4. Live coding (20 min)

PART A — Design Patterns & SOLID (15 min)

👨‍💻 Huy: Our booking engine uses multiple payment gateways —

Stripe, VNPay, Momo. When a user checks out, we select the right

gateway based on their payment method. How would you design this

in Java? What design patterns apply?

👨 Khanh: This is a classic use case for two patterns together:

Strategy Pattern + Factory Pattern.

Strategy Pattern — each payment gateway implements the same

interface, so the booking service doesn't know or care which one

it's talking to:

public interface PaymentGateway {
    PaymentResult charge(PaymentRequest request);
    RefundResult refund(String transactionId, BigDecimal amount);
}

@Component("stripe")
public class StripeGateway implements PaymentGateway {
    @Override
    public PaymentResult charge(PaymentRequest request) {
        // Stripe-specific API call
    }
    // ...
}

@Component("vnpay")
public class VnPayGateway implements PaymentGateway {
    @Override
    public PaymentResult charge(PaymentRequest request) {
        // VNPay-specific API call
    }
    // ...
}

Factory Pattern — selects the right strategy at runtime based

on the payment method:

@Component
public class PaymentGatewayFactory {
    private final Map<String, PaymentGateway> gateways;

    // Spring injects all PaymentGateway beans into this map
    // key = bean name ("stripe", "vnpay", "momo")
    public PaymentGatewayFactory(Map<String, PaymentGateway> gateways) {
        this.gateways = gateways;
    }

    public PaymentGateway getGateway(String paymentMethod) {
        PaymentGateway gateway = gateways.get(paymentMethod);
        if (gateway == null) {
            throw new UnsupportedPaymentException(paymentMethod);
        }
        return gateway;
    }
}

Usage in booking service:

public BookingResult processBooking(BookingRequest request) {
    PaymentGateway gateway = gatewayFactory.getGateway(request.getPaymentMethod());
    PaymentResult payment = gateway.charge(request.toPaymentRequest());
    // ... create booking record, send confirmation
}

Why this is powerful:

  • Adding a new gateway (e.g., GrabPay) = one new class implementing

PaymentGateway. Zero changes to booking service. Open/Closed Principle.

  • Each gateway is independently testable — mock the interface in booking

service tests.

  • Spring handles the wiring — Map<String, PaymentGateway> auto-collects

all implementations.

👨‍💻 Huy: Good. When would you use the Observer Pattern

in a booking system?

👨 Khanh: When a booking is confirmed, multiple things need

to happen: send confirmation email, update inventory, notify the

hotel, award loyalty points, trigger analytics. The booking service

shouldn't know about all of these — that would make it a god class.

Observer pattern (or its modern equivalent — event-driven):

// Domain event
public record BookingConfirmedEvent(
    String bookingId, String hotelId,
    String userId, LocalDate checkIn, LocalDate checkOut
) {}

// Publisher (booking service)
@Service
public class BookingService {
    private final ApplicationEventPublisher eventPublisher;

    public BookingResult confirm(BookingRequest request) {
        Booking booking = createBooking(request);
        // Publish event — don't care who listens
        eventPublisher.publishEvent(new BookingConfirmedEvent(
            booking.getId(), booking.getHotelId(), ...
        ));
        return BookingResult.success(booking);
    }
}

// Listeners (each handles their own concern)
@Component
public class EmailListener {
    @EventListener
    public void onBookingConfirmed(BookingConfirmedEvent event) {
        emailService.sendConfirmation(event.userId(), event.bookingId());
    }
}

@Component
public class InventoryListener {
    @EventListener
    @Async // non-blocking
    public void onBookingConfirmed(BookingConfirmedEvent event) {
        inventoryService.decrementRooms(event.hotelId(), event.checkIn());
    }
}

Benefits: booking service stays focused. New listeners can be added

without touching booking code. Listeners can be async.

For cross-service communication, I'd replace ApplicationEventPublisher

with Kafka — same pattern, distributed.

👨‍💻 Huy: Walk me through the SOLID principles quickly —

and for each, give me a one-sentence violation you've actually seen.

👨 Khanh:

S — Single Responsibility: One class, one reason to change.

Violation I've seen: A BookingController that validates input,

calls the payment API, sends emails, AND updates the database —

all in one 600-line method.

O — Open/Closed: Open for extension, closed for modification.

Violation: A giant switch statement on payment type —

if (type == "stripe") ... else if (type == "vnpay") ...

Adding a new type means editing the switch. The Strategy pattern

I showed earlier fixes this.

L — Liskov Substitution: Subclasses must be substitutable for

their parent.

Violation: A ReadOnlyRepository extends Repository that throws

UnsupportedOperationException on save(). Calling code that

expects Repository will crash. Better: separate Readable and

Writable interfaces.

I — Interface Segregation: Don't force clients to depend on

methods they don't use.

Violation: A UserService interface with 20 methods. Some consumers

only need findById(). Better: split into UserReader and

UserWriter interfaces.

D — Dependency Inversion: Depend on abstractions, not concretions.

Violation: BookingService directly instantiates

new StripeGateway(). If you want to switch to VNPay, you edit

BookingService. Solution: inject PaymentGateway interface via

constructor — the caller decides the implementation.

PART B — Spring Boot Internals (10 min)

👨‍💻 Huy: How does Spring Boot auto-configuration work?

When I add spring-boot-starter-data-jpa to my pom.xml, how does

Spring "magically" set up a DataSource and EntityManager?

👨 Khanh: The magic is in three pieces:

1. spring.factories / @AutoConfiguration (Java 21+ style)

Each starter JAR includes a file listing its auto-configuration

classes. Spring Boot scans these on startup.

For JPA, it finds DataSourceAutoConfiguration,

HibernateJpaAutoConfiguration, etc.

2. @ConditionalOn... annotations

Auto-configuration classes are loaded only IF conditions are met:

@AutoConfiguration
@ConditionalOnClass(DataSource.class)    // only if JDBC driver on classpath
@ConditionalOnProperty(name = "spring.datasource.url") // only if URL configured
public class DataSourceAutoConfiguration {
    @Bean
    @ConditionalOnMissingBean  // only if user hasn't defined their own
    public DataSource dataSource(DataSourceProperties props) {
        return props.initializeDataSourceBuilder().build();
    }
}

@ConditionalOnMissingBean is the key one — if I define my own

DataSource bean, Spring Boot's auto-configured one backs off.

This is how "opinionated defaults with easy overrides" works.

3. Property binding

application.yml values (spring.datasource.url, etc.) are bound

to @ConfigurationProperties classes using relaxed binding.

These properties feed into the auto-configured beans.

The startup sequence:

  1. @SpringBootApplication triggers @EnableAutoConfiguration
  2. Spring reads all auto-configuration candidates from classpath
  3. Filters by @ConditionalOn... — most are skipped
  4. Remaining ones create beans in order (respecting @DependsOn)
  5. Your @Bean definitions override auto-configured ones

I can see what was auto-configured by running with --debug flag —

Spring prints a "CONDITIONS EVALUATION REPORT" showing what was

matched and what was excluded.

👨‍💻 Huy: How about @Cacheable in Spring? How does it

work, and what pitfalls have you seen?

👨 Khanh: @Cacheable caches method return values based on

parameters. First call → executes method, stores result. Subsequent

calls with same params → returns cached result, skips method.

@Cacheable(value = "hotels", key = "#hotelId")
public Hotel findById(String hotelId) {
    return hotelRepository.findById(hotelId); // only called on cache miss
}

@CacheEvict(value = "hotels", key = "#hotel.id")
public void update(Hotel hotel) {
    hotelRepository.save(hotel); // evicts cache after update
}

How it works internally: Spring AOP proxy intercepts the call.

Before executing the method, it checks the cache manager (Redis,

Caffeine, etc.). If key exists → return cached value. If not →

execute method, store result, return.

Pitfalls:

  1. Self-invocation (same as @Transactional!) — if findById()

calls another @Cacheable method in the same class, the proxy

is bypassed. The cache is never checked.

  1. Cache key collision — if two methods use the same cache name

but different return types, you get ClassCastException. Always

use distinct cache names or explicit key generators.

  1. Stale data — if data changes in DB but cache isn't evicted,

users see outdated data. Solutions:

- TTL-based expiry (e.g., Redis TTL = 5 minutes)

- Active eviction on write operations (@CacheEvict)

- For critical data: cache-aside pattern (app controls cache)

  1. Serialization cost — with Redis, objects are serialized/

deserialized (Jackson/Kryo). For large objects or high throughput,

this CPU cost can be significant. Profile before caching everything.

  1. Cold start — after deployment, cache is empty. First requests

hit DB directly. For critical paths: implement cache warm-up on

startup using @PostConstruct.

PART C — System Design: Hotel Booking (20 min)

👨‍💻 Huy: Design the core hotel room booking system for

TravelNow. Focus on:

  • How a user searches for available rooms and books one
  • How to prevent double-booking (two users booking the last room)
  • Scale: 300K bookings/day, 50K concurrent searches

👨 Khanh: [Thinks for 90 seconds]

High-Level Flow:

Search → Select Room → Reserve (hold) → Payment → Confirm Booking

Architecture:

         ┌───────────────┐
Users →  │  API Gateway   │ (rate limit, auth)
         └───────┬───────┘
                 │
    ┌────────────┼────────────┐
    ▼            ▼            ▼
┌────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐
│ Search │ │ Booking  │ │ Payment  │
│Service │ │ Service  │ │ Service  │
└───┬────┘ └────┬─────┘ └────┬─────┘
    │           │             │
    ▼           ▼             ▼
┌────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐
│Elastic │ │PostgreSQL│ │ Payment  │
│Search  │ │(bookings)│ │ Gateway  │
└────────┘ └──────────┘ └──────────┘
    ▲
    │ sync via Kafka
┌───┴────────┐
│ Inventory  │ (source of truth for room availability)
│ Service    │
│ (Redis +   │
│  Postgres) │
└────────────┘

Search Flow (READ — high volume, 50K concurrent):

Users search by city, dates, guests, price range.

This hits Elasticsearch — pre-indexed hotel data with

availability. ES is optimized for this: full-text search,

filtering, sorting, pagination.

Availability data is synced from the Inventory Service via Kafka

events. ES data may be slightly stale (30-second delay) —

that's acceptable for search results. Exact availability is

checked at booking time.

The Double-Booking Problem (WRITE — critical path):

This is the hardest part. Two users see "1 room left" and both

click "Book Now" at the same time.

Solution: Pessimistic Locking with Inventory Hold

Step 1: HOLD (temporary reservation)

When user clicks "Book Now":

BEGIN;
SELECT available_rooms FROM room_inventory
  WHERE hotel_id = ? AND room_type = ? AND date = ?
  FOR UPDATE;  -- pessimistic lock: blocks other transactions

IF available_rooms > 0:
  UPDATE room_inventory SET available_rooms = available_rooms - 1
    WHERE hotel_id = ? AND room_type = ? AND date = ?;
  INSERT INTO booking_hold (booking_id, hotel_id, ..., expires_at)
    VALUES (?, ?, ..., NOW() + INTERVAL '10 minutes');
COMMIT;
-- return: hold_id, user has 10 minutes to complete payment
ELSE:
  ROLLBACK;
-- return: "SOLD OUT"

FOR UPDATE ensures only one transaction decrements at a time.

Second user's transaction blocks, sees available_rooms = 0, returns "SOLD OUT".

Step 2: PAYMENT (within 10-minute hold window)

User submits payment. Payment Service processes it.

If successful → booking status = CONFIRMED.

If failed → release hold (increment available_rooms back).

Step 3: HOLD EXPIRY (background job)

A scheduler runs every minute: find holds where expires_at < NOW()

and status != CONFIRMED. For these:

  • Set status = EXPIRED
  • UPDATE room_inventory SET available_rooms = available_rooms + 1
  • Notify user: "Your hold expired"

Why pessimistic locking, not optimistic?

For the LAST room scenario, optimistic locking would cause many

retries and poor UX. Pessimistic locking is simpler and more

deterministic. The lock duration is milliseconds (just the DB

transaction), so it doesn't create a bottleneck.

Alternative for extreme scale:

If PostgreSQL FOR UPDATE becomes a bottleneck, I'd move the

inventory counter to Redis with atomic DECR (like the flash

sale design). But for 300K bookings/day (~3.5/second), PostgreSQL

handles this easily.

Data Model:

CREATE TABLE room_inventory (
  hotel_id VARCHAR(50),
  room_type VARCHAR(50),
  date DATE,
  total_rooms INT,
  available_rooms INT,
  PRIMARY KEY (hotel_id, room_type, date)
);

CREATE TABLE bookings (
  id UUID PRIMARY KEY,
  user_id BIGINT NOT NULL,
  hotel_id VARCHAR(50),
  room_type VARCHAR(50),
  check_in DATE, check_out DATE,
  status VARCHAR(20), -- HELD, CONFIRMED, CANCELLED, EXPIRED
  total_price DECIMAL(12,2),
  payment_id VARCHAR(100),
  created_at TIMESTAMP,
  confirmed_at TIMESTAMP
);

Search → Book Data Consistency:

ES shows "3 rooms available" but by the time user clicks Book,

2 others booked. The hold step catches this — if available_rooms = 0,

we return "sold out" instantly. The search result was a best-effort

approximation, the booking step is the source of truth.

👨‍💻 Huy: What about booking a room for 5 nights — that's 5

dates that all need rooms available?

👨 Khanh: Good catch. The hold step must lock ALL 5 date rows

atomically:

SELECT available_rooms FROM room_inventory
  WHERE hotel_id = ? AND room_type = ?
    AND date BETWEEN '2026-01-10' AND '2026-01-14'
  FOR UPDATE;

-- Check ALL 5 dates have available_rooms > 0
-- If any date has 0 → ROLLBACK, show "not available for those dates"
-- If all > 0 → decrement all 5 rows

Always lock rows in the same order (by date ascending) to prevent

deadlocks between concurrent bookings for overlapping date ranges.

PART D — Live Coding (20 min)

👨‍💻 Huy: Coding time!

*"You are given coins of different denominations and a total amount.

Find the minimum number of coins needed to make that amount.

If it's impossible, return -1. You have infinite supply of each coin."*

Input: coins = [1, 5, 11], amount = 15
Output: 3  (three coins of 5)

👨 Khanh: OK. Let me think about this.

First instinct — Greedy? Take the largest coin first.

For coins = [1,5,11], amount = 15: greedy picks 11 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 5 coins.

But optimal is 5 + 5 + 5 = 3 coins. Greedy fails!

Greedy doesn't work for coin change because local optimal ≠ global optimal.

Correct approach — Dynamic Programming.

Define: dp[i] = minimum coins needed to make amount i.

Recurrence: for each amount i, try every coin c:

dp[i] = min(dp[i], dp[i - c] + 1) for all coins c ≤ i

Base case: dp[0] = 0 (0 coins to make amount 0).

Initialize all other dp values to amount + 1 (impossible sentinel).

public int coinChange(int[] coins, int amount) {
    // dp[i] = min coins to make amount i
    int[] dp = new int[amount + 1];
    Arrays.fill(dp, amount + 1); // "infinity" placeholder
    dp[0] = 0; // base case: 0 coins for amount 0

    for (int i = 1; i <= amount; i++) {
        for (int coin : coins) {
            if (coin <= i) { // can we use this coin?
                // Use this coin: 1 coin + min coins for remainder
                dp[i] = Math.min(dp[i], dp[i - coin] + 1);
            }
        }
    }

    // If dp[amount] is still "infinity", it's impossible
    return dp[amount] > amount ? -1 : dp[amount];
}

Trace: coins = [1,5,11], amount = 15

dp[0]  = 0
dp[1]  = dp[0]+1 = 1    (use coin 1)
dp[2]  = dp[1]+1 = 2    (use coin 1)
dp[3]  = 3
dp[4]  = 4
dp[5]  = min(dp[4]+1, dp[0]+1) = min(5, 1) = 1   (use coin 5!)
dp[6]  = min(dp[5]+1, dp[1]+1) = min(2, 2) = 2
dp[10] = min(dp[9]+1, dp[5]+1) = min(6, 2) = 2   (5+5)
dp[11] = min(dp[10]+1, dp[6]+1, dp[0]+1) = min(3, 3, 1) = 1  (coin 11!)
dp[15] = min(dp[14]+1, dp[10]+1, dp[4]+1)
       = min(5, 3, 5) = 3                          (5+5+5) ✓

Why amount + 1 as infinity? Because the maximum possible coins

is amount (all 1-cent coins). So amount + 1 is guaranteed to

be larger than any valid answer. Using Integer.MAX_VALUE risks

overflow when adding 1.

Edge cases:

  • amount = 0 → return 0 (no coins needed)
  • coins = [2], amount = 3 → impossible → return -1
  • coins = [1], amount = 100 → dp[100] = 100

Complexity:

  • Time: O(amount × coins.length)
  • Space: O(amount)

👨‍💻 Huy: Clean solution. You caught the greedy trap — a lot

of candidates go straight to greedy and get stuck. The amount + 1

vs MAX_VALUE insight shows attention to detail.

Any questions for me?

👨 Khanh: Yes! In the booking engine, do you use event sourcing

for booking state changes? I'm imagining: BookingCreated →

PaymentProcessed → BookingConfirmed → CheckInRecorded. Storing

every state transition as an immutable event gives you full

auditability and the ability to replay history.

👨‍💻 Huy: We don't currently — we use a status column with an

audit log table. But it's something we've discussed. If you join,

you might be the person to evaluate whether event sourcing is worth

the complexity for our use case.

👨 Khanh: I'd love that kind of architectural investigation.

Thanks Huy — great interview!

📊 FULL SCORECARD — Interview #5

BehavioralPush back on deadline10/10

Preparing for traffic spike10/10

SOLID principles10/10

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

📊 Complete 5-Interview Master Map

DomainFintechLogisticsE-CommerceBankingTravel/OTA
