🎭 Full Mock Interview #8 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: ShopNow (SEA Marketplace, 6000+ engineers, IPO'd)

Context: ShopNow is a marketplace like Shopee/Lazada — connecting

5M+ sellers with 200M+ buyers across 7 SEA countries. 150M+ product

listings, 3M+ orders/day. The Product Catalog & Search team owns

the platform that powers product discovery — search, filtering,

ranking, and recommendations. Based in Ho Chi Minh City.

Why different from Mock #3 (CloudMart): CloudMart was a single-seller

B2C e-commerce. ShopNow is a multi-seller marketplace — fundamentally

different challenges: catalog scale (150M products), seller management,

search ranking, and cross-seller order splitting.

📞 ROUND 1 — RECRUITER SCREENING (Video Call, 25 min)

👩 Lisa (Recruiter): Hi Khanh! I'm Lisa from ShopNow engineering

talent. I see you're currently a Senior Engineer at FPT working on

the Sony Global project. Let me tell you about our opportunity first.

We're hiring for the Product Catalog team. This team owns the backbone

of ShopNow — every product listing, every search query, every filter

click flows through our systems. 150 million products, 50,000 search

queries per second at peak, sub-200ms response time requirement.

The team is 15 engineers. We need someone who can handle distributed

data systems and has strong Java fundamentals. Sound interesting?

👨 Khanh: Very much. Product search at that scale — 50K QPS with

sub-200ms latency across 150M products — is an incredible engineering

challenge. My experience with high-availability systems at Sony and

Elasticsearch at Axon Active makes me particularly drawn to this role.

👩 Lisa: You mentioned Elasticsearch — that's actually our core

search technology. Tell me about your background.

👨 Khanh: I've spent 4.5 years in backend engineering with a

deliberate progression in scale and complexity.

At TMA Solutions, I built healthcare microservices — strict compliance,

Kubernetes deployments, CI/CD pipelines. This was my foundation

in production-grade engineering.

At Axon Active, I worked on Valiant, a banking platform migration

from monolith to microservices. Crucially, this is where I worked

with Elasticsearch — we integrated it for account and transaction

search. I learned how to model search indices, handle data sync

between PostgreSQL and ES, and tune relevance scoring.

Now at FPT/Sony Global, I design cloud-native microservices on AWS

serving global users. I own system reliability — monitoring, incident

response, and performance optimization under strict SLAs.

The combination of my ES experience, cloud-native architecture skills,

and reliability ownership maps directly to what your catalog team needs.

👩 Lisa: That's a strong match. Why leave your current role?

👨 Khanh: Two reasons. First, I want to work on consumer-facing

products where I can directly impact millions of users. At FPT,

I'm building enterprise systems for Sony — excellent engineering,

but the user impact is indirect.

Second, ShopNow's scale is a meaningful step up. 50K search QPS is

10x anything I've handled. I want that challenge, and I want to

learn from engineers who've solved it.

👩 Lisa: Our process is 3 rounds: this call, behavioral with EM,

then a technical deep-dive. Can you do all three this week?

👨 Khanh: Absolutely.

🎭 ROUND 2 — BEHAVIORAL (Video Call, 40 min)

Setting: Wednesday, 3 PM. Google Meet. Engineering Manager.

👨‍💼 David (EM): Hey Khanh. I'm David, EM for Product Catalog.

Today I want to understand how you deal with ambiguity, influence,

and ownership. These are critical because our team makes decisions

that affect the entire shopping experience for 200 million users.

👨‍💼 David: First question. Tell me about a time you had to

make a significant technical decision with incomplete information.

You didn't have all the data you wanted, but you had to move forward.

👨 Khanh: This happened at Sony, about 4 months ago.

[Situation] We needed to choose a caching strategy for a new

service that served user preferences. The challenge: we didn't have

clear traffic patterns yet — the service was being launched for the

first time. We didn't know the read:write ratio, the cache hit rate,

or the hot key distribution. But we had a deadline — the launch was

in 3 weeks.

[Task] I needed to design a caching layer that would work well

enough for launch, with the ability to optimize once we had real data.

[Action] Instead of trying to predict the perfect strategy,

I designed for observability first, optimization later.

I implemented cache-aside with Redis and added extensive instrumentation:

cache hit/miss ratio, key access frequency distribution, TTL

effectiveness, and eviction rate. All exposed as CloudWatch custom

metrics with Grafana dashboards.

For the initial configuration, I made conservative choices:

  • TTL of 5 minutes (short enough that stale data isn't a big problem)
  • allkeys-lru eviction (safe default)
  • Redis cluster with enough memory for 2x our estimated working set

I documented my assumptions explicitly in the design doc:

"Assuming 70% read ratio. If actual is higher, increase cache size.

If lower, consider write-through instead of cache-aside."

[Result] We launched on time. After 2 weeks of production data,

the actual read ratio was 92% — much higher than assumed. Cache hit

rate was 78%. I tuned the TTL to 15 minutes (data changed rarely)

and the hit rate jumped to 94%. The key insight: **ship with good

defaults and instrumentation, optimize with real data.**

👨‍💼 David: That's a mature approach. Too many engineers try

to design the perfect system upfront. "Observability first" is

exactly the philosophy we follow here.

👨‍💼 David: Second question. Tell me about a time you

influenced a decision you didn't have authority over. You weren't

the tech lead, but you changed the outcome.

👨 Khanh: At TMA, on the Humana healthcare project.

[Situation] Our team was about to adopt a custom-built framework

for API gateway routing that another team had created. The tech lead

had already approved it. But after reviewing the framework's code,

I found several problems: no connection pooling, no circuit breaker,

no metrics, and poor error handling. It would be a liability in

production.

[Task] I was a mid-level engineer at the time. The decision was

already made by my tech lead and the other team's lead. I needed

to change their minds without overstepping.

[Action] I took three steps:

First, I built credibility by doing homework. I spent one evening

writing a comparison document: the custom framework vs Spring Cloud

Gateway. Side-by-side feature comparison, performance benchmarks

I ran locally, and a risk assessment for each option.

Second, I chose the right moment. Instead of challenging the

decision in a team meeting (public disagreement = defensiveness),

I scheduled a 1:1 with my tech lead. I said: "I've been looking at

the gateway framework and I have some concerns. Can I share my

analysis?" Non-threatening, respectful.

Third, I proposed a compromise. Rather than "reject their

framework entirely" (politically difficult), I suggested: "Let's

use Spring Cloud Gateway for the core routing, but we can integrate

their team's custom authentication plugin since that part is solid."

This gave the other team partial credit and avoided a political battle.

[Result] My tech lead reviewed the comparison, agreed with my

concerns, and proposed the compromise to the other team lead.

We adopted Spring Cloud Gateway with their auth plugin. Six months

later, when we handled 3x traffic during open enrollment, the

circuit breaker and connection pooling in Spring Cloud Gateway

prevented what would have been outages with the custom framework.

My tech lead mentioned this in my performance review as an example

of "senior-level thinking from a mid-level engineer."

👨‍💼 David: Three things I love about that answer: you did the

homework, you chose the right forum, and you found a political

compromise. That's influencing 101.

👨‍💼 David: Last behavioral question. You're going to encounter

this at ShopNow constantly: **How do you prioritize technical debt

vs feature delivery?** Give me your framework, not just a story.

👨 Khanh: I think about tech debt in four categories, and each

gets different treatment:

Category 1: Safety debt — bugs or design flaws that could cause

data loss, security vulnerabilities, or outages. These get fixed

immediately, no negotiation. I frame these to PMs as risk: "If we

don't fix this, there's a 30% chance of a P1 incident this quarter."

PMs understand risk.

Category 2: Velocity debt — things that slow the team down

every day. Flaky tests, slow builds, poor local dev experience.

I advocate for dedicating 15-20% of each sprint to this. ROI is

immediate: fix the flaky test that wastes 30 minutes per developer

per day × 8 developers = 4 engineer-hours saved daily.

Category 3: Scalability debt — the system works today but won't

at 2x scale. This is where I use the "ticket with data" approach:

create a tech debt ticket with load test results showing exactly

when we'll hit the wall. Review quarterly with the EM and PM.

Schedule before it becomes urgent.

Category 4: Aesthetic debt — code that's ugly but works.

Inconsistent naming, outdated patterns, code that "should be

refactored." I deprioritize this aggressively. Refactor only when

you're already modifying that code for a feature — the "boy scout

rule" (leave code better than you found it).

The framework: **Safety = now, Velocity = every sprint, Scalability

= planned, Aesthetic = opportunistic.**

👨‍💼 David: That's the best tech debt framework I've heard in

an interview. I'm going to steal that four-category model for my

next planning meeting. Seriously.

Any questions for me?

👨 Khanh: Two. First — how does the team handle search relevance?

Is it ML-driven or rule-based?

👨‍💼 David: Hybrid. We have an ML-powered ranking model, but

the retrieval phase uses Elasticsearch with hand-tuned boosting

rules. The ML team provides a relevance score, and our team owns

the infrastructure that serves it at 50K QPS.

👨 Khanh: Interesting — so your team is the serving layer, not

the model training layer. Second question — what's the oncall

situation?

👨‍💼 David: Weekly rotation, shared across 15 engineers. So

roughly every 4 months. We have extensive runbooks and auto-remediation

for common issues. Most months, oncall is boring — which is how

we like it.

👨 Khanh: Boring oncall is the best oncall. Thanks David!

📊 ROUND 1–2 SCORECARD

Round 1 — Recruiter ✅

CategoryScoreNotes
Career story10/10Connected ES experience at Axon directly to catalog role
Why this role10/10"10x scale step-up" — honest and ambitious
Domain knowledge9/10Mentioned search QPS, latency requirements specifically

Round 2 — Behavioral ✅

CategoryScoreNotes
Incomplete information10/10"Observability first, optimization later" — senior mindset
Influencing without authority10/10Homework + right forum + political compromise
Tech debt framework10/10Four-category model with specific actions per category
Questions asked10/10Probed the ML/infra boundary — shows system thinking

🔑 New Techniques (Not in Mocks #2–#7)

TechniqueWhy It's Valuable
"Observability first" decision-makingFor ambiguous requirements — ship with metrics, optimize later
Influencing via 1:1 (not public forum)Politically smart — avoids defensiveness
Tech debt 4-category frameworkStructured approach PMs and EMs immediately understand
"Boring oncall is best oncall"Shows you value reliability over heroics

Next: See [Part 2](file:///Users/tuongkhanh/Desktop/Mock%20Interview/mock_interview_8_part2.md)

for the Technical Round — System Design (product search at 50K QPS)

+ Live Coding (design a concurrent LFU cache).

🎭 Full Mock Interview #8 — Part 2: Technical Round

Company: ShopNow | Role: Senior Java Engineer, Product Catalog & Search

💻 ROUND 3 — TECHNICAL INTERVIEW (Video Call, 75 min)

Setting: Friday, 2 PM. Zoom + CoderPad. Staff Eng + Senior Eng.

👨‍💻 Hao (Staff Eng): Hey Khanh, I'm Hao — Staff Engineer on the

catalog platform. And this is Thảo from the search team.

👩‍💻 Thảo: Hi!

👨‍💻 Hao: Three parts today:

  1. Elasticsearch & data deep-dive (15 min)
  2. System design: product search platform (35 min)
  3. Live coding (25 min)

Let's go.

PART A — Elasticsearch & Data Deep-Dive (15 min)

👨‍💻 Hao: You used Elasticsearch at Axon Active. **Explain

how an Elasticsearch query executes across a distributed cluster.**

Not just "it searches" — I want the full distributed flow.

👨 Khanh: Sure. Let me trace a search query end-to-end.

Say we have an index products with 5 primary shards and 1 replica

each, spread across 3 nodes.

Client sends: GET /products/_search { "query": { "match": { "name": "wireless headphones" } } }

Step 1: COORDINATING NODE
  - Any node can be the coordinator
  - The coordinator determines which shards contain the index
  - For a search, it queries ALL primary shards (or their replicas)

Step 2: QUERY PHASE (scatter)
  - Coordinator sends the query to one copy of each shard (5 requests)
  - Each shard locally:
    a. Tokenizes "wireless headphones" → ["wireless", "headphones"]
    b. Looks up each token in the inverted index
    c. Computes relevance score (BM25) for matching docs
    d. Returns top-N document IDs + scores (NOT full documents)
  - Each shard returns its local top-K results

Step 3: MERGE + SORT (gather)
  - Coordinator merges all 5 shard results
  - Global sort by score → selects final top-K document IDs
  - This is why pagination gets expensive: page 100 means
    each shard returns 100×page_size results to the coordinator

Step 4: FETCH PHASE
  - Coordinator sends a multi-get request for the winning doc IDs
    to the specific shards that hold them
  - Shards return full document _source
  - Coordinator assembles final response → returns to client

Key performance implications:

  • Query latency = slowest shard response (tail latency matters)
  • More shards = more parallelism but more coordination overhead
  • Deep pagination is expensive — use search_after instead of from/size
  • Replicas help read throughput — coordinator can route to any copy

👨‍💻 Hao: Very good. Follow-up — **how do you handle the situation

where your Elasticsearch index gets out of sync with your database?**

👨 Khanh: This is one of the hardest problems in search systems.

I've dealt with three approaches:

Detection: Run a reconciliation job periodically — compare

a sample of DB records against ES documents. Flag mismatches.

We ran this hourly at Axon Active. Key metric: "sync drift percentage."

If > 0.1%, alert and investigate.

Prevention: Use CDC (Change Data Capture) with Debezium instead

of application-level dual writes. The pipeline reads the PostgreSQL

WAL (Write-Ahead Log) — it captures every change, including those

made by batch jobs, migrations, or direct DB fixes that bypass

the application layer.

PostgreSQL WAL → Debezium → Kafka → ES Consumer → Elasticsearch

Recovery: When drift is detected, the ES consumer supports

both incremental sync (process specific document IDs) and full

reindex. For full reindex without downtime, I use the **alias

swap pattern:**

1. Create new index: products_v2
2. Bulk index all data from DB → products_v2
3. Atomic alias swap: products → products_v2
4. Delete old index: products_v1

Users always query the alias products — they see no disruption.

👩‍💻 Thảo: The alias swap is exactly what we do here. Good.

👩‍💻 Thảo: Quick question — **how do you tune search relevance

in Elasticsearch?** A user searches "iPhone case" but gets iPhone

phones ranked above actual cases. How do you fix it?

👨 Khanh: Several levers:

1. Field boosting. Boost exact matches in the name field higher

than matches in description:

{
  "multi_match": {
    "query": "iPhone case",
    "fields": ["name^3", "description^1", "category^2"],
    "type": "best_fields"
  }
}

2. Category context. If the query "iPhone case" matches category

"Phone Accessories" with high confidence, add a filter boost:

{
  "function_score": {
    "query": { "match": { "name": "iPhone case" } },
    "functions": [
      { "filter": { "term": { "category": "accessories" } }, "weight": 5 }
    ]
  }
}

3. Behavioral signals. Boost products with higher click-through

rate and conversion rate for similar queries. This requires an

ML ranking layer on top of ES retrieval.

4. Synonym and intent mapping. Map "iPhone case" to also match

"phone cover," "phone protector" using custom analyzers with

synonym filters.

The general pattern: ES handles retrieval (finding candidates),

and an ML model handles ranking (ordering by relevance + business

value). Two-stage architecture.

PART B — SYSTEM DESIGN: Product Search Platform (35 min)

👨‍💻 Hao: Main event. Here's the design problem:

*"Design ShopNow's product search system. 150 million product listings

from 5 million sellers, updated constantly. 50,000 search queries/second

at peak. Users expect results in under 200ms. Support text search,

filters (price, category, rating, location), and sorting. The search

results must reflect product updates (price changes, out-of-stock)

within 60 seconds."*

👨 Khanh: [Takes 2 minutes to think]

Clarifying questions first:

  • Do we need personalized ranking? → "Yes, basic — boost items from nearby sellers"
  • Autocomplete/typeahead? → "Not in scope for today"
  • Multi-language support? → "Yes, 7 languages across SEA countries"

High-Level Architecture:

                    ┌───────────────┐
                    │   CDN / API   │
                    │   Gateway     │
                    └───────┬───────┘
                            │
                    ┌───────▼───────┐
                    │  Search API   │  (stateless, auto-scaled)
                    │  Service      │  (Java 17 + Spring Boot)
                    └──┬─────────┬──┘
                       │         │
              ┌────────▼──┐  ┌──▼──────────┐
              │   ES      │  │  Ranking    │
              │  Cluster  │  │  Service    │
              │ (retrieve)│  │ (re-rank ML)│
              └────────┬──┘  └─────────────┘
                       │
              ┌────────▼──────────────┐
              │  Indexing Pipeline    │
              │                      │
              │  Product DB (Aurora)  │
              │      ↓ CDC           │
              │  Kafka → ES Indexer  │
              └──────────────────────┘

Two-stage search architecture:

User Query → Search API
    │
    ▼
Stage 1: RETRIEVAL (Elasticsearch)
    - Full-text search + filters
    - Returns top 1000 candidates with basic BM25 scoring
    - Sub-100ms target
    │
    ▼
Stage 2: RANKING (ML Service)
    - Re-ranks 1000 candidates using ML model
    - Features: relevance, CTR, conversion rate, seller rating,
      delivery distance, profit margin
    - Returns final top 50 (paginated)
    - Sub-50ms target
    │
    ▼
Total: < 200ms end-to-end

Elasticsearch Index Design:

{
  "mappings": {
    "properties": {
      "product_id":    { "type": "keyword" },
      "name":          { "type": "text", "analyzer": "custom_sea" },
      "description":   { "type": "text", "analyzer": "custom_sea" },
      "category_path": { "type": "keyword" },
      "price":         { "type": "scaled_float", "scaling_factor": 100 },
      "currency":      { "type": "keyword" },
      "seller_id":     { "type": "keyword" },
      "seller_rating": { "type": "float" },
      "seller_city":   { "type": "keyword" },
      "seller_geo":    { "type": "geo_point" },
      "stock_status":  { "type": "keyword" },
      "sales_count":   { "type": "integer" },
      "images":        { "type": "keyword", "index": false },
      "updated_at":    { "type": "date" }
    }
  },
  "settings": {
    "number_of_shards": 30,
    "number_of_replicas": 2,
    "analysis": {
      "analyzer": {
        "custom_sea": {
          "type": "custom",
          "tokenizer": "icu_tokenizer",
          "filter": ["icu_folding", "lowercase", "synonym_filter"]
        }
      }
    }
  }
}

Why 30 shards?

  • 150M products × 1KB avg = 150GB data
  • Target 5GB per shard for optimal performance
  • 150GB / 5GB = 30 shards
  • With 2 replicas: 30 × 3 = 90 total shard copies
  • Distributed across 15 data nodes → 6 shards per node

👩‍💻 Thảo: Good shard sizing rationale. How do you handle the

60-second freshness requirement? Price changes, out-of-stock?

👨 Khanh: Near-real-time indexing pipeline:

Product DB (Aurora)
    │
    ▼ (CDC via Debezium, < 5s latency)
Kafka topic: "product.changes"
    │
    ▼
ES Indexer Service (consumer group)
    │
    ├── Batch updates using Bulk API (every 5 seconds)
    │   - Groups changes into batches of 500
    │   - Uses _bulk API for efficiency
    │
    └── Priority channel for critical changes
        - Stock = 0 (out-of-stock) → index immediately, no batching
        - Price drops > 20% → immediate (flash sale scenario)

With CDC latency (~5s) + batching interval (5s) + ES refresh

interval (default 1s) = approximately 11 seconds from DB change

to searchable. Well within the 60-second requirement.

For the out-of-stock edge case — I wouldn't rely solely on the

indexing pipeline. The Search API also does a **lightweight stock

check** at response time:

// After ES returns results, before sending to client
List<Product> results = esClient.search(query);
List<String> productIds = results.stream().map(Product::getId).toList();

// Fast Redis multi-get for stock status
Map<String, Boolean> stockMap = redisClient.mget(
    productIds.stream().map(id -> "stock:" + id).toList()
);

// Filter out-of-stock products
results.removeIf(p -> Boolean.FALSE.equals(stockMap.get("stock:" + p.getId())));

This Redis check adds ~2ms but guarantees no out-of-stock products

appear in results, even if ES index is a few seconds behind.

👨‍💻 Hao: That double-check pattern is smart. Worst case:

ES has stale data but Redis is real-time. The user never sees

out-of-stock products.

👨‍💻 Hao: What about handling 50K QPS on Elasticsearch?

Walk me through the scaling strategy.

👨 Khanh: Several layers:

1. Caching — the biggest win.

Most search queries are repetitive. "iPhone" is searched thousands

of times per minute. I'd add a query result cache in Redis:

Cache key: hash(query + filters + sort + page)
Cache value: serialized search results
TTL: 30 seconds (short enough for freshness, long enough for perf)

Expected cache hit rate: 60-70% for popular queries. This reduces

ES load from 50K to ~15-20K QPS.

2. Elasticsearch cluster sizing.

For 20K QPS on ES (after caching):

  • 15 data nodes, each handling ~1,300 QPS
  • Each node: 32GB RAM (50% to JVM heap = 16GB, 50% to OS page cache)
  • SSD storage for fast IO
  • 2 replicas per shard → reads distributed across 3 copies

3. Routing optimization.

For queries filtered by category, use custom routing:

PUT /products/_doc/123?routing=electronics

This ensures all "electronics" products are on the same shard.

Category-filtered queries hit 1 shard instead of 30 → 30x faster.

4. Pre-computed aggregations.

Facet counts (e.g., "Electronics: 5,234 results") are expensive.

Pre-compute popular filter counts in a background job → store in Redis.

Only compute real-time aggs for rare filter combinations.

Monitoring dashboard for search:

MetricTargetAlert
P99 search latency< 200ms> 300ms
Cache hit ratio> 60%< 40%
ES cluster CPU< 70%> 85%
Indexing lag (CDC to searchable)< 30s> 60s
Zero-result rate< 5%> 10%

PART C — LIVE CODING (25 min)

👨‍💻 Hao: Let's code. Here's the problem:

*"Implement a TopKFrequent class that processes a stream of

product search queries and can return the top-K most frequent

queries at any time."*

Interface:
  void record(String query)     — record a search query
  List<String> topK(int k)      — return top-K most frequent queries

Example:
  record("iPhone")
  record("Samsung")
  record("iPhone")
  record("laptop")
  record("iPhone")
  record("Samsung")
  topK(2) → ["iPhone", "Samsung"]  (3 and 2 occurrences)

Requirements: record() should be O(1) or O(log n).

topK(k) should be efficient for small k.

👨 Khanh: Let me think about the data structures.

I need two things:

  1. A way to count frequencies — HashMap<String, Integer>
  2. A way to efficiently find top-K — min-heap of size K

For record(): update the count in the HashMap. O(1).

For topK(k): I have two options.

  • Option A: Maintain a sorted structure at all times → O(log n) per record
  • Option B: Build the top-K on demand → O(n log k) per topK call

Since the problem says topK() should be efficient and record() is

called much more frequently (every search query), let me go with

Option A — maintain a more efficient structure.

Actually, the cleanest approach for a stream with frequent record()

and occasional topK(): use a HashMap for counts, and build the

heap on demand for topK(). With n=50K unique queries and k=10,

O(n log k) is fast enough.

import java.util.*;

public class TopKFrequent {
    private final Map<String, Integer> counts = new HashMap<>();

    public void record(String query) {
        counts.merge(query, 1, Integer::sum); // O(1)
    }

    public List<String> topK(int k) {
        // Min-heap of size k — keeps the k largest
        PriorityQueue<Map.Entry<String, Integer>> minHeap =
            new PriorityQueue<>(Comparator.comparingInt(Map.Entry::getValue));

        for (Map.Entry<String, Integer> entry : counts.entrySet()) {
            minHeap.offer(entry);
            if (minHeap.size() > k) {
                minHeap.poll(); // remove smallest
            }
        }

        // Extract results in descending order
        List<String> result = new ArrayList<>(k);
        while (!minHeap.isEmpty()) {
            result.add(minHeap.poll().getKey());
        }
        Collections.reverse(result); // most frequent first
        return result;
    }
}

Let me trace through the example:

record("iPhone")   → counts: {"iPhone": 1}
record("Samsung")  → counts: {"iPhone": 1, "Samsung": 1}
record("iPhone")   → counts: {"iPhone": 2, "Samsung": 1}
record("laptop")   → counts: {"iPhone": 2, "Samsung": 1, "laptop": 1}
record("iPhone")   → counts: {"iPhone": 3, "Samsung": 1, "laptop": 1}
record("Samsung")  → counts: {"iPhone": 3, "Samsung": 2, "laptop": 1}

topK(2):
  Process "iPhone":3 → heap: [(iPhone,3)]
  Process "Samsung":2 → heap: [(Samsung,2), (iPhone,3)]
  Process "laptop":1 → heap: [(laptop,1), ...], size=3 > k=2
    → poll laptop (smallest) → heap: [(Samsung,2), (iPhone,3)]

  Extract: Samsung, iPhone → reverse → ["iPhone", "Samsung"] ✓

Complexity:

  • record(): O(1) amortized
  • topK(k): O(n log k) where n = unique queries
  • Space: O(n) for the counts map

👨‍💻 Hao: Clean. Now — follow-up. Make it thread-safe for

a multi-threaded web server. Multiple threads call record()

concurrently.

👨 Khanh: For thread safety, I need to handle concurrent writes

to the counts map.

import java.util.concurrent.*;

public class TopKFrequent {
    private final ConcurrentHashMap<String, LongAdder> counts =
        new ConcurrentHashMap<>();

    public void record(String query) {
        // computeIfAbsent is atomic; LongAdder is lock-free
        counts.computeIfAbsent(query, k -> new LongAdder()).increment();
    }

    public List<String> topK(int k) {
        PriorityQueue<Map.Entry<String, LongAdder>> minHeap =
            new PriorityQueue<>(
                Comparator.comparingLong(e -> e.getValue().sum())
            );

        for (Map.Entry<String, LongAdder> entry : counts.entrySet()) {
            minHeap.offer(entry);
            if (minHeap.size() > k) {
                minHeap.poll();
            }
        }

        List<String> result = new ArrayList<>(k);
        while (!minHeap.isEmpty()) {
            result.add(minHeap.poll().getKey());
        }
        Collections.reverse(result);
        return result;
    }
}

Key changes:

  1. ConcurrentHashMap — thread-safe map with lock striping
  2. LongAdder instead of Integer — better than AtomicLong

for high-contention counters. It uses internal striped cells

to reduce CAS contention. increment() is lock-free.

  1. computeIfAbsent is atomic — no race between check-and-create

Why LongAdder over AtomicInteger?

With 50K QPS, many threads call record() concurrently. AtomicInteger

uses a single CAS variable — under high contention, threads spin

retrying. LongAdder distributes the count across multiple cells —

each thread updates a different cell, and sum() aggregates them.

Much better throughput under contention.

The topK() method reads a snapshot — it's eventually consistent.

The counts might change while iterating, but for a "trending searches"

feature, exact accuracy isn't required. The snapshot is good enough.

👩‍💻 Thảo: Excellent — LongAdder is exactly the right choice

for this use case. Most candidates use AtomicInteger and don't

know about the contention issue.

👨‍💻 Hao: Final follow-up. In production at ShopNow, this runs

across multiple servers. How would you distribute this?

👨 Khanh: For a distributed trending queries system:

Search API Servers (each records locally)
    │
    ├── Local ConcurrentHashMap (per instance, 5-second window)
    │
    ▼ (every 5 seconds)
Kafka topic: "query.counts"
    │   Payload: { "query": "iPhone", "count": 142, "server": "api-3" }
    │
    ▼
Aggregator Service (single consumer group)
    │
    ├── Merges counts from all servers
    ├── Maintains global top-K in Redis sorted set
    │     ZINCRBY trending:queries 142 "iPhone"
    │
    ▼
Redis Sorted Set: "trending:queries"
    │
    ▼
ZREVRANGE trending:queries 0 9  → Global top-10 trending queries

Each API server batches local counts and flushes to Kafka every

5 seconds — this reduces Kafka traffic by 1000x compared to

per-request events. The aggregator merges and maintains a global

Redis sorted set. Any server can read the global top-K from Redis

in O(log n + k) time.

👨‍💻 Hao: That's the production design. Perfect.

Any questions for us?

👨 Khanh: Yes — Hao, what's the most counterintuitive thing

you've learned about search at this scale?

👨‍💻 Hao: That less accuracy can be better. Early on, we

tried to make search results perfectly relevant. But users care

more about speed and freshness. A slightly less relevant result

that loads in 100ms beats a perfect result at 500ms every time.

We had to learn to optimize for user satisfaction, not search

precision.

👨 Khanh: That's a great insight. Thanks both!

📊 FULL INTERVIEW SCORECARD

Round 3 — Technical ✅

CategoryScoreNotes
ES distributed query flow10/10Full 4-phase trace: coordinate → query → merge → fetch
ES sync / alias swap10/10CDC + reconciliation + zero-downtime reindex
Search relevance tuning10/10Field boosting, category context, ML re-ranking
System Design: Search10/10Two-stage retrieval/ranking, shard sizing math, custom routing
Freshness guarantee10/10CDC pipeline + Redis stock check double-guard
Scaling to 50K QPS10/10Query cache, cluster sizing, routing, pre-computed aggs
Coding: TopKFrequent10/10Clean solution, correct trace, edge cases
Thread safety10/10LongAdder > AtomicInteger, eventually-consistent snapshot
Distributed extension10/10Local batch → Kafka → aggregator → Redis sorted set

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

📊 Complete 8-Interview Master Map

Mock #CompanyDomainSystem DesignCodingUnique Topic
2LogiTechLogisticsShipment trackingGroup AnagramsWebSocket real-time
3CloudMartE-CommerceFlash sale checkoutLRU CacheRedis atomic reservation
4VietBankBankingNotification systemValid ParenthesesSpring Security
5TravelNowTravelHotel bookingTwo Sum variantDesign Patterns
6HealthSyncHealthTechAPI rate limiterMerge IntervalsTesting strategy
7GrabTechPaymentsPayment pipelineRate LimiterIdempotency, Saga
8ShopNowMarketplaceProduct search 50K QPSTopK FrequentElasticsearch, LongAdder

🎭 Full Mock Interview #9 — Senior Software Engineer

Company: Amazon Web Services (Cloud Infrastructure, Seattle/HCMC)

Context: Amazon is hiring Senior SDEs for the AWS Lambda team.

The team builds serverless compute — millions of function invocations

per second globally. Office in HCMC, reporting to Seattle.

Why this matters: Amazon interviews are LP (Leadership Principles)

heavy. This mock teaches you the Amazon-specific format — different

from all previous mocks. Also covers a unique system design domain.

📞 ROUND 1 — PHONE SCREEN (45 min)

Setting: Tuesday, 10 AM. Amazon Chime. Senior SDE.

👨‍💻 Ryan (Sr SDE, Seattle): Hi Khanh, I'm Ryan on the Lambda

team. Today is 45 minutes — 15 min behavioral, 30 min coding.

We use our Leadership Principles to evaluate behavioral answers.

Ready?

👨 Khanh: Ready!

Behavioral — Leadership Principles (15 min)

👨‍💻 Ryan: First LP — Ownership. *"Tell me about a time

you took on something significant that was outside your area of

responsibility."*

👨 Khanh: At Sony/FPT, about 5 months ago.

[Situation] Our DevOps engineer left the company unexpectedly.

No one on the backend team knew the CI/CD pipeline deeply —

CodeCommit, CodeBuild, CodeDeploy. Deployments started failing,

and the team was blocked.

[Action] I volunteered to own the pipeline even though it wasn't

my role. I spent a weekend understanding the entire CodePipeline

configuration, fixed the immediate build failures (a dependency

version conflict in the buildspec), and documented every stage.

Over the next month, I improved the pipeline: added automated

test gates, reduced build time from 12 minutes to 7 by caching

Maven dependencies in S3, and created a Slack notification bot

for deployment status.

[Result] The team was unblocked within 2 days. When we hired

a new DevOps engineer a month later, I handed over a fully

documented, improved pipeline. My manager noted this as "ownership

beyond job scope" in my review.

[LP tie-back] I didn't wait for someone to assign this. The team

was blocked, I had enough AWS knowledge from my certification

studies, so I stepped up.

👨‍💻 Ryan: Good. Dive Deep. *"Tell me about a time you

used data to make a decision that others disagreed with."*

👨 Khanh: At Axon Active, during the Valiant banking migration.

[Situation] The team wanted to use a message queue (RabbitMQ)

for async communication between microservices. I believed Kafka

was the better choice, but the team was more familiar with RabbitMQ

and wanted to move fast.

[Action] Instead of arguing on opinions, I built a benchmark.

I set up both systems locally and tested:

  • Throughput: Kafka handled 100K msg/sec vs RabbitMQ's 20K msg/sec
  • Replay capability: Kafka retains messages, RabbitMQ deletes after ack
  • Consumer groups: Kafka's partition-based scaling vs RabbitMQ's

competing consumers

I presented three data points that mattered for banking:

  1. Audit trail: Banking regulations require message replay for

auditing. Kafka retains events; RabbitMQ doesn't without plugins.

  1. Scale projection: Current volume was 5K msg/sec, but projected

growth to 50K. RabbitMQ would hit a wall.

  1. Event sourcing: Our architecture plan included event sourcing

for transaction history. Kafka IS an event log; RabbitMQ is not.

[Result] The data convinced the team. We adopted Kafka. Six months

later, when we needed to replay events to rebuild a corrupted read

model, having Kafka's retention saved us from a major data incident.

[LP tie-back] Dive Deep means going beyond surface-level preference

to find the data that matters. The benchmark took me 2 days but

saved months of potential migration pain.

👨‍💻 Ryan: Strong answers. Now let's code.

Coding (30 min)

👨‍💻 Ryan: *"You're given a list of tasks with dependencies.

Each task has an ID and a list of prerequisite task IDs. Also,

each task has an estimated execution time.*

*Return the minimum total time to complete ALL tasks, assuming

you can run independent tasks in parallel on unlimited workers."*

Input:
  tasks = [
    {id: "A", time: 3, deps: []},
    {id: "B", time: 2, deps: ["A"]},
    {id: "C", time: 4, deps: ["A"]},
    {id: "D", time: 2, deps: ["B", "C"]}
  ]

Output: 9

Explanation:
  t=0:  Start A (3s)
  t=3:  A done → Start B (2s) AND C (4s) in parallel
  t=5:  B done (but D waits for C too)
  t=7:  C done → Start D (2s)
  t=9:  D done. Total = 9

👨 Khanh: Let me think about this step by step.

This is a DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph) scheduling problem. The

minimum time is determined by the critical path — the longest

path through the dependency graph.

Key insight: the earliest a task can START is the maximum finish

time of all its dependencies. Because we have unlimited parallelism,

independent tasks never block each other.

Algorithm:

  1. Topological sort to process tasks in valid order
  2. For each task: earliest_start[task] = max(earliest_finish[dep] for dep in deps)
  3. earliest_finish[task] = earliest_start[task] + task.time
  4. Answer = max of all earliest_finish values
public int minCompletionTime(List<Task> tasks) {
    // Build adjacency list and in-degree map
    Map<String, Task> taskMap = new HashMap<>();
    Map<String, List<String>> dependents = new HashMap<>(); // dep → tasks that need it
    Map<String, Integer> indegree = new HashMap<>();

    for (Task t : tasks) {
        taskMap.put(t.id, t);
        indegree.put(t.id, t.deps.size());
        dependents.putIfAbsent(t.id, new ArrayList<>());
        for (String dep : t.deps) {
            dependents.computeIfAbsent(dep, k -> new ArrayList<>()).add(t.id);
        }
    }

    // Earliest finish time for each task
    Map<String, Integer> finishTime = new HashMap<>();

    // Kahn's algorithm — process tasks in topological order
    Queue<String> queue = new ArrayDeque<>();
    for (var entry : indegree.entrySet()) {
        if (entry.getValue() == 0) {
            queue.offer(entry.getKey());
            finishTime.put(entry.getKey(), taskMap.get(entry.getKey()).time);
        }
    }

    int totalMin = 0;

    while (!queue.isEmpty()) {
        String current = queue.poll();
        int curFinish = finishTime.get(current);
        totalMin = Math.max(totalMin, curFinish);

        for (String next : dependents.getOrDefault(current, List.of())) {
            // Earliest start of 'next' = max finish of all its dependencies
            int nextStart = Math.max(
                finishTime.getOrDefault(next, 0),
                curFinish
            );
            finishTime.put(next, nextStart + taskMap.get(next).time);

            indegree.merge(next, -1, Integer::sum);
            if (indegree.get(next) == 0) {
                queue.offer(next);
            }
        }
    }

    return totalMin;
}

Let me trace through:

Process A (no deps): finishTime[A] = 0 + 3 = 3
  → Dependents: B, C
  → finishTime[B] = max(0, 3) + 2 = 5
  → finishTime[C] = max(0, 3) + 4 = 7

Process B (indegree=0 after A): finishTime[B] = 5
  → Dependent: D
  → finishTime[D] = max(0, 5) + 2 = 7

Process C (indegree=0 after A): finishTime[C] = 7
  → Dependent: D
  → finishTime[D] = max(7, 7) + 2 = 9  ← updated!
  → D's indegree = 0, add to queue

Process D: finishTime[D] = 9
  → No dependents

totalMin = max(3, 5, 7, 9) = 9 ✓

Complexity:

  • Time: O(V + E) — standard topological sort
  • Space: O(V + E) for the graph

Edge cases:

  • No dependencies → all tasks run in parallel → answer = max(task.time)
  • Linear chain (A→B→C) → sum of all times (no parallelism)
  • Cycle in dependencies → detect via indegree: if queue empties

before all tasks processed, there's a cycle

👨‍💻 Ryan: Clean solution, correct trace. How would you

detect the cycle case?

👨 Khanh: After the while loop, check:

if (finishTime.size() != tasks.size()) {
    throw new IllegalArgumentException(
        "Cycle detected — cannot schedule all tasks"
    );
}

If we processed fewer tasks than exist, some tasks had unresolvable

dependencies — a cycle. This is the standard cycle detection in

Kahn's algorithm.

👨‍💻 Ryan: Perfect. Moving you to the on-site rounds.

🎭 ROUND 2 — BEHAVIORAL (On-site, 55 min)

Setting: Thursday, 10 AM. Two interviewers, one is Bar Raiser.

👩 Mei (Bar Raiser, different team): Hi Khanh, I'm Mei — Bar

Raiser from the DynamoDB team. I have 4 LP questions.

👩 Mei: Earn Trust. *"Tell me about a time you had to deliver

bad news to your team or manager."*

👨 Khanh: At Sony, 3 months ago.

[Situation] I was assigned to estimate a feature — adding a new

authentication flow for a partner integration. The PM estimated

2 weeks. After analyzing the requirements, I realized it would

take at least 5 weeks due to security review requirements, a

third-party SDK integration with poor documentation, and the need

for backward compatibility with the existing auth flow.

[Action] I wrote a detailed breakdown:

ComponentPM EstimateMy EstimateWhy
SDK integration3 days8 daysSDK docs are incomplete, need reverse-engineering
Security review0 days5 daysRequired for any auth change, compliance
Backward compat2 days7 daysMust support both old and new flows simultaneously
Testing2 days5 daysSecurity-critical feature needs extensive test coverage

I presented this to my manager and the PM in a private meeting —

not in the sprint planning where it would catch them off guard.

I said: "I know this isn't what we hoped, but I'd rather give an

honest estimate now than miss the deadline later. Here are the

specific risks."

I also offered a compromise: "If we can defer backward compatibility

and do a hard cutover, I can do it in 3.5 weeks."

[Result] The PM chose the 5-week plan with backward compatibility.

We delivered on time. The PM later told me: "I'd rather have a

realistic estimate than a comfortable lie." That built trust that

carried forward into future planning sessions.

👩 Mei: Bias for Action. *"Tell me about a time you made

a decision without getting all the approvals you normally would."*

👨 Khanh: At TMA, on the Humana healthcare project.

[Situation] On a Friday evening, our production monitoring showed

memory usage climbing steadily — one service would OOM within 4 hours

based on the trend. Our tech lead was on vacation, unreachable.

[Action] I didn't wait for approval. I:

  1. Analyzed the memory growth pattern — it was a memory leak in a

connection pool that wasn't releasing HTTP connections after

timeout errors

  1. Applied a temporary fix: added a scheduled task to force-close

stale connections every 10 minutes

  1. Deployed the fix to production myself (our CI/CD allowed it with

2 approvals — I got a teammate to review)

  1. Wrote a detailed incident report and sent it to my tech lead

before going to bed

  1. On Monday, implemented the proper fix: configured the HTTP client

with evictIdleConnections(60, TimeUnit.SECONDS) and set

connection TTL

[Result] The service would have crashed at ~11 PM on Friday,

affecting weekend operations. My temporary fix prevented that.

When my tech lead returned, he said: "You made the right call.

If you'd waited for me, we'd have had an outage."

[LP tie-back] Bias for Action means acting when the cost of

inaction exceeds the cost of being wrong. A temporary fix deployed

fast is better than a perfect fix deployed after the crash.

👩 Mei: Insist on Highest Standards. *"Tell me about a time

you refused to compromise on quality."*

👨 Khanh: At Axon Active, Valiant banking platform.

[Situation] We were under pressure to deliver a payment

reconciliation feature by month-end. A teammate submitted a PR

that skipped error handling for edge cases — mismatched currencies,

partial payments, negative amounts. His reasoning: "These edge

cases are rare. We can add error handling in the next sprint."

[Action] I blocked the PR. In banking, "rare" edge cases with

money are unacceptable — even one miscalculated transaction could

trigger regulatory consequences.

I didn't just block it — I pair-programmed with him for 2 hours.

We identified 6 edge cases, wrote test cases for each, and

implemented proper error handling with specific error codes that

the operations team could act on.

I also added a validation layer that rejected malformed inputs

before they reached the business logic — a guard clause pattern

that made the code safer AND simpler.

[Result] We delivered 2 days late — I took responsibility for

the delay with the PM, explaining why. Two months later, one of

those "rare" edge cases (negative amount from a refund reversal)

actually occurred in production. Our error handling caught it,

logged it, and alerted operations. Without the validation, it

would have been a silent data corruption in the ledger.

👩 Mei: Learn and Be Curious. *"What's a technology

you've recently learned on your own? Why?"*

👨 Khanh: I recently deep-dived into GraalVM native images

for Spring Boot 3.

[Why] At Sony, our ECS services take 15-20 seconds to start.

During auto-scaling events, this means 15-20 seconds where the

new task isn't serving traffic. I wanted to understand if native

images could reduce this.

[What I did]

  • Read the GraalVM documentation and Spring Boot 3 native support docs
  • Built a prototype: took one of our smaller services and compiled

it as a native image

  • Benchmarked: startup went from 18 seconds to 0.8 seconds. Memory

dropped from 512MB to 180MB.

  • Discovered the trade-offs: build time went from 30 seconds to

8 minutes. Reflection-heavy libraries needed manual configuration.

Some Spring features don't work natively.

[Current status] I wrote an internal tech note recommending native

images for our lightweight proxy services (fast startup matters)

but NOT for our core business services (reflection-heavy, complex

Spring context). We're piloting it for one service.

[LP tie-back] I wasn't asked to research this. I identified a

scaling bottleneck and explored a solution on my own time. Even

though we're not adopting it broadly yet, the knowledge influenced

our architecture discussions.

👩 Mei: Four strong LP stories. Moving to the system design round.

💻 ROUND 3 — SYSTEM DESIGN (55 min)

Setting: Same day, 11 AM. Staff SDE.

👨‍💻 Jin (Staff SDE): Khanh, design problem:

*"Design a distributed task scheduler — like a simplified AWS Step

Functions. Users define workflows as DAGs of tasks. The scheduler

executes them respecting dependencies, retries on failure, and

scales to 10,000 concurrent workflows."*

👨 Khanh: [Takes 2 minutes]

Clarifying questions:

  • Task granularity? → "Each task is an HTTP call to a user-defined endpoint"
  • Max workflow size? → "Up to 100 tasks per workflow"
  • Retry policy? → "Up to 3 retries with exponential backoff"
  • SLA? → "Task should start within 5 seconds of becoming ready"

High-Level Architecture:

┌──────────────┐
│  Workflow     │  POST /workflows — define DAG
│  API         │  POST /workflows/{id}/start — trigger
└──────┬───────┘
       │
       ▼
┌──────────────┐     ┌──────────────┐
│  Scheduler   │────►│  Task Queue  │  (Kafka or SQS)
│  Service     │     │              │
│  (state mgmt)│     └──────┬───────┘
└──────────────┘            │
       │                    ▼
       │             ┌──────────────┐
       │             │  Worker Pool │  (auto-scaled)
       │             │  (execute    │
       │             │   HTTP calls)│
       │             └──────┬───────┘
       │                    │
       ▼                    ▼
┌──────────────┐     ┌──────────────┐
│  Workflow DB │     │  Result      │
│  (state,     │     │  Callback    │
│   DAG def)   │     │  → Scheduler │
└──────────────┘     └──────────────┘

Core Components:

1. Workflow Definition (stored in DB):

{
  "workflow_id": "wf-123",
  "tasks": {
    "extract":   { "endpoint": "http://...", "deps": [] },
    "transform": { "endpoint": "http://...", "deps": ["extract"] },
    "validate":  { "endpoint": "http://...", "deps": ["extract"] },
    "load":      { "endpoint": "http://...", "deps": ["transform", "validate"] }
  }
}

2. Scheduler Service — the brain:

// When a task completes, evaluate which tasks are now ready
public void onTaskComplete(String workflowId, String taskId, TaskResult result) {
    Workflow wf = workflowDb.get(workflowId);

    if (result.isFailure() && wf.getRetryCount(taskId) < 3) {
        // Retry with exponential backoff
        long delay = (long) Math.pow(2, wf.getRetryCount(taskId)) * 1000;
        taskQueue.scheduleWithDelay(workflowId, taskId, delay);
        wf.incrementRetry(taskId);
    } else if (result.isFailure()) {
        wf.markTaskFailed(taskId);
        wf.markWorkflowFailed(); // or continue other branches
    } else {
        wf.markTaskCompleted(taskId);

        // Find newly ready tasks (all deps completed)
        List<String> readyTasks = wf.getReadyTasks();
        for (String ready : readyTasks) {
            taskQueue.enqueue(workflowId, ready);
        }

        if (wf.allTasksCompleted()) {
            wf.markWorkflowCompleted();
            notifyUser(workflowId, "COMPLETED");
        }
    }

    workflowDb.save(wf);
}

3. Database Choice:

DataStorageWhy
Workflow definitionsDynamoDBKey-value access, high throughput
Workflow execution stateAurora PostgreSQLACID for state transitions
Task queueSQS with delayBuilt-in retry, dead-letter, delay support
Execution historyDynamoDB + S3Append-only logs, cheap long-term storage

Why Aurora for state, not DynamoDB? The scheduler does

read-modify-write on workflow state: "read current state, check

dependencies, update task status." This needs optimistic locking

(WHERE version = expected_version) to prevent race conditions

when two tasks complete simultaneously. Aurora's ACID transactions

handle this cleanly.

4. Scaling to 10K concurrent workflows:

  • 10K workflows × avg 20 tasks = 200K task executions
  • Scheduler Service: stateless, 4-5 instances behind LB
  • Workers: auto-scale based on SQS queue depth
  • DB: Aurora with read replicas for status queries

5. Distributed locking for state transitions:

When two tasks in the same workflow complete simultaneously, both

trigger onTaskComplete. Without coordination, both might try to

enqueue the same downstream task.

Solution: Optimistic locking in the DB:

UPDATE workflow_tasks
SET status = 'COMPLETED', version = version + 1
WHERE workflow_id = ? AND task_id = ? AND version = ?
-- If version mismatch → another thread already updated → retry

👨‍💻 Jin: Good design. What happens if a worker dies mid-execution?

👨 Khanh: SQS visibility timeout handles this. When a worker

picks up a task, SQS hides it for N seconds. If the worker doesn't

delete (acknowledge) the message within that time, SQS makes it

visible again → another worker picks it up.

We set visibility timeout to 2× the max expected task duration.

Combined with the idempotency key on the HTTP endpoint call,

re-execution is safe.

After 3 failed attempts, SQS moves the message to a Dead Letter

Queue. A monitoring alarm fires, and the scheduler marks that task

and workflow as FAILED_NEEDS_ATTENTION.

👨‍💻 Jin: What about hot partitions? What if one workflow has

100 tasks all becoming ready at once?

👨 Khanh: That creates a burst on the scheduler for that one

workflow. Two mitigations:

  1. Batch enqueue: Instead of enqueuing one at a time, batch all

ready tasks into a single SQS SendMessageBatch call (up to 10).

  1. Rate limit per workflow: No single workflow can consume more

than 20% of worker capacity. Implemented via a semaphore per

workflow_id in the scheduler.

This prevents one large workflow from starving others — a fairness

guarantee.

👨‍💻 Jin: Excellent. Strong design, good failure handling.

📊 FULL INTERVIEW SCORECARD

Phone Screen ✅

CategoryScoreNotes
LP: Ownership10/10Took over CI/CD pipeline, improved build time, documented
LP: Dive Deep10/10Kafka vs RabbitMQ benchmark with 3 banking-specific data points
Coding: Task scheduler10/10Topological sort, correct trace, cycle detection

Behavioral (On-site) ✅

CategoryScoreNotes
LP: Earn Trust10/10Honest estimate with data, offered compromise
LP: Bias for Action10/10Fixed memory leak on Friday night without waiting for TL
LP: Highest Standards10/10Blocked PR in banking code, pair-programmed fix, took delay responsibility
LP: Learn & Be Curious10/10GraalVM native image research with benchmark data

System Design ✅

CategoryScoreNotes
Scheduler architecture10/10Clear separation: API → Scheduler → Queue → Workers
State management10/10Optimistic locking for concurrent task completion
Failure handling10/10SQS visibility timeout + DLQ + idempotency
Scaling & fairness10/10Per-workflow rate limiting, batch enqueue

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE — SENIOR SDE

📊 Complete 9-Interview Master Map

#CompanyDomainSystem DesignCodingUnique Focus
2LogiTechLogisticsShipment trackingGroup AnagramsWebSocket
3CloudMartE-CommerceFlash saleLRU CacheRedis atomic
4VietBankBankingNotificationsValid ParenthesesSpring Security
5TravelNowTravelHotel bookingTwo Sum variantDesign Patterns
6HealthSyncHealthTechRate limiterMerge IntervalsTesting
7GrabTechPaymentsPayment pipelineRate LimiterIdempotency, Saga
8ShopNowMarketplaceProduct searchTopK FrequentElasticsearch, LongAdder
