🎭 Full Mock Interview #10 — Senior Software Engineer

Company: Google Cloud (GCP, Singapore/Remote)

Context: Google Cloud is hiring Senior SWEs for the Cloud Run team

(serverless containers). Google interviews are coding-heavy with a unique

"Googleyness" behavioral assessment. This mock teaches the Google format.

Domains covered so far: Logistics(2), E-Commerce(3), Banking(4),

Travel(5), HealthTech(6), Payments(7), Marketplace(8), Cloud/AWS(9).

Mock #10 = Google-style — different interview structure from all others.

💻 ROUND 1 — CODING (45 min)

Setting: Google Meet + Google Docs (no IDE, no autocomplete).

👩‍💻 Priya (L5 SWE): Hi Khanh! Today is pure coding. Two problems,

45 minutes total. Think out loud. I care about your approach more than

syntax perfection. We use Google Docs — no autocomplete.

Problem 1: Design a Time-Based Key-Value Store (25 min)

👩‍💻 Priya: "Create a TimeMap class:

  • set(key, value, timestamp) — stores key-value at given timestamp
  • get(key, timestamp) — returns the value with the largest timestamp ≤ given timestamp"
set("config", "v1", 1)
set("config", "v2", 5)
set("config", "v3", 10)
get("config", 6)   → "v2"  (largest timestamp ≤ 6 is 5)
get("config", 15)  → "v3"
get("config", 0)   → ""    (no timestamp ≤ 0)

👨 Khanh: Let me analyze the access pattern.

set() always receives increasing timestamps (I'll confirm — yes?).

→ "Yes, timestamps are strictly increasing for the same key."

Since timestamps are sorted per key, I can use binary search on

get() to find the largest timestamp ≤ target. This gives O(log n) reads.

Data structure: HashMap<String, List<Pair<Integer, String>>> —

each key maps to a sorted list of (timestamp, value) pairs.

class TimeMap {
    private final Map<String, List<int[]>> map = new HashMap<>();
    // Using int[] for timestamp index, storing values separately
    private final Map<String, List<String>> values = new HashMap<>();
    private final Map<String, List<Integer>> times = new HashMap<>();

    public void set(String key, String value, int timestamp) {
        times.computeIfAbsent(key, k -> new ArrayList<>()).add(timestamp);
        values.computeIfAbsent(key, k -> new ArrayList<>()).add(value);
    }

    public String get(String key, int timestamp) {
        if (!times.containsKey(key)) return "";

        List<Integer> ts = times.get(key);
        // Binary search: find rightmost index where ts[i] <= timestamp
        int lo = 0, hi = ts.size() - 1, result = -1;

        while (lo <= hi) {
            int mid = lo + (hi - lo) / 2;
            if (ts.get(mid) <= timestamp) {
                result = mid;   // candidate — try to find a later one
                lo = mid + 1;
            } else {
                hi = mid - 1;
            }
        }

        if (result == -1) return "";
        return values.get(key).get(result);
    }
}

Trace:

set("config","v1",1)  → times: [1],     values: [v1]
set("config","v2",5)  → times: [1,5],   values: [v1,v2]
set("config","v3",10) → times: [1,5,10], values: [v1,v2,v3]

get("config", 6):
  Binary search for largest ≤ 6 in [1,5,10]
  lo=0,hi=2: mid=1, ts[1]=5 ≤ 6 → result=1, lo=2
  lo=2,hi=2: mid=2, ts[2]=10 > 6 → hi=1
  loop ends. result=1 → values[1] = "v2" ✓

get("config", 0):
  Binary search for largest ≤ 0 in [1,5,10]
  lo=0,hi=2: mid=1, ts[1]=5 > 0 → hi=0
  lo=0,hi=0: mid=0, ts[0]=1 > 0 → hi=-1
  result=-1 → "" ✓

Complexity: set() O(1), get() O(log n). Space O(total entries).

👩‍💻 Priya: Clean. Follow-up — what if timestamps are NOT

guaranteed to be increasing?

👨 Khanh: Then the list isn't naturally sorted. Two options:

  1. Use a TreeMap<Integer, String> per key — floorEntry(timestamp) gives the answer in O(log n). Both set and get become O(log n).
  2. Keep ArrayList but use insertion sort (binary search to find position, insert). O(log n) search + O(n) shift though.

TreeMap is cleaner:

Map<String, TreeMap<Integer, String>> map = new HashMap<>();

public void set(String key, String value, int timestamp) {
    map.computeIfAbsent(key, k -> new TreeMap<>()).put(timestamp, value);
}

public String get(String key, int timestamp) {
    TreeMap<Integer, String> tree = map.get(key);
    if (tree == null) return "";
    Map.Entry<Integer, String> entry = tree.floorEntry(timestamp);
    return entry == null ? "" : entry.getValue();
}

floorEntry() returns the entry with largest key ≤ given key. Exactly what we need, built into Java's TreeMap.

👩‍💻 Priya: Perfect — knowing floorEntry shows Java depth.

Problem 2: Find All Anagram Positions (20 min)

👩‍💻 Priya: *"Given strings s and p, find all start indices

in s where an anagram of p begins."*

Input: s = "cbaebabacd", p = "abc"
Output: [0, 6]
Explanation: "cba" at index 0, "bac" at index 6

👨 Khanh: This is a sliding window with a frequency match.

Window size is fixed = p.length(). I slide a window of that size

across s and check if the character frequencies match p.

Instead of comparing frequency arrays every step (O(26) per step),

I'll maintain a matchCount variable — number of characters where

the window frequency equals p's frequency. When matchCount == 26,

all characters match → it's an anagram.

public List<Integer> findAnagrams(String s, String p) {
    List<Integer> result = new ArrayList<>();
    if (s.length() < p.length()) return result;

    int[] pFreq = new int[26];
    int[] wFreq = new int[26]; // window frequency
    for (char c : p.toCharArray()) pFreq[c - 'a']++;

    int matches = 0;
    // Count initial matches (how many of 26 chars have equal freq)
    // Initially all zeros match zeros — we'll adjust as we build window

    for (int i = 0; i < s.length(); i++) {
        // Add right character to window
        int rIdx = s.charAt(i) - 'a';
        wFreq[rIdx]++;
        if (wFreq[rIdx] == pFreq[rIdx]) matches++;
        else if (wFreq[rIdx] == pFreq[rIdx] + 1) matches--;

        // Remove left character when window exceeds p.length()
        if (i >= p.length()) {
            int lIdx = s.charAt(i - p.length()) - 'a';
            if (wFreq[lIdx] == pFreq[lIdx]) matches++;
            // Wait — need to remove BEFORE checking
            // Let me reconsider the order
        }

        // Actually, let me use a cleaner approach:
        // Remove left first, check matches after
    }

    // Let me rewrite cleanly:
    result.clear();
    wFreq = new int[26];

    // Build initial window of size p.length()
    for (int i = 0; i < p.length(); i++) {
        wFreq[s.charAt(i) - 'a']++;
    }
    if (Arrays.equals(wFreq, pFreq)) result.add(0);

    // Slide window
    for (int i = p.length(); i < s.length(); i++) {
        wFreq[s.charAt(i) - 'a']++;                    // add right
        wFreq[s.charAt(i - p.length()) - 'a']--;       // remove left
        if (Arrays.equals(wFreq, pFreq)) result.add(i - p.length() + 1);
    }

    return result;
}

Trace with s="cbaebabacd", p="abc":

pFreq: a=1, b=1, c=1

Window [0,2] "cba": wFreq a=1,b=1,c=1 → matches pFreq → add 0
Slide to [1,3] "bae": remove c, add e → a=1,b=1,e=1 → no match
Slide to [2,4] "aeb": remove b, add b → a=1,b=1,e=1 → no
Slide to [3,5] "eba": remove a, add a → a=1,b=1,e=1 → no
Slide to [4,6] "bab": remove e, add b → a=1,b=2 → no
Slide to [5,7] "aba": remove b, add a → a=2,b=1 → no
Slide to [6,8] "bac": remove a, add c → a=1,b=1,c=1 → match → add 6
Slide to [7,9] "acd": remove b, add d → a=1,c=1,d=1 → no

Result: [0, 6] ✓

Complexity: O(n) time where n = s.length(). O(1) space (fixed 26-char array).

The Arrays.equals comparison is O(26) = O(1). Total: O(26n) = O(n).

👩‍💻 Priya: Good recovery — you caught the bug in your first

approach and rewrote cleanly. That's what we want to see.

🎭 ROUND 2 — GOOGLEYNESS & LEADERSHIP (45 min)

Setting: Google Meet. Engineering Manager.

👨‍💼 Kevin (EM): Hi Khanh! This round is about how you collaborate,

handle ambiguity, and grow. Not Amazon LP-style — we focus on

collaboration and intellectual humility.

👨‍💼 Kevin: *"Tell me about a time you changed your mind about

a technical decision after someone challenged your approach."*

👨 Khanh: At Sony, during the design of our caching layer.

I initially proposed write-through caching — write to Redis and

Aurora simultaneously. My reasoning: strong consistency, no stale

reads. I was confident this was correct for our SLA requirements.

A junior engineer, Huy, raised a concern in the design review:

"Write-through doubles our write latency. Our P99 write latency

is already 150ms against Aurora. Adding Redis in the hot path

would push it to 200ms+. Since our SLA is 250ms, that leaves

almost no headroom."

I almost dismissed it — write consistency felt more important. But

Huy had done the math. He was right.

I changed my recommendation to cache-aside with short TTL.

I publicly credited Huy in the design doc: "Huy identified the

latency risk — this design incorporates his analysis."

Two things I learned: first, the junior engineer was closer to

the performance data than I was. Seniority doesn't equal correctness.

Second, publicly crediting his contribution encouraged the whole

team to speak up in design reviews. That culture of constructive

challenge is more valuable than any single design decision.

👨‍💼 Kevin: That's Googleyness right there — intellectual humility

and creating an environment where the best idea wins regardless

of who says it.

👨‍💼 Kevin: *"Describe a project where the requirements kept

changing. How did you deliver?"*

👨 Khanh: The Sony partner authentication integration.

Over 6 weeks, the requirements changed 4 times:

  • Week 1: "Support OAuth2 for one partner"
  • Week 2: "Actually, make it work for any partner" (generic)
  • Week 3: "Add SAML support too"
  • Week 5: "The first partner now needs custom claims mapping"

My approach was three-layered defense against changing requirements:

First, architecture for extensibility. After the second change,

I realized more changes were coming. I refactored to a Strategy

pattern: AuthenticationStrategy interface with OAuth2Strategy,

SAMLStrategy implementations. Adding a new auth method became

a new class, not a modification to existing code.

Second, incremental delivery. Instead of waiting for "final"

requirements, I delivered working software every week. OAuth2

working in week 2, SAML added in week 3, custom claims in week 5.

Each increment was tested, deployed, and usable.

Third, explicit scope management. For each change, I told the

PM: "This adds 3 days. Here's what I'll defer to fit the timeline."

I never just absorbed scope changes silently — that leads to burnout

and missed deadlines.

Result: Delivered on the original timeline. The Strategy pattern

has since been used to add 2 more auth providers with zero changes

to existing code.

👨‍💼 Kevin: Great — the Strategy pattern refactoring shows you

can smell when "one-off" changes are actually a pattern. That's

senior engineering judgment.

💻 ROUND 3 — SYSTEM DESIGN (45 min)

👨‍💻 Sanjay (Staff SWE): Design problem:

*"Design a real-time group chat system — like Google Chat or Slack.

Support 1:1 and group conversations (up to 500 members). 50M DAU,

message delivery within 1 second, support offline message sync."*

👨 Khanh: [2 minutes to think]

Requirements:

  • Send messages (text, 4KB max)
  • Real-time delivery to online users (< 1 second)
  • Offline users receive messages when they reconnect
  • Groups up to 500 members
  • Message ordering within a conversation
  • Read receipts

Estimations:

50M DAU × 40 messages/day = 2B messages/day
2B / 86400s ≈ 23K messages/second
Peak: ~70K messages/second
Storage: 2B × 4KB = 8TB/day

High-Level Architecture:

┌──────────┐   WebSocket   ┌──────────────┐
│  Mobile  │◄─────────────►│  Gateway     │
│  / Web   │               │  Service     │
│  Client  │               │  (WS mgmt)  │
└──────────┘               └──────┬───────┘
                                  │
                           ┌──────▼───────┐
                           │   Message    │
                           │   Service   │
                           └──┬──────┬───┘
                              │      │
                    ┌─────────▼┐  ┌──▼──────────┐
                    │  Kafka   │  │  Message DB  │
                    │ (fanout) │  │  (Cassandra) │
                    └─────────┬┘  └─────────────┘
                              │
                    ┌─────────▼──────────┐
                    │  Delivery Service  │
                    │  (routes to the    │
                    │   right Gateway)   │
                    └────────────────────┘

Message Flow — Send:

1. Client A sends message via WebSocket → Gateway
2. Gateway → Message Service
3. Message Service:
   a. Generate message_id (Snowflake ID — time-ordered, globally unique)
   b. Write to Cassandra (persistent storage)
   c. Publish to Kafka topic: "chat.{conversation_id}"
4. Delivery Service consumes from Kafka
   a. For each member in the conversation:
      - If ONLINE: find which Gateway holds their WebSocket → push
      - If OFFLINE: skip (they'll sync later)
5. Gateway pushes message to recipient's WebSocket

Database: Why Cassandra?

RequirementCassandra Fit
70K writes/secExcellent write throughput
Time-ordered queriesPartition key: conversation_id, clustering key: message_id (time-ordered)
Scale horizontallyLinear scaling — add nodes
Read patternAlways "messages in conversation X after time Y" — partition-local

Schema:

CREATE TABLE messages (
    conversation_id UUID,
    message_id      BIGINT,      -- Snowflake ID (time-ordered)
    sender_id       UUID,
    content         TEXT,
    created_at      TIMESTAMP,
    PRIMARY KEY (conversation_id, message_id)
) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (message_id DESC);

Querying: SELECT * FROM messages WHERE conversation_id = ? AND message_id > ? LIMIT 50 — gets latest messages after a cursor. Partition-local, O(1) seek.

Online/Offline & Presence:

┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  User-to-Gateway mapping (Redis)         │
│                                          │
│  "user:u123" → "gateway-3:ws-conn-456"  │
│  "user:u456" → "gateway-1:ws-conn-789"  │
│  TTL: auto-expire on disconnect          │
└──────────────────────────────────────────┘

When Delivery Service needs to push to user U:

  1. Check Redis: is U online? Which gateway?
  2. If online → send to that Gateway via internal gRPC
  3. If not in Redis → user is offline → message already in Cassandra → they'll sync

Offline Sync:

When user reconnects:

  1. Client sends last_seen_message_id per conversation
  2. Server queries Cassandra: WHERE message_id > last_seen
  3. Returns missed messages in batches of 50

Group Message Fanout (up to 500 members):

Small groups (≤ 50): Write-time fanout. Delivery Service iterates

all members and pushes individually. At 70K msg/sec × 50 = 3.5M

pushes/sec — manageable.

Large groups (50-500): Read-time fanout with push notification.

Don't push to all 500 WebSockets. Instead:

  • Push to actively-viewing users (tracked by last interaction)
  • Send push notification to others
  • Full message loaded when they open the conversation

👨‍💻 Sanjay: Good design. How do you guarantee message ordering?

👨 Khanh: Three levels:

  1. Per-conversation ordering: Snowflake IDs are time-ordered.

Kafka partitioned by conversation_id → single partition per

conversation → ordered consumption.

  1. Display ordering: Client sorts by message_id before rendering.

Even if messages arrive out of order via WebSocket, the client

inserts them in the correct position.

  1. Conflict: If two users send at the exact same millisecond,

Snowflake IDs include a sequence number — guaranteed unique and

ordered even within the same millisecond.

👨‍💻 Sanjay: What if a Gateway server crashes with 10K WebSocket

connections?

👨 Khanh: Those connections drop. Clients auto-reconnect to

another Gateway (load balancer routes them). On reconnect:

  1. New Gateway registers user in Redis
  2. Client triggers offline sync using last_seen_message_id
  3. Messages stored in Cassandra are never lost — only the

real-time push path is interrupted

Recovery time: ~2-3 seconds (client reconnect + sync). No data loss.

👨‍💻 Sanjay: Strong design. Thanks Khanh!

📊 FULL SCORECARD

RoundCategoryScoreHighlight
Coding 1TimeMap (Binary Search)10/10Knew TreeMap.floorEntry() for follow-up
Coding 2Find Anagrams (Sliding Window)9/10Caught own bug, rewrote cleanly
GoogleynessChanged mind on caching10/10Credited junior engineer publicly
GoogleynessChanging requirements10/10Strategy pattern + incremental delivery
System DesignReal-time group chat10/10Cassandra + Kafka + WebSocket + offline sync

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

📊 Complete 10-Interview Master Map

#CompanyDomainSystem DesignCodingUnique Focus
2LogiTechLogisticsShipment trackingGroup AnagramsWebSocket
3CloudMartE-CommerceFlash saleLRU CacheRedis atomic
4VietBankBankingNotificationsValid ParenthesesSpring Security
5TravelNowTravelHotel bookingTwo Sum variantDesign Patterns
6HealthSyncHealthTechRate limiterMerge IntervalsTesting
7GrabTechPaymentsPayment pipelineRate LimiterIdempotency, Saga
8ShopNowMarketplaceProduct searchTopK FrequentElasticsearch
9AmazonCloudTask schedulerDAG schedulingLeadership Principles
10GoogleMessagingGroup chat systemTimeMap + AnagramsCassandra, Googleyness

🎭 Full Mock Interview #11 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: PayWave (Digital Wallet / Neobank, Series C, Vietnam)

Context: PayWave is a digital wallet like MoMo/ZaloPay — P2P transfers,

bill payments, QR payments at merchants. 30M users, 5M transactions/day.

Engineering team of 200. Hiring for the Ledger & Transactions team.

Why unique: First fintech/wallet mock. Covers double-entry bookkeeping,

money transfer atomicity, fraud detection — different from GrabTech (#7)

which was payment gateway integration. This is the MONEY side.

📞 ROUND 1 — PHONE SCREEN + CODING (50 min)

Setting: Monday, 2 PM. Zoom. Senior Staff Engineer.

👨‍💻 Tùng (Sr Staff Eng): Hi Khanh. I'm Tùng, I lead the

transaction platform. 15 minutes discussion, 35 minutes coding. Let's go.

👨‍💻 Tùng: Your CV shows banking at Axon Active and payments

exposure at Sony. **What's the most important thing you learned about

handling money in software?**

👨 Khanh: The most important lesson: **money operations must be

idempotent and auditable, always.**

At Axon Active, I learned that every financial operation must have

a unique transaction ID that prevents double-processing. And every

state change to money — credit, debit, refund — must produce an

immutable audit trail. You never UPDATE a transaction record; you

INSERT a new ledger entry.

The second lesson: use BigDecimal, never double. Floating

point arithmetic is the silent killer. 0.1 + 0.2 != 0.3 in

floating point. In banking, that rounding error across millions

of transactions means real money discrepancy.

// WRONG
double balance = 0.1 + 0.2; // 0.30000000000000004

// CORRECT
BigDecimal balance = new BigDecimal("0.1").add(new BigDecimal("0.2"));
// 0.3 exactly

👨‍💻 Tùng: Good fundamentals. Let's code.

Coding: Detect Suspicious Transfer Patterns (35 min)

👨‍💻 Tùng: *"Given a list of money transfers, detect 'circular

transfers' — chains where money flows A→B→C→...→A, returning to the

original sender. These patterns indicate potential money laundering.*

*Each transfer has: from, to, amount. Find all users involved

in any circular transfer chain."*

Input:
  [("A","B"), ("B","C"), ("C","A"),   ← cycle: A→B→C→A
   ("D","E"), ("E","F"),              ← no cycle
   ("G","H"), ("H","I"), ("I","G")]   ← cycle: G→H→I→G

Output: {"A","B","C","G","H","I"}

👨 Khanh: This is cycle detection in a directed graph. I need to

find all nodes that participate in any cycle.

Approach: DFS with 3-color marking:

  • WHITE (0): unvisited
  • GRAY (1): currently in DFS stack (being explored)
  • BLACK (2): fully processed

When I visit a GRAY node from another GRAY node, I've found a cycle.

I need to track the path to identify all nodes in the cycle.

public Set<String> findCircularTransfers(List<String[]> transfers) {
    // Build adjacency list
    Map<String, List<String>> graph = new HashMap<>();
    for (String[] t : transfers) {
        graph.computeIfAbsent(t[0], k -> new ArrayList<>()).add(t[1]);
    }

    Map<String, Integer> color = new HashMap<>(); // 0=white, 1=gray, 2=black
    Map<String, String> parent = new HashMap<>();  // track DFS path
    Set<String> inCycle = new HashSet<>();

    for (String node : graph.keySet()) {
        color.putIfAbsent(node, 0);
    }

    for (String node : graph.keySet()) {
        if (color.getOrDefault(node, 0) == 0) {
            dfs(node, graph, color, parent, inCycle);
        }
    }

    return inCycle;
}

private void dfs(String u, Map<String, List<String>> graph,
                 Map<String, Integer> color, Map<String, String> parent,
                 Set<String> inCycle) {
    color.put(u, 1); // GRAY — in current path

    for (String v : graph.getOrDefault(u, List.of())) {
        if (color.getOrDefault(v, 0) == 1) {
            // Found cycle! Trace back from u to v to collect all nodes
            String curr = u;
            while (!curr.equals(v)) {
                inCycle.add(curr);
                curr = parent.get(curr);
            }
            inCycle.add(v); // add the cycle start too
        } else if (color.getOrDefault(v, 0) == 0) {
            parent.put(v, u);
            dfs(v, graph, color, parent, inCycle);
        }
    }

    color.put(u, 2); // BLACK — fully processed
}

Trace:

DFS from A:
  A(gray) → B(gray) → C(gray) → A is GRAY!
    Cycle found! Trace: C → parent[C]=B → parent[B]=A → reached A
    inCycle = {C, B, A} ✓

DFS from D:
  D(gray) → E(gray) → F(gray) → no GRAY neighbor → F=black, E=black, D=black
  No cycle ✓

DFS from G:
  G(gray) → H(gray) → I(gray) → G is GRAY!
    Cycle found! Trace: I → H → G → reached G
    inCycle = {A, B, C, I, H, G} ✓

Complexity: O(V + E) time, O(V) space — standard DFS.

Edge cases:

  • Self-loop (A→A): GRAY→GRAY detected, adds A
  • Multiple cycles sharing nodes: all cycle nodes collected
  • No cycles: returns empty set
  • Disconnected graph: DFS from each unvisited node handles it

👨‍💻 Tùng: Clean. In production at PayWave, we run this as a

batch job every hour on the last 24 hours of transactions. How

would you optimize it for 5 million daily transfers?

👨 Khanh: For 5M transactions/day:

  1. Pre-filter: Only include transfers above a threshold amount

(money laundering uses significant amounts, not $1 transfers).

This might reduce to 500K edges.

  1. Incremental processing: Instead of rebuilding the full graph

hourly, maintain a rolling window. Add new edges, remove expired

ones. Use Kafka Streams for real-time graph updates.

  1. Partition by geography: Users mostly transact within their

city. Build separate graphs per region, reducing each graph size.

  1. Flag, don't block: The algorithm flags suspicious users for

human review. False positives are expensive (blocking legitimate

users), so the compliance team makes the final call.

🎭 ROUND 2 — BEHAVIORAL (40 min)

👩 Lan (EM): Hi Khanh! Three questions focused on how you

handle high-stakes, high-pressure situations. In fintech, mistakes

cost real money.

👩 Lan: *"Tell me about a time you had to balance speed of delivery

with code quality in a high-stakes environment."*

👨 Khanh: At Axon Active, Valiant banking platform.

[Situation] A regulatory deadline required us to implement new

KYC (Know Your Customer) verification within 6 weeks. Missing

the deadline meant potential fines for the bank. But the existing

identity verification module was tightly coupled — changing it

safely required careful refactoring.

[Action] I proposed a parallel implementation strategy:

Week 1-2: Build the new KYC flow as a completely separate service,

not touching the existing code. Clean interface, full test coverage.

Week 3-4: Integration testing against sandbox APIs. I wrote 40+

test cases covering normal flow, edge cases, and failure scenarios

(provider timeout, invalid documents, partial matches).

Week 5: Deploy behind a feature flag. Route 5% of new registrations

to the new flow. Monitor for 3 days.

Week 6: Ramp to 100%. Keep old flow as fallback for 2 more weeks.

The key insight: I didn't compromise on quality. Instead, I found

a way to deliver fast AND safely by isolating the new code from

the old system. No risky refactoring of existing code under deadline

pressure.

[Result] Delivered 4 days before the deadline. Zero incidents

during rollout. The compliance team was impressed that we had such

thorough test coverage for an audit-critical feature.

👩 Lan: *"Tell me about a time a teammate was consistently

underperforming. How did you handle it?"*

👨 Khanh: At TMA, on the Humana project.

[Situation] A mid-level engineer on the team was consistently

delivering PRs with issues: missing error handling, no tests,

and the same review feedback repeated sprint after sprint.

The team was frustrated because reviewing his code took 3-4x

longer than anyone else's.

[Task] I wasn't his manager, but I was the senior engineer

whose review he needed. I had to help him improve without

damaging the relationship or overstepping my role.

[Action] First, I had a private, empathetic conversation.

Not "your code has problems" but "I noticed you're getting similar

review feedback. Is there something blocking you? Do you feel you

have enough context on our patterns?"

He was honest: he came from a startup where there were no code

reviews and no testing culture. He didn't know WHAT "good" looked

like in our context.

Second, I created a PR checklist — a simple markdown file:

"Before requesting review, verify: ✅ error handling for external

calls, ✅ at least one test per public method, ✅ no TODO without

a ticket number, ✅ logging at entry/exit of service methods."

Third, I pair-programmed with him once a week for a month. Not

reviewing his finished code — building it together. This was the

most time-intensive but most effective step.

[Result] After 6 weeks, his PR quality matched the team average.

Review time dropped from 2 hours to 30 minutes. He thanked me

privately — said no one had invested in his growth before.

The checklist became a team standard that all new engineers use

during onboarding.

👩 Lan: *"How do you approach on-call for a financial system

where downtime means users can't access their money?"*

👨 Khanh: Financial on-call is different from other domains

because the stakes are existential — users trust you with their money.

My framework has three phases:

Preparation (before on-call week):

  • Review recent deployments and known issues
  • Verify runbooks are current — I never trust a runbook I haven't

read in the last month

  • Test alerting: make sure PagerDuty actually reaches my phone
  • Pre-write incident communication templates: "We're aware of [X],

investigating, ETA [Y]." Having templates ready saves critical

minutes during an incident.

During incident:

  • Minute 0-1: Assess blast radius. Is it ALL users or a subset?

This determines severity.

  • Minute 1-5: Communicate first, diagnose second. Post in incident

channel immediately. For financial systems, also notify the

compliance/risk team.

  • Minute 5-15: Diagnose. Check the golden signals: latency,

errors, traffic, saturation.

  • Decision framework for financial incidents:

- Can users see their balance? If NO → P1, all hands.

- Can users transact? If NO → P1.

- Is data correct? If UNCERTAIN → halt transactions, investigate.

It's better to temporarily block payments than to process

incorrect amounts.

Post-incident:

  • Blameless post-mortem within 48 hours
  • Update runbooks with what we learned
  • Add monitoring for the specific failure mode

*"The golden rule for financial on-call: when in doubt, fail closed.

It's better to reject a transaction than to process a wrong one.

You can retry a rejected transaction. You can't easily un-transfer

money from the wrong account."*

👩 Lan: "Fail closed" — that's exactly the mindset we need.

Outstanding answers, Khanh.

💻 ROUND 3 — SYSTEM DESIGN: P2P Money Transfer (40 min)

👨‍💻 Tùng: Design PayWave's core feature: **peer-to-peer money

transfer.** User A sends money to User B. 5M transfers/day, must be

exactly-once, real-time balance updates, full audit trail.

👨 Khanh: [2 minutes to think]

Requirements:

  • Transfer money from wallet A to wallet B
  • Exactly-once (never double-charge, never lose money)
  • Balance must never go negative
  • Real-time: recipient sees money within 2 seconds
  • Full audit trail for regulatory compliance
  • 5M/day ≈ 60 TPS average, ~200 TPS peak

The Core Problem: Double-Entry Bookkeeping

Every transfer creates TWO ledger entries:

Transfer $100 from A to B:
  Entry 1: DEBIT  A's wallet  -$100
  Entry 2: CREDIT B's wallet  +$100

These two entries MUST be atomic. If one succeeds and the other

fails, money is created or destroyed.

Architecture:

┌──────────┐
│  Client  │  POST /transfers {from, to, amount, idempotency_key}
└────┬─────┘
     │
     ▼
┌──────────────┐
│ Transfer API │  (validates, rate-limits)
└──────┬───────┘
       │
       ▼
┌──────────────┐
│ Transfer     │  (orchestrates the transfer)
│ Service      │
└──────┬───────┘
       │
       ▼ (single DB transaction)
┌──────────────────────────────────────┐
│  PostgreSQL (Aurora)                 │
│                                      │
│  BEGIN TRANSACTION                   │
│    1. Check idempotency_key exists   │
│    2. Check sender balance >= amount │
│    3. INSERT ledger: debit sender    │
│    4. INSERT ledger: credit receiver │
│    5. UPDATE wallet balance: sender  │
│    6. UPDATE wallet balance: receiver│
│    7. INSERT idempotency record      │
│  COMMIT                             │
└──────────────────────────────────────┘
       │
       ▼ (after commit)
┌──────────────┐
│  Kafka       │  Event: "transfer.completed"
│              │  → Notification, analytics, fraud detection
└──────────────┘

Why single DB transaction, not distributed Saga?

For P2P within the same platform, both wallets are in OUR database.

No need for distributed transactions — a single PostgreSQL

transaction guarantees atomicity. This is simpler, faster, and

more reliable than a Saga.

Saga is for cross-system transfers (PayWave → bank account) where

different databases are involved.

Database Schema:

-- Wallet balances (current state)
CREATE TABLE wallets (
    user_id     UUID PRIMARY KEY,
    balance     DECIMAL(15,2) NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
    currency    VARCHAR(3) NOT NULL DEFAULT 'VND',
    version     INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,  -- optimistic lock
    CONSTRAINT positive_balance CHECK (balance >= 0)
);

-- Immutable ledger (audit trail)
CREATE TABLE ledger_entries (
    id              BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    transfer_id     UUID NOT NULL,
    wallet_id       UUID NOT NULL,
    entry_type      VARCHAR(6) NOT NULL, -- DEBIT or CREDIT
    amount          DECIMAL(15,2) NOT NULL,
    balance_after   DECIMAL(15,2) NOT NULL,
    created_at      TIMESTAMP NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW()
);
CREATE INDEX idx_ledger_wallet ON ledger_entries(wallet_id, created_at DESC);

-- Idempotency
CREATE TABLE idempotency_keys (
    key             VARCHAR(64) PRIMARY KEY,
    transfer_id     UUID NOT NULL,
    created_at      TIMESTAMP NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW()
);

Key design decisions:

1. CHECK (balance >= 0) at DB level. Even if application

logic has a bug, the database physically prevents negative balances.

Defense in depth.

2. balance_after in ledger entries. At any point, I can

reconstruct a wallet's balance history by reading the ledger.

The wallets.balance is a materialized view for fast reads.

3. Optimistic locking on wallets:

UPDATE wallets
SET balance = balance - 100, version = version + 1
WHERE user_id = 'A' AND version = 5 AND balance >= 100;
-- If 0 rows affected → concurrent modification → retry

Transfer Service code:

@Transactional(isolation = Isolation.SERIALIZABLE)
public TransferResult transfer(TransferRequest req) {
    // 1. Idempotency check
    if (idempotencyRepo.existsByKey(req.getIdempotencyKey())) {
        return idempotencyRepo.findByKey(req.getIdempotencyKey()).getResult();
    }

    // 2. Lock wallets in consistent order (prevent deadlock)
    String first = min(req.getFrom(), req.getTo());
    String second = max(req.getFrom(), req.getTo());
    Wallet w1 = walletRepo.findForUpdate(first);  // SELECT FOR UPDATE
    Wallet w2 = walletRepo.findForUpdate(second);

    Wallet sender = req.getFrom().equals(first) ? w1 : w2;
    Wallet receiver = req.getFrom().equals(first) ? w2 : w1;

    // 3. Balance check
    if (sender.getBalance().compareTo(req.getAmount()) < 0) {
        throw new InsufficientBalanceException();
    }

    // 4. Debit + Credit
    UUID transferId = UUID.randomUUID();
    sender.debit(req.getAmount());
    receiver.credit(req.getAmount());

    // 5. Ledger entries
    ledgerRepo.save(LedgerEntry.debit(transferId, sender, req.getAmount()));
    ledgerRepo.save(LedgerEntry.credit(transferId, receiver, req.getAmount()));

    // 6. Save idempotency
    idempotencyRepo.save(req.getIdempotencyKey(), transferId);

    // 7. Publish event (after commit via @TransactionalEventListener)
    events.publishEvent(new TransferCompleted(transferId, req));

    return TransferResult.success(transferId);
}

Critical detail: Lock ordering (line: min/max). If transfer

A→B and B→A happen simultaneously, both lock wallets in the

SAME order (alphabetically). This prevents deadlock.

👨‍💻 Tùng: That lock ordering detail is crucial — most candidates

miss it. What about scaling beyond a single PostgreSQL?

👨 Khanh: At 200 TPS peak, single Aurora handles it easily.

When we hit 2,000+ TPS:

Option 1: Partition by user ID. Users whose IDs hash to shard 1

have their wallet on DB shard 1. Same-shard transfers stay atomic.

Cross-shard transfers need a Saga (less common — most transfers

are within the same region).

Option 2: CQRS. Keep writes on Aurora (source of truth). Read

balances from Redis cache (updated after each transaction). Most

"check balance" requests hit Redis, not the DB. This reduces DB

read load by 90%.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

RoundCategoryScoreHighlight
PhoneBigDecimal, idempotency knowledge10/10"Never UPDATE, always INSERT"
CodingCycle detection (fraud)10/10DFS 3-color + production optimization ideas
BehavioralSpeed vs quality10/10Parallel implementation + feature flag rollout
BehavioralUnderperforming teammate10/10Private conversation + checklist + pair programming
BehavioralFinancial on-call10/10"Fail closed" principle for money systems
DesignP2P money transfer10/10Double-entry ledger, deadlock prevention via lock ordering

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

📊 Complete 11-Interview Master Map

#CompanyDomainSystem DesignCodingUnique Focus
2LogiTechLogisticsShipment trackingGroup AnagramsWebSocket
3CloudMartE-CommerceFlash saleLRU CacheRedis atomic
4VietBankBankingNotificationsValid ParenthesesSpring Security
5TravelNowTravelHotel bookingTwo Sum variantDesign Patterns
6HealthSyncHealthTechRate limiterMerge IntervalsTesting
7GrabTechPaymentsPayment pipelineRate LimiterIdempotency, Saga
8ShopNowMarketplaceProduct searchTopK FrequentElasticsearch
9AmazonCloudTask schedulerDAG schedulingLeadership Principles
10GoogleMessagingGroup chatTimeMap + AnagramsCassandra, Googleyness
11PayWaveFintech/WalletP2P money transferFraud cycle detectionDouble-entry ledger, lock ordering

🎭 Full Mock Interview #12 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: StreamBox (Video Streaming Platform, 100M users, Tokyo/HCMC)

Context: StreamBox is a video streaming platform (like Netflix/YouTube).

100M monthly users, 500K concurrent viewers at peak, 50TB of new video

uploaded daily. Hiring for the Video Processing Pipeline team in HCMC.

Why unique: First media/streaming mock. Covers video transcoding

pipelines, CDN delivery, adaptive bitrate streaming — completely different

architecture patterns from all previous mocks.

📞 ROUND 1 — BEHAVIORAL + CODING (55 min)

👨‍💻 Kenji (EM, Tokyo): Hi Khanh! I'm Kenji, EM for Video

Platform. I see you work on the Sony Global project — we're actually

a Sony subsidiary! Let's do 20 min behavioral, 35 min coding.

Behavioral (20 min)

👨‍💻 Kenji: *"Tell me about a time you had to make a system

significantly more efficient. What was the bottleneck and how did

you solve it?"*

👨 Khanh: At Sony, 3 months ago.

[Situation] Our user profile API had P99 latency of 800ms — well

above our 500ms SLA. The service was handling 3K RPS and the latency

was slowly degrading week over week.

[Action] I investigated layer by layer:

Step 1: Identify the bottleneck. Added timing logs at each layer.

Found that 650ms of the 800ms was spent in two database queries

that were called sequentially — one for user preferences, one for

user settings.

Step 2: Parallelize. These queries were independent. I refactored

them to run concurrently using CompletableFuture.allOf():

CompletableFuture<Prefs> prefs = supplyAsync(() -> prefsRepo.find(id));
CompletableFuture<Settings> settings = supplyAsync(() -> settingsRepo.find(id));
CompletableFuture.allOf(prefs, settings).join();

This dropped latency from 800ms to 450ms — both queries running

simultaneously instead of sequentially.

Step 3: Cache the hot path. 80% of profile reads were for the

same 10K active users (power users). Added Redis cache with 5-minute

TTL for preferences (rarely change). Cache hit rate reached 85%.

Step 4: Optimize the queries. The settings query was doing a

full table scan on a 50M row table. Added a composite index on

(user_id, setting_type). Query went from 350ms to 8ms.

[Result] P99 latency dropped from 800ms to 120ms — 6.5x improvement.

The fix was deployed in stages over 2 sprints. I wrote an internal

tech blog post documenting the investigation methodology so the

team could apply it to other slow endpoints.

👨‍💻 Kenji: *"Describe a situation where you had to work with

a team that had very different engineering practices than yours."*

👨 Khanh: When I joined FPT for the Sony project, coming from

Axon Active.

[Situation] Axon Active used Quarkus, trunk-based development,

and weekly releases. Sony/FPT used Spring Boot, long-lived feature

branches (sometimes 3-4 weeks), and monthly releases with heavy

change advisory board (CAB) approval processes.

[Honest first reaction] I thought the long branches were wrong.

I'd experienced merge conflicts, stale code, and integration pain

from long branches before.

[Action] Instead of complaining, I took time to understand WHY.

I talked to the tech lead and the Sony client. The reason: Sony

operates under strict change management policies. Every production

deployment requires documentation, security review, and client

approval. Monthly releases with CAB weren't inefficiency — they

were a contractual requirement for a global consumer electronics

company.

I adapted my workflow: I broke my work into smaller, reviewable

chunks within the feature branch. I rebased against main daily

to minimize merge conflicts. I proposed (and got approved) adding

a staging environment where we could validate features continuously,

even if production deployment was monthly.

[Result] My staging proposal reduced our production deployment

failures by 40% because features were validated earlier. I earned

the team's trust by adapting to their process first, then improving

it from within — not by fighting the process from day one.

[Key lesson] Engineering practices exist in a business context.

What seems "wrong" often makes sense when you understand the

constraints the team operates under.

Coding: Meeting Room Scheduler (35 min)

👨‍💻 Kenji: Coding time. This is relevant to our scheduling

systems for video encoding jobs.

"Design a MeetingScheduler class:

  • book(int start, int end) — books a meeting in [start, end). Returns true if no conflict.
  • cancel(int start, int end) — cancels exactly this meeting.
  • getNextAvailable(int earliest, int duration) — returns the earliest start time ≥ earliest where a meeting of duration length can fit."
book(1, 5)   → true
book(3, 8)   → false (overlaps with [1,5))
book(6, 10)  → true
getNextAvailable(0, 3) → 10  (slots [0,1) too short, [5,6) too short, [10,∞) works)
getNextAvailable(0, 1) → 0   ([0,1) is free and long enough)

👨 Khanh: I need a data structure that:

  1. Stores non-overlapping intervals
  2. Efficiently checks for overlap
  3. Finds gaps between intervals

A TreeMap is perfect here. Key = start time, Value = end time.

TreeMap keeps intervals sorted, and floorEntry/ceilingEntry

let me find neighbors in O(log n).

import java.util.TreeMap;

public class MeetingScheduler {
    // Key: start time, Value: end time
    private final TreeMap<Integer, Integer> meetings = new TreeMap<>();

    public boolean book(int start, int end) {
        // Check overlap with the meeting starting before or at 'start'
        var prev = meetings.floorEntry(start);
        if (prev != null && prev.getValue() > start) {
            return false; // previous meeting extends past our start
        }

        // Check overlap with the meeting starting after 'start'
        var next = meetings.ceilingEntry(start);
        if (next != null && next.getKey() < end) {
            return false; // next meeting starts before our end
        }

        meetings.put(start, end);
        return true;
    }

    public boolean cancel(int start, int end) {
        Integer existing = meetings.get(start);
        if (existing != null && existing == end) {
            meetings.remove(start);
            return true;
        }
        return false;
    }

    public int getNextAvailable(int earliest, int duration) {
        // Start searching from 'earliest'
        int candidate = earliest;

        // Check if 'earliest' falls inside an existing meeting
        var prev = meetings.floorEntry(candidate);
        if (prev != null && prev.getValue() > candidate) {
            candidate = prev.getValue(); // jump past this meeting
        }

        // Now iterate through meetings to find a gap >= duration
        while (true) {
            var next = meetings.ceilingEntry(candidate);
            if (next == null) {
                // No more meetings — infinite free space
                return candidate;
            }
            if (next.getKey() - candidate >= duration) {
                // Gap between candidate and next meeting is big enough
                return candidate;
            }
            // Gap too small — jump past this meeting
            candidate = next.getValue();
        }
    }
}

Trace:

book(1, 5) → prev=null(ok), next=null(ok) → {1:5} → true
book(3, 8) → prev={1:5}, 5 > 3 → overlap → false
book(6, 10) → prev={1:5}, 5 ≤ 6(ok). next=null(ok) → {1:5, 6:10} → true

getNextAvailable(0, 3):
  candidate=0, prev=null, next={1:5}
  gap = 1 - 0 = 1 < 3 → too small
  candidate = 5
  next = {6:10}, gap = 6 - 5 = 1 < 3 → too small
  candidate = 10
  next = null → return 10 ✓

getNextAvailable(0, 1):
  candidate=0, prev=null, next={1:5}
  gap = 1 - 0 = 1 >= 1 → return 0 ✓

Complexity:

  • book(): O(log n)
  • cancel(): O(log n)
  • getNextAvailable(): O(n) worst case (scan all meetings), O(log n) average

👨‍💻 Kenji: How would you make getNextAvailable O(log n)?

👨 Khanh: Augment the tree: at each node, store the maximum

gap size in its subtree. Then I can binary search for the first

node with a gap ≥ duration. This is an interval tree / augmented

BST. Java's TreeMap doesn't support custom augmentation, so I'd

need a custom balanced BST or use a skip list.

For our video job scheduler, if we have millions of encoding jobs,

this optimization would matter. For meeting rooms (thousands of

bookings), O(n) scan is fast enough.

👨‍💻 Kenji: Good pragmatic answer.

💻 ROUND 2 — SYSTEM DESIGN: Video Upload & Streaming (45 min)

👩‍💻 Yuki (Staff Eng): Design StreamBox's **video upload and

streaming pipeline.** A creator uploads a video. Viewers watch it

on any device (phone, tablet, TV, browser) with adaptive quality.

Scale: 50TB new video/day, 500K concurrent viewers.

👨 Khanh: [2 minutes to think]

Requirements:

  • Upload: accept videos up to 10GB, any common format
  • Process: transcode to multiple resolutions and formats
  • Store: durable, globally distributed
  • Stream: adaptive bitrate, low start-up latency
  • Scale: 50TB uploads/day, 500K concurrent streams

High-Level Architecture:

Creator                                    Viewer
  │                                          │
  │ Upload                          Stream   │
  ▼                                          ▼
┌────────────┐                    ┌────────────┐
│ Upload API │                    │  CDN       │ (CloudFront)
│            │                    │  (edge)    │
└─────┬──────┘                    └─────┬──────┘
      │                                 │ cache miss
      ▼                                 ▼
┌────────────┐                    ┌────────────┐
│ S3 (raw)   │                    │ S3 (processed)│
│ input      │                    │ output       │
│ bucket     │                    │ bucket       │
└─────┬──────┘                    └──────────────┘
      │ S3 event                        ▲
      ▼                                 │
┌────────────┐    ┌─────────────┐       │
│ Transcode  │───►│ Transcode   │───────┘
│ Orchestrator│   │ Workers     │
│ (SQS/Step  │   │ (FFmpeg on  │
│  Functions)│   │  ECS Spot)  │
└─────┬──────┘   └─────────────┘
      │
      ▼
┌────────────┐
│ Metadata   │
│ DB (Aurora)│
│ video info,│
│ status,    │
│ thumbnails │
└────────────┘

Upload Flow:

1. Client requests upload URL → API returns pre-signed S3 URL
2. Client uploads directly to S3 (bypasses our servers — handles 10GB files)
3. S3 triggers event → SQS → Transcode Orchestrator
4. Orchestrator creates transcoding jobs for each resolution:
   - 1080p (H.264, 5 Mbps)
   - 720p  (H.264, 2.5 Mbps)
   - 480p  (H.264, 1 Mbps)
   - 360p  (H.264, 0.5 Mbps)
5. Each job runs on ECS Spot instances (cost: 60-90% cheaper)
   - FFmpeg transcodes the video
   - Generates HLS segments (.m3u8 manifest + .ts chunks)
   - Uploads to S3 output bucket
6. Orchestrator marks video as "ready" in metadata DB
7. Creator gets notification: "Video is live!"

Why pre-signed S3 upload? Our servers never touch the raw video

file. S3 handles the 10GB upload with multipart upload, checksums,

and retry. We save bandwidth and compute.

Why ECS Spot instances for transcoding? Transcoding is CPU-heavy,

fault-tolerant (can restart), and bursty. Spot instances give 60-90%

cost savings. If a Spot instance is reclaimed, SQS visibility timeout

ensures the job is retried on another instance.

Streaming Flow (Adaptive Bitrate):

1. Viewer opens video → client requests manifest: /video/123/master.m3u8
2. CDN serves manifest (cached). Contains pointers to quality variants:
   #EXT-X-STREAM-INF:BANDWIDTH=5000000,RESOLUTION=1920x1080
   1080p/index.m3u8
   #EXT-X-STREAM-INF:BANDWIDTH=2500000,RESOLUTION=1280x720
   720p/index.m3u8
   ...

3. Client estimates bandwidth → picks appropriate quality
4. Requests 10-second video segments: /video/123/1080p/segment_001.ts
5. CDN serves from cache (hit rate ~95% for popular content)
6. Client continuously monitors bandwidth:
   - Bandwidth drops → switch to 720p (next segment)
   - Bandwidth recovers → switch back to 1080p
   This is HLS Adaptive Bitrate Streaming (ABR)

CDN Strategy:

ContentCache Policy
Manifests (.m3u8)Cache 10s (short — might change for live)
Video segments (.ts)Cache 30 days (immutable content)
ThumbnailsCache 7 days
API responsesNo cache (dynamic)

Popular videos (top 1%) = 90% of traffic. CDN cache hit rate ~95%.

Monitoring:

MetricTargetAlert
Upload-to-ready time< 30 min for 1h video> 60 min
Transcoding failure rate< 1%> 5%
Video start time (viewer)< 2s> 5s
Rebuffering ratio< 0.5%> 2%
CDN cache hit rate> 90%< 80%

👩‍💻 Yuki: How do you handle a viral video — suddenly 100K

concurrent viewers in 5 minutes?

👨 Khanh: The CDN handles this naturally. Once the first viewer

fetches a segment, it's cached at the edge. All subsequent viewers

get the cached copy — our origin S3 bucket sees almost no load.

For a truly viral video (millions of viewers), I'd:

  1. Pre-warm CDN: Push segments to edge locations proactively

(CloudFront supports origin shield — single cache layer before origin)

  1. S3 Transfer Acceleration: For viewers in regions far from our

S3 region

  1. Multiple CDN providers: Use a multi-CDN strategy (CloudFront +

Akamai) with DNS-based routing. If one CDN is saturated, route

to the other.

👩‍💻 Yuki: Excellent. What about copyright / content moderation?

👨 Khanh: After upload, before making the video "ready":

  1. Automated scan: AWS Rekognition for NSFW content detection
  2. Audio fingerprinting: Compare against a database of copyrighted

music (like YouTube's Content ID)

  1. Manual review queue: Flagged content goes to human moderators
  2. Only after passing all checks → video status changes to "published"

This is implemented as additional steps in the Orchestrator pipeline,

running in parallel with transcoding.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

RoundCategoryScoreHighlight
BehavioralPerformance optimization10/10Layer-by-layer investigation: parallel, cache, index
BehavioralDifferent practices10/10Adapted first, improved from within, staging proposal
CodingMeeting scheduler10/10TreeMap with floor/ceiling, clean gap-finding
DesignVideo pipeline10/10Pre-signed S3, Spot transcoding, HLS ABR, CDN strategy
Viral handlingCDN + multi-CDN10/10Origin shield, pre-warming, multi-CDN failover

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

2LogiTechLogisticsShipment trackingGroup AnagramsWebSocket
3CloudMartE-CommerceFlash saleLRU CacheRedis atomic
4VietBankBankingNotificationsValid ParenthesesSpring Security
5TravelNowTravelHotel bookingTwo Sum variantDesign Patterns
6HealthSyncHealthTechRate limiterMerge IntervalsTesting
7GrabTechPaymentsPayment pipelineRate LimiterIdempotency, Saga

9AmazonCloudTask schedulerDAG schedulingLeadership Principles
10GoogleMessagingGroup chatTimeMap+AnagramsCassandra, Googleyness

🎭 Full Mock Interview #13 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: QuickBite (Food Delivery, 80M orders/month, SEA)

Context: QuickBite is a food delivery platform (like GrabFood/ShopeeFood).

80M orders/month, 500K drivers, 300K restaurants across SEA. Hiring for

the Dispatch & Matching team — the algorithm that assigns drivers to

orders in real-time.

Why unique: First food delivery / real-time matching mock. Covers

geospatial algorithms, driver-order matching, ETA estimation —

fundamentally different from all previous system designs.

📞 ROUND 1 — BEHAVIORAL + TECH SCREEN (50 min)

👩 Mai (EM): Hi Khanh! 15 min behavioral, 35 min coding.

Behavioral (15 min)

👩 Mai: *"Tell me about a time you improved a process that

everyone else accepted as 'just how things are.'"*

👨 Khanh: At Sony/FPT — our deployment process.

[Situation] Every production deployment took 4 hours. The process:

build locally, create a ticket, wait for CAB approval (1-2 hours),

manually trigger CodePipeline, watch logs for 30 minutes, verify

in production, update the ticket. Everyone accepted this as normal

because "Sony requires it."

[Action] I analyzed where the 4 hours actually went:

  • 1.5 hours: waiting for CAB email approval
  • 30 min: manual build steps
  • 1 hour: watching deployment logs for errors
  • 1 hour: manual smoke testing in production

I couldn't change the CAB process (contractual), but I could

automate everything else:

  1. Pre-approved deployment windows. I negotiated with the Sony

PM to pre-approve Tuesday and Thursday as deployment days. CAB

approval became a checkbox, not an email chain.

  1. Automated smoke tests. Wrote a suite of 15 API health checks

that ran automatically post-deployment. Slack notification: "✅

All health checks passed" or "❌ /api/users returning 500."

  1. Single-click deployment. Created a Slack bot: /deploy staging

or /deploy production triggered the full pipeline with proper

parameters. No manual CodePipeline clicking.

[Result] Deployment time: 4 hours → 45 minutes. Team velocity

increased because engineers no longer dreaded deployments. We went

from deploying once a week to twice a week within the same CAB

constraints. The Sony PM praised the improvement.

👩 Mai: *"How do you handle disagreements between team members

during code reviews?"*

👨 Khanh: I have a simple rule: **if the debate takes more than

3 comments, move it to a 10-minute call.**

Written code review comments lose tone and create defensiveness.

I've seen 20-comment threads that could've been resolved in 5

minutes face-to-face.

Real example: at TMA, two engineers debated for 2 days in a PR

about whether to use inheritance vs composition for a new feature.

I pulled them into a 15-minute whiteboard session. We listed the

trade-offs, agreed on composition (more flexible for our use case),

and documented the decision as an ADR (Architecture Decision Record)

so we wouldn't rehave the debate later.

My principles:

  • Style disagreements → follow the linter/team convention. Not worth debating.
  • Design disagreements → whoever has data wins. No data? Prototype both.
  • Fundamental disagreements → escalate to tech lead for a tiebreaker. No shame in this.

Coding: Find K Nearest Drivers (35 min)

👩 Mai: *"Given a list of driver locations (id, lat, lng) and

a restaurant location (lat, lng), find the K nearest available

drivers. Distance is Euclidean for simplicity."*

Drivers: [(D1, 1.0, 1.0), (D2, 3.0, 3.0), (D3, 0.5, 0.5), (D4, 10.0, 10.0)]
Restaurant: (0.0, 0.0), K=2
Output: [D3, D1]  (closest two)

👨 Khanh: This is the K-closest-points problem. I'll use a

max-heap of size K — it keeps the K smallest distances by

evicting the largest whenever the heap exceeds K.

public List<String> findNearestDrivers(
        List<Driver> drivers, double restLat, double restLng, int k) {

    // Max-heap by distance (largest distance at top)
    PriorityQueue<Driver> maxHeap = new PriorityQueue<>(
        (a, b) -> Double.compare(
            distance(b, restLat, restLng),
            distance(a, restLat, restLng)
        )
    );

    for (Driver driver : drivers) {
        maxHeap.offer(driver);
        if (maxHeap.size() > k) {
            maxHeap.poll(); // remove the farthest
        }
    }

    // Extract results (closest first)
    List<String> result = new ArrayList<>();
    while (!maxHeap.isEmpty()) {
        result.add(maxHeap.poll().id);
    }
    Collections.reverse(result);
    return result;
}

private double distance(Driver d, double lat, double lng) {
    double dx = d.lat - lat;
    double dy = d.lng - lng;
    return dx * dx + dy * dy; // skip sqrt — comparing relative distances
}

Key optimization: I use dxdx + dydy instead of sqrt(dxdx + dydy).

Since sqrt is monotonic, the ordering is the same without the

expensive square root operation.

Trace:

D1(1,1): dist²=1+1=2, heap=[D1(2)]
D2(3,3): dist²=9+9=18, heap=[D2(18), D1(2)]
D3(0.5,0.5): dist²=.25+.25=.5, heap=[D2(18),D1(2),D3(.5)], size=3>2
  → poll D2(18, farthest) → heap=[D1(2), D3(.5)]
D4(10,10): dist²=200, heap=[D4(200),D1(2),D3(.5)], size=3>2
  → poll D4(200) → heap=[D1(2), D3(.5)]

Extract: D1, D3 → reverse → [D3, D1] ✓

Complexity: O(n log k) time, O(k) space. For 500K drivers and

K=10, this is very efficient.

👩 Mai: Good. Follow-up — in production with 500K drivers and

thousands of orders per minute, how would you optimize this?

👨 Khanh: At production scale, scanning all 500K drivers per

order is wasteful. Spatial indexing:

1. Geohash grid. Divide the map into geohash cells (e.g., 1km²).

Store drivers in Redis by geohash:

GEOADD drivers:online <lng> <lat> "D1"
GEORADIUS drivers:online <rest_lng> <rest_lat> 5 km COUNT 20 ASC

Redis GEORADIUS returns the nearest 20 drivers within 5km — O(log n + k).

2. Expanding radius search. Start with 2km radius. If < K

results, expand to 5km, then 10km. Most urban orders find drivers

within 2km, so we rarely need to expand.

3. Pre-filter by status. Only search among AVAILABLE drivers.

Maintain a separate Redis set drivers:available and intersect

with geo results.

👩 Mai: Perfect production answer.

💻 ROUND 2 — SYSTEM DESIGN: Order Dispatch System (45 min)

👨‍💻 Dũng (Staff Eng): Design QuickBite's **real-time order

dispatch system.** When a customer places an order, we need to

match it with the best available driver and dispatch them to the

restaurant. 3K orders/minute at peak, 500K drivers, real-time.

👨 Khanh: [2 minutes to think]

Requirements:

  • Customer places order → system assigns a driver within 30 seconds
  • Driver gets notification → accepts/declines within 15 seconds
  • If declined/timeout → reassign to next best driver
  • Optimize for: delivery time, driver utilization, fairness
  • 3K orders/min = 50 orders/second

The Matching Problem:

This isn't just "find nearest driver." A good dispatch system

considers:

  1. Distance to restaurant (ETA)
  2. Driver direction — a driver heading toward the restaurant is better
  3. Current load — driver already carrying an order?
  4. Fairness — driver who's been idle longest should get priority
  5. Restaurant prep time — no point dispatching a driver for

a meal that takes 20 min to cook

Architecture:

┌──────────┐     ┌──────────┐     ┌──────────────────┐
│ Order    │────►│ Dispatch │────►│  Driver Matching  │
│ Service  │     │ Queue    │     │  Engine           │
│          │     │ (Kafka)  │     │                   │
└──────────┘     └──────────┘     └─────┬──────┬──────┘
                                        │      │
                                  ┌─────▼──┐ ┌─▼──────────┐
                                  │ Driver │ │ Redis       │
                                  │ Location│ │ (driver     │
                                  │ Service│ │  state,     │
                                  │ (Redis │ │  geohash)   │
                                  │  Geo)  │ └─────────────┘
                                  └────────┘
                                        │
                                  ┌─────▼──────┐
                                  │ Notification│
                                  │ Service     │
                                  │ (FCM push)  │
                                  └─────────────┘

Dispatch Flow:

1. Order placed → Order Service publishes to Kafka "orders.new"

2. Matching Engine consumes the order:
   a. Query Redis GEORADIUS: find 20 nearest available drivers
   b. Score each driver:
      score = w1 × (1/distance) + w2 × (1/idle_time) + w3 × direction_bonus
   c. Sort by score, pick top candidate

3. Send dispatch offer to Driver #1 via push notification
   - Start 15-second countdown timer (Redis key with TTL)

4. Driver responds:
   - ACCEPT → assign order, update driver status to BUSY
   - DECLINE or TIMEOUT → try Driver #2, then #3...
   - After 5 attempts → widen search radius, recalculate

5. Once assigned:
   - Send driver ETA to customer (via WebSocket)
   - Track driver location updates (GPS every 10s)
   - Update order status: ASSIGNED → PICKED_UP → DELIVERED

Driver Location Tracking:

// Driver app sends location every 10 seconds
@PostMapping("/drivers/{id}/location")
public void updateLocation(@PathVariable String id,
                           @RequestBody LocationUpdate loc) {
    // 1. Update in Redis Geo
    redis.geoAdd("drivers:online", loc.getLng(), loc.getLat(), id);

    // 2. Update driver heading (for direction bonus)
    redis.hset("driver:" + id, Map.of(
        "lat", String.valueOf(loc.getLat()),
        "lng", String.valueOf(loc.getLng()),
        "heading", String.valueOf(loc.getHeading()),
        "speed", String.valueOf(loc.getSpeed()),
        "updated_at", String.valueOf(System.currentTimeMillis())
    ));

    // 3. Publish for live tracking
    kafka.send("driver.location." + id, loc);
}

Handling Peak Hours (lunch/dinner rush):

During peak, demand > supply. Strategies:

  1. Batch matching. Instead of matching orders instantly, collect

orders for 5-second windows and run a batch optimization:

- Hungarian algorithm or min-cost bipartite matching

- Globally optimizes driver-order assignments instead of greedy

  1. Stacking orders. Assign a second order to a driver who's

already delivering, if the new restaurant is on their route.

  1. Dynamic pricing. Increase delivery fee to reduce demand /

attract more drivers (business decision, not engineering).

Database:

DataStorageWhy
Driver real-time locationRedis GeoO(log n) geospatial queries
Driver state (available/busy)Redis HashFast state checks
Order recordsAurora PostgreSQLACID, complex queries for analytics
Dispatch eventsKafkaEvent sourcing for debugging dispatch decisions
Historical locationsTimescaleDBTime-series for route optimization ML

Key Metric: Dispatch Rate

MetricTargetAlert
Order-to-dispatch time< 30s (P95)> 60s
Driver acceptance rate> 80%< 60%
Failed dispatches (no driver)< 2%> 5%
ETA accuracy±3 min±10 min

👨‍💻 Dũng: What if a driver's app crashes after accepting but

before picking up?

👨 Khanh: The system monitors driver location updates. If a

driver stops sending GPS pings for > 2 minutes after accepting:

  1. Mark driver as UNREACHABLE
  2. Auto-reassign the order to the next best driver
  3. Notify the customer: "We're reassigning your order. New ETA: X"
  4. When the original driver's app comes back online, notify them

the order was reassigned

We also track "driver at restaurant" — if the driver hasn't arrived

at the restaurant within 2× the estimated ETA, we proactively

reassign. Better to reassign early than have a cold meal.

👨‍💻 Dũng: Excellent — the proactive reassignment shows you

think about the customer experience, not just the technical system.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

RoundCategoryScoreHighlight
BehavioralProcess improvement10/104h→45min deploys, automated smoke tests, Slack bot
BehavioralCode review conflicts10/10"3-comment rule → call", ADR for decisions
CodingK-nearest drivers10/10Max-heap, skip sqrt, Redis GEORADIUS for production
DesignDispatch system10/10Multi-factor scoring, batch matching for peak hours
Failure handlingDriver crash recovery10/10GPS monitoring, proactive reassignment

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE
