🎭 Full Mock Interview #14 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: BuzzNet (Social Media Platform, 200M MAU, Singapore)

Context: BuzzNet is a social media platform (like Twitter/Instagram).

200M monthly active users, 50K posts/second at peak, 500K feed

refreshes/second. Hiring for the Feed & Timeline team.

Why unique: First social media mock. Covers news feed generation

(fan-out on write vs read), social graph, content ranking —

the most classic system design topic not yet covered.

📞 ROUND 1 — BEHAVIORAL + CODING (50 min)

👨‍💻 Raj (Staff Eng): Hi Khanh! 15 min behavioral, 35 min coding.

Behavioral (15 min)

👨‍💻 Raj: *"Tell me about a time you had to make a trade-off

between building something the 'right' way vs shipping fast.

How did you decide?"*

👨 Khanh: At TMA, Humana healthcare project.

[Situation] We needed to integrate with a third-party insurance

verification API for patient eligibility checks. The "right" way:

build an abstraction layer, handle all error codes, add retries,

circuit breaker, and comprehensive logging. Estimated: 2 weeks.

The PM needed it in 4 days for a demo to the client.

[Decision framework] I asked myself three questions:

  1. Who sees it? Demo audience only, not production users.
  2. What's the blast radius of failure? Zero — demo environment.
  3. Can I upgrade it later? Yes, if the client approves the feature.

[Action] I built a "demo-grade" integration in 3 days:

  • Direct HTTP client call (no abstraction layer)
  • Basic error handling (try-catch with generic error message)
  • Happy-path logging only

BUT — I did one thing that saved future me: I defined a clean

InsuranceVerificationService interface. The demo implementation

was quick-and-dirty BEHIND the interface. When we later built

the production version, we swapped the implementation without

changing any calling code.

[Result] Demo succeeded. Client approved. I then spent 2 weeks

building the production implementation with full resilience patterns.

The interface I defined on day 1 made the upgrade seamless.

[Key principle] **Decide speed vs quality based on blast radius,

not ego.** Demo code can be messy. Production code cannot. But

always leave a clean seam for the upgrade.

👨‍💻 Raj: "How do you onboard to a new, large codebase quickly?"

👨 Khanh: I've done this three times — TMA, Axon Active, and

Sony. My system:

Day 1-2: Understand the WHY. Read the product documentation,

not the code. What does this system do? Who are the users? What

are the SLAs? I can't understand code without understanding its

purpose.

Day 3-5: Map the architecture. Run the system locally. Follow

one request end-to-end: HTTP entry → controller → service →

database. Draw the dependency graph. Identify the 3-5 most

important classes/services.

Day 6-10: Contribute with small PRs. I pick 2-3 small bugs

or improvements. This forces me to navigate the codebase, set up

the dev environment, and go through the PR process. I learn more

from fixing one bug than from reading code for a week.

Ongoing: Read every PR. For the first month, I review or at

least read every PR from the team. This shows me the active areas,

the team's coding style, and the current priorities.

Secret weapon: I write a personal "architecture notes" document

as I learn. Key services, their responsibilities, where the tricky

parts are. This becomes invaluable 3 months later when I've

forgotten the details.

Coding: Merge K Sorted Feeds (35 min)

👨‍💻 Raj: *"You're building a social media timeline. Each user

you follow has a sorted feed (newest first). Merge K sorted feeds

into one unified timeline, returning the N most recent posts."*

Feed A: [Post(t=10), Post(t=7), Post(t=3)]
Feed B: [Post(t=9), Post(t=5)]
Feed C: [Post(t=8), Post(t=6), Post(t=1)]

mergeFeeds(feeds, N=5) →
  [Post(t=10), Post(t=9), Post(t=8), Post(t=7), Post(t=6)]

👨 Khanh: This is the classic "merge K sorted lists" problem —

same pattern as merging K sorted linked lists.

I'll use a max-heap since we want the most recent (largest

timestamp) first. Put the first element of each feed into the heap.

Pop the max, then push the next element from that same feed.

public List<Post> mergeFeeds(List<List<Post>> feeds, int n) {
    List<Post> result = new ArrayList<>();

    // Max-heap: {feedIndex, postIndex, post}
    // Sorted by timestamp descending
    PriorityQueue<int[]> maxHeap = new PriorityQueue<>(
        (a, b) -> Long.compare(
            feeds.get(b[0]).get(b[1]).timestamp,
            feeds.get(a[0]).get(a[1]).timestamp
        )
    );

    // Initialize: add first post from each feed
    for (int i = 0; i < feeds.size(); i++) {
        if (!feeds.get(i).isEmpty()) {
            maxHeap.offer(new int[]{i, 0}); // {feedIndex, postIndex}
        }
    }

    while (!maxHeap.isEmpty() && result.size() < n) {
        int[] top = maxHeap.poll();
        int feedIdx = top[0];
        int postIdx = top[1];

        result.add(feeds.get(feedIdx).get(postIdx));

        // If this feed has more posts, add next one
        if (postIdx + 1 < feeds.get(feedIdx).size()) {
            maxHeap.offer(new int[]{feedIdx, postIdx + 1});
        }
    }

    return result;
}

Trace:

Init heap: [A:0(t=10), B:0(t=9), C:0(t=8)]

Pop A:0(t=10) → result=[10]. Push A:1(t=7). Heap: [B:0(9),C:0(8),A:1(7)]
Pop B:0(t=9)  → result=[10,9]. Push B:1(t=5). Heap: [C:0(8),A:1(7),B:1(5)]
Pop C:0(t=8)  → result=[10,9,8]. Push C:1(t=6). Heap: [A:1(7),C:1(6),B:1(5)]
Pop A:1(t=7)  → result=[10,9,8,7]. Push A:2(t=3). Heap: [C:1(6),B:1(5),A:2(3)]
Pop C:1(t=6)  → result=[10,9,8,7,6]. size=5=n → STOP

Result: [10, 9, 8, 7, 6] ✓

Complexity: O(n log k) where k = number of feeds, n = posts needed.

Space: O(k) for the heap.

Edge cases:

  • Empty feeds → skip in initialization
  • All feeds from same user → just return first N of that feed
  • N > total posts → return all posts
  • K = 1 → just return first N of the single feed (heap unnecessary but works)

👨‍💻 Raj: Clean. How would you handle this when a user follows

100K people and each has thousands of posts?

👨 Khanh: Two optimizations:

1. Don't merge all 100K feeds. Pre-compute a "relevant follows"

list — the 500 accounts this user interacts with most (likes,

comments, clicks). Only merge feeds from these 500.

2. Fan-out on write (pre-computed timeline). When a user posts,

push the post ID into each follower's timeline cache (Redis list):

LPUSH timeline:{followerId} postId
LTRIM timeline:{followerId} 0 999  // keep last 1000

When the user opens their feed, the timeline is already assembled

— just fetch from Redis. No merge needed at read time.

The trade-off: fan-out on write is expensive for users with millions

of followers (celebrities). For those, use hybrid fan-out: push

to active followers only, merge on read for inactive ones.

💻 ROUND 2 — SYSTEM DESIGN: News Feed System (45 min)

👨‍💻 Raj: Expand on what you just described. Design BuzzNet's

news feed system end-to-end. 200M MAU, 50K posts/second,

500K feed refreshes/second.

👨 Khanh: [2 minutes to think]

Requirements:

  • User posts content (text, image, video)
  • User follows other users
  • Feed shows posts from followed users, ranked by relevance
  • Feed refresh must be < 500ms
  • New posts should appear in followers' feeds within 30 seconds

Estimations:

200M MAU, ~50M DAU
50K new posts/second
500K feed reads/second (10:1 read-heavy)
Average user follows 200 accounts
Top 1% "celebrity" users have 1M+ followers

High-Level Architecture:

┌──────────┐      ┌──────────────┐      ┌──────────────┐
│ Post     │─────►│ Post Service │─────►│ Fan-out      │
│ Creation │      │ (validate,   │      │ Service      │
│          │      │  store)      │      │ (write to    │
└──────────┘      └──────┬───────┘      │  timelines)  │
                         │              └──────┬───────┘
                         ▼                     │
                  ┌──────────────┐             ▼
                  │ Post DB      │      ┌──────────────┐
                  │ (Aurora)     │      │ Timeline     │
                  └──────────────┘      │ Cache (Redis)│
                                        └──────┬───────┘
                                               │
┌──────────┐      ┌──────────────┐             │
│ Feed     │─────►│ Feed Service │◄────────────┘
│ Read     │      │ (assemble,   │
│          │      │  rank, serve)│
└──────────┘      └──────────────┘

The Fan-Out Decision — Hybrid Approach:

User TypeFollowersStrategyWhy
Normal (99%)< 10KFan-out on WRITEPre-compute timeline, fast reads
Celebrity (1%)> 10KFan-out on READWriting to 1M timelines is too slow

Fan-out on Write (normal users):

User A posts → Fan-out Service:
  1. Get A's follower list (from Social Graph Service)
  2. For each follower F:
     LPUSH timeline:{F} {postId}
     LTRIM timeline:{F} 0 799  // keep last 800 posts
  3. If follower count < 10K, do this synchronously via Kafka consumers

Fan-out on Read (celebrities):

User opens feed → Feed Service:
  1. Fetch pre-computed timeline from Redis (normal users' posts)
  2. Fetch latest posts from celebrity accounts user follows
  3. Merge both lists (same merge-K-sorted algorithm from coding!)
  4. Rank by ML model
  5. Return top 20 posts (first page)

Data Storage:

DataStorageWhy
PostsAurora PostgreSQLACID, rich queries
Post mediaS3 + CloudFront CDNLarge files, global delivery
Social graph (follows)Redis + AuroraRedis for fast follower lookup, Aurora for persistence
Pre-computed timelinesRedis ListsFast reads, auto-trim
Feed ranking featuresFeature Store (Redis)ML model input: user engagement history

Post Table:

CREATE TABLE posts (
    id          BIGINT PRIMARY KEY,     -- Snowflake ID
    user_id     BIGINT NOT NULL,
    content     TEXT,
    media_urls  JSONB,
    created_at  TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
    like_count  INT DEFAULT 0,
    reply_count INT DEFAULT 0
);
CREATE INDEX idx_posts_user ON posts(user_id, created_at DESC);

Feed Ranking:

Raw timeline order (chronological) isn't enough. We re-rank using:

score = w1 × recency
      + w2 × engagement_prediction (ML)
      + w3 × relationship_strength (interaction frequency)
      + w4 × content_type_preference
      - w5 × seen_penalty (posts already scrolled past)

The ML model predicts P(user engages with this post) based on:

  • Historical interactions between this user and the author
  • Post type (image, video, text) vs user's preference
  • Time of day
  • Content similarity to user's recent likes

Scaling to 500K feed reads/second:

  1. Redis cluster for timelines — easily handles 500K reads/sec
  2. Timeline already pre-computed — no complex query at read time
  3. Pagination with cursor — LRANGE timeline:{userId} cursor cursor+19
  4. Celebrity posts cached separately — shared across all their

followers, not duplicated per timeline

Post Delivery Latency:

Post created (t=0)
  → Kafka "posts.new" topic (t=50ms)
  → Fan-out consumer reads follower list (t=100ms)
  → Writes to Redis timelines (t=200ms for 5K followers)
  → Follower refreshes feed (t=next read)

Total: post appears in feeds within ~1 second for normal users
Celebrity posts: available on next feed refresh (read-time merge)

Monitoring:

MetricTargetAlert
Feed latency P99< 500ms> 800ms
Fan-out lag< 5s> 30s
Timeline cache hit rate> 95%< 85%
Post storage success> 99.99%< 99.9%

👨‍💻 Raj: What about deleting a post? How does that propagate?

👨 Khanh: Two levels:

Immediate: Soft-delete in Post DB (deleted_at = NOW()). The

Feed Service filters out deleted posts at read time. Even if the

post ID is still in Redis timelines, it won't render.

Async cleanup: A background job scans timelines and removes

deleted post IDs. This is best-effort and not urgent — the

soft-delete filter already prevents display.

Why not remove from all timelines immediately? If a post was

fanned out to 10K timelines, removing from all of them synchronously

is slow and unnecessary. The read-time filter is instant.

👨‍💻 Raj: Smart — lazy cleanup with a filter at read time. Clean.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

RoundCategoryScoreHighlight
BehavioralSpeed vs quality10/10"Decide based on blast radius, not ego" + clean interface seam
BehavioralOnboarding to codebase10/10System: WHY → architecture → small PRs → read all PRs
CodingMerge K sorted feeds10/10Max-heap with feed/post index, correct trace
Coding follow-upFan-out on write10/10Redis LPUSH+LTRIM, hybrid for celebrities
DesignNews feed system10/10Hybrid fan-out, ML ranking formula, lazy delete

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

📊 Complete 14-Interview Master Map

#CompanyDomainSystem DesignCodingUnique Focus
2LogiTechLogisticsShipment trackingGroup AnagramsWebSocket
3CloudMartE-CommerceFlash saleLRU CacheRedis atomic
4VietBankBankingNotificationsValid ParenthesesSpring Security
5TravelNowTravelHotel bookingTwo Sum variantDesign Patterns
6HealthSyncHealthTechRate limiterMerge IntervalsTesting
7GrabTechPaymentsPayment pipelineRate LimiterIdempotency, Saga
8ShopNowMarketplaceProduct searchTopK FrequentElasticsearch
9AmazonCloudTask schedulerDAG schedulingLeadership Principles
10GoogleMessagingGroup chatTimeMap+AnagramsCassandra
11PayWaveFintechP2P transferFraud detectionDouble-entry ledger
12StreamBoxStreamingVideo pipelineMeeting schedulerHLS, CDN, FFmpeg
13QuickBiteFood DeliveryOrder dispatchK-nearest driversGeospatial, matching
14BuzzNetSocial MediaNews feedMerge K feedsFan-out hybrid, ML ranking

🎭 Full Mock Interview #15 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: CloudCRM (Multi-Tenant SaaS, B2B, Series D, Singapore)

Context: CloudCRM is a B2B SaaS CRM platform (like Salesforce/HubSpot).

15,000 tenant companies, 2M end users, 100M records across all tenants.

Hiring for the Platform Infrastructure team that owns multi-tenancy,

data isolation, and API platform.

Why unique: First SaaS/multi-tenant mock. Covers tenant isolation,

data partitioning strategies, noisy neighbor prevention — fundamentally

different architecture from consumer-facing systems.

📞 ROUND 1 — BEHAVIORAL + CODING (50 min)

👩 Chen (EM): Hi Khanh! 15 min behavioral, 35 min coding.

Behavioral (15 min)

👩 Chen: *"Tell me about a time you had to design something

that needed to serve very different customer needs with the same

codebase."*

👨 Khanh: At Axon Active, Valiant banking platform.

[Situation] Valiant served both retail banking (individual

customers) and private banking (wealth management) clients.

Same backend, but radically different requirements: retail needed

high-throughput simple transactions, private banking needed complex

portfolio operations with multi-level approval workflows.

[Action] I designed a configuration-driven architecture:

  1. Feature flags per client type. Instead of if (clientType == RETAIL),

we used a config map: features.approval.workflow.enabled = true/false.

The code path was identical — the behavior changed based on config.

  1. Strategy pattern for business rules. Transaction validation

had a ValidationStrategy interface. Retail used SimpleValidation

(balance check only). Private banking used MultiLevelValidation

(balance + compliance + advisor approval). New client types could

add new strategies without touching existing code.

  1. Shared data model with optional extensions. Core Account

entity was shared. Private banking fields (risk profile, advisor)

were in a separate AccountExtension table joined only when needed.

Retail queries never touched the extension table — no performance

penalty.

[Result] One codebase served both client types. Adding corporate

banking later took 2 weeks instead of the estimated 2 months

because we just added new strategies and config values.

👩 Chen: *"How do you handle a situation where you're asked to

estimate something you've never built before?"*

👨 Khanh: I use a decomposition + uncertainty multiplier approach.

Step 1: Decompose. Break the unknown into smaller knowns. Even

if I've never built "a real-time analytics dashboard," I've built

APIs, database queries, and WebSocket connections. Those pieces

I can estimate.

Step 2: Spike the unknowns. For the part I genuinely can't

estimate (e.g., integrating a new charting library), I timebox

a spike: "Give me 4 hours to prototype this one piece, then I'll

give you a real estimate."

Step 3: Uncertainty multiplier.

  • Built something similar before → 1.2x my estimate
  • New tech but familiar pattern → 1.5x
  • Completely new domain → 2x
  • Plus 20% buffer for code review, testing, and "things I didn't

think of"

Step 4: Communicate ranges, not points. "I estimate 8-12 days"

is more honest and useful than "10 days." I tell the PM: "8 days

if everything goes smoothly, 12 if we hit unknowns. I'll update

you at day 4 with a refined estimate."

This approach has kept my estimates within 20% accuracy across

the last 2 years.

Coding: Autocomplete with Trie (35 min)

👩 Chen: *"Build an autocomplete system. Implement a Trie

that supports:*

  • insert(word) — add a word
  • search(prefix) — return all words starting with the prefix
  • topK(prefix, k) — return the K most-searched words with that prefix"

Track how many times each word has been searched (call frequency).

👨 Khanh: I'll build a Trie where each node stores a character

and leaf nodes store word frequency. For topK, I'll collect all

words under the prefix and use a min-heap.

public class AutoComplete {

    private static class TrieNode {
        TrieNode[] children = new TrieNode[26];
        String word = null;      // non-null at end of a word
        int frequency = 0;       // search frequency
    }

    private final TrieNode root = new TrieNode();

    public void insert(String word) {
        TrieNode node = root;
        for (char c : word.toCharArray()) {
            int idx = c - 'a';
            if (node.children[idx] == null) {
                node.children[idx] = new TrieNode();
            }
            node = node.children[idx];
        }
        node.word = word;
        node.frequency++; // track insertions as frequency
    }

    public List<String> search(String prefix) {
        TrieNode node = findNode(prefix);
        if (node == null) return List.of();

        List<String> results = new ArrayList<>();
        collectWords(node, results);
        return results;
    }

    public List<String> topK(String prefix, int k) {
        TrieNode node = findNode(prefix);
        if (node == null) return List.of();

        // Min-heap of size K (smallest freq at top)
        PriorityQueue<TrieNode> heap = new PriorityQueue<>(
            Comparator.comparingInt(n -> n.frequency)
        );

        collectForTopK(node, heap, k);

        // Extract in descending frequency order
        List<String> result = new ArrayList<>();
        while (!heap.isEmpty()) {
            result.add(heap.poll().word);
        }
        Collections.reverse(result);
        return result;
    }

    private TrieNode findNode(String prefix) {
        TrieNode node = root;
        for (char c : prefix.toCharArray()) {
            int idx = c - 'a';
            if (node.children[idx] == null) return null;
            node = node.children[idx];
        }
        return node;
    }

    private void collectWords(TrieNode node, List<String> results) {
        if (node.word != null) results.add(node.word);
        for (TrieNode child : node.children) {
            if (child != null) collectWords(child, results);
        }
    }

    private void collectForTopK(TrieNode node,
                                 PriorityQueue<TrieNode> heap, int k) {
        if (node.word != null) {
            heap.offer(node);
            if (heap.size() > k) heap.poll();
        }
        for (TrieNode child : node.children) {
            if (child != null) collectForTopK(child, heap, k);
        }
    }
}

Trace:

insert("search"), insert("search"), insert("senior"), insert("server")
insert("send"), insert("send"), insert("send")

Trie structure:
  s → e → a → r → c → h  (word="search", freq=2)
       ↘ n → i → o → r    (word="senior", freq=1)
          ↘ d              (word="send", freq=3)
       ↘ r → v → e → r    (word="server", freq=1)

topK("se", 2):
  findNode("se") → node at 'e' under 's'
  Collect all words: search(2), senior(1), send(3), server(1)
  Min-heap(size 2): [search(2), send(3)]
  Result reversed: ["send", "search"] ✓ (top 2 by frequency)

Complexity:

  • insert(): O(L) where L = word length
  • search(): O(P + W) where P = prefix length, W = total matching words
  • topK(): O(P + N log K) where N = words under prefix

👩 Chen: How would you handle this at scale for a search engine

with billions of queries?

👨 Khanh: Two key changes:

1. Pre-compute top-K at each Trie node. Instead of traversing

the subtree on every query, store the top-10 words directly at

each prefix node. Update asynchronously when frequencies change.

This makes topK() O(prefix length) — instant.

2. Distributed Trie. Partition by first 2 characters:

  • Shard "aa-am" → Server 1
  • Shard "an-az" → Server 2
  • etc.

API Gateway routes the prefix to the correct shard. Each shard

fits in memory (26² = 676 shards for uniform distribution).

3. Frequency updates via streaming. Every search query →

Kafka event → consumer updates frequency counters and periodically

rebuilds the top-K cache at each node.

💻 ROUND 2 — SYSTEM DESIGN: Multi-Tenant SaaS Platform (45 min)

👨‍💻 Anil (Staff Eng): Design CloudCRM's **multi-tenant data

platform.** 15,000 tenants sharing the same infrastructure.

Requirements: data isolation between tenants, no tenant can see

another's data, one large tenant can't degrade performance for

others, and it must be cost-efficient.

👨 Khanh: [2 minutes to think]

Requirements:

  • 15K tenants, 2M users total
  • Strict data isolation (tenant A never sees tenant B's data)
  • Noisy neighbor prevention (one tenant's heavy query can't slow others)
  • Cost-efficient (can't run 15K separate databases)
  • Tenant-level customization (custom fields, workflows)

Multi-Tenancy Models — The Key Decision:

ModelHowProsCons
Separate DB per tenantEach tenant gets own databasePerfect isolationExpensive, 15K databases impossible
Separate schema per tenantShared DB, separate schemasGood isolationConnection pool per schema, migration pain
Shared tables, tenant_id columnAll data in same tablesCost-efficient, simple opsMust enforce isolation in every query

My recommendation: Shared tables with tenant_id for most tenants,

dedicated databases for enterprise tier (top 20 largest customers).

Architecture:

┌──────────────┐
│  API Gateway │  ← extracts tenant_id from JWT
└──────┬───────┘
       │ tenant_id in request context
       ▼
┌──────────────┐
│  CRM API     │  ← TenantContext ThreadLocal
│  Service     │
└──────┬───────┘
       │
       ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           Tenant Router                  │
│                                          │
│  Standard tenants → Shared DB Pool       │
│  Enterprise tenants → Dedicated DB       │
└──────────────────────────────────────────┘
       │                    │
       ▼                    ▼
┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐
│ Shared Aurora │    │ Dedicated    │
│ (14,980      │    │ Aurora       │
│  tenants)    │    │ (20 large    │
│              │    │  tenants)    │
└──────────────┘    └──────────────┘

Tenant Isolation — Defense in Depth:

Layer 1: API Gateway. Extract tenant_id from JWT token.

Every request is stamped with tenant identity at the edge.

Layer 2: Application — TenantContext.

public class TenantContext {
    private static final ThreadLocal<String> currentTenant =
        new ThreadLocal<>();

    public static void set(String tenantId) { currentTenant.set(tenantId); }
    public static String get() { return currentTenant.get(); }
    public static void clear() { currentTenant.remove(); }
}

// Filter sets tenant at the start of every request
@Component
public class TenantFilter extends OncePerRequestFilter {
    @Override
    protected void doFilterInternal(HttpServletRequest req, ...) {
        String tenantId = extractFromJwt(req);
        TenantContext.set(tenantId);
        try {
            filterChain.doFilter(req, res);
        } finally {
            TenantContext.clear(); // CRITICAL — prevent leaks
        }
    }
}

Layer 3: Hibernate Filter — Automatic query modification.

@Entity
@FilterDef(name = "tenantFilter",
           parameters = @ParamDef(name = "tenantId", type = String.class))
@Filter(name = "tenantFilter", condition = "tenant_id = :tenantId")
public class Contact {
    @Column(nullable = false)
    private String tenantId;
    // ... other fields
}

// Every query automatically gets: WHERE tenant_id = 'xxx'
// Developers CAN'T forget to add tenant_id filter

Layer 4: Database — Row Level Security (PostgreSQL).

ALTER TABLE contacts ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;

CREATE POLICY tenant_isolation ON contacts
    USING (tenant_id = current_setting('app.current_tenant'));

-- Even if application has a bug, the DB enforces isolation

Four layers: JWT → ThreadLocal → Hibernate Filter → DB RLS.

If ANY single layer fails, the others still prevent data leakage.

Noisy Neighbor Prevention:

ProblemSolution
One tenant runs expensive queryPer-tenant query timeout: 10s. Kill queries exceeding limit.
One tenant consumes all API quotaPer-tenant rate limiting: 1000 req/min default, configurable
One tenant's bulk import eats all DB connectionsPer-tenant connection pool limit: max 5 connections from shared pool of 100
One tenant's data skews indexesPartition large tables by tenant_id range

Rate Limiter implementation:

@Aspect
@Component
public class TenantRateLimiter {
    private final Map<String, RateLimiter> limiters = new ConcurrentHashMap<>();

    @Around("@annotation(RateLimited)")
    public Object limit(ProceedingJoinPoint pjp) throws Throwable {
        String tenant = TenantContext.get();
        RateLimiter limiter = limiters.computeIfAbsent(
            tenant, k -> RateLimiter.create(getTenantLimit(k))
        );

        if (!limiter.tryAcquire(1, TimeUnit.SECONDS)) {
            throw new TooManyRequestsException(
                "Rate limit exceeded for tenant " + tenant
            );
        }
        return pjp.proceed();
    }
}

Tenant-Specific Customization (Custom Fields):

CRM customers need custom fields. Two approaches:

ApproachHowProsCons
EAV (Entity-Attribute-Value)Separate table: (entity_id, field_name, field_value)Unlimited flexibilityComplex queries, poor performance
JSONB columncustom_fields JSONB on main tablePostgreSQL indexes JSONB, simple queriesMax document size, can't enforce types

My choice: JSONB for simplicity with GIN index:

ALTER TABLE contacts ADD COLUMN custom_fields JSONB DEFAULT '{}';
CREATE INDEX idx_contacts_custom ON contacts USING GIN(custom_fields);

-- Query custom field:
SELECT * FROM contacts
WHERE tenant_id = 'xxx'
  AND custom_fields->>'industry' = 'tech';

Monitoring:

MetricPer-TenantAlert
API request countYes> 2x normal for that tenant
DB query time P99Yes> 5s
Storage usageYes> 80% of plan limit
Connection pool usageYes> 80% of tenant limit

👨‍💻 Anil: How do you handle schema migrations across 15K tenants?

👨 Khanh: Since we use shared tables (not schema-per-tenant),

migrations run once on the shared database. Standard Flyway migration.

The risk: a migration on a table with 100M rows (all tenants combined)

could lock the table. Mitigation:

  1. Online DDL: ALTER TABLE ... ADD COLUMN in PostgreSQL is

instant for nullable columns (no table rewrite).

  1. Large migrations: Use pg_repack or create new table + backfill

for operations that rewrite data.

  1. Feature flags: Deploy code that handles both old and new

schema, run migration, then remove old-schema code path.

For enterprise tenants on dedicated databases, we run the same

Flyway migration independently — automated via a migration

orchestrator that tracks each tenant's schema version.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

RoundCategoryScoreHighlight
BehavioralServing different needs10/10Config-driven + Strategy pattern + optional extensions
BehavioralEstimation framework10/10Decompose + spike + uncertainty multiplier + ranges
CodingAutocomplete Trie10/10Trie + min-heap topK, distributed scaling approach
DesignMulti-tenant SaaS10/104-layer isolation, noisy neighbor prevention, JSONB custom fields

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

🎭 Full Mock Interview #16 — Part 1: HR + Behavioral

Company: AdPulse (Real-Time Advertising Platform, $2B valuation, Singapore)

Context: AdPulse is a Demand-Side Platform (DSP) in the programmatic

advertising space. When you see an ad on a website, AdPulse's system

decided which ad to show — in under 100ms. They process **3 million bid

requests per second** globally. The engineering team is 250 people.

Hiring for the Bidding Engine team — the core system that receives

ad requests, runs ML models to predict click probability, calculates

bid prices, and responds within 100ms. Based in Ho Chi Minh City.

Why this is the hardest mock so far: AdTech has the most extreme

latency and throughput requirements of any domain. Every millisecond

of latency = lost revenue. This interview tests performance engineering,

data pipeline thinking, and decision-making under extreme constraints.

📞 ROUND 1 — RECRUITER SCREENING (Video Call, 30 min)

Setting: Monday, 10 AM. Google Meet. Senior Talent Partner.

👩 Nadia (Recruiter): Hi Khanh! I'm Nadia from AdPulse. Thanks

for your time. I'll tell you about the role, then we'll chat about

your background.

AdPulse is a DSP — Demand-Side Platform. Here's the simplified flow:

User visits website
    → Publisher's ad exchange sends "bid request" to DSPs
    → AdPulse has 100ms to decide:
        1. Should we bid on this ad slot?
        2. For which advertiser?
        3. How much should we bid?
    → We respond with our bid (or "no bid")
    → Exchange runs auction, highest bidder's ad is shown
    → User sees the ad → maybe clicks → advertiser pays us

We process 3 million of these bid requests every second. Our bidding

engine must respond in under 50ms (leaving 50ms for network). Every

millisecond of added latency means we time out on more auctions and

lose revenue.

The team is 8 senior engineers. We work on the bidding engine itself,

the ML model serving layer, and the real-time budget management system.

The tech stack is Java 21, Spring Boot, Kafka, Redis, and we deploy on

bare-metal servers (not cloud — latency-sensitive).

Does this sound interesting?

👨 Khanh: Absolutely. 3 million bid requests per second with sub-50ms

response time — that's an order of magnitude beyond anything I've

worked on. But I find the challenge fascinating.

At Sony, I've optimized Spring Boot services for P99 latency under

strict SLAs, and I understand the principles: minimize allocations,

reduce GC pressure, cache aggressively, and eliminate unnecessary

IO. Applying those principles at AdPulse's scale would be an

incredible learning opportunity and challenge.

I'm also curious about one thing — you mentioned bare-metal servers.

That tells me latency matters so much that even the virtualization

overhead of cloud instances is too expensive. That level of

performance engineering is exactly what excites me.

👩 Nadia: You picked up on the bare-metal thing — most candidates

don't ask about that. You're right, we benchmarked AWS vs bare-metal

and the consistent tail latency on bare-metal was 30% better.

Tell me about your career trajectory.

👨 Khanh: I'll frame it around the skills most relevant to AdPulse.

Foundation (TMA Solutions / Humana): Built healthcare microservices

with strict compliance requirements. This taught me disciplined

engineering — code reviews, test coverage, and production monitoring.

Healthcare has zero tolerance for bugs, which I see paralleling

AdTech's zero tolerance for latency.

Architecture growth (Axon Active / Valiant banking): Led a

monolith-to-microservices migration for a banking platform. Here I

developed deep skills in system decomposition, Elasticsearch

integration, and performance-sensitive data processing. Financial

systems have similar requirements to bidding engines — you process

monetary transactions where errors mean real money lost.

Scale and reliability (FPT / Sony Global): Designing cloud-native

services serving global users under strict SLAs. I own production

monitoring, incident response, and performance optimization. Key

achievement: reduced API P99 latency from 800ms to 120ms through

systematic bottleneck identification — parallelizing independent

queries, adding strategic caching, and database index optimization.

The thread: Each role increased my performance engineering

sophistication. TMA gave me discipline, Axon gave me architecture

skills, Sony gave me production-scale optimization experience.

AdPulse would be the next level — where performance isn't just

important, it's the entire product.

👩 Nadia: That 800ms → 120ms optimization story is exactly

the kind of work you'd do here, but at much higher stakes.

Why leave Sony/FPT now?

👨 Khanh: Three reasons, ordered by importance.

First: Technical ceiling. At Sony, I've mastered our scale

and our problems. The services are stable, the architecture is

sound. I'm not growing as fast as I want. AdPulse's scale (3M QPS,

sub-50ms) would push me into territory where I'd be learning

intensely again — JVM tuning, lock-free data structures, cache-line

optimization. That discomfort is where growth happens.

Second: Product fascination. Advertising technology combines

ML, systems engineering, and economics in a unique way. Every bid

decision is a real-time prediction — "will this user click this ad?"

— and the answer must come in microseconds. That fusion of ML and

low-latency systems is unlike anything I've encountered.

Third: Direct impact. At FPT, I build for a client's internal

systems. At AdPulse, the bidding engine IS the product. If I make

it 5ms faster, that directly translates to more auctions won and

more revenue. I can measure my impact in dollars per millisecond.

👩 Nadia: "Dollars per millisecond" — I've never heard anyone

frame it that way. That shows you understand our business.

Compensation expectations?

👨 Khanh: For a senior role at a company with AdPulse's technical

depth and scale, I'm looking at competitive total compensation in

the Singapore tech market range. But honestly, the technical

challenge of this specific role is what drives my decision. I'd

love to understand what the band looks like for this position.

👩 Nadia: We're competitive with Grab, Shopee, and Google for

senior roles in HCMC. Equity is significant given our growth.

Process: 3 rounds after this.

  1. Behavioral deep-dive with EM (45 min)
  2. System design + coding (90 min)
  3. Culture fit with VP Engineering (30 min)

Questions for me?

👨 Khanh: Two questions.

First — what's the team's approach to testing? At 3M QPS, a bug

in the bidding engine affects millions of auctions instantly. How

do you safely deploy changes?

👩 Nadia: Great question. We use a "shadow bidding" approach —

new code runs in parallel with production, processing the same

requests but not actually submitting bids. We compare output for

a week before promoting to production. Plus canary deployments to

1% of traffic first.

👨 Khanh: Shadow bidding — that's brilliant. The cost of a bad

deployment is so high that you essentially run A/B tests on your

own infrastructure before going live. Love it.

Second question — what does the on-call look like?

👩 Nadia: Weekly rotation, 8 engineers. When the bidding engine

goes down, we lose approximately $50K per minute. So on-call is

high-pressure but we've invested heavily in auto-remediation and

runbooks. Most incidents are auto-resolved.

👨 Khanh: $50K per minute makes the importance very tangible.

Thanks Nadia!

🎭 ROUND 2 — BEHAVIORAL DEEP-DIVE (45 min)

Setting: Wednesday, 2 PM. Zoom. Engineering Manager + Senior Engineer.

👨‍💼 James (EM, Singapore): Hey Khanh. I'm James, EM for the

Bidding Engine team. And this is Linh, one of our senior engineers

in HCMC.

👩‍💻 Linh (Sr Eng, HCMC): Hi!

👨‍💼 James: This round is 100% behavioral. At AdPulse, we care

as much about how you work as what you know technically. Five

questions, deep follow-ups. Ready?

👨 Khanh: Ready.

👨‍💼 James: Question 1. *"Tell me about the most technically

complex project you've led or contributed significantly to. Not

just 'built a feature' — I want something where you had to solve

genuinely hard engineering problems."*

👨 Khanh: The Valiant banking platform migration at Axon Active.

[Situation] Valiant Bank had a 15-year-old Java monolith —

500K lines of code, single Oracle database, 400+ database tables

with deeply intertwined foreign keys. The system processed all

retail banking transactions. The mandate: decompose it into

microservices without any downtime.

[Why it was hard — three specific challenges]

Challenge 1: Data decomposition. The monolith had a single

database where tables had foreign keys across what should be

different domains. accounts referenced customers referenced

transactions referenced audit_logs. You couldn't just split

the database — you'd break referential integrity.

My contribution: I mapped every table to a domain boundary

using Domain-Driven Design. I identified 6 bounded contexts:

Customer, Account, Transaction, Compliance, Notification, Reporting.

For cross-domain references, I introduced domain events: when a

Transaction was created, it published an event that the Reporting

service consumed to build its read model.

Challenge 2: Strangler Fig pattern execution. We couldn't

rewrite everything at once. I designed an API gateway that routed

requests either to the monolith or the new microservice, depending

on which features had been migrated.

Request → API Gateway
    ├── /api/customers/* → New Customer Service (migrated)
    ├── /api/accounts/*  → New Account Service (migrated)
    └── /api/legacy/*    → Old Monolith (not yet migrated)

We migrated one bounded context at a time, over 8 months. At any

point, if a new service had issues, we could reroute back to the

monolith within seconds.

Challenge 3: Data consistency during migration. During the

transition, some data lived in the old Oracle DB and some in the

new PostgreSQL databases. I built a dual-write adapter that

wrote to both systems during migration, with the old system as

the source of truth. A reconciliation job verified consistency

every hour. Once we were confident the new service was correct

for 2 weeks, we cut over.

[Result] Migrated 3 of 6 bounded contexts during my time there.

Zero downtime during migration. Transaction processing latency

improved 40% in the new architecture (smaller services, optimized

queries). The pattern I established was followed for the remaining

3 contexts after I left.

👨‍💼 James: That's genuinely complex. Follow-up — what's the

one thing you'd do differently if you did it again?

👨 Khanh: I would have invested in contract testing from

day one. We caught several API incompatibilities between the new

services during integration testing that contract tests would have

caught earlier and cheaper. We added Spring Cloud Contract later,

but by then we'd already spent debugging time we could have saved.

👩‍💻 Linh: Question 2. *"Tell me about a time you had to

optimize something for performance. I want specifics — numbers,

tools you used, what worked and what didn't."*

👨 Khanh: The Sony API P99 latency optimization. Let me go

deeper than my previous summary.

[Starting state] User profile API, P99 = 800ms, SLA = 500ms.

3K RPS. Trending upward — we were going to breach SLA within a month.

[Tools I used]

  • Micrometer + CloudWatch: custom metrics per layer (controller,

service, repository). Each method annotated with @Timed.

  • Async Profiler: sampled CPU and allocation profiles in staging

under load.

  • Aurora Performance Insights: identified slow queries with

execution plans.

  • JFR (Java Flight Recorder): captured GC pauses and lock

contention in production.

[Investigation — layer by layer]

Finding 1: Sequential DB queries (650ms of 800ms).

Two independent queries ran sequentially. Fixed with

CompletableFuture.allOf(). Impact: 800ms → 450ms.

Finding 2: Missing composite index.

The settings query did a sequential scan on 50M rows. Added

index on (user_id, setting_type). Impact: 450ms → 250ms.

Finding 3: Jackson serialization overhead.

Profiler showed 40ms spent in Jackson serializing the response.

The response object had nested collections with lazy-loaded

Hibernate proxies. Jackson triggered N+1 queries during

serialization. Fixed by using DTOs with explicit projections

instead of serializing entities directly. Impact: 250ms → 180ms.

Finding 4: GC pauses.

JFR showed G1GC pauses of 30-50ms every 10 seconds. The young

generation was sized too small, causing frequent minor GCs.

Tuned: -XX:NewRatio=2 -XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=20. Impact: 180ms → 120ms.

[What DIDN'T work]

I initially tried adding a Redis cache before optimizing queries.

The cache helped for hot data but masked the underlying problem.

When cache misses occurred, latency was still 800ms. I learned:

optimize the source first, cache second. Caching a slow query

just means your cache misses are still slow.

[Result] P99: 800ms → 120ms. Each optimization was deployed

separately with A/B metrics comparison. Documented the entire

investigation as a "performance playbook" for the team.

👩‍💻 Linh: The Jackson N+1 during serialization — that's a

subtle one that most people miss. And the lesson about optimizing

before caching is exactly right.

👨‍💼 James: Question 3. *"Describe a production incident

that taught you the most. Not just what happened — tell me how it

changed the way you build systems."*

👨 Khanh: The database index lock incident at Axon Active that

I've mentioned before taught me the mechanics. But the incident that

changed my philosophy happened at Sony, 2 months ago.

[Incident] A seemingly harmless config change — increasing the

Tomcat max thread pool from 200 to 400 — caused cascading failures.

[What happened]

We increased threads because we were seeing thread pool exhaustion

during traffic spikes. The fix seemed obvious: more threads = more

capacity. We deployed it, and within 30 minutes, CPU spiked to 100%,

response times went from 200ms to 5 seconds, and our health checks

started failing. ECS tried to restart tasks, but new tasks also

immediately overloaded.

[Root cause]

400 threads all making database queries simultaneously overwhelmed

our Aurora connection pool (max 50 connections). HikariCP's queue

filled up, threads blocked waiting for connections, context switching

between 400 blocked threads consumed all CPU. We didn't get more

throughput — we got less.

The real bottleneck was the database, not the thread pool. Adding

more threads just moved the bottleneck and made it catastrophic.

[How it changed how I build systems]

Principle 1: Understand the full pipeline capacity. Before

changing any resource limit, I now map the entire chain:

Client → Load Balancer → Tomcat threads → Service logic
→ HikariCP connections → Aurora → Response

Each component has a maximum throughput. The system's throughput =

min(all components). Increasing one beyond the weakest link causes

queuing and cascading failure.

Principle 2: Little's Law. L = λ × W (concurrent requests =

arrival rate × latency). If latency increases (DB slow), concurrent

requests increase, which fills thread pools, which increases latency

further. It's a vicious cycle. The fix isn't more threads — it's

reducing latency or rejecting excess traffic.

Principle 3: Load test before changing resource limits. We now

have a staging environment with production-like load testing (using

Gatling). Any configuration change is tested under load before

deploying to production.

Principle 4: Circuit breaker on connection pools. I added

Resilience4j circuit breaker wrapping the database calls. When

connection wait time exceeds 3 seconds, the circuit opens and

returns a fallback response. This prevents cascading failure —

the system degrades gracefully instead of collapsing.

[Result] We reverted to 200 threads, added connection pool

monitoring with alerts at 80% utilization, and implemented the

circuit breaker. The system now handles traffic spikes by shedding

load gracefully (429 responses) instead of collapsing.

👨‍💼 James: Little's Law and understanding the full pipeline

capacity — that's Staff Engineer level thinking. Most people

treat configuration changes as safe. You understand why they're not.

👩‍💻 Linh: Question 4. *"How do you approach technical

disagreements when you're confident you're right but can't

convince the other person with words alone?"*

👨 Khanh: I let the code and data speak.

Most recent example: at Sony, a colleague insisted on using

synchronized blocks for a shared cache in a high-traffic service.

I believed ConcurrentHashMap was sufficient and that synchronized

would create a contention bottleneck.

Instead of arguing, I wrote a benchmark:

@BenchmarkMode(Mode.Throughput)
@Fork(2) @Warmup(iterations = 3) @Measurement(iterations = 5)
public class CacheBenchmark {

    @Benchmark
    public void synchronizedMap(Blackhole bh) {
        synchronized (syncMap) {
            bh.consume(syncMap.get(randomKey()));
        }
    }

    @Benchmark
    public void concurrentHashMap(Blackhole bh) {
        bh.consume(concurrentMap.get(randomKey()));
    }
}

Results at 16 threads:

  • synchronized: 2.3M ops/sec
  • ConcurrentHashMap: 45M ops/sec — 20x faster

I shared the results without any "I told you so." I said: "I ran

this benchmark to help us both decide. Here are the numbers."

My colleague reviewed the benchmark, confirmed it was fair, and

agreed to use ConcurrentHashMap. No ego bruised, and we had a

documented decision with evidence.

My principle: benchmarks > opinions. If a disagreement can be

resolved with data, get the data. Save persuasion for decisions

that can't be benchmarked (like architecture choices or team processes).

👨‍💼 James: Question 5. *"What's your approach to learning

a new domain — not just a new technology, but an entirely new

business domain you know nothing about?"*

👨 Khanh: I've done this three times: healthcare (TMA), banking

(Axon Active), and consumer electronics (Sony). My method:

Week 1: Become a user. Before reading any code, I use the

product as a customer would. At TMA, I actually scheduled mock

appointments through the patient portal. At Axon Active, I studied

retail banking flows — opening accounts, making transfers. This

gave me intuition about what the system SHOULD do before I saw

how it DOES it.

Week 2: Learn the vocabulary. Every domain has jargon. In

healthcare: "eligibility verification," "prior authorization,"

"claim adjudication." In banking: "double-entry bookkeeping,"

"settlement," "T+2." I keep a personal glossary and actively use

domain terms in conversations — it builds credibility with domain

experts.

Week 3-4: Map the domain to engineering concepts. I translate

domain rules into technical patterns:

  • "A patient can't book two appointments at the same time" → unique

constraint with conflict detection

  • "Every financial transaction must have two entries" → double-entry

ledger pattern

  • "Ad bid must respond within 100ms" → latency budget decomposition

Ongoing: Find a domain expert and learn from them. At every

company, I identify the person who understands the business best

and buy them coffee. Fifteen minutes with a domain expert saves

me 15 hours of reading documentation.

If I joined AdPulse, I'd spend my first week understanding

programmatic advertising: what's an impression, what's a bid

request, what's a second-price auction, what's eCPM. Before

touching any code.

👨‍💼 James: That's mature. Most engineers skip the domain

understanding and go straight to the code. Then they build

technically correct but business-wrong systems.

Any questions for us?

👨 Khanh: Yes, two detailed ones.

First — James, what's the biggest risk to the Bidding Engine team

in the next 12 months?

👨‍💼 James: Privacy regulations. Cookies are dying, and we rely

on cookie-based user identification for targeting. We need to

rebuild our targeting system to work with first-party data and

contextual signals instead. It's a fundamental architecture change.

👨 Khanh: That's a fascinating challenge — moving from

identity-based to context-based targeting while maintaining bid

accuracy. My experience with architecture migrations at Axon Active

would be directly relevant.

Second — Linh, what's the most surprising thing about working

on a system that processes 3M requests per second?

👩‍💻 Linh: That the boring things matter most. It's not the

algorithm that makes or breaks latency — it's things like object

allocation patterns, GC tuning, TCP socket reuse, and how you

structure your logs. A single String.format() in the hot path

can add 5μs per request. At 3M QPS, that's 15 seconds of CPU

time wasted per second. You develop an obsession with allocation.

👨 Khanh: "Obsession with allocation" — that resonates with

my GC tuning work at Sony. Thank you both!

📊 ROUND 1–2 SCORECARD

Round 1 — Recruiter ✅

CategoryScoreNotes
Career narrative10/10Framed as "performance engineering progression"
Why this role10/10"Dollars per millisecond" shows business understanding
Technical curiosity10/10Caught bare-metal detail, asked about shadow bidding
Questions asked10/10Testing strategy + on-call = operational maturity

Round 2 — Behavioral ✅

CategoryScoreNotes
Complex project10/10Strangler Fig + dual-write + reconciliation. Real migration
Performance optimization10/104 layers of optimization with specific tools and numbers

Technical disagreements10/10JMH benchmark resolved synchronized vs ConcurrentHashMap

🔑 New Techniques in This Mock

TechniqueWhy It's Elite
"Dollars per millisecond"Shows you think in business impact, not just technical metrics
Little's Law referenceMathematical framework for capacity planning — Staff-level knowledge
JMH benchmark for disagreementsData > opinions. Professional conflict resolution

for the 90-minute Technical Round — System Design (real-time bidding engine)

+ Live Coding (implement a budget-aware rate limiter).

🎭 Full Mock Interview #16 — Part 2: Technical Round

Company: AdPulse | Role: Senior Java Engineer, Bidding Engine

💻 ROUND 3 — TECHNICAL DEEP-DIVE (90 min)

Setting: Friday, 10 AM. Zoom + CoderPad. Two Staff Engineers.

👨‍💻 Marcus (Staff Eng, Singapore): Hey Khanh, I'm Marcus —

Staff Engineer, Bidding Engine. And this is Hanh from the ML

Serving team.

👩‍💻 Hanh (Staff Eng, HCMC): Hey!

👨‍💻 Marcus: Three parts:

  1. Java performance deep-dive (25 min)
  2. System design: real-time bidding engine (40 min)
  3. Live coding (25 min)

At AdPulse, every microsecond counts. I'll push you on performance

details. Ready?

👨 Khanh: Let's go!

PART A — Java Performance Deep-Dive (25 min)

👨‍💻 Marcus: At 3M QPS, JVM performance IS the product. Let me

test your depth.

**Question 1: Explain how G1GC works and how you'd tune it for a

low-latency application processing 3M requests/second.**

👨 Khanh: G1GC divides the heap into equal-sized regions

(typically 2-32MB each). Each region can be Eden, Survivor, or Old.

How G1GC works:

Heap Layout:
┌─────┬─────┬─────┬─────┬─────┬─────┬─────┬─────┐
│ Eden│ Eden│ Surv│ Old │ Old │ Eden│ Old │ Free│
└─────┴─────┴─────┴─────┴─────┴─────┴─────┴─────┘

Young GC (minor): Copies live objects from Eden → Survivor → Old
  - Stop-the-world, but only processes young regions
  - Duration: 5-20ms typically

Mixed GC: Collects young + some old regions
  - G1 picks old regions with most garbage ("garbage first" → name)
  - Concurrent marking phase identifies garbage

Full GC: Last resort — compacts entire heap
  - VERY slow (100ms+). Must be avoided.

Tuning for 3M QPS low-latency:

java \
  -Xms8g -Xmx8g              # Fixed heap — no resize pauses
  -XX:+UseG1GC                # G1 is default since Java 9
  -XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=10     # Target 10ms max pause
  -XX:G1NewSizePercent=40     # Larger young gen — fewer minor GCs
  -XX:G1MaxNewSizePercent=50  # Cap young gen at 50%
  -XX:InitiatingHeapOccupancyPercent=35  # Start marking early
  -XX:G1HeapRegionSize=4m     # 4MB regions for 8GB heap
  -XX:+ParallelRefProcEnabled # Parallel reference processing
  -XX:+AlwaysPreTouch         # Pre-touch pages — no page faults later
  -Xlog:gc*:file=gc.log:time  # Detailed GC logging

Key tuning decisions:

SettingValueWhy
-Xms = -Xmx8g = 8gPrevent heap resize pauses
MaxGCPauseMillis10msOur latency budget allows only 10ms for GC
G1NewSizePercent40%At 3M QPS, we allocate fast. Large young gen = fewer minor GCs
InitiatingHeapOccupancyPercent35%Start concurrent marking early to avoid Full GC
AlwaysPreTouchenabledPre-allocates memory pages at startup — no OS page faults during serving

What to monitor:

  • GC pause time distribution (P99 should be < 15ms)
  • Allocation rate (MB/sec) — if increasing, we're creating too many objects
  • Promotion rate — objects moving to old gen too fast = problem
  • Full GC count — should be ZERO in steady state

👨‍💻 Marcus: Good. Have you considered ZGC for ultra-low latency?

👨 Khanh: Yes. ZGC (Java 15+) promises sub-1ms pauses regardless

of heap size. It uses colored pointers and load barriers instead

of stop-the-world pauses.

For a bidding engine, ZGC would be ideal IF:

  • We're on Java 17+ (we are — Java 21)
  • Our workload is allocation-heavy but long-lived objects are manageable
  • We can accept ~5% throughput reduction (ZGC's barrier overhead)

The trade-off: G1GC has better raw throughput, ZGC has better latency.

For a bidding engine where latency matters more than throughput

(we have horizontal scaling for throughput), ZGC is likely the

better choice.

I'd benchmark both under production-like load and compare P99 pause

times. If ZGC gives us sub-1ms pauses vs G1's 10-15ms, that's

10ms saved per request — enormous at our scale.

# ZGC configuration
java \
  -XX:+UseZGC \
  -XX:+ZGenerational \     # Generational ZGC (Java 21)
  -Xms8g -Xmx8g \
  -XX:SoftMaxHeapSize=6g   # Target 6GB, use 8GB only under pressure

👩‍💻 Hanh: **Question 2: How do you minimize object allocation

in a hot path? Give specific Java patterns.**

👨 Khanh: At 3M QPS, every allocation triggers eventual GC.

Key patterns:

1. Object pooling for expensive objects:

// BAD — 3M new byte arrays per second
public BidResponse processBid(BidRequest req) {
    byte[] buffer = new byte[4096]; // allocated every call
    // ...
}

// GOOD — thread-local reuse
private static final ThreadLocal<byte[]> BUFFER =
    ThreadLocal.withInitial(() -> new byte[4096]);

public BidResponse processBid(BidRequest req) {
    byte[] buffer = BUFFER.get(); // reused, zero allocation
    // ...
}

2. Avoid autoboxing in hot paths:

// BAD — Integer.valueOf() on every call
Map<Integer, Double> scores = new HashMap<>(); // boxing int→Integer, double→Double

// GOOD — primitive-specialized collections (Eclipse Collections or custom)
IntDoubleHashMap scores = new IntDoubleHashMap(); // no boxing

3. Pre-size collections:

// BAD — ArrayList resizes multiple times
List<String> results = new ArrayList<>();

// GOOD — pre-sized
List<String> results = new ArrayList<>(expectedSize);

4. StringBuilder reuse:

// BAD — String concatenation in loop creates garbage
String result = "";
for (String s : parts) result += s; // O(n²) allocations

// GOOD — ThreadLocal StringBuilder
private static final ThreadLocal<StringBuilder> SB =
    ThreadLocal.withInitial(() -> new StringBuilder(256));

public String buildKey(String... parts) {
    StringBuilder sb = SB.get();
    sb.setLength(0); // reset without deallocating
    for (String s : parts) sb.append(s);
    return sb.toString();
}

5. Avoid lambda captures in hot paths:

// BAD — lambda captures 'threshold', creates anonymous class instance each time
bidRequests.stream()
    .filter(r -> r.getFloorPrice() > threshold) // captures threshold
    .collect(Collectors.toList());

// GOOD — old-school loop, zero allocation
for (BidRequest r : bidRequests) {
    if (r.getFloorPrice() > threshold) {
        results.add(r);
    }
}

6. Flyweight pattern for frequently created value objects:

// Cache frequently used AdSize objects
public class AdSize {
    private static final Map<String, AdSize> CACHE = Map.of(
        "300x250", new AdSize(300, 250),
        "728x90",  new AdSize(728, 90),
        "320x50",  new AdSize(320, 50)
    );

    public static AdSize of(int w, int h) {
        return CACHE.getOrDefault(w + "x" + h, new AdSize(w, h));
    }
}

How I verify: Java Flight Recorder (JFR) allocation profiling

in staging under load. Sort by allocation count — the top allocating

call sites are your optimization targets.

👨‍💻 Marcus: Excellent depth. The ThreadLocal buffer reuse and

autoboxing avoidance are exactly what we do here.

PART B — SYSTEM DESIGN: Real-Time Bidding Engine (40 min)

👨‍💻 Marcus: The main challenge. Design AdPulse's bidding engine.

*"You receive bid requests from ad exchanges. For each request, you

must decide: which ad to show and how much to bid. You have 50ms to

respond. Scale: 3 million requests/second. Each advertiser has a

daily budget that must not be overspent."*

👨 Khanh: [Takes 2 minutes to think]

What's in a bid request:

{
  "request_id": "abc-123",
  "user_id": "cookie-xyz",       // anonymized user
  "site": "news-site.com",
  "ad_slot": { "width": 300, "height": 250 },
  "floor_price": 0.50,           // minimum bid in USD CPM
  "geo": { "country": "VN", "city": "HCMC" },
  "device": { "type": "mobile", "os": "android" },
  "timestamp": 1700000000
}

What we must respond with (within 50ms):

{
  "request_id": "abc-123",
  "bid_price": 1.25,             // USD CPM
  "ad_creative_id": "creative-456",
  "advertiser_id": "adv-789"
}

Latency Budget Decomposition:

Total: 50ms
  ├── Network (exchange → us → exchange): ~15ms
  ├── Request parsing + validation: ~2ms
  ├── Campaign filtering: ~5ms
  ├── ML prediction (P(click)): ~10ms
  ├── Bid price calculation: ~3ms
  ├── Budget check: ~5ms
  ├── Response serialization: ~2ms
  └── Buffer: ~8ms

Every component must hit its latency target. No room for database

queries or remote service calls in the hot path.

High-Level Architecture:

Ad Exchanges (Google, OpenRTB)
         │
         │ HTTP POST (bid requests)
         ▼
┌────────────────────────────────┐
│       Load Balancer            │  (L4, no TLS termination — too slow)
└────────────┬───────────────────┘
             │
    ┌────────▼────────┐
    │  Bidding Server  │  ×100 instances (bare-metal)
    │                  │
    │  ┌────────────┐  │
    │  │ 1. Parse   │  │
    │  │ 2. Filter  │  │  ALL in-process, no remote calls
    │  │ 3. Predict │  │
    │  │ 4. Price   │  │
    │  │ 5. Budget  │  │
    │  └────────────┘  │
    └────────┬─────────┘
             │ async (after response sent)
             ▼
    ┌──────────────┐     ┌──────────────┐
    │   Kafka      │────►│  Budget      │
    │  (bid log)   │     │  Aggregator  │
    └──────────────┘     └──────────────┘
                              │
                         ┌────▼─────┐
                         │  Budget  │
                         │  DB      │
                         └──────────┘

Key Principle: EVERYTHING is in-memory on the bidding server.

No database calls, no remote service calls during bid processing.

Step 1: Campaign Data — Pre-loaded in Memory

// Loaded from DB every 30 seconds by background thread
public class CampaignCache {
    // All active campaigns, indexed for fast lookup
    private volatile CampaignIndex index;

    // Background refresh
    @Scheduled(fixedRate = 30_000)
    public void refresh() {
        List<Campaign> campaigns = campaignDb.findAllActive();
        CampaignIndex newIndex = CampaignIndex.build(campaigns);
        this.index = newIndex; // volatile swap — atomic, no lock
    }

    // Called in hot path — O(1) lookup, zero allocation
    public List<Campaign> match(BidRequest req) {
        return index.findMatching(
            req.getGeo(), req.getDevice(), req.getAdSlot()
        );
    }
}

The CampaignIndex uses pre-built lookup maps:

geo → device_type → ad_size → List<Campaign>

Nested hash maps for O(1) filtering. No iteration over all campaigns.

Step 2: ML Prediction — In-Process Model

// ONNX Runtime for in-process ML inference (~5ms)
public class ClickPredictor {
    private final OrtSession session;

    public ClickPredictor() {
        OrtEnvironment env = OrtEnvironment.getEnvironment();
        this.session = env.createSession("click_model.onnx");
    }

    public float predictClickProbability(BidRequest req, Campaign camp) {
        // Feature extraction — pre-computed, no allocation
        float[] features = featureExtractor.extract(req, camp);

        // Run inference in-process — no network call
        OnnxTensor input = OnnxTensor.createTensor(env, new float[][]{features});
        OrtSession.Result result = session.run(Map.of("input", input));

        return ((float[][]) result.get(0).getValue())[0][0];
    }
}

Why ONNX in-process vs ML service? A network call to an ML

service would add 10-20ms. ONNX Runtime runs the model directly

in the JVM process in ~5ms. The model file (~50MB) is loaded at

startup and shared across all threads.

Step 3: Bid Price Calculation

// Second-price auction: bid slightly above expected value
public double calculateBid(float pClick, Campaign campaign, BidRequest req) {
    double expectedValue = pClick * campaign.getPayPerClick();

    // Bid shading: don't bid our full value (second-price auction)
    double shadedBid = expectedValue * 0.7; // keep 30% margin

    // Floor price check
    if (shadedBid < req.getFloorPrice()) {
        return 0; // no bid — not profitable
    }

    return shadedBid;
}

Step 4: Budget Management — The Hardest Part

The challenge: 100 servers process bids concurrently. Each server

can't call a central DB to check budget — too slow. But we can't

overspend the advertiser's daily budget.

Solution: Distributed Budget with Token Buckets

Budget Aggregator (central, single source of truth)
    │
    │ Every 10 seconds: allocate budget tokens to each server
    │
    ├──► Server 1: "You can spend $50 in the next 10 seconds"
    ├──► Server 2: "You can spend $50 in the next 10 seconds"
    └──► Server 100: "You can spend $50 in the next 10 seconds"

Each server has a LOCAL budget counter:
  - Bid wins → decrement local counter
  - Counter reaches 0 → stop bidding for that advertiser
  - Every 10 seconds → request new token allocation
public class BudgetManager {
    // Per-advertiser local budget (in microdollars to avoid floating point)
    private final ConcurrentHashMap<String, AtomicLong> localBudgets =
        new ConcurrentHashMap<>();

    public boolean trySpend(String advertiserId, long microDollars) {
        AtomicLong budget = localBudgets.get(advertiserId);
        if (budget == null) return false;

        long remaining = budget.addAndGet(-microDollars);
        if (remaining < 0) {
            budget.addAndGet(microDollars); // rollback
            return false; // budget exhausted on this server
        }
        return true;
    }

    // Called by background thread every 10 seconds
    public void refreshBudgets(Map<String, Long> allocations) {
        allocations.forEach((advId, amount) ->
            localBudgets.computeIfAbsent(advId, k -> new AtomicLong())
                        .addAndGet(amount)
        );
    }
}

Why this works:

  • Hot path: AtomicLong.addAndGet() — lock-free, nanoseconds
  • No remote calls during bid processing
  • Budget accuracy: ±$50 per 10-second window (acceptable for $10K daily budgets)
  • If a server crashes, its unspent allocation is reclaimed by the aggregator

👩‍💻 Hanh: What if an advertiser's budget runs out mid-day but

they want to spread it evenly across 24 hours?

👨 Khanh: Pacing algorithm. Instead of spending the full

daily budget as fast as possible, the Budget Aggregator implements

pacing:

Hourly allocation = (remaining_budget / remaining_hours) × pacing_factor

pacing_factor adjusts based on actual vs expected spend rate:
  - Underspending → pacing_factor > 1.0 (bid more aggressively)
  - Overspending → pacing_factor < 1.0 (reduce bid frequency)

In practice, this means some bid requests get a "no bid" response

even when the budget isn't exhausted — we're throttling intentionally

to spread the budget evenly. The pacing is recalculated every minute.

👨‍💻 Marcus: Perfect. That's exactly how our pacing works.

PART C — LIVE CODING (25 min)

👨‍💻 Marcus: Code time. Related to what we just discussed:

*"Implement a TokenBucketLimiter that supports per-key rate limiting

with configurable refill rates. This simulates our budget allocation."*

Interface:
  boolean tryConsume(String key, int tokens)
  void addTokens(String key, int tokens)

Behavior:
  - Each key starts with 0 tokens
  - addTokens() adds tokens (called by the budget aggregator)
  - tryConsume() atomically removes tokens if available
  - Must be thread-safe for concurrent access

👨 Khanh: This is a concurrent data structure problem. I need

atomic operations on per-key counters.

import java.util.concurrent.ConcurrentHashMap;
import java.util.concurrent.atomic.AtomicLong;

public class TokenBucketLimiter {
    private final ConcurrentHashMap<String, AtomicLong> buckets =
        new ConcurrentHashMap<>();
    private final long maxTokens; // cap per key

    public TokenBucketLimiter(long maxTokens) {
        this.maxTokens = maxTokens;
    }

    public boolean tryConsume(String key, int tokens) {
        AtomicLong bucket = buckets.get(key);
        if (bucket == null) return false;

        // CAS loop for atomic consume
        while (true) {
            long current = bucket.get();
            if (current < tokens) return false; // insufficient

            if (bucket.compareAndSet(current, current - tokens)) {
                return true; // successfully consumed
            }
            // CAS failed — another thread modified — retry
        }
    }

    public void addTokens(String key, int tokens) {
        buckets.computeIfAbsent(key, k -> new AtomicLong(0))
               .updateAndGet(current -> Math.min(current + tokens, maxTokens));
    }

    // Utility: check remaining tokens
    public long getRemaining(String key) {
        AtomicLong bucket = buckets.get(key);
        return bucket == null ? 0 : bucket.get();
    }

    // Cleanup: remove keys with zero budget (called periodically)
    public void removeExhausted() {
        buckets.entrySet().removeIf(e -> e.getValue().get() <= 0);
    }
}

Trace:

addTokens("adv-1", 100) → buckets: {"adv-1": 100}
addTokens("adv-2", 50)  → buckets: {"adv-1": 100, "adv-2": 50}

tryConsume("adv-1", 30) → CAS(100, 70) ✓ → buckets: {"adv-1": 70}
tryConsume("adv-1", 30) → CAS(70, 40) ✓ → buckets: {"adv-1": 40}
tryConsume("adv-1", 50) → 40 < 50 → false (insufficient)
tryConsume("adv-3", 10) → bucket=null → false (unknown key)

addTokens("adv-1", 200) → min(40+200, maxTokens) → capped at maxTokens

Why CAS loop instead of synchronized?

  • synchronized: acquires monitor lock, forces memory barrier,

risk of contention under 3M QPS

  • CAS loop: lock-free, only retries if actual contention occurs.

In practice, contention on a single advertiser's bucket is low

because requests are distributed across 100 servers.

Thread safety analysis:

  • ConcurrentHashMap.get(): thread-safe, O(1)
  • AtomicLong.compareAndSet(): hardware-level atomic (CPU CAS instruction)
  • computeIfAbsent(): atomic insertion
  • No locks held during the entire operation

Complexity: O(1) amortized for both operations.

👩‍💻 Hanh: What if we need to support time-based auto-refill

(X tokens per second automatically)?

👨 Khanh: Instead of storing a raw count, store the last refill

timestamp and compute tokens on-the-fly:

public boolean tryConsume(String key, int tokens) {
    BucketState state = buckets.get(key);
    if (state == null) return false;

    synchronized (state) { // per-key lock, fine-grained
        long now = System.nanoTime();
        long elapsed = now - state.lastRefill;
        long newTokens = (elapsed / 1_000_000_000L) * state.refillRate;

        state.tokens = Math.min(state.tokens + newTokens, maxTokens);
        state.lastRefill = now;

        if (state.tokens >= tokens) {
            state.tokens -= tokens;
            return true;
        }
        return false;
    }
}

This is the lazy refill pattern — tokens are calculated at

access time, not by a background timer. Zero overhead when a key

isn't accessed.

👨‍💻 Marcus: Perfect. CAS for the simple version, lazy refill

for the time-based version. Both are production patterns we use.

Outstanding session. Questions?

👨 Khanh: Yes — Marcus, what's the biggest latency optimization

the team has ever made? What was the "aha" moment?

👨‍💻 Marcus: Switching from JSON to Protocol Buffers for bid

request parsing. JSON parsing was consuming 8ms per request.

Protobuf brought it to 0.5ms. We saved 7.5ms — which at our scale

was worth about $2M per year in additional auctions won. The aha:

serialization format is not a boring choice. It's a business decision.

👨 Khanh: "$2M per year from changing serialization format."

That's the ultimate "dollars per millisecond" story. Thank you!

🤝 ROUND 4 — CULTURE FIT + VP (30 min)

👨‍💼 Tan (VP Engineering): Khanh, one question. This is the

most important one.

*"At AdPulse, we operate at extreme scale. We lose $50K per minute

of downtime. We process money. Engineers here carry enormous

responsibility. Tell me — when have you broken something in

production, and how do you carry that weight?"*

👨 Khanh: [Pauses thoughtfully]

I've broken production twice in my career. The index lock at Axon

Active and the thread pool change at Sony.

The weight is real. After the Axon incident, I couldn't sleep that

night. I kept replaying what I should have done differently.

But here's what I've learned about carrying that weight productively:

First, I converted guilt into systems. Every mistake I've made

has resulted in a checklist, a monitoring rule, or an automated

test that prevents it from happening again. The weight doesn't

go away — it transforms into defensive engineering habits.

Second, I became the "pre-mortem" person. Before every major

deployment, I ask: "What could go wrong? What's our rollback plan?

What's the blast radius?" My team sometimes finds this annoying.

But they've also never had a repeat of the incidents I've experienced.

**Third, I accept that in a system processing millions of requests,

perfection is impossible.** What matters is: detection time (how

fast do we know?), mitigation time (how fast do we act?), and

learning (do we prevent the next one?). A 5-minute incident with

a thorough post-mortem is infinitely better than a "perfect" system

that nobody understands.

If I join AdPulse and manage a system where downtime costs $50K/minute,

that weight would be heavy. But it's also clarifying — it forces

you to build the best monitoring, the best runbooks, the best

team processes. The highest-stakes systems I've worked on have

also been my best-engineered systems, precisely because of that weight.

👨‍💼 Tan: That's the answer I was looking for. Welcome to AdPulse.

📊 FULL INTERVIEW SCORECARD

G1GC tuning10/10Full flag set with rationale, ZGC comparison
Allocation optimization10/106 patterns with code: ThreadLocal, autoboxing, flyweight
Bidding engine design10/10In-memory everything, ONNX for ML, latency budget decomposition
Budget management10/10Distributed token bucket with pacing algorithm
Coding: TokenBucket10/10CAS loop, lazy refill follow-up, thread safety analysis

🏆 OVERALL VERDICT: STRONG HIRE — SENIOR ENGINEER

🎭 Mock Interview #17 — Senior Java Engineer (Outsourcing)

Company: KMS Technology (IT Outsourcing, 2000+ engineers, HCMC/Danang)

Context: KMS Technology is a top-tier Vietnamese IT outsourcing company.

They work with US/EU clients building SaaS products. The client for this

role is a US healthcare insurance company building a claims processing

platform. Team: 8 developers in Vietnam + 3 in the US.

Why this matters: This is how 80% of senior Java interviews actually

work in Vietnam's outsourcing market. Practical questions, no LeetCode

hard, focus on Spring Boot mastery, client communication, and Agile.

Very different from FAANG — and MORE relevant for your job search.

📞 ROUND 1 — HR SCREENING (Video Call, 20 min)

👩 Hồng (HR): Chào anh Khanh! Em là Hồng, HR của KMS. Mình nói

tiếng Việt nha, round technical sẽ bằng tiếng Anh. Anh ổn không?

👨 Khanh: Chào Hồng, ok em!

👩 Hồng: Anh giới thiệu ngắn về bản thân đi ạ.

👨 Khanh: Anh là Tuấn Khanh, gần 5 năm kinh nghiệm Java Backend.

Anh đang làm Senior Engineer ở FPT Software, project Sony Global —

thiết kế microservices trên AWS. Trước đó anh ở Axon Active làm

banking platform với Quarkus, và TMA Solutions làm healthcare cho

Humana. Anh có AWS SA Pro và GCP PCA.

👩 Hồng: Project hiện tại anh làm bao nhiêu người?

👨 Khanh: Team khoảng 8-10 dev, anh làm Senior — review code,

thiết kế technical, và communicate trực tiếp với client Nhật.

👩 Hồng: Anh nói tiếng Anh communication với client ra sao?

👨 Khanh: Hàng ngày anh join standup với team Nhật, viết technical

document bằng tiếng Anh, và present design proposal cho stakeholder.

Anh tự tin communication technical bằng tiếng Anh.

👩 Hồng: Lý do anh muốn chuyển?

👨 Khanh: Hai lý do chính. Một là anh muốn làm product có impact

trực tiếp đến end-user — healthcare thì mỗi feature ảnh hưởng đến

bệnh nhân thật. Hai là anh muốn career path rõ ràng hơn — ở FPT

thì path lên architect hoặc tech lead hơi limited do phụ thuộc

vào project.

👩 Hồng: Mức lương anh expect bao nhiêu?

👨 Khanh: Với vị trí Senior và kinh nghiệm của anh, anh mong

muốn mức cạnh tranh với market cho senior role ở các công ty

outsourcing top-tier. Anh flexible về con số cụ thể, quan trọng

là opportunity phù hợp. Em có thể share range của position này không?

👩 Hồng: Range cho vị trí này là 30-45 triệu gross tùy level.

Với kinh nghiệm của anh thì khoảng 38-45. Anh ok không?

👨 Khanh: Anh thấy reasonable. Mình có thể discuss chi tiết

hơn nếu qua technical round ạ.

👩 Hồng: OK anh. Round tiếp theo là technical interview bằng

tiếng Anh với Tech Lead và client representative. Khoảng 60 phút.

💻 ROUND 2 — TECHNICAL INTERVIEW (60 min, English)

Setting: Thursday, 2 PM. MS Teams. Tech Lead (VN) + Client Tech Lead (US).

👨‍💻 Minh (Tech Lead, KMS): Hi Khanh! I'm Minh, Tech Lead on

the healthcare claims project. And this is David, our client-side

Tech Lead from the US.

👨‍💼 David (Client TL, US): Hey Khanh, nice to meet you!

👨‍💻 Minh: Today we'll cover:

  1. Java & Spring Boot practical questions (20 min)
  2. Project experience walkthrough (15 min)
  3. Scenario-based discussion (15 min)
  4. Small coding exercise (10 min)

No trick questions. We want to see if you can build real production

software and communicate clearly with the team.

PART A — Java & Spring Boot Practical (20 min)

👨‍💻 Minh: Let's start simple. **Explain the difference between

@RestController and @Controller. When do you use each?**

👨 Khanh: @Controller is for MVC applications — it returns

view names (like Thymeleaf templates). You need @ResponseBody

on each method to return data directly.

@RestController combines @Controller + @ResponseBody. Every

method automatically serializes the return value to JSON. I use

@RestController for all REST APIs, which is 99% of what we build

in microservices.

@RestController
@RequestMapping("/api/claims")
public class ClaimController {

    @GetMapping("/{id}")
    public ClaimResponse getClaim(@PathVariable Long id) {
        return claimService.findById(id); // auto-serialized to JSON
    }
}

👨‍💻 Minh: Good. **How do you handle exceptions in a Spring Boot

REST API? Show me your approach.**

👨 Khanh: I use @ControllerAdvice with @ExceptionHandler —

one central place to handle all exceptions consistently.

@RestControllerAdvice
public class GlobalExceptionHandler {

    @ExceptionHandler(ResourceNotFoundException.class)
    public ResponseEntity<ErrorResponse> handleNotFound(ResourceNotFoundException ex) {
        ErrorResponse error = new ErrorResponse(
            HttpStatus.NOT_FOUND.value(),
            ex.getMessage(),
            LocalDateTime.now()
        );
        return ResponseEntity.status(404).body(error);
    }

    @ExceptionHandler(MethodArgumentNotValidException.class)
    public ResponseEntity<ErrorResponse> handleValidation(MethodArgumentNotValidException ex) {
        String message = ex.getBindingResult().getFieldErrors().stream()
            .map(e -> e.getField() + ": " + e.getDefaultMessage())
            .collect(Collectors.joining(", "));

        return ResponseEntity.badRequest()
            .body(new ErrorResponse(400, message, LocalDateTime.now()));
    }

    @ExceptionHandler(Exception.class)
    public ResponseEntity<ErrorResponse> handleGeneral(Exception ex) {
        log.error("Unexpected error", ex);
        return ResponseEntity.status(500)
            .body(new ErrorResponse(500, "Internal server error", LocalDateTime.now()));
    }
}

Key points: all APIs return the same error format, validation errors

show which fields failed, and unexpected exceptions are logged but

don't leak stack traces to the client.

👨‍💻 Minh: **How do you manage database migrations in your

projects?**

👨 Khanh: I use Flyway with version-numbered SQL scripts:

src/main/resources/db/migration/
  V1__create_claims_table.sql
  V2__add_status_column.sql
  V3__create_audit_table.sql

Rules I follow:

  1. Never modify a migration that's been applied. Create a new one.
  2. Each migration is small and focused — one table change per file.
  3. Always test on a copy of production data before deploying.
  4. Rollback plan: I always write a corresponding rollback SQL

(even if Flyway doesn't enforce it).

# application.yml
spring:
  flyway:
    enabled: true
    locations: classpath:db/migration
    baseline-on-migrate: true

At Sony, we have a strict rule: no ALTER TABLE that locks the

table for more than 1 second. If it would, we use the expand-and-

contract pattern.

👨‍💼 David: **How do you handle authentication and authorization

in your APIs?**

👨 Khanh: Spring Security with JWT tokens. Typical setup:

@Configuration
@EnableWebSecurity
public class SecurityConfig {

    @Bean
    public SecurityFilterChain filterChain(HttpSecurity http) throws Exception {
        return http
            .csrf(csrf -> csrf.disable()) // REST API, no CSRF needed
            .sessionManagement(s -> s.sessionCreationPolicy(STATELESS))
            .authorizeHttpRequests(auth -> auth
                .requestMatchers("/api/auth/**").permitAll()
                .requestMatchers("/api/admin/**").hasRole("ADMIN")
                .requestMatchers("/api/claims/**").hasAnyRole("USER", "ADMIN")
                .anyRequest().authenticated()
            )
            .addFilterBefore(jwtFilter, UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter.class)
            .build();
    }
}

The JWT filter extracts the token from the Authorization header,

validates it, and sets the SecurityContext. Each request is

stateless — no server-side session.

For role-based access, I use @PreAuthorize on sensitive endpoints:

@PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN') or #userId == authentication.principal.id")
@GetMapping("/users/{userId}/claims")
