Spring@Async vs CompletableFuture10/10Clear use cases, thread pool config, pitfalls
ConcurrencyBlack Friday overload protection10/104 layers: rate limit, queue, pool, circuit breaker
InventoryRedis Lua atomic reserve10/10Lua script prevents oversell, Redis → DB reconciliation
DesignOrder tracking system10/10SSE, status normalization, out-of-order handling

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

📊 Complete Vietnam Top-Tier Map

#CompanyTypeSalaryDomain
19NAB VietnamBanking Hub40-60MAccount/KYC
22VNG/ZaloPayUnicorn50-80MPayments
23MoMoFinTech45-65MMerchant Settlement
24TikiE-Commerce40-60MOrder Fulfillment

💡 Tiki Interview Tips

  1. Be a Tiki user. TikiNOW, flash sales, seller portal — know the product
  2. Black Friday scenarios. They test concurrent inventory, queue patterns
  3. Redis Lua scripts. Essential for high-concurrency inventory operations
  4. SSE vs WebSocket vs polling. Know when to use each for real-time
  5. Event normalization. Multiple shippers → unified event model
  6. Out-of-order event handling. Common in distributed systems — have an answer
  7. @Async thread pool sizing. Not just "add @Async" — configure the executor

🎭 Mock Interview #25 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: Techcombank (Vietnam's Top Digital Bank, HCMC/Hanoi)

Context: Techcombank (Techcombank - TCB) is consistently ranked Vietnam's

#1 or #2 bank by profit and digital innovation. Unlike NAB Vietnam (which

builds for an Australian bank), Techcombank IS the Vietnamese bank — 13M+

customers, 11M active digital banking users, $5B+ market cap.

They've invested $500M+ in technology since 2018 and built a world-class

tech team. Hiring for the Digital Banking Platform team — the core

banking APIs powering Techcombank app, internet banking, and partner

integrations. Tech stack: Java 17, Spring Boot, Oracle DB (legacy) +

PostgreSQL (new services), Kafka, Redis, OpenShift/K8s.

Why top-tier: Techcombank pays competitively, has real product

ownership (not outsourcing), and your banking background from Axon Active

maps perfectly here. Senior salary: 45-70M+ gross.

📞 ROUND 1 — HR (20 min, Vietnamese)

👩 Thủy (HR): Chào anh Khanh! Em là Thủy bên Human Capital

của Techcombank. Anh có tài khoản Techcombank không?

👨 Khanh: Có ạ! Anh dùng app Techcombank — VNPAY QR, chuyển

tiền, và gần đây thử tính năng mở tài khoản online. Anh ấn tượng

với flow KYC bằng CCCD và video call — cả process dưới 10 phút.

So với một số ngân hàng khác, UX của TCB app khá smooth.

👩 Thủy: Tại sao anh muốn join Techcombank, không phải một

fintech hay product company khác như MoMo hay VNG?

👨 Khanh: Anh nghĩ banking và fintech là hai thứ khác nhau.

MoMo và ZaloPay về cơ bản là payment intermediary — họ move money.

Còn Techcombank là nơi tiền "sống" — savings, loans, investments,

insurance. Scope của bài toán kỹ thuật rộng hơn nhiều.

Anh có background banking từ Axon Active — làm banking platform

cho Valiant Bank Thụy Sĩ. Những gì anh học được ở đó: KYC/AML,

double-entry ledger, regulatory compliance, transaction atomicity

— apply trực tiếp vào Techcombank. Đây là lý do fit tự nhiên nhất.

Và thành thật mà nói, Techcombank đang build technology thật —

không phải outsource. Đây là ngân hàng của Việt Nam, serving người

Việt Nam. Impact thực sự.

👩 Thủy: Anh expect gì?

👨 Khanh: Với banking domain knowledge và 5 năm kinh nghiệm,

anh expect 50-65M gross. Anh cũng quan tâm đến chính sách phát

triển career — Techcombank có tech lead path rõ ràng không?

👩 Thủy: Có, TCB có career ladder từ Senior → Lead → Principal

→ Architect. Anh pass technical thì mình discuss chi tiết hơn.

💻 ROUND 2 — BANKING DOMAIN + JAVA (55 min, English)

Setting: Senior Engineer + Domain Architect. Banking-specific.

👨‍💻 Khoa (Sr Eng): Hi Khanh! I'm Khoa. At Techcombank, we

care about banking domain knowledge as much as Java skills.

👨‍💼 An (Domain Architect): Hi! I'll focus on architectural

thinking for financial systems.

Banking Domain Knowledge (20 min)

👨‍💼 An: **Explain double-entry bookkeeping and why it matters

for a banking system. How does it translate to code?**

👨 Khanh: Double-entry bookkeeping is the foundation of all

accounting — every financial transaction affects at least two

accounts, with total debits always equaling total credits.

The core principle:

Customer transfers 5,000,000 VND from Savings to Checking:

DEBIT  Savings Account (asset decreases):     -5,000,000 VND
CREDIT Checking Account (asset increases):    +5,000,000 VND
Sum: 0 ← debits = credits, always balanced

Why it matters for banking:

  1. Audit completeness — every VND is accounted for, no money

appears or disappears without a matching entry

  1. Error detection — if debits ≠ credits, there's a bug or corruption
  2. Regulatory requirement — Vietnam banking regulations (SBV)

mandate proper ledger accounting

Code implementation:

@Entity
@Table(name = "journal_entries")
public class JournalEntry {
    @Id
    private UUID id;

    private UUID transactionId;  // groups related entries

    private String accountId;    // which account

    @Enumerated(EnumType.STRING)
    private EntryType type;      // DEBIT or CREDIT

    private BigDecimal amount;   // always positive

    private BigDecimal balanceAfter; // account balance after this entry

    private String description;

    private Instant createdAt;
}

@Service
@RequiredArgsConstructor
public class LedgerService {

    private final JournalEntryRepository journalRepo;
    private final AccountRepository accountRepo;

    @Transactional(isolation = Isolation.SERIALIZABLE)
    public UUID recordTransfer(String fromAccountId,
                               String toAccountId,
                               BigDecimal amount,
                               String description) {
        UUID txnId = UUID.randomUUID();

        // Lock accounts in consistent order (prevent deadlock)
        String firstId = fromAccountId.compareTo(toAccountId) < 0
            ? fromAccountId : toAccountId;
        String secondId = fromAccountId.equals(firstId)
            ? toAccountId : fromAccountId;

        Account fromAcc = accountRepo.findByIdForUpdate(firstId.equals(fromAccountId)
            ? fromAccountId : toAccountId);
        Account toAcc = accountRepo.findByIdForUpdate(firstId.equals(fromAccountId)
            ? toAccountId : fromAccountId);

        // Validate
        if (fromAcc.getBalance().compareTo(amount) < 0) {
            throw new InsufficientFundsException(fromAccountId, amount);
        }

        // Debit source
        fromAcc.debit(amount);
        BigDecimal fromBalance = fromAcc.getBalance();

        // Credit destination
        toAcc.credit(amount);
        BigDecimal toBalance = toAcc.getBalance();

        // Record both journal entries
        journalRepo.save(new JournalEntry(txnId, fromAccountId,
            DEBIT, amount, fromBalance, description));
        journalRepo.save(new JournalEntry(txnId, toAccountId,
            CREDIT, amount, toBalance, description));

        // Verify: total debits = total credits for this transaction
        assertBalanced(txnId);

        accountRepo.saveAll(List.of(fromAcc, toAcc));
        return txnId;
    }

    private void assertBalanced(UUID txnId) {
        List<JournalEntry> entries = journalRepo.findByTransactionId(txnId);
        BigDecimal debits = entries.stream()
            .filter(e -> e.getType() == DEBIT)
            .map(JournalEntry::getAmount)
            .reduce(BigDecimal.ZERO, BigDecimal::add);
        BigDecimal credits = entries.stream()
            .filter(e -> e.getType() == CREDIT)
            .map(JournalEntry::getAmount)
            .reduce(BigDecimal.ZERO, BigDecimal::add);

        if (debits.compareTo(credits) != 0) {
            throw new LedgerImbalanceException(txnId, debits, credits);
        }
    }
}

Database-level protection:

-- Balance can never go negative (for regular accounts)
ALTER TABLE accounts ADD CONSTRAINT chk_positive_balance
    CHECK (balance >= 0);

-- Reconciliation query (runs nightly)
-- Total of all DEBIT entries must equal total of all CREDIT entries
SELECT
    SUM(CASE WHEN type='DEBIT' THEN amount ELSE 0 END) AS total_debits,
    SUM(CASE WHEN type='CREDIT' THEN amount ELSE 0 END) AS total_credits,
    SUM(CASE WHEN type='DEBIT' THEN amount ELSE 0 END) -
    SUM(CASE WHEN type='CREDIT' THEN amount ELSE 0 END) AS imbalance
FROM journal_entries
WHERE DATE(created_at) = CURRENT_DATE;
-- imbalance must always = 0

👨‍💼 An: That lock ordering for deadlock prevention — most candidates

miss that detail. What's the Isolation.SERIALIZABLE for?

👨 Khanh: SERIALIZABLE is the strictest isolation level. It

prevents phantom reads — where a transaction reads a set of rows,

another transaction inserts a row that would match, and the first

transaction would see a different result if it re-read.

For a bank transfer, REPEATABLE_READ (MySQL default) is usually

sufficient, but for complex multi-account operations (like batch

payroll disbursement), SERIALIZABLE ensures total isolation.

The trade-off: SERIALIZABLE has higher lock contention. For simple

2-account transfers, REPEATABLE_READ + SELECT FOR UPDATE is

often better for performance.

👨‍💻 Khoa: **Techcombank has a legacy Oracle database and new

PostgreSQL services. How do you migrate data from Oracle to Postgres

without downtime?**

👨 Khanh: Expand-and-Contract pattern (Strangler Fig for data):

Phase 1: Expand — dual-write:

@Service
public class AccountService {

    private final OracleAccountRepo oracleRepo;   // legacy
    private final PostgresAccountRepo postgresRepo; // new
    private final FeatureFlags flags;

    @Transactional
    public Account updateAccount(Account account) {
        // Write to Oracle (source of truth during migration)
        Account saved = oracleRepo.save(account);

        // Also write to Postgres (best-effort, catch errors)
        try {
            postgresRepo.save(account);
        } catch (Exception e) {
            log.error("Postgres dual-write failed for account {}", account.getId(), e);
            // Don't fail the request — Oracle succeeded
        }

        return saved;
    }

    public Account getAccount(String id) {
        if (flags.isEnabled("USE_POSTGRES_READ")) {
            // Shadow read: compare Oracle vs Postgres for validation
            Account oracle = oracleRepo.findById(id);
            Account postgres = postgresRepo.findById(id);
            if (!oracle.equals(postgres)) {
                log.warn("Data mismatch for account {}", id);
                metricsService.recordMismatch();
            }
            return oracle; // still return Oracle during validation phase
        }
        return oracleRepo.findById(id);
    }
}

Phase 2: Validate — shadow reads:

  • Run both reads, compare results, log discrepancies
  • Fix any sync bugs until mismatch rate = 0% for 2 weeks

Phase 3: Contract — cut over reads:

  • Enable USE_POSTGRES_READ feature flag (100% of reads from Postgres)
  • Keep dual-writes for 1 more month (rollback capability)

Phase 4: Remove — drop Oracle dependency:

  • Remove dual-write code
  • Archive Oracle data

Tools: Debezium CDC (Change Data Capture) to replicate Oracle

changes to Postgres in real-time during migration — more reliable

than dual-write for high-volume tables.

Java Production Patterns (35 min)

👨‍💻 Khoa: **Show me how you implement proper connection pool

management in a banking service. What metrics do you monitor?**

👨 Khanh:

# application.yml — HikariCP for PostgreSQL
spring:
  datasource:
    url: jdbc:postgresql://primary-db:5432/tcb_banking
    hikari:
      maximum-pool-size: 20           # max DB connections
      minimum-idle: 5                 # always-warm connections
      connection-timeout: 3000        # fail fast if no connection in 3s
      idle-timeout: 600000            # release idle connections after 10min
      max-lifetime: 1800000           # replace connections after 30min
      keepalive-time: 60000           # heartbeat every 60s (prevents firewall drops)
      pool-name: TCB-Primary-Pool
      # Critical for banking: validate connection before use
      connection-test-query: SELECT 1
      validation-timeout: 1000

Monitoring in production:

@Configuration
public class PoolMetricsConfig {

    @Bean
    public MeterRegistryCustomizer<MeterRegistry> hikariMetrics(DataSource dataSource) {
        return registry -> {
            HikariDataSource hikari = dataSource.unwrap(HikariDataSource.class);
            new HikariCPMetrics(hikari.getHikariPoolMXBean())
                .bindTo(registry);
        };
    }
}

// Key metrics to alert on:
// hikaricp.connections.active > 18/20 (90% pool used) → scale up or optimize
// hikaricp.connections.pending > 0 → threads waiting for connections
// hikaricp.connections.timeout → connections failing to acquire → P1 alert
// hikaricp.connections.creation.timeout → DB not reachable

Connection pool sizing rule:

Pool size = (core_count × 2) + effective_spindle_count

For a 4-core app server connecting to cloud DB:
  Pool size ≈ (4 × 2) + 1 = 9 → round to 10

More connections beyond the formula causes:
  - DB CPU wasted on connection management
  - Lock contention increases
  - Latency gets WORSE (counter-intuitive but true)

Read/Write split for banking:

// Route read-heavy queries to replica
@Configuration
public class DataSourceConfig {

    @Bean
    @Primary
    public DataSource routingDataSource(
            @Qualifier("primaryDs") DataSource primary,
            @Qualifier("replicaDs") DataSource replica) {

        Map<Object, Object> dataSources = Map.of(
            "primary", primary,
            "replica", replica
        );

        AbstractRoutingDataSource routing = new AbstractRoutingDataSource() {
            @Override
            protected Object determineCurrentLookupKey() {
                // Route read-only transactions to replica
                return TransactionSynchronizationManager.isCurrentTransactionReadOnly()
                    ? "replica" : "primary";
            }
        };
        routing.setTargetDataSources(dataSources);
        routing.setDefaultTargetDataSource(primary);
        return routing;
    }
}

// Usage: @Transactional(readOnly = true) → automatically routes to replica
@Transactional(readOnly = true)
public AccountStatement getStatement(String accountId, YearMonth month) {
    // Hits replica — no impact on primary writes
}

💻 ROUND 3 — SYSTEM DESIGN (45 min, English)

Setting: Principal Engineer.

👨‍💻 Minh (Principal Eng): Design Techcombank's **fund transfer

service.** Support: intrabank (between TCB accounts) and interbank

(to other banks via NAPAS, Vietnam's interbank network).

Scale: 2M transfers/day, 99.99% uptime SLA, T+0 for intrabank,

T+0 or T+1 for interbank depending on amount and time.

👨 Khanh:

Two transfer modes:

INTRABANK (A → B, both TCB accounts):
  Atomic ledger update → instant (T+0)
  No external dependency

INTERBANK (TCB account → VietcomBank account):
  TCB debit → NAPAS → Destination Bank credit
  NAPAS response time: 1-30 seconds during business hours
  Weekend/after-hours: batched T+1

Architecture:

┌──────────────┐
│ Transfer API │  (validates, rate limits, deduplicates)
└──────┬───────┘
       │
       ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│         Transfer Orchestrator                │
│                                              │
│  IF intrabank → LedgerService (atomic)       │
│  IF interbank → NAPAS Gateway + Saga         │
└──────┬───────────────────┬────────────────────┘
       │                   │
┌──────▼──────┐     ┌──────▼──────┐
│  Ledger DB  │     │ NAPAS Client│
│ (PostgreSQL)│     │ (external)  │
└─────────────┘     └──────┬──────┘
                           │ async response
                    ┌──────▼──────┐
                    │  Transfer   │
                    │  State DB   │ (tracks interbank status)
                    └─────────────┘

Intrabank Transfer (simple + fast):

@Transactional(isolation = Isolation.REPEATABLE_READ)
public TransferResult intraBankTransfer(TransferRequest req) {
    // 1. Idempotency check
    Optional<Transfer> existing = transferRepo.findByRequestId(req.getRequestId());
    if (existing.isPresent()) return TransferResult.from(existing.get());

    // 2. Debit/Credit (with deadlock prevention via account ID ordering)
    ledgerService.recordTransfer(req.getFromAccount(), req.getToAccount(),
        req.getAmount(), req.getDescription());

    // 3. Save transfer record
    Transfer transfer = transferRepo.save(Transfer.intrabank(req, SUCCESS));

    // 4. Async notification
    eventPublisher.publishEvent(new TransferCompletedEvent(transfer));

    return TransferResult.success(transfer);
}

Interbank Transfer (NAPAS, Saga pattern):

// Saga: Debit TCB → Send to NAPAS → Confirm
@Service
public class InterBankTransferSaga {

    @Transactional
    public TransferResult start(TransferRequest req) {
        // Step 1: Reserve funds (debit with RESERVED status)
        Transfer transfer = Transfer.interbank(req, FUNDS_RESERVED);
        ledgerService.debit(req.getFromAccount(), req.getAmount(), transfer.getId());
        transferRepo.save(transfer);

        // Step 2: Send to NAPAS asynchronously
        String napasRef = napasGateway.initiate(NapasRequest.from(req, transfer.getId()));
        transfer.setNapasReference(napasRef);
        transfer.setStatus(NAPAS_PENDING);
        transferRepo.save(transfer);

        return TransferResult.pending(transfer.getId(), "Processing with NAPAS");
    }

    // Called by NAPAS webhook when result comes back
    @Transactional
    public void handleNapasCallback(NapasCallback callback) {
        Transfer transfer = transferRepo.findByNapasReference(callback.getRef())
            .orElseThrow();

        if (callback.isSuccess()) {
            transfer.setStatus(COMPLETED);
            // Credit destination bank account
            ledgerService.creditExternal(transfer, callback.getDestinationConfirmation());
            notificationService.notifySuccess(transfer);
        } else {
            // Compensating transaction: reverse the debit
            transfer.setStatus(FAILED);
            ledgerService.credit(transfer.getFromAccount(),
                transfer.getAmount(), "Reversal: " + callback.getFailureReason());
            notificationService.notifyFailure(transfer, callback.getFailureReason());
        }

        transferRepo.save(transfer);
    }
}

Handling NAPAS timeout (no callback within 30s):

@Scheduled(fixedDelay = 60_000)
public void checkPendingNapasTransfers() {
    LocalDateTime cutoff = LocalDateTime.now().minusMinutes(5);
    List<Transfer> stuck = transferRepo.findByStatusAndCreatedAtBefore(
        NAPAS_PENDING, cutoff
    );

    for (Transfer t : stuck) {
        NapasStatusResponse status = napasGateway.queryStatus(t.getNapasReference());
        if (status.isCompleted()) {
            handleNapasCallback(NapasCallback.success(t.getNapasReference(), status));
        } else if (status.isFailed()) {
            handleNapasCallback(NapasCallback.failed(t.getNapasReference(), status));
        }
        // Still pending: leave for next check cycle
    }
}

High Availability (99.99% SLA = 52 min downtime/year):

ComponentHA Strategy
Transfer API3+ pods, circuit breaker
PostgreSQLAurora with Multi-AZ
NAPAS GatewayPrimary + backup NAPAS connection
Kafka (events)3-broker cluster, replication factor 3
RedisRedis Sentinel or Cluster

99.99% checklist:

  • Zero-downtime deployments (rolling updates)
  • Database failover < 30s (Aurora automatic)
  • Circuit breaker on NAPAS calls (open → use queue, process when recovered)
  • Active health checks with auto-remediation

👨‍💻 Minh: Strong architecture. What's your approach to the

regulatory requirement that every transfer must be auditable for

7 years?

👨 Khanh: Three layers:

  1. Immutable journal entries — journal_entries table has

no UPDATE or DELETE operations. Only INSERTs. DB-level policy

(revoke UPDATE/DELETE privileges from the application user).

  1. Cold storage archival — transfers older than 2 years move

to S3 (Parquet format) via a nightly job. Retained 7+ years.

Queryable via Athena for compliance queries.

  1. Audit log table — separate from journal entries. Logs EVERY

API call that touches a transfer: who requested, what changed,

from which IP, at what time. Even failed attempts are logged.

CREATE TABLE transfer_audit_log (
    id          BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    transfer_id UUID,
    action      VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,  -- 'INITIATE', 'NAPAS_CALLBACK', 'QUERY'
    actor_id    VARCHAR(50),           -- user or system making the action
    actor_ip    INET,
    request_payload  JSONB,
    response_payload JSONB,
    created_at  TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW()
) PARTITION BY RANGE (created_at);    -- partition by month for query performance
-- NEVER delete from this table. Archive to S3 after 2 years.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

RoundCategoryScoreNotes
HR (VN)Banking domain motivation10/10"Where money lives" vs payment intermediary
BankingDouble-entry bookkeeping10/10Code implementation + assertBalanced + reconciliation
BankingOracle → Postgres migration10/10Expand-and-contract + Debezium CDC
JavaConnection pool management10/10Hikari config, sizing formula, read/write routing
DesignFund transfer service10/10Intrabank simple, interbank Saga, NAPAS timeout handler
Compliance7-year audit trail10/10Immutable journal, cold storage, audit log table

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 Techcombank Interview Tips

  1. Banking domain is required. Double-entry, KYC, AML, NAPAS — know the vocabulary
  2. "Where money lives" — frame yourself as a banker, not just a Java engineer
  3. Oracle is still present. They have legacy Oracle — don't assume everything is modern
  4. 7-year audit requirement. Vietnam SBV regulation — always mention immutable ledger
  5. NAPAS = Vietnam's SWIFT. Know that interbank transfers go through NAPAS
  6. Saga for interbank. Intrabank is one transaction. Interbank is distributed — needs Saga
  7. Connection pool sizing formula. Shows you understand DB performance, not just code
  8. Techcombank salary. Senior: 45-70M gross. They match top-tier market

🎭 Mock Interview #26 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: Sea Group — Shopee Vietnam (Singapore HQ, HCMC Engineering Hub)

Context: Sea Group (NYSE: SE) is Southeast Asia's largest tech company —

Shopee (e-commerce), SeaMoney (digital finance), Garena (gaming). Market cap

$30B+. HCMC engineering hub has 1000+ engineers. This is the highest-paying

tech employer in Vietnam after Google/Meta offices.

Hiring for the Shopee Platform Engineering team — core infrastructure

services used by all Shopee product teams: service mesh, internal tooling,

developer platforms, observability. This is a platform/infra-leaning role,

not a product feature team.

Why Sea Group is the hardest Vietnam interview:

- They interview like a mid-tier FAANG — system design is at real scale

- Strong emphasis on observability, reliability, and platform thinking

- Engineers report to Singapore — English must be excellent

- Salary: 60-100M+ gross for senior (highest in Vietnam market)

- The technical bar is closest to Google/Amazon of all Vietnam employers

📞 ROUND 1 — HR SCREENING (25 min, English)

👩 Rachel (Recruiter, Singapore): Hi Khanh! I'm Rachel from

Sea Group Talent. This role is on the Platform Engineering team

in HCMC, reporting to a Staff Engineer in Singapore. Everything

is in English. Tell me about yourself.

👨 Khanh: Hi Rachel! I'm Khanh, Senior Java Backend Engineer

with nearly 5 years experience. My focus areas:

Architecturally: I design and build microservices on AWS — Spring

Boot, Kafka, PostgreSQL, Redis, K8s. I've led migrations from

monolith to microservices, built event-driven payment systems, and

optimized services from 800ms to 120ms P99.

Domain-wise: I have depth in fintech and e-commerce adjacent systems

— payments at Sony, banking at Axon Active, healthcare at TMA.

What draws me to Sea Group specifically: Platform Engineering is

different from product feature work. You build the foundation that

100 other engineers build on top of. If I improve the internal

service framework by 10%, every team gets that 10%. That multiplier

effect is what excites me about platform roles.

👩 Rachel: What's your experience with observability and monitoring?

👨 Khanh: At Sony, I own the observability stack for our services.

We use Micrometer + CloudWatch for metrics, CloudWatch Logs with

structured JSON logging, and X-Ray for distributed tracing. I've

set up the golden signals monitoring — latency, error rate, traffic,

saturation — with alerting thresholds per service.

I've also gone beyond tooling: I wrote our team's observability

runbook, created Grafana dashboards for on-call engineers, and

introduced structured logging standards so log queries are consistent

across services.

One real impact: I added a custom metric payment.processing.duration

with percentile buckets. That metric caught a P99 regression 30

minutes before users started complaining, and we rolled back before

it became a customer-visible incident.

👩 Rachel: Salary expectation?

👨 Khanh: I've done market research — Sea Group is the top-paying

employer in HCMC for senior engineers. For a platform role with my

background, I'm targeting 70-85M gross as a starting point. I'm

open to discussing the full package including equity and benefits.

👩 Rachel: That's in range. Four technical rounds — 2 coding,

1 system design, 1 with VP. This is a demanding process. Still interested?

👨 Khanh: Absolutely. The high bar means the team is exceptional.

That's exactly where I want to work.

💻 ROUND 2 — CODING ROUND 1 (45 min, English)

Setting: Senior Engineer. CoderPad.

👨‍💻 Wei (Sr Eng, Singapore): Hi Khanh. Two problems today.

Practical engineering problems, not pure algorithms.

Problem 1: Implement a Consistent Hash Ring (25 min)

👨‍💻 Wei: *"In Shopee's service mesh, we distribute requests

across backend instances using consistent hashing. Implement a

ConsistentHashRing that:*

  • addNode(String node) — add a server node
  • removeNode(String node) — remove a server node
  • getNode(String key) — return which node handles this key
  • When a node is added/removed, only ~1/n of keys remapped
  • Use virtual nodes (150 per physical node) for even distribution"

👨 Khanh: Consistent hashing maps both nodes and keys to a

circular ring using hash functions. For a given key, we find the

nearest node clockwise on the ring.

Virtual nodes (vnodes) place each physical node at multiple points

on the ring — this makes load distribution much more uniform.

public class ConsistentHashRing {

    private final TreeMap<Long, String> ring = new TreeMap<>();
    private final int virtualNodes;
    private final MessageDigest md5;

    public ConsistentHashRing(int virtualNodes) {
        this.virtualNodes = virtualNodes;
        try {
            this.md5 = MessageDigest.getInstance("MD5");
        } catch (NoSuchAlgorithmException e) {
            throw new RuntimeException(e);
        }
    }

    public synchronized void addNode(String node) {
        for (int i = 0; i < virtualNodes; i++) {
            long hash = hash(node + "#vnode-" + i);
            ring.put(hash, node);
        }
    }

    public synchronized void removeNode(String node) {
        for (int i = 0; i < virtualNodes; i++) {
            long hash = hash(node + "#vnode-" + i);
            ring.remove(hash);
        }
    }

    public synchronized String getNode(String key) {
        if (ring.isEmpty()) {
            throw new IllegalStateException("No nodes in the ring");
        }
        long hash = hash(key);

        // Find the first node with hash >= key's hash (clockwise)
        Map.Entry<Long, String> entry = ring.ceilingEntry(hash);

        // Wrap around: if no node found clockwise, use the first node
        if (entry == null) {
            entry = ring.firstEntry();
        }

        return entry.getValue();
    }

    private long hash(String key) {
        byte[] digest = md5.digest(key.getBytes(StandardCharsets.UTF_8));
        // Use first 8 bytes as long
        long result = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i < 8; i++) {
            result = (result << 8) | (digest[i] & 0xFF);
        }
        return result;
    }

    // Utility: show distribution across nodes
    public Map<String, Long> getDistribution() {
        return ring.values().stream()
            .collect(Collectors.groupingBy(v -> v, Collectors.counting()));
    }
}

Trace:

addNode("server1") → places 150 vnodes for server1 on ring
addNode("server2") → places 150 vnodes for server2 on ring
addNode("server3") → places 150 vnodes for server3 on ring

getNode("user-123") → hash("user-123") = 5432...
  → ring.ceilingEntry(5432) → "server2" (nearest clockwise)

removeNode("server2"):
  → removes all 150 server2 vnodes
  → requests that were going to server2 now go to server3 (next clockwise)
  → server1 and server3 requests unaffected ✓

Why 150 virtual nodes?

With 150 vnodes per physical node and 3 servers:

  • 450 total points on the ring
  • Statistical distribution becomes very uniform (σ² is low)
  • Too few vnodes → uneven load (some nodes get 2x traffic of others)
  • Too many → memory overhead

Why synchronized? Ring operations must be atomic — concurrent

addNode + getNode could cause race conditions on the TreeMap.

For production, use ReadWriteLock (multiple concurrent reads, exclusive writes):

private final ReadWriteLock lock = new ReentrantReadWriteLock();

public String getNode(String key) {
    lock.readLock().lock();
    try {
        // ... read operation
    } finally {
        lock.readLock().unlock();
    }
}

public void addNode(String node) {
    lock.writeLock().lock();
    try {
        // ... write operation
    } finally {
        lock.writeLock().unlock();
    }
}

Problem 2: Design a Rate Limiter for Internal APIs (20 min)

👨‍💻 Wei: *"Shopee's internal service mesh needs per-service

rate limiting. Implement a SlidingWindowRateLimiter that limits

requests per service per second. Thread-safe, no external dependencies."*

Limit: 100 requests/second per serviceId
isAllowed("payment-service") → true/false

👨 Khanh: I'll use a sliding window with a circular buffer —

O(1) time and space per check.

public class SlidingWindowRateLimiter {

    private final int maxRequests;
    private final long windowMs;

    // Per-service tracking
    private final ConcurrentHashMap<String, ServiceWindow> windows =
        new ConcurrentHashMap<>();

    public SlidingWindowRateLimiter(int maxRequests, long windowMs) {
        this.maxRequests = maxRequests;
        this.windowMs = windowMs;
    }

    public boolean isAllowed(String serviceId) {
        ServiceWindow window = windows.computeIfAbsent(
            serviceId, k -> new ServiceWindow(maxRequests)
        );
        return window.tryAcquire(windowMs);
    }

    private static class ServiceWindow {
        private final long[] timestamps; // circular buffer
        private int head = 0;            // oldest entry
        private int count = 0;           // entries in window
        private final int capacity;
        private final Object lock = new Object();

        ServiceWindow(int capacity) {
            this.capacity = capacity;
            this.timestamps = new long[capacity];
        }

        boolean tryAcquire(long windowMs) {
            synchronized (lock) {
                long now = System.currentTimeMillis();
                long windowStart = now - windowMs;

                // Remove expired entries from the front
                while (count > 0 && timestamps[head] <= windowStart) {
                    head = (head + 1) % capacity;
                    count--;
                }

                if (count >= capacity) {
                    return false; // window full — rate limit exceeded
                }

                // Add current timestamp at tail
                int tail = (head + count) % capacity;
                timestamps[tail] = now;
                count++;
                return true;
            }
        }
    }
}

Test:

@Test
void shouldAllowUpToLimit() {
    var limiter = new SlidingWindowRateLimiter(5, 1000);
    for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
        assertThat(limiter.isAllowed("svc-A")).isTrue();
    }
    assertThat(limiter.isAllowed("svc-A")).isFalse(); // 6th denied
}

@Test
void shouldAllowAfterWindowExpires() throws InterruptedException {
    var limiter = new SlidingWindowRateLimiter(2, 100); // 2 req per 100ms
    limiter.isAllowed("svc");
    limiter.isAllowed("svc");
    assertThat(limiter.isAllowed("svc")).isFalse();

    Thread.sleep(150); // wait for window to expire
    assertThat(limiter.isAllowed("svc")).isTrue(); // window reset
}

@Test
void shouldIsolatePerService() {
    var limiter = new SlidingWindowRateLimiter(1, 1000);
    assertThat(limiter.isAllowed("svc-A")).isTrue();
    assertThat(limiter.isAllowed("svc-A")).isFalse();
    assertThat(limiter.isAllowed("svc-B")).isTrue(); // different service
}

👨‍💻 Wei: Clean. The circular buffer avoids list allocations.

Strong solutions for both problems.

💻 ROUND 3 — SYSTEM DESIGN (60 min, English)

Setting: Staff Engineer, Platform team.

👨‍💻 Ryan (Staff Eng, Singapore): Design Shopee's **internal

observability platform.** Every service (500+) must emit metrics,

logs, and traces. Platform team provides the infrastructure. 50K

metrics/second, 1M log lines/second, engineers must be able to

debug any incident in < 5 minutes.

👨 Khanh:

Three Pillars of Observability:

METRICS   → "Is the system healthy?"  (numbers over time)
LOGS      → "What happened?"          (events with context)
TRACES    → "Where did time go?"      (request flow across services)

Architecture — each pillar has its own pipeline:

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Services (500+)                                        │
│  Java agent (auto-instruments): Micrometer + OTel SDK  │
└───┬─────────────────┬──────────────────────┬────────────┘
    │ METRICS          │ LOGS                 │ TRACES
    ▼                  ▼                      ▼
┌───────────┐   ┌─────────────┐       ┌─────────────┐
│Prometheus │   │ Kafka       │       │ Kafka       │
│Pushgateway│   │"logs.raw"   │       │"traces.raw" │
│or OTel    │   └──────┬──────┘       └──────┬──────┘
│Collector  │          │                      │
└─────┬─────┘          ▼                      ▼
      │         ┌─────────────┐       ┌─────────────┐
      ▼         │ Log         │       │ Trace       │
┌──────────┐    │ Processor   │       │ Processor   │
│ Thanos   │    │(parse, tag, │       │(sample,     │
│(long-term│    │ enrich)     │       │ store)      │
│ metrics  │    └──────┬──────┘       └──────┬──────┘
│ storage) │           │                     │
└──────────┘           ▼                     ▼
      │         ┌─────────────┐       ┌─────────────┐
      │         │Elasticsearch│       │  Jaeger /   │
      │         │(log storage │       │  Tempo      │
      │         │& search)    │       │  (trace DB) │
      │         └─────────────┘       └─────────────┘
      │                 │                     │
      └─────────────────┴─────────────────────┘
                        │
                   ┌────▼────┐
                   │ Grafana │  (unified dashboard: metrics + logs + traces)
                   └─────────┘

Metrics Pipeline (50K/sec):

// Service-side: auto-instrumented with Micrometer
@Configuration
public class MetricsConfig {
    @Bean
    MeterRegistryCustomizer<MeterRegistry> commonTags(
            @Value("${spring.application.name}") String appName) {
        return r -> r.config()
            .commonTags("service", appName, "env", "prod");
        // Auto-adds service + env to every metric
    }
}

// Custom business metric example
@Service
public class OrderService {
    private final Counter orderCounter;
    private final Timer orderProcessingTime;

    public OrderService(MeterRegistry registry) {
        orderCounter = Counter.builder("shopee.orders.total")
            .tag("status", "created")
            .register(registry);
        orderProcessingTime = Timer.builder("shopee.orders.processing.time")
            .percentiles(0.5, 0.95, 0.99)
            .register(registry);
    }
}

Log Pipeline (1M lines/sec):

Key design: structured JSON logging — every log line is parseable.

// logback-spring.xml — structured JSON output
// {"timestamp":"2024-01-15T10:30:00Z","level":"INFO",
//  "service":"order-service","traceId":"abc123","message":"Order created",
//  "orderId":"ORD-456","userId":"USR-789"}

@Slf4j
@Service
public class OrderService {
    public Order createOrder(OrderRequest req) {
        log.info("Order created",
            // Structured fields via MDC or SLF4J key-value
            kv("orderId", order.getId()),
            kv("userId", req.getUserId()),
            kv("amount", req.getAmount())
        );
    }
}

Kafka as the log transport: services → Kafka → Logstash processor →

Elasticsearch. Kafka decouples: if Elasticsearch is slow, services

don't block. Kafka retains logs for reprocessing if needed.

Trace Pipeline:

// Auto-instrumented via OpenTelemetry Java agent
// No code changes needed — agent instruments Spring Boot, Kafka, HTTP clients

// Just add to JVM args:
// -javaagent:opentelemetry-javaagent.jar
// -Dotel.service.name=order-service
// -Dotel.exporter.otlp.endpoint=http://collector:4317

Sampling strategy:

  • 100% sampling for errors
  • 10% sampling for normal requests (reduce storage)
  • 100% for requests > 1s (slow requests always traced)

Correlating the three pillars:

Every request gets a traceId (from OTel).
traceId is added to:
  - All log entries (via MDC: MDC.put("traceId", span.getTraceId()))
  - All metrics tags (tag: "traceId" on error metrics)
  - The trace itself

In Grafana:
  Engineer sees a spike in error rate (METRIC)
  → Clicks to see error logs for that time window (LOG)
  → Picks a specific error log → clicks traceId (TRACE)
  → Sees the full request journey across 5 services
  → Identifies which service and which line failed
  Total time: < 5 minutes ✓

Scaling to 500 services:

ConcernSolution
Kafka ingestion20-partition topics, consumer groups per region
Elasticsearch30-node cluster, index per day, hot-warm-cold tiers
Thanos metricsObject storage (S3) for long-term, Prometheus for recent
GrafanaSeparate dashboard per team, shared platform dashboards
Alert fatigueAlert on symptoms (user-visible), not causes (CPU)

Onboarding new services:

# New service just needs these in their K8s deployment:
annotations:
  prometheus.io/scrape: "true"
  prometheus.io/port: "8080"
  prometheus.io/path: "/actuator/prometheus"
# OTel agent auto-attached via K8s mutating webhook
# Logs auto-collected by Fluentd DaemonSet
# Zero code changes needed from service teams

👨‍💻 Ryan: That Grafana correlation flow — metric → log → trace

in < 5 minutes — is exactly our goal. The auto-instrumentation via

K8s webhook means service teams don't have to do anything. Strong.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

RoundCategoryScoreNotes
HRPlatform role motivation10/10"Multiplier effect" framing — platform thinking
HRObservability experience10/10Custom metric caught regression 30 min early
Coding 1Consistent hash ring10/10Virtual nodes, circular ring, ReadWriteLock
Coding 2Sliding window rate limiter10/10Circular buffer O(1), per-service isolation
DesignObservability platform10/103 pillars, structured logs, trace correlation
Auto-instrumentationK8s webhook zero-config10/10Service teams do nothing — platform handles it

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 Sea Group / Shopee Interview Tips

  1. Platform thinking. "My change helps 100 teams" > "my feature helps 10K users"
  2. Observability is core. They expect golden signals, trace correlation, alert design
  3. Consistent hashing is a classic platform interview question — know it cold
  4. Structured logging. JSON logs with traceId/spanId — not free-text log.info()
  5. OpenTelemetry — the standard. Know: metrics (Micrometer), logs (Logback), traces (OTel SDK)
  6. Sea Group is English-only. Communication quality matters as much as technical
  7. Salary negotiation. 60-100M range — anchor high (70-85M), they expect negotiation
  8. 4 rounds. Prepare for the long process — they're thorough

📊 FINAL: Complete Vietnam Top-Tier Mock Map

#CompanyTypeSalaryKey Topics
19NAB VietnamBanking Hub40-60MKYC, Envers, state machine
22VNG/ZaloPayUnicorn50-80MQR payment, Lua, Java concurrency
23MoMoFinTech45-65MSpring Batch settlement, fee calc
24TikiE-Commerce40-60MInventory reserve, SSE tracking
25TechcombankDigital Bank45-70MDouble-entry, NAPAS, HikariCP

🎭 Mock Interview #27 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: Got It (EdTech, Vietnamese Startup, HCMC/Hanoi/US)

Context: Got It is Vietnam's most successful EdTech startup.

Products: Got It Study (tutoring platform connecting 5M students with

50K tutors in the US/Vietnam), Trada (visual commerce AI for e-commerce).

Backed by $25M+ funding. Offices in HCMC, Hanoi, and San Francisco.

Hiring for the Core Platform team — the matching engine that connects

students to available tutors in real-time, handles session management,

and manages payments across USD (US students) and VND (Vietnam students).

Why Got It is interesting:

- True cross-border product (US + Vietnam users simultaneously)

- Real-time matching is a technically interesting problem

- Multi-currency payments (USD + VND in same platform)

- Strong engineering culture — CTO is ex-Amazon

- Senior salary: 40-60M + equity with potential upside

- English-first (US market is primary)

📞 ROUND 1 — HR SCREENING (20 min, English)

👩 Linh (HR): Hi Khanh! I'm Linh, People Operations. Got It is

English-first — our US team works directly with the Vietnam team.

How's your English communication in a fast-paced startup?

👨 Khanh: Very comfortable — at Sony, I've been communicating

directly with Japanese stakeholders in English daily for 2 years.

I write technical documentation, run design reviews, and have async

conversations with engineers in different time zones. Startup pace

is something I actively want — I find structured corporate processes

slow me down. I prefer shipping and iterating.

👩 Linh: Got It's culture is "builder mindset" — you own the

feature from design to deployment. No hand-offs. What does that

mean to you?

👨 Khanh: To me, ownership means I can't say "that's not my

job." If a feature I shipped breaks at 2 AM, it's my incident.

If the database schema I designed makes queries slow 6 months later,

I own fixing it.

At Sony, I once took ownership of our entire CI/CD pipeline when

our DevOps engineer left — not because I was asked to, but because

the team was blocked and I had enough knowledge to help. That's

the builder mentality I operate by.

👩 Linh: Why EdTech? You have fintech background.

👨 Khanh: Honest answer: the technical problems are what attract

me, not just the domain. Real-time matching at scale, multi-currency

payments, live tutoring session management — these are genuinely

hard distributed systems problems.

And EdTech has a clear social impact angle. In Vietnam, access to

quality education is often limited by geography or cost. Got It

removes those barriers digitally. That mission matters to me.

💻 ROUND 2 — TECHNICAL (60 min, English)

Setting: Staff Engineer (ex-Amazon).

👨‍💻 David (Staff Eng): Hi Khanh. I'm David, ex-Amazon. No

fluff today — practical engineering problems for our actual system.

Spring & Java Production (25 min)

👨‍💻 David: **Got It's matching engine receives 1K+ session

requests per minute at peak (US school dismissal time ~3-6 PM ET).

How do you design a Spring Boot service that's non-blocking during

matching — which takes 200-2000ms depending on availability?**

👨 Khanh: Classic async request handling. The client shouldn't

wait 2 seconds for a match — they should get an immediate response

and receive the match result asynchronously.

// Request-accept pattern with polling or WebSocket callback
@RestController
@RequiredArgsConstructor
public class MatchingController {

    private final MatchingService matchingService;

    // Step 1: Accept request immediately
    @PostMapping("/sessions/request")
    public ResponseEntity<SessionRequestResponse> requestSession(
            @Valid @RequestBody SessionRequest req,
            @AuthenticationPrincipal StudentPrincipal student) {

        String sessionId = UUID.randomUUID().toString();

        // Start matching asynchronously — don't block HTTP thread
        matchingService.startMatchingAsync(sessionId, req, student.getId());

        return ResponseEntity.accepted()
            .body(new SessionRequestResponse(
                sessionId,
                "FINDING_TUTOR",
                "/sessions/" + sessionId + "/status"
            ));
    }

    // Step 2: Client polls for result (or gets WebSocket push)
    @GetMapping("/sessions/{id}/status")
    public SessionStatusResponse getStatus(@PathVariable String id) {
        return matchingService.getStatus(id);
    }
}

@Service
@RequiredArgsConstructor
public class MatchingService {

    private final TutorAvailabilityCache tutorCache;
    private final SessionRepository sessionRepo;
    private final NotificationService notificationService;
    private final ApplicationEventPublisher eventPublisher;

    @Async("matchingExecutor")
    public CompletableFuture<Void> startMatchingAsync(
            String sessionId, SessionRequest req, String studentId) {

        try {
            // Create session record
            Session session = Session.requested(sessionId, studentId, req.getSubject());
            sessionRepo.save(session);

            // Run matching algorithm (200-2000ms)
            Optional<Tutor> match = findBestMatch(req);

            if (match.isPresent()) {
                session.assignTutor(match.get().getId());
                sessionRepo.save(session);

                // Notify student AND tutor
                notificationService.notifyStudent(studentId, session);
                notificationService.notifyTutor(match.get().getId(), session);
            } else {
                session.setStatus(NO_TUTOR_AVAILABLE);
                sessionRepo.save(session);
                notificationService.notifyNoTutor(studentId, session);
            }

        } catch (Exception e) {
            log.error("Matching failed for session {}", sessionId, e);
            sessionRepo.updateStatus(sessionId, MATCHING_FAILED);
        }

        return CompletableFuture.completedFuture(null);
    }

    private Optional<Tutor> findBestMatch(SessionRequest req) {
        // Check Redis for available tutors by subject
        List<Tutor> available = tutorCache.getAvailableBySubject(req.getSubject());
        return available.stream()
            .filter(t -> t.getRating() >= req.getMinRating())
            .max(Comparator.comparing(Tutor::getMatchScore));
    }
}

// Thread pool sized for matching workload
@Bean("matchingExecutor")
public Executor matchingExecutor() {
    ThreadPoolTaskExecutor exec = new ThreadPoolTaskExecutor();
    exec.setCorePoolSize(20);
    exec.setMaxPoolSize(50);
    exec.setQueueCapacity(200);
    exec.setThreadNamePrefix("matching-");
    exec.setRejectedExecutionHandler(new ThreadPoolExecutor.CallerRunsPolicy());
    exec.initialize();
    return exec;
}

Real-time push via WebSocket (better than polling):

@EventListener
public void onSessionMatched(SessionMatchedEvent event) {
    // Push update to student's browser in real-time
    messagingTemplate.convertAndSendToUser(
        event.getStudentId(),
        "/queue/session-updates",
        new SessionUpdateMessage(event.getSessionId(), "TUTOR_FOUND",
            event.getTutorName(), event.getEstimatedStartTime())
    );
}

👨‍💻 David: **Multi-currency is tricky. How do you store and

process payments in both USD (US students) and VND (Vietnam students)?**

👨 Khanh: Three key decisions:

1. Store currency alongside every monetary amount:

@Embeddable
public record Money(
    @Column(name = "amount") BigDecimal amount,
    @Column(name = "currency") String currency  // "USD" or "VND"
) {
    public static Money usd(BigDecimal amount) { return new Money(amount, "USD"); }
    public static Money vnd(long amount) { return new Money(BigDecimal.valueOf(amount), "VND"); }

    // Never compare amounts across currencies directly
    public Money add(Money other) {
        if (!this.currency.equals(other.currency)) {
            throw new CurrencyMismatchException(this.currency, other.currency);
        }
        return new Money(this.amount.add(other.amount), this.currency);
    }
}

@Entity
public class Session {
    @Embedded
    @AttributeOverrides({
        @AttributeOverride(name = "amount",   column = @Column(name = "price_amount")),
        @AttributeOverride(name = "currency", column = @Column(name = "price_currency"))
    })
    private Money price; // e.g., USD 15.00 or VND 200,000
}

2. Decimal places per currency:

public enum CurrencyConfig {
    USD(2, BigDecimal.class),   // $15.99 — 2 decimal places
    VND(0, Long.class);         // 200,000 — no decimal places

    private final int decimalPlaces;

    public BigDecimal round(BigDecimal amount) {
        return amount.setScale(decimalPlaces, RoundingMode.HALF_UP);
    }
}

3. Never convert currencies implicitly:

// ❌ BAD — implicit conversion hides bugs
BigDecimal usdAmount = session.getPrice(); // is this USD or VND?

// ✅ GOOD — always explicit
Money sessionPrice = session.getPrice();
if (sessionPrice.getCurrency().equals("VND")) {
    // Handle VND-specific logic
}

Payment processing:

  • US students → Stripe (USD, credit card)
  • VN students → MoMo or ZaloPay SDK (VND, local wallet)

Each payment processor is behind an interface:

public interface PaymentProcessor {
    PaymentResult charge(Money amount, PaymentMethod method);
    String getSupportedCurrency();
}

@Component
public class PaymentProcessorRouter {
    private final Map<String, PaymentProcessor> processors;

    public PaymentResult process(Money amount, PaymentMethod method) {
        PaymentProcessor processor = processors.get(amount.getCurrency());
        if (processor == null) {
            throw new UnsupportedCurrencyException(amount.getCurrency());
        }
        return processor.charge(amount, method);
    }
}

Design: Tutor Availability System (35 min)

👨‍💻 David: Got It's core challenge: 50K tutors, each with

their own weekly schedule. When a student requests a session NOW,

we need to find available tutors in < 500ms. How do you design

the tutor availability system?

👨 Khanh:

Two types of availability:

  1. Scheduled availability — tutor sets recurring hours: "I'm

available Mon-Fri 6-10 PM ET"

  1. Online now — tutor is actively online and accepting requests

Architecture:

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  TUTOR APP                                      │
│  "Go Online" button → real-time availability    │
└──────────────────┬──────────────────────────────┘
                   │
         ┌─────────▼──────────┐
         │  Availability API  │
         └─────────┬──────────┘
                   │
         ┌─────────▼──────────┐      ┌──────────────────┐
         │  Redis              │      │ PostgreSQL        │
         │  (online now)       │      │ (scheduled hours) │
         │  SET online:tutors  │      │ tutor_schedules   │
         │  TTL: 60 seconds    │      │                  │
         └─────────────────────┘      └──────────────────┘

"Online Now" via Redis with heartbeat:

// Tutor app sends heartbeat every 30 seconds
@PostMapping("/tutors/{id}/heartbeat")
public void heartbeat(@PathVariable String id) {
    // Add to Redis sorted set with current timestamp as score
    redisTemplate.opsForZSet().add(
        "tutors:online:" + getSubjects(id),  // one set per subject
        id,
        System.currentTimeMillis()
    );
    // TTL handled by scheduled cleanup (see below)
}

// Remove tutors who haven't sent heartbeat in 90 seconds
@Scheduled(fixedDelay = 30_000)
public void cleanupOfflineTutors() {
    long cutoff = System.currentTimeMillis() - 90_000;
    redisTemplate.opsForZSet().removeRangeByScore(
        "tutors:online:math",   // one per subject
        0, cutoff
    );
}

// Matching: get available tutors for a subject
public List<String> getAvailableTutors(String subject) {
    long cutoff = System.currentTimeMillis() - 90_000;
    // Get tutors active in the last 90 seconds
    return new ArrayList<>(redisTemplate.opsForZSet()
        .rangeByScore("tutors:online:" + subject, cutoff, Double.MAX_VALUE));
}

Scheduled availability in PostgreSQL:

CREATE TABLE tutor_schedules (
    id          BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    tutor_id    VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    day_of_week INT NOT NULL,       -- 1=Mon, 7=Sun
    start_time  TIME NOT NULL,      -- in tutor's timezone
    end_time    TIME NOT NULL,
    timezone    VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    subjects    TEXT[] NOT NULL,    -- array of subjects: {'math','physics'}
    is_active   BOOLEAN DEFAULT TRUE
);

-- Query: find tutors scheduled NOW for 'math'
SELECT DISTINCT tutor_id FROM tutor_schedules
WHERE is_active = TRUE
  AND day_of_week = EXTRACT(DOW FROM NOW() AT TIME ZONE timezone)
  AND start_time <= (NOW() AT TIME ZONE timezone)::TIME
  AND end_time >= (NOW() AT TIME ZONE timezone)::TIME
  AND 'math' = ANY(subjects);

Combined matching strategy:

public List<Tutor> findAvailableTutors(String subject, Instant requestTime) {
    // Priority 1: Online NOW (real-time, fastest match)
    List<String> onlineTutorIds = getAvailableTutors(subject);

    if (onlineTutorIds.size() >= 5) {
        // Enough online tutors — no need for schedule query
        return enrichAndRank(onlineTutorIds);
    }

    // Priority 2: Scheduled (should be available now, may need to ping)
    List<String> scheduledIds = scheduleRepo.findAvailableNow(subject, requestTime);

    // Combine, deduplicate, rank by: rating, response time, match history
    return Stream.concat(onlineTutorIds.stream(), scheduledIds.stream())
        .distinct()
        .map(this::enrich)
        .sorted(Comparator.comparing(Tutor::getMatchScore).reversed())
        .limit(10)
        .collect(Collectors.toList());
}

👨‍💻 David: The Redis sorted set with timestamp score for

heartbeat tracking — that's clean. How do you handle timezone

complexity for scheduled tutors?

👨 Khanh: Store ALL times in UTC in the database. The timezone

column on tutor_schedules is for display and input conversion only.

When a tutor in EST sets "I'm available 6-10 PM," we convert to

UTC (11 PM - 3 AM UTC) before storing. When querying, we compare

against current UTC time.

public void setSchedule(String tutorId, ScheduleRequest req) {
    ZoneId tutorZone = ZoneId.of(req.getTimezone()); // e.g., "America/New_York"

    LocalTime startLocal = req.getStartTime();  // 18:00 in EST
    LocalTime endLocal = req.getEndTime();       // 22:00 in EST

    // Convert to UTC for storage
    ZonedDateTime startUtc = startLocal.atDate(LocalDate.now())
        .atZone(tutorZone)
        .withZoneSameInstant(ZoneOffset.UTC);

    scheduleRepo.save(TutorSchedule.builder()
        .tutorId(tutorId)
        .startTimeUtc(startUtc.toLocalTime())
        .dayOfWeekUtc(startUtc.getDayOfWeek().getValue())
        .timezone(req.getTimezone())  // keep for display
        .build());
}

Edge case: US Daylight Saving Time shifts. By storing in UTC,

we're immune — 6 PM EST in summer is 10 PM UTC, in winter is

11 PM UTC. The schedule automatically adjusts.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

RoundCategoryScoreNotes
HRBuilder mindset10/10CI/CD ownership story, startup mentality authentic
SpringAsync matching (202+polling)10/10Executor config, WebSocket push, CallerRunsPolicy
Multi-currencyMoney record + processor router10/10Currency-aware type, no implicit conversion
DesignTutor availability system10/10Redis sorted set heartbeat, scheduled availability, combined matching
TimezoneUTC storage, display conversion10/10DST immune, practical implementation

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 Got It Interview Tips

  1. English fluency is essential — US market primary, CTO is ex-Amazon
  2. Builder mindset — show you own things end-to-end, no hand-offs
  3. Real-time matching is the core product — understand async patterns deeply
  4. Multi-currency — Money value object, never bare BigDecimal
  5. Timezone handling — always store UTC, convert at boundaries
  6. Redis sorted set — elegant for "online now" with TTL via score
  7. Got It salary — 40-60M + meaningful equity (startup, not listed)
  8. EdTech is growing — online tutoring market post-COVID is massive

🎭 Mock Interview #28 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: Grab Vietnam (Super-App, Singapore HQ, HCMC Hub, 800+ engineers VN)

Context: Grab is Southeast Asia's #1 super-app — GrabCar, GrabFood,

GrabPay, GrabMart. Revenue $2.4B, 35M monthly active users across 8 countries.

HCMC engineering hub is their second-largest after Singapore.

Role: Marketplace Platform team — the engine that matches drivers/merchants

to riders/customers in real-time. Tech stack: Java 17, Spring Boot, Kafka,

Redis, PostgreSQL, K8s on AWS.

Salary range: 55-90M+ gross. One of the top 3 employers in VN.

📞 ROUND 1 — HR SCREENING (20 min, English)

👩 Priya (Recruiter, Singapore): Hi Khanh! Tell me about yourself

and why Grab.

👨 Khanh: Hi Priya! Senior Java Engineer, 5 years, currently at

FPT/Sony. My most relevant experience for Grab: at Sony I designed

high-throughput event-driven services on AWS at 3K RPS with strict

SLAs. At Axon Active, I led distributed system design for a banking

platform.

Why Grab specifically: the Marketplace platform is one of the hardest

real-time matching problems in Southeast Asia. Matching 50K+ active

drivers to 2M+ daily ride requests, with dynamic pricing and live

GPS tracking — that's a genuinely hard distributed systems problem.

I want to work on systems at this scale and complexity.

Also — I use Grab daily. That direct connection between what I build

and what I experience as a user keeps engineering grounded in real impact.

👩 Priya: Grab moves fast — we ship multiple times per day.

How do you balance speed and quality?

👨 Khanh: Feature flags and incremental rollouts are my answer.

At Sony, every risky change ships behind a feature flag to 1% of

traffic first. We measure latency and error rates for 30 minutes,

then promote to 10%, 50%, 100%. That way we move fast without big bang risk.

For quality: I write tests for the happy path and the two most likely

failure modes. Not 100% coverage — pragmatic coverage. A well-tested

core with fast iteration beats a perfectly tested system that ships slowly.

👩 Priya: Expected compensation?

👨 Khanh: I've researched Grab's HCMC senior range — 55-90M.

With my background in distributed systems and payments, I'm targeting

70-80M gross as a starting point. I'm also interested in the RSU structure.

💻 ROUND 2 — TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE (75 min, English)

Setting: Staff Engineer from Singapore + Senior Engineer HCMC.

👨‍💻 Kai (Staff Eng, SG): Hi Khanh. Three parts: Java/distributed

systems (20 min), system design (35 min), coding (20 min).

PART A — Java & Distributed Systems (20 min)

👨‍💻 Kai: **Explain the difference between optimistic and

pessimistic locking. For a ride booking system where two users

might book the same driver simultaneously, which do you use and why?**

👨 Khanh: Two fundamentally different strategies for concurrent access:

Pessimistic locking: Lock the resource before reading it.

No other transaction can modify it until you release the lock.

// SELECT ... FOR UPDATE — locks the row
@Query("SELECT d FROM Driver d WHERE d.id = :id")
@Lock(LockModeType.PESSIMISTIC_WRITE)
Driver findByIdForUpdate(@Param("id") String id);

// Usage
@Transactional
public Booking bookDriver(String driverId, String riderId) {
    Driver driver = driverRepo.findByIdForUpdate(driverId); // LOCKED
    if (!driver.isAvailable()) throw new DriverUnavailableException();
    driver.setStatus(BUSY);
    driverRepo.save(driver);
    return bookingRepo.save(new Booking(driverId, riderId));
    // Lock released on commit
}

Optimistic locking: No lock taken. Use a version column.

If two transactions read the same version and both try to update,

the second one fails with OptimisticLockException.

@Entity
public class Driver {
    @Id private String id;
    private DriverStatus status;

    @Version
    private Long version; // auto-incremented on every update
}

// Service — no explicit lock
@Transactional
public Booking bookDriver(String driverId, String riderId) {
    Driver driver = driverRepo.findById(driverId).orElseThrow();
    if (!driver.isAvailable()) throw new DriverUnavailableException();
    driver.setStatus(BUSY); // if concurrent update happened → OptimisticLockException
    driverRepo.save(driver);
    return bookingRepo.save(new Booking(driverId, riderId));
}

For Grab's ride booking — I use pessimistic:

Reason: at Grab's scale, a driver gets booking requests from potentially

hundreds of nearby riders simultaneously during peak hours. Optimistic

locking would cause a thundering herd of retries — every concurrent

booking except one would fail and retry. With pessimistic locking,

only one succeeds and others fail fast with a clear "Driver unavailable."

However, locking duration must be minimal. The entire book-driver

transaction completes in < 100ms so lock contention is brief.

Alternative at true Grab scale: Use Redis atomic operations

instead of DB locks — much faster:

// Atomic: mark driver as busy only if currently available
Boolean reserved = redisTemplate.execute(
    RESERVE_DRIVER_SCRIPT,
    List.of("driver:status:" + driverId),
    "AVAILABLE", "BUSY"
);
// Lua: if GET key == AVAILABLE, SET key = BUSY, return 1; else return 0
if (!Boolean.TRUE.equals(reserved)) {
    throw new DriverUnavailableException();
}

Redis operations are 10x faster than DB SELECT FOR UPDATE at scale.

👨‍💻 Kai: **What is eventual consistency? Give a concrete

example of where Grab would accept it and where we would not.**

👨 Khanh: Eventual consistency means that after all updates

propagate, all nodes converge to the same state — but there's a

window where different nodes may return different values.

Where Grab ACCEPTS eventual consistency:

Driver location updates (GPS every 5 seconds):

Driver moves → GPS update → Redis (primary updated)
→ Redis replica might show old location for 100ms
→ Nearby riders see slightly outdated driver position on map

100ms stale position for a car moving 40 km/h = 1.1 meter error.

Completely acceptable for map display.

Surge pricing calculation:

Supply/demand ratio calculated every 30 seconds
→ Different app servers might use pricing calculated 10-30 sec ago
→ Rider might see 1.2x but pay 1.3x (or vice versa)

Acceptable — close enough for dynamic pricing.

Where Grab does NOT accept eventual consistency:

Ride booking — must be strongly consistent:

WRONG: Driver marked BUSY on primary DB
       Replica still shows AVAILABLE
       Second rider books same driver from replica read
→ Two riders in same car — unacceptable

Payment deduction:

WRONG: Rider's balance deducted on primary
       Replica shows old (higher) balance
       Rider books another ride using stale balance
→ Overdraft / double-spending — unacceptable

Rule: User-visible money and exclusive resource assignment → strong consistency.

Analytics, location display, recommendations → eventual consistency is fine.

PART B — SYSTEM DESIGN: Ride Matching (35 min)

👨‍💻 Kai: *"Design Grab's real-time driver-rider matching.

A rider requests a ride from point A to B. Find the best available

driver within 3km. Respond in < 2 seconds. Scale: 2M rides/day,

peak 500 ride requests/second in HCMC alone."*

👨 Khanh: [2 min thinking]

Core Problem:

  • 50K+ active drivers in HCMC at peak, each with changing GPS position
  • 500 ride requests/second — each needs nearest available driver found < 2s
  • Driver availability changes every second (accepting/completing rides)

Key Insight: This is a geospatial + availability problem.

Two data structures needed:

  1. Where is each driver? (geospatial index)
  2. Is this driver available? (availability state)

Architecture:

Rider App → Matching API
               │
    ┌──────────▼────────────┐
    │   Matching Service     │
    │   1. Geo search        │──► Redis GEO (driver locations)
    │   2. Availability check│──► Redis HASH (driver status)
    │   3. Score & rank      │
    │   4. Send offer        │──► Kafka "driver.offer"
    └────────────────────────┘
               │
    ┌──────────▼────────────┐
    │   Driver App           │ (accept/reject within 15s)
    └────────────────────────┘

Driver Location in Redis GEO:

// Driver sends GPS every 5 seconds
@PostMapping("/drivers/{id}/location")
public void updateLocation(@PathVariable String id,
                           @RequestBody GpsUpdate gps) {
    // Redis GEO — stores lat/lng as geohash, enables radius search
    redisTemplate.opsForGeo().add(
        "drivers:locations",
        new Point(gps.getLongitude(), gps.getLatitude()),
        id
    );
    // Also update last-seen timestamp
    redisTemplate.opsForHash().put("drivers:lastseen", id,
        String.valueOf(System.currentTimeMillis()));
}

// Find drivers within 3km of pickup point
public List<String> findNearbyDrivers(double lat, double lng) {
    Circle searchArea = new Circle(
        new Point(lng, lat),
        new Distance(3, Metrics.KILOMETERS)
    );
    GeoResults<GeoLocation<String>> results =
        redisTemplate.opsForGeo()
            .radius("drivers:locations", searchArea,
                GeoRadiusCommandArgs.newGeoRadiusArgs()
                    .includeDistance()
                    .sortAscending()
                    .limit(20)); // top 20 nearest

    return results.getContent().stream()
        .map(r -> r.getContent().getName())
        .collect(Collectors.toList());
}

Driver Availability Filter:

public List<String> getAvailableDrivers(List<String> nearbyIds) {
    // Batch check availability in Redis HASH
    List<Object> statuses = redisTemplate.opsForHash()
        .multiGet("drivers:status", new ArrayList<>(nearbyIds));

    List<String> available = new ArrayList<>();
    for (int i = 0; i < nearbyIds.size(); i++) {
        if ("AVAILABLE".equals(statuses.get(i))) {
            available.add(nearbyIds.get(i));
        }
    }
    return available;
}

Matching + Scoring:

public MatchResult matchDriver(RideRequest req) {
    // 1. Find nearby drivers (Redis GEO)
    List<String> nearby = findNearbyDrivers(req.getPickupLat(), req.getPickupLng());

    // 2. Filter available (Redis HASH multiGet — single round trip)
    List<String> available = getAvailableDrivers(nearby);

    if (available.isEmpty()) return MatchResult.noDriver();

    // 3. Score drivers
    Driver best = available.stream()
        .map(driverCache::get)  // local in-memory cache of driver profiles
        .max(Comparator.comparingDouble(d ->
            scoreDriver(d, req)))
        .orElseThrow();

    // 4. Tentatively reserve driver (atomic Redis)
    boolean reserved = reserveDriver(best.getId());
    if (!reserved) {
        // Driver just became unavailable — retry with next best
        available.remove(best.getId());
        // ... retry logic
    }

    return MatchResult.matched(best, req);
}

private double scoreDriver(Driver d, RideRequest req) {
    double distanceScore = 1.0 / (d.getDistanceKm() + 0.1);  // closer = better
    double ratingScore = d.getRating() / 5.0;
    double acceptanceScore = d.getAcceptanceRate();
    // Weighted composite score
    return 0.5 * distanceScore + 0.3 * ratingScore + 0.2 * acceptanceScore;
}

Surge Pricing:

@Scheduled(fixedDelay = 30_000)
public void recalculateSurge() {
    // For each city zone: calculate supply/demand ratio
    cityZones.forEach(zone -> {
        long activeDrivers = countAvailableDrivers(zone);
        long pendingRequests = countPendingRequests(zone);

        double ratio = (double) pendingRequests / Math.max(activeDrivers, 1);
        double surgeMultiplier = calculateSurge(ratio);
        // Store in Redis for fast read by Matching Service
        redisTemplate.opsForValue().set(
            "surge:" + zone.getId(),
            String.valueOf(surgeMultiplier),
            Duration.ofSeconds(60)
        );
    });
}

private double calculateSurge(double demandSupplyRatio) {
    if (ratio < 0.5) return 1.0;       // plenty of drivers
    if (ratio < 1.0) return 1.2;       // slight shortage
    if (ratio < 2.0) return 1.5;       // moderate shortage
    if (ratio < 3.0) return 2.0;       // high demand
    return 2.5;                         // cap surge at 2.5x
}

Offer + Timeout:

// Send offer to driver via push notification / WebSocket
kafkaTemplate.send("driver.offers", driverId,
    new DriverOffer(rideRequest, surgeMultiplier, offerExpiresAt));

// If driver doesn't accept in 15 seconds → try next driver
scheduledExecutor.schedule(
    () -> handleOfferTimeout(rideRequest, driverId),
    15, TimeUnit.SECONDS
);

👩‍💻 Anh (Sr Eng, HCMC): What if all nearby drivers reject?

👨 Khanh: Expand the search radius progressively:

  • Round 1: 3km, top 3 drivers, 15s timeout each
  • Round 2: 5km, next 5 drivers, 15s timeout
  • Round 3: 8km, next 10 drivers
  • After 3 minutes total → notify rider "No driver available"

Each round runs sequentially, expanding outward. We don't blast

all 50 nearby drivers at once — that creates a poor driver experience

(many accept a ride that's already booked).

PART C — LIVE CODING (20 min)

👨‍💻 Kai: *"Implement a function that, given a list of ride

events (REQUESTED, MATCHED, STARTED, COMPLETED, CANCELLED),

calculates metrics per driver: total rides completed, total rides

cancelled, cancellation rate, average ride duration (minutes)."*

Input events:
  {driverId: "D1", type: MATCHED,   rideId: "R1", timestamp: t=0}
  {driverId: "D1", type: STARTED,   rideId: "R1", timestamp: t=5}
  {driverId: "D1", type: COMPLETED, rideId: "R1", timestamp: t=25}
  {driverId: "D1", type: MATCHED,   rideId: "R2", timestamp: t=30}
  {driverId: "D1", type: CANCELLED, rideId: "R2", timestamp: t=35}

👨 Khanh:

public record RideEvent(
    String driverId,
    String rideId,
    EventType type,
    long timestamp  // epoch seconds
) {}

public record DriverMetrics(
    String driverId,
    int totalCompleted,
    int totalCancelled,
    double cancellationRate,        // cancelled / (completed + cancelled)
    double avgRideDurationMinutes
) {}

public List<DriverMetrics> calculateMetrics(List<RideEvent> events) {
    // Group events by driverId
    Map<String, List<RideEvent>> byDriver = events.stream()
        .collect(Collectors.groupingBy(RideEvent::driverId));

    return byDriver.entrySet().stream()
        .map(e -> computeDriverMetrics(e.getKey(), e.getValue()))
        .collect(Collectors.toList());
}

private DriverMetrics computeDriverMetrics(String driverId,
                                            List<RideEvent> events) {
    // Group events by rideId to process each ride
    Map<String, List<RideEvent>> byRide = events.stream()
        .collect(Collectors.groupingBy(RideEvent::rideId));

    int completed = 0;
    int cancelled = 0;
    long totalDurationSeconds = 0;

    for (Map.Entry<String, List<RideEvent>> rideEntry : byRide.entrySet()) {
        List<RideEvent> rideEvents = rideEntry.getValue();

        boolean isCompleted = rideEvents.stream()
            .anyMatch(e -> e.type() == EventType.COMPLETED);
        boolean isCancelled = rideEvents.stream()
            .anyMatch(e -> e.type() == EventType.CANCELLED);

        if (isCompleted) {
            completed++;
            // Duration = COMPLETED timestamp - STARTED timestamp
            OptionalLong startTime = rideEvents.stream()
                .filter(e -> e.type() == EventType.STARTED)
                .mapToLong(RideEvent::timestamp).findFirst();
            OptionalLong endTime = rideEvents.stream()
                .filter(e -> e.type() == EventType.COMPLETED)
                .mapToLong(RideEvent::timestamp).findFirst();

            if (startTime.isPresent() && endTime.isPresent()) {
                totalDurationSeconds += endTime.getAsLong() - startTime.getAsLong();
            }
        } else if (isCancelled) {
            cancelled++;
        }
    }

    int totalRides = completed + cancelled;
    double cancellationRate = totalRides == 0 ? 0.0
        : (double) cancelled / totalRides * 100;
    double avgDuration = completed == 0 ? 0.0
        : (totalDurationSeconds / 60.0) / completed;

    return new DriverMetrics(driverId, completed, cancelled,
        Math.round(cancellationRate * 100.0) / 100.0,
        Math.round(avgDuration * 100.0) / 100.0);
}

Trace for D1:

R1: MATCHED(t=0), STARTED(t=5), COMPLETED(t=25) → duration=20s → completed++
R2: MATCHED(t=30), CANCELLED(t=35) → cancelled++

Result: completed=1, cancelled=1
cancellationRate = 1/2 * 100 = 50.00%
avgDuration = (20/60) / 1 = 0.33 minutes

Tests:

@Test
void singleCompletedRide() {
    var events = List.of(
        new RideEvent("D1","R1", MATCHED,   0),
        new RideEvent("D1","R1", STARTED,   300),
        new RideEvent("D1","R1", COMPLETED, 1500)
    );
    var metrics = calculator.calculateMetrics(events);
    assertThat(metrics).hasSize(1);
    assertThat(metrics.get(0).totalCompleted()).isEqualTo(1);
    assertThat(metrics.get(0).cancellationRate()).isEqualTo(0.0);
    assertThat(metrics.get(0).avgRideDurationMinutes()).isEqualTo(20.0);
}

@Test
void mixedCompletedAndCancelled() {
    var events = List.of(
        new RideEvent("D1","R1", MATCHED,   0),
        new RideEvent("D1","R1", STARTED,   10),
        new RideEvent("D1","R1", COMPLETED, 310),
        new RideEvent("D1","R2", MATCHED,   400),
        new RideEvent("D1","R2", CANCELLED, 500)
    );
    var metrics = calculator.calculateMetrics(events).get(0);
    assertThat(metrics.totalCompleted()).isEqualTo(1);
    assertThat(metrics.totalCancelled()).isEqualTo(1);
    assertThat(metrics.cancellationRate()).isEqualTo(50.0);
}

@Test
void multipleDrivers() {
    var events = List.of(
        new RideEvent("D1","R1", COMPLETED, 0),
        new RideEvent("D2","R2", CANCELLED, 0)
    );
    var metrics = calculator.calculateMetrics(events);
    assertThat(metrics).hasSize(2);
}

👨‍💻 Kai: Java records for immutable event data, clean grouping

logic, separate concerns (ride-level vs driver-level), comprehensive

tests. Strong solution.

🤝 ROUND 3 — CULTURE FIT + VP (25 min, English)

👨‍💼 Marcus (VP Eng): Khanh, Grab operates in 8 countries

with different regulations, user behaviors, and infrastructure

reliability. How do you design systems that work across markets

with very different conditions?

👨 Khanh: Three principles I've developed:

1. Design for the worst connection, not the average.

Vietnam's 4G is inconsistent. If my API assumes a stable 100ms

network, it breaks in rural areas. I design all APIs with:

  • Idempotency keys (retries are safe)
  • Short-lived tokens that can be refreshed (not long-lived sessions)
  • Compressed payloads (Protobuf or gzipped JSON)
  • Aggressive client-side caching of reference data

2. Feature flags per market.

Not all markets have the same payment options, legal requirements,

or user preferences. I use feature flags keyed by countryCode

so the same codebase serves different behaviors:

if (featureFlags.isEnabled("CASH_PAYMENT", country)) {
    // Only Vietnam, Thailand enable cash rides
}

No separate codebases per country — one platform, configurable behavior.

3. Graceful degradation over hard failures.

If surge pricing service is down, don't reject the ride request —

default to 1.0x multiplier and log for reconciliation. If driver

rating service is slow, use cached rating. The core flow (rider

gets a driver) must work even when non-critical services fail.

👨‍💼 Marcus: "Design for the worst connection" — that's a

real production lesson most engineers learn the hard way. Welcome

to Grab.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

RoundCategoryScoreNotes
HRWhy Grab + salary10/10Daily user, distributed systems motivation, market-researched range
JavaPessimistic vs optimistic locking10/10Context-dependent choice + Redis alternative at scale
JavaEventual consistency10/10Location/surge = AP, booking/payment = CP. Clear examples
DesignDriver-rider matching10/10Redis GEO + HASH, progressive radius, surge calc, offer timeout
CodingDriver metrics calculator10/10Records, grouping, 3 comprehensive tests
CultureMulti-market design10/10Feature flags per country, worst-connection design, graceful degradation

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 Grab Interview Tips

  1. Redis GEO commands — GEOADD, GEORADIUS — know the API cold
  2. Pessimistic vs optimistic — always frame with "at Grab's scale" context
  3. Eventual vs strong consistency — have concrete examples ready
  4. Multi-market thinking — Grab operates in 8 countries, show global design sense
  5. Progressive radius expansion — don't blast all drivers at once
  6. Surge pricing — scheduled recalculation, zone-based, capped multiplier
  7. Grab salary — 55-90M gross. Strong negotiation position with your background
  8. English is mandatory — SG team leads most design discussions
