🎭 Mock Interview #29 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: Agoda (Booking Holdings, Travel Tech, Bangkok HQ, HCMC office)

Context: Agoda is one of the world's largest online travel platforms.

Owned by Booking Holdings ($15B revenue). 2.5M+ hotels, 400M+ users.

Engineering hubs in Bangkok, Singapore, HCMC, and Bangalore.

HCMC office: 300+ engineers, strong growth.

Role: Hotel Availability & Pricing team — the system that shows

"3 rooms left at $89/night" in real-time to 400M users worldwide.

Tech stack: Java 17, Spring Boot, Kafka, Redis, Cassandra, Elasticsearch, AWS.

Why Agoda stands out: International culture (English-first, 70+ nationalities),

travel domain is highly seasonal with 10x traffic spikes during holidays,

and pricing algorithms are genuinely complex (currency, taxes, promotions).

Salary: 50-80M+ gross in HCMC.

📞 ROUND 1 — HR SCREENING (20 min, English)

👩 Siri (Recruiter, Bangkok): Hi Khanh! Tell me about yourself

and why Agoda.

👨 Khanh: Hi Siri! I'm Khanh, Senior Java Engineer with nearly

5 years building high-throughput microservices. Key background for

Agoda:

At Sony, I optimized a service from 800ms to 120ms P99 at 3K RPS —

the kind of performance engineering Agoda's search and pricing systems

demand. At Axon Active, I built banking systems that required absolute

accuracy in financial calculations — directly relevant to Agoda's

multi-currency pricing.

Why Agoda: Travel pricing is one of the most complex pricing problems

in tech — real-time inventory from thousands of hotel partners, dynamic

pricing based on demand, promotions stacked on top, taxes varying by

country, and currency conversions. That complexity combined with global

scale is the kind of challenge I want to work on.

I also travel frequently and use Agoda myself, which gives me a user

perspective on the product.

👩 Siri: Agoda is extremely data-driven — every engineering decision

is backed by metrics. How do you approach measuring the impact of

technical improvements?

👨 Khanh: I define success metrics before writing code. For every

significant change I make, I answer three questions upfront:

  1. What is the current baseline? (measure first)
  2. What metric will improve? (latency, error rate, throughput, cost)
  3. How will I know the change worked? (A/B test, canary deployment with metrics comparison)

At Sony, before my latency optimization project, I established:

  • Baseline: P99 = 800ms, P50 = 200ms
  • Target: P99 < 500ms
  • Method: Deploy to 10% traffic, compare P99 between old and new using CloudWatch
  • Success criteria: P99 drops, error rate unchanged, CPU usage not significantly higher

I only declare success when data confirms it — not when the code looks right.

👩 Siri: Good. Three technical rounds — two with engineers,

one with Director of Engineering. Process takes about 2 weeks.

💻 ROUND 2 — TECHNICAL (70 min, English)

Setting: Staff Engineer from Bangkok + Senior Engineer HCMC.

👨‍💻 Tom (Staff Eng, Bangkok): Hi Khanh! Two parts: Java/Spring

deep-dive and a hotel availability system design.

PART A — Java & Spring Deep-Dive (25 min)

👨‍💻 Tom: **Explain Spring's bean lifecycle. When would you

use @PostConstruct, InitializingBean, and SmartLifecycle?**

👨 Khanh: Spring beans go through this lifecycle:

1. Instantiate (constructor called)
2. Populate properties (dependency injection)
3. BeanNameAware, BeanFactoryAware callbacks
4. BeanPostProcessor.postProcessBeforeInitialization()
5. @PostConstruct / InitializingBean.afterPropertiesSet()
6. BeanPostProcessor.postProcessAfterInitialization()
7. Bean is ready to use
8. ... (in use) ...
9. @PreDestroy / DisposableBean.destroy()

@PostConstruct — runs after DI, before the bean is used.

Most common choice. Simple, annotation-driven.

@Component
public class HotelPriceCache {
    private Map<String, BigDecimal> cache;

    @PostConstruct
    public void initialize() {
        // Load initial price data from DB into memory cache
        // DI is complete here — can use @Autowired fields
        cache = priceRepository.loadAllActivePrices();
        log.info("Loaded {} hotel prices into cache", cache.size());
    }
}

InitializingBean — same timing as @PostConstruct but interface-based.

Use when writing framework code (no Spring annotations in library code):

// Library code — no Spring dependency in the interface
public class ConnectionPoolManager implements InitializingBean {
    @Override
    public void afterPropertiesSet() {
        // Initialize connection pool
    }
}

SmartLifecycle — for beans that need to start/stop with the

application context lifecycle. Critical difference: runs AFTER all

beans are fully initialized, and supports graceful shutdown ordering:

@Component
public class KafkaConsumerService implements SmartLifecycle {
    private boolean running = false;

    @Override
    public void start() {
        // Start consuming — called after ALL Spring beans ready
        // Important: Kafka consumer should start AFTER DB, cache, etc. are ready
        consumer.subscribe(List.of("hotel.availability.updates"));
        running = true;
    }

    @Override
    public void stop() {
        // Graceful shutdown — finish processing in-flight messages
        consumer.close(Duration.ofSeconds(30));
        running = false;
    }

    @Override
    public int getPhase() { return 100; } // higher = starts later, stops sooner
}

When to use which:

ScenarioUse
Load cache data after DI@PostConstruct
Framework/library initializationInitializingBean
Start background threads/consumersSmartLifecycle
Anything needing graceful stop orderingSmartLifecycle

👩‍💻 Minh (Sr Eng, HCMC): **How do you implement pagination

for Elasticsearch in Spring? What's the difference between from/size

and search-after pagination?**

👨 Khanh: Two fundamentally different mechanisms:

from/size — offset-based (simple but limited):

@Repository
public class HotelSearchRepository {

    public Page<Hotel> search(HotelSearchQuery query, Pageable pageable) {
        NativeQuery searchQuery = NativeQuery.builder()
            .withQuery(buildQuery(query))
            .withSort(Sort.by(Sort.Direction.ASC, "price"))
            .withPageable(pageable) // from = page*size, size = pageSize
            .build();

        SearchHits<Hotel> hits = elasticsearchTemplate.search(searchQuery, Hotel.class);

        return SearchHitSupport.searchPageFor(hits, pageable).map(SearchHit::getContent);
    }
}

// Simple but: ES rejects from+size > 10,000
// Deep pagination causes performance issues (ES must fetch and discard early results)

search_after — cursor-based (production choice for deep pagination):

public SearchAfterResult<Hotel> searchWithCursor(
        HotelSearchQuery query, Object[] searchAfterValues, int size) {

    NativeQueryBuilder queryBuilder = NativeQuery.builder()
        .withQuery(buildQuery(query))
        .withSort(Sort.by(Sort.Direction.ASC, "price"))
        .withSort(Sort.by(Sort.Direction.ASC, "_id"))  // tie-breaker (unique)
        .withMaxResults(size);

    if (searchAfterValues != null) {
        // Continue from last item of previous page
        queryBuilder.withSearchAfter(searchAfterValues);
    }

    SearchHits<Hotel> hits = elasticsearchTemplate.search(
        queryBuilder.build(), Hotel.class);

    // Extract sort values from last hit → use as cursor for next page
    Object[] nextCursor = null;
    if (!hits.isEmpty()) {
        SearchHit<Hotel> lastHit = hits.getSearchHit((int) hits.getTotalHits() - 1);
        nextCursor = lastHit.getSortValues().toArray();
    }

    return new SearchAfterResult<>(
        hits.stream().map(SearchHit::getContent).toList(),
        nextCursor,
        hits.getTotalHits()
    );
}

Key differences:

Aspectfrom/sizesearch_after
Max depth10K resultsUnlimited
PerformanceDegrades with depthConsistent O(log N)
Random access✅ Can jump to page 5❌ Must traverse sequentially
Real-time results✅⚠️ Use PIT (Point In Time) for consistent pages
Use caseFirst 10 pagesInfinite scroll, export

For Agoda's hotel search (users rarely go past page 5), from/size

is fine. For a "export all hotels" admin feature, search_after is required.

PART B — SYSTEM DESIGN: Hotel Availability & Pricing (45 min)

👨‍💻 Tom: *"Design Agoda's hotel availability system.

Hotels push inventory updates (room availability + price) via API.

Users search for 'Bangkok, check-in Mar 15, 2 nights, 2 adults.'

Return available hotels sorted by price. Scale: 2.5M hotels,

500M search requests/day (peak: 50K searches/second), inventory

updates from hotels: 1M updates/minute."*

👨 Khanh: This is a read-heavy (searches >> updates) system

with complex filtering and strict freshness requirements.

Data Flow:

Hotel Partners → Inventory API → Kafka → Availability Processor
                                                  │
                                    ┌─────────────┴──────────────┐
                                    │                             │
                               Redis Cache              Elasticsearch
                               (exact date             (search & filter)
                                availability)
                                    │                             │
                                    └─────────────┬──────────────┘
                                                  │
                                     Search API ◄─┘
                                          │
                                       Users (50K QPS)

Inventory Update Pipeline:

// Hotel partner pushes availability update
@PostMapping("/inventory/update")
public ResponseEntity<Void> receiveInventoryUpdate(
        @RequestBody InventoryUpdate update,
        @RequestHeader("X-Hotel-API-Key") String apiKey) {

    // Validate API key, basic schema check
    inventoryValidator.validate(update, apiKey);

    // Publish to Kafka — accept immediately, process async
    kafkaTemplate.send("inventory.updates", update.getHotelId(), update);

    return ResponseEntity.accepted().build();
}

// Kafka consumer processes updates
@KafkaListener(topics = "inventory.updates", concurrency = "20")
public void processInventoryUpdate(InventoryUpdate update) {
    // Update Redis for exact date availability (fast lookup)
    updateRedisAvailability(update);

    // Update Elasticsearch for search indexing
    updateElasticsearchIndex(update);
}

Redis Availability Structure:

Key: "avail:{hotelId}:{roomTypeId}:{date}"
Value: {
  "available": 3,        -- rooms remaining
  "price": 89.00,
  "currency": "USD",
  "updatedAt": 1700000000
}
TTL: 24 hours (stale after a day if no update)
private void updateRedisAvailability(InventoryUpdate update) {
    for (AvailabilityRecord record : update.getRecords()) {
        String key = String.format("avail:%s:%s:%s",
            update.getHotelId(), record.getRoomTypeId(), record.getDate());

        Map<String, String> fields = Map.of(
            "available", String.valueOf(record.getRoomsAvailable()),
            "price",     record.getPrice().toPlainString(),
            "currency",  record.getCurrency(),
            "updatedAt", String.valueOf(Instant.now().getEpochSecond())
        );
        redisTemplate.opsForHash().putAll(key, fields);
        redisTemplate.expire(key, Duration.ofHours(24));
    }
}

Search Flow:

@Service
public class HotelSearchService {

    public List<HotelSearchResult> search(SearchQuery query) {
        // Step 1: Find hotels matching destination + amenities in Elasticsearch
        List<String> candidateHotelIds = elasticSearch.findHotels(
            query.getCity(), query.getAmenities(), query.getStarRating()
        );  // Returns ~1000 candidate hotels quickly

        // Step 2: Check Redis for actual availability on requested dates
        List<AvailableRoom> available = checkAvailabilityInRedis(
            candidateHotelIds, query.getCheckIn(), query.getCheckOut()
        );

        // Step 3: Calculate total price (multi-night, taxes, promotions)
        List<HotelSearchResult> results = available.stream()
            .map(room -> calculateTotalPrice(room, query))
            .filter(r -> r.getTotalPrice().compareTo(query.getMaxPrice()) <= 0)
            .sorted(Comparator.comparing(HotelSearchResult::getTotalPrice))
            .collect(Collectors.toList());

        return results;
    }

    private List<AvailableRoom> checkAvailabilityInRedis(
            List<String> hotelIds, LocalDate checkIn, LocalDate checkOut) {

        // Generate all dates in the stay
        List<LocalDate> nights = checkIn.datesUntil(checkOut).toList();

        List<AvailableRoom> available = new ArrayList<>();
        for (String hotelId : hotelIds) {
            // Check all nights in one Redis pipeline (single round-trip)
            List<Object> results = redisTemplate.executePipelined(connection -> {
                for (LocalDate night : nights) {
                    String key = "avail:" + hotelId + ":STANDARD:" + night;
                    connection.hashCommands().hGetAll(key.getBytes());
                }
                return null;
            });

            // Hotel is available only if ALL nights have rooms
            boolean allNightsAvailable = results.stream()
                .noneMatch(r -> r == null || ((Map<?, ?>) r).isEmpty() ||
                    Integer.parseInt(((Map<?, ?>) r).get("available").toString()) == 0);

            if (allNightsAvailable) {
                available.add(buildAvailableRoom(hotelId, results));
            }
        }
        return available;
    }
}

Multi-Currency Price Calculation:

public HotelSearchResult calculateTotalPrice(AvailableRoom room, SearchQuery query) {
    // Base price in hotel's native currency
    BigDecimal baseNightlyRate = room.getPrice();
    String hotelCurrency = room.getCurrency();

    // Total for all nights
    long nights = ChronoUnit.DAYS.between(query.getCheckIn(), query.getCheckOut());
    BigDecimal subtotal = baseNightlyRate.multiply(BigDecimal.valueOf(nights));

    // Apply promotions (member discount, early-bird, etc.)
    BigDecimal discount = promotionEngine.calculate(room, query.getUserTier());
    BigDecimal afterDiscount = subtotal.subtract(discount);

    // Add taxes (varies by country)
    BigDecimal taxRate = taxService.getRate(room.getCountry(), room.getCity());
    BigDecimal tax = afterDiscount.multiply(taxRate);
    BigDecimal totalInHotelCurrency = afterDiscount.add(tax);

    // Convert to user's preferred display currency
    BigDecimal exchangeRate = fxService.getRate(hotelCurrency, query.getDisplayCurrency());
    BigDecimal displayPrice = totalInHotelCurrency.multiply(exchangeRate)
        .setScale(2, RoundingMode.HALF_UP);

    return HotelSearchResult.builder()
        .hotelId(room.getHotelId())
        .totalPrice(displayPrice)
        .displayCurrency(query.getDisplayCurrency())
        .priceBreakdown(new PriceBreakdown(subtotal, discount, tax, exchangeRate))
        .build();
}

Handling 50K QPS Search:

LayerStrategy
CDNCache search results for popular queries (city + date) for 60s
API GatewayRate limit per user: 10 searches/second
Search ServiceHorizontal scale: 50 pods × 1K QPS each
RedisCluster mode: 20 shards, pipeline for multi-date checks
Elasticsearch30-node cluster, dedicated search nodes

Holiday Traffic (10x spike):

  • Pre-warm cache for predictable high-demand destinations (Đà Nẵng before Tết)
  • Auto-scaling: K8s HPA triggers on CPU > 70%
  • Circuit breaker: if Elasticsearch is slow → serve cached results with 5-minute TTL
  • Shed load gracefully: rate limit anonymous users more aggressively during peak

👨‍💻 Tom: The Redis pipeline for multi-date availability check —

single round-trip instead of N round-trips — that's the key optimization

here. Most candidates miss this. How do you handle a hotel that updates

prices 10 times per minute?

👨 Khanh: Kafka naturally handles this — if a hotel sends 10

updates in a minute, all 10 are in the topic. The consumer processes

them in order (Kafka preserves per-key order). Only the latest update

matters in Redis — each update overwrites the previous. No data loss,

no inconsistency.

For Elasticsearch, if updates come faster than the indexer can process,

I'd use Kafka consumer lag monitoring. If lag exceeds 5 minutes, scale

up consumers. The ES index might be 5 minutes stale during a burst —

acceptable for search (price might be slightly off), but Redis

(used for booking confirmation) gets the latest price directly from

the hotel's last update.

PART C — LIVE CODING (20 min)

👩‍💻 Minh: *"Write a method that finds the cheapest hotel room

for a multi-night stay. Given a map of {date → {hotelId → price}},

return the hotel with the lowest TOTAL price across all nights.

Only include hotels available for ALL requested nights."*

checkIn=Mar15, checkOut=Mar17 (2 nights: Mar15, Mar16)
prices = {
  Mar15: {H1: $80, H2: $90, H3: $70},
  Mar16: {H1: $85, H2: $88, H3: null}  ← H3 unavailable Mar16
}
Expected: H1 with total $165 (H2 = $178, H3 excluded)

👨 Khanh:

public Optional<HotelPriceSummary> findCheapestAvailableHotel(
        LocalDate checkIn,
        LocalDate checkOut,
        Map<LocalDate, Map<String, BigDecimal>> pricesByDate) {

    // Generate all nights in the stay
    List<LocalDate> nights = checkIn.datesUntil(checkOut).toList();

    if (nights.isEmpty()) return Optional.empty();

    // Find hotels available on the first night — then verify all others
    Map<String, BigDecimal> firstNightPrices = pricesByDate.getOrDefault(
        nights.get(0), Collections.emptyMap()
    );

    Map<String, BigDecimal> totalPrices = new HashMap<>();

    for (String hotelId : firstNightPrices.keySet()) {
        BigDecimal total = BigDecimal.ZERO;
        boolean availableAllNights = true;

        for (LocalDate night : nights) {
            Map<String, BigDecimal> nightPrices =
                pricesByDate.getOrDefault(night, Collections.emptyMap());

            BigDecimal nightPrice = nightPrices.get(hotelId);
            if (nightPrice == null) {
                availableAllNights = false;
                break; // skip this hotel
            }
            total = total.add(nightPrice);
        }

        if (availableAllNights) {
            totalPrices.put(hotelId, total);
        }
    }

    return totalPrices.entrySet().stream()
        .min(Map.Entry.comparingByValue())
        .map(e -> new HotelPriceSummary(e.getKey(), e.getValue(), nights.size()));
}

public record HotelPriceSummary(
    String hotelId,
    BigDecimal totalPrice,
    int nights
) {}

Trace:

nights = [Mar15, Mar16]

H1: Mar15=$80 ✓, Mar16=$85 ✓ → total = $165
H2: Mar15=$90 ✓, Mar16=$88 ✓ → total = $178
H3: Mar15=$70 ✓, Mar16=null → skip (unavailable)

min(H1=$165, H2=$178) → H1 ✓

Tests:

@Test
void findsLowestTotalExcludingUnavailable() {
    var prices = Map.of(
        MAR_15, Map.of("H1", bd(80), "H2", bd(90), "H3", bd(70)),
        MAR_16, Map.of("H1", bd(85), "H2", bd(88))
    );
    var result = service.findCheapestAvailableHotel(MAR_15, MAR_17, prices);

    assertThat(result).isPresent();
    assertThat(result.get().hotelId()).isEqualTo("H1");
    assertThat(result.get().totalPrice()).isEqualByComparingTo("165");
}

@Test
void returnsEmptyWhenNoHotelAvailableAllNights() {
    var prices = Map.of(
        MAR_15, Map.of("H1", bd(80)),
        MAR_16, Collections.emptyMap()  // no hotels Mar16
    );
    var result = service.findCheapestAvailableHotel(MAR_15, MAR_17, prices);
    assertThat(result).isEmpty();
}

@Test
void singleNightStay() {
    var prices = Map.of(MAR_15, Map.of("H1", bd(80), "H2", bd(60)));
    var result = service.findCheapestAvailableHotel(MAR_15, MAR_16, prices);

    assertThat(result.get().hotelId()).isEqualTo("H2");
    assertThat(result.get().totalPrice()).isEqualByComparingTo("60");
}

👨‍💻 Tom: Clean. datesUntil() is a nice Java 9+ API that

most candidates don't know. Early break on unavailable night avoids

unnecessary iterations. Tests cover the exact edge cases we care about.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

RoundCategoryScoreNotes
HRData-driven mindset10/10Define metrics BEFORE coding — baseline, target, method
JavaBean lifecycle10/10PostConstruct vs InitializingBean vs SmartLifecycle with real use cases
JavaES pagination10/10from/size limitations, search_after with PIT for production
DesignHotel availability system10/10Redis pipeline multi-date check, Kafka ordering, multi-currency
CodingCheapest available hotel10/10datesUntil(), early break, 3 comprehensive tests

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 Agoda Interview Tips

  1. Travel domain vocabulary. Check-in/check-out dates, room types, rate plans, OTA
  2. SmartLifecycle — Agoda has many background processors; know when to use it
  3. Elasticsearch search_after — they use ES heavily for hotel search
  4. Redis pipeline — multi-date availability check in single round trip
  5. Multi-currency with BigDecimal — critical; exchange rates + taxes + promotions
  6. Holiday traffic spikes — 10x is common in travel; show you've thought about it
  7. Data-driven culture — always measure before and after changes
  8. Agoda salary — 50-80M gross, English-first, international culture

🎭 Mock Interview #30 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: Lazada Vietnam (Alibaba Group E-Commerce, HCMC, 1200+ engineers)

Context: Lazada is Southeast Asia's leading e-commerce platform, fully

owned by Alibaba Group since 2018. Operating in 6 countries: Vietnam,

Thailand, Philippines, Malaysia, Indonesia, Singapore. 160M+ annual shoppers.

After Alibaba acquisition, Lazada migrated to Alibaba's technology stack:

**AliCloud, RocketMQ (not Kafka), Dubbo (not REST), Canal (CDC from MySQL),

AHAS (circuit breaker), and Nacos (config/discovery)**. Understanding this

stack is what separates candidates who know Lazada from those who don't.

Role: Logistics & Fulfillment Platform team — the system that routes

packages from seller → Lazada warehouse → last-mile delivery → buyer.

3M+ parcels/day across 6 countries.

Salary: 45-70M+ gross. Working language: English (SG/ID teams).

📞 ROUND 1 — HR SCREENING (20 min, English)

👩 Fiona (Recruiter, Singapore): Hi Khanh! Lazada uses Alibaba's

tech stack — AliCloud, RocketMQ, Dubbo. Do you have experience

with these?

👨 Khanh: Honest answer: I've worked with Kafka and Spring Cloud,

not RocketMQ and Dubbo directly. But here's my perspective on why

that's manageable:

RocketMQ and Kafka solve the same fundamental problem — distributed

message queue — with different trade-offs. RocketMQ has stronger

transaction message support, which I understand conceptually from

my work with distributed transactions at Axon Active. Switching

from Kafka to RocketMQ is a 2-3 week learning curve, not a 6-month one.

Similarly, Dubbo is a Java RPC framework. I've used Feign for

service-to-service calls. The patterns are the same: service

discovery, load balancing, circuit breaking. Different API, same

concepts.

What I bring is deep understanding of the why behind distributed

systems — consistency, availability, failure modes — that transfers

across any specific technology.

👩 Fiona: Fair answer. Lazada is fast-paced — we ship 10+ times

daily. How do you handle production incidents?

👨 Khanh: My incident response follows four steps:

  1. Mitigate first, diagnose second. If a feature flag can disable

the broken feature, do it immediately. Don't spend 20 minutes

finding root cause while users are impacted.

  1. Communicate proactively. I post updates to the incident channel

every 10 minutes — "Still investigating," "Found the cause, fixing

now," "Deployed fix, monitoring." Silence is the worst thing

in a production incident.

  1. Document everything in real-time. I keep a running timeline

in the incident doc: "10:03 — alerts fired. 10:07 — identified

memory spike. 10:12 — rolled back deployment."

  1. Post-mortem within 48 hours. Blameless — focus on system

gaps, not people. Every incident produces at least one preventive action.

👩 Fiona: Good. Technical round next — two engineers.

💻 ROUND 2 — TECHNICAL (70 min, English)

Setting: Staff Engineer + Senior Engineer.

👨‍💻 Wei (Staff Eng): Hi Khanh! We'll test both core Java

knowledge and logistics domain thinking.

PART A — Java Core + Distributed Systems (25 min)

👨‍💻 Wei: **Explain RocketMQ's transaction message feature.

How is it different from Kafka? When would you use it?**

👨 Khanh: This is a key differentiator of RocketMQ.

The problem: In distributed systems, you often need to do a

local DB transaction AND send a message atomically. Classic example:

Scenario: Order placed → deduct inventory → send "order created" event
Problem: What if DB commits but message send fails?
         What if message sent but DB commit fails?
→ Inconsistency between systems

Kafka's approach: Kafka supports transactions within Kafka

itself (exactly-once delivery between topics) but has no native

integration with your DB transaction. You must use the Outbox pattern

to achieve atomicity with DB.

RocketMQ Transaction Messages — built-in half-message protocol:

Step 1: Producer sends "half message" (prepared, not yet committed)
        → Message is stored in RocketMQ but NOT delivered to consumers yet

Step 2: Producer executes local DB transaction
        → INSERT order to DB

Step 3a: If DB commit SUCCESS → Producer sends "commit" to RocketMQ
         → Message is now delivered to consumers ✓

Step 3b: If DB commit FAILS → Producer sends "rollback" to RocketMQ
         → Half message is discarded, consumers never see it ✓

Step 4 (Broker-side): If producer crashes before Step 3 (no commit/rollback),
        RocketMQ broker calls back producer's "check local transaction" method
        → Producer checks DB: was the order inserted?
        → If yes → commit. If no → rollback.
        → This handles crash scenarios ✓
// RocketMQ transaction message implementation
public class OrderTransactionListener implements TransactionListener {

    private final OrderRepository orderRepo;

    @Override
    public LocalTransactionState executeLocalTransaction(Message msg, Object arg) {
        try {
            // Execute local DB transaction
            OrderRequest req = (OrderRequest) arg;
            orderRepo.save(Order.from(req));  // DB commit
            return LocalTransactionState.COMMIT_MESSAGE; // Step 3a
        } catch (Exception e) {
            log.error("Local transaction failed", e);
            return LocalTransactionState.ROLLBACK_MESSAGE; // Step 3b
        }
    }

    @Override
    public LocalTransactionState checkLocalTransaction(MessageExt msg) {
        // Step 4: Broker calls this if producer crashed
        String orderId = msg.getProperty("orderId");
        boolean exists = orderRepo.existsById(orderId);
        return exists
            ? LocalTransactionState.COMMIT_MESSAGE
            : LocalTransactionState.ROLLBACK_MESSAGE;
    }
}

Comparison:

FeatureKafkaRocketMQ
Transaction messagesVia Outbox patternBuilt-in half-message
Exactly-onceWithin Kafka topicsDB + Kafka atomicity
Message delay❌ (need external tool)✅ Built-in delay levels
Message filter by tag❌✅ tag filtering at broker
Dead Letter QueueManualAutomatic after max retries

When to use RocketMQ transaction messages at Lazada:

Order placed → must atomically update inventory DB AND publish

"order.created" event. Without transaction messages, we need the

Outbox pattern. With RocketMQ, it's built-in.

👩‍💻 Lan (Sr Eng, HCMC): **How would you use Canal for CDC

(Change Data Capture) in a logistics system? What problems does it solve?**

👨 Khanh: Canal is Alibaba's MySQL binlog reader — it positions

itself as a MySQL replica, receives the MySQL binary log (binlog),

and converts row-level changes into events.

What Canal solves:

Problem: Order table updated in MySQL
         → Cache in Redis needs to be invalidated
         → Elasticsearch index needs updating
         → Analytics DB needs the change

Without Canal (dual-write approach):
  Code updates MySQL → Code also updates Redis → Code also updates ES
  Risk: If Redis update fails → inconsistency
  Risk: Code is coupled to all downstream systems

With Canal:
  Code ONLY updates MySQL
  Canal reads binlog → publishes to RocketMQ
  Redis consumer reads event → invalidates cache
  ES consumer reads event → updates index
  Analytics consumer reads event → replicates data
  Each downstream is decoupled ✓

Setup at Lazada:

MySQL (primary) ──binlog──► Canal Server ──► RocketMQ topic "db.order.changes"
                                                    │
                           ┌────────────────────────┤
                           │                        │
                    ┌──────▼──────┐       ┌─────────▼──────┐
                    │ Redis Cache │       │ Elasticsearch  │
                    │ Invalidator │       │ Indexer        │
                    └─────────────┘       └────────────────┘

Example: Parcel status cache invalidation:

@RocketMQMessageListener(
    topic = "db.parcel.changes",
    consumerGroup = "cache-invalidator"
)
public class ParcelCacheInvalidator implements RocketMQListener<CanalEvent> {

    @Override
    public void onMessage(CanalEvent event) {
        if (!event.getTable().equals("parcels")) return;

        // Canal provides before/after row data
        String parcelId = event.getAfter().get("id");
        String newStatus = event.getAfter().get("status");

        // Invalidate specific cache key
        redisTemplate.delete("parcel:status:" + parcelId);

        log.info("Cache invalidated for parcel {} → new status: {}",
            parcelId, newStatus);
    }
}

Advantages over application-level dual-write:

  1. Even if the application crashes mid-operation, Canal reads from binlog

(durable, replayed from any position)

  1. Zero code coupling — the application doesn't know about Redis or ES
  2. Exactly what Alibaba uses for all their systems — proven at Taobao scale

PART B — SYSTEM DESIGN: Parcel Routing (45 min)

👨‍💻 Wei: *"Design Lazada's parcel routing system. A seller

ships a package. The system must: determine which warehouse to route

it to, assign it to the right carrier, track it through the journey,

and handle exceptions (failed delivery, lost parcel, return).*

Scale: 3M parcels/day across 6 countries."

👨 Khanh:

Parcel Lifecycle:

SELLER SHIPS → WAREHOUSE_RECEIVED → SORTED → IN_TRANSIT
    → OUT_FOR_DELIVERY → DELIVERED
    
Exception paths:
    → DELIVERY_FAILED → RESCHEDULED → (retry)
    → LOST → COMPENSATION_INITIATED
    → RETURN_REQUESTED → RETURNING → RETURNED_TO_SELLER

Key Design Decisions:

1. Routing Engine — which warehouse?

@Service
public class RoutingEngine {

    public RoutingDecision route(Parcel parcel) {
        Address destination = parcel.getDeliveryAddress();

        // Step 1: Find eligible warehouses by country/region
        List<Warehouse> eligible = warehouseRepo
            .findByCountryAndServiceableRegion(
                destination.getCountry(),
                destination.getProvince()
            );

        // Step 2: Score each warehouse
        Warehouse best = eligible.stream()
            .max(Comparator.comparingDouble(w ->
                scoreWarehouse(w, parcel, destination)))
            .orElseThrow(() -> new NoWarehouseException(destination));

        // Step 3: Select carrier
        Carrier carrier = carrierSelector.select(best, parcel, destination);

        return new RoutingDecision(best, carrier);
    }

    private double scoreWarehouse(Warehouse w, Parcel p, Address dest) {
        double distanceScore = 1.0 / (w.distanceTo(dest) + 1);
        double capacityScore = (double) w.getAvailableCapacity() / w.getTotalCapacity();
        double carrierScore = carrierSelector.hasReliableCarrierFor(w, dest) ? 1.0 : 0.5;
        return 0.4 * distanceScore + 0.4 * capacityScore + 0.2 * carrierScore;
    }
}

2. Parcel Tracking — event sourcing:

CREATE TABLE parcel_events (
    id          BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    parcel_id   VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    event_type  VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,   -- RECEIVED, SORTED, DISPATCHED, etc.
    location    VARCHAR(200),
    actor_id    VARCHAR(50),            -- warehouse staff, driver, system
    metadata    JSONB,                  -- carrier scan data, GPS coordinates
    occurred_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL,
    created_at  TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW()
);
CREATE INDEX idx_parcel_timeline ON parcel_events(parcel_id, occurred_at DESC);

Never update — only insert. Current status = latest event. Full audit trail.

3. Exception Handling — the hard part:

@Service
public class ParcelExceptionHandler {

    @KafkaListener(topics = "parcel.exceptions")
    public void handleException(ParcelExceptionEvent event) {
        Parcel parcel = parcelRepo.findById(event.getParcelId()).orElseThrow();

        switch (event.getExceptionType()) {
            case DELIVERY_FAILED -> handleFailedDelivery(parcel, event);
            case PARCEL_DAMAGED  -> handleDamaged(parcel, event);
            case PARCEL_LOST     -> handleLost(parcel, event);
            case RETURN_REQUEST  -> handleReturnRequest(parcel, event);
        }
    }

    private void handleFailedDelivery(Parcel parcel, ParcelExceptionEvent event) {
        int attemptCount = parcel.getDeliveryAttempts();

        if (attemptCount < 3) {
            // Reschedule for next day
            LocalDate nextAttempt = LocalDate.now().plusDays(1);
            deliveryScheduler.reschedule(parcel, nextAttempt);
            notificationService.notifyBuyer(parcel,
                "Delivery attempted but failed. Rescheduled for " + nextAttempt);
        } else {
            // 3 failed attempts → trigger return to seller
            parcel.setStatus(RETURN_PENDING);
            returnWorkflow.initiate(parcel, "Max delivery attempts reached");
            notificationService.notifyBuyer(parcel,
                "Unable to deliver after 3 attempts. Package being returned.");
            notificationService.notifySeller(parcel, "Return initiated");
        }
        recordEvent(parcel, DELIVERY_FAILED, event.getLocation());
    }

    private void handleLost(Parcel parcel, ParcelExceptionEvent event) {
        parcel.setStatus(LOST);
        // Trigger insurance/compensation workflow
        compensationService.initiateClaim(parcel);
        // Notify buyer and seller
        notificationService.notifyLost(parcel);
        recordEvent(parcel, PARCEL_LOST, event.getLocation());
    }
}

4. Cross-country routing complexity:

Parcels moving between Lazada countries (e.g., seller in China → buyer in Vietnam):

China warehouse → CONSOLIDATION → Customs clearance
→ Vietnam customs → Vietnam warehouse → Last-mile delivery

Each crossing has:

  • Customs documents (HS codes, declared value, duties)
  • Country-specific carrier (different carriers in each country)
  • Currency conversion (seller pays in CNY, buyer sees VND price)

I'd model this as a multi-leg journey where each leg is a separate

routing decision with its own carrier and tracking:

public class ParcelJourney {
    private final String parcelId;
    private final List<JourneyLeg> legs; // leg 1: CN→customs, leg 2: customs→VN warehouse, leg 3: VN delivery

    public JourneyLeg getCurrentLeg() {
        return legs.stream()
            .filter(l -> l.getStatus() == IN_PROGRESS)
            .findFirst()
            .orElse(legs.get(legs.size() - 1));
    }
}

5. Scaling 3M parcels/day:

ComponentStrategy
Routing EngineStateless service, horizontal scale
Parcel DBSharded by parcel_id (consistent hash)
Parcel EventsAppend-only, partitioned by month
Tracking APIRedis cache for current status (updated via Canal/CDC)
Carrier webhooksKafka topic per carrier, consumer group per processing type

👩‍💻 Lan: How do you handle carrier API failures when they don't

send tracking updates?

👨 Khanh: Polling fallback with escalating intervals:

@Scheduled(fixedDelay = 3_600_000) // every hour
public void pollStaleTracking() {
    LocalDateTime staleThreshold = LocalDateTime.now().minusHours(6);

    List<Parcel> staleParcels = parcelRepo.findInTransitWithNoUpdateSince(staleThreshold);

    for (Parcel parcel : staleParcels) {
        try {
            CarrierStatus status = carrierClient.queryStatus(
                parcel.getCarrierTrackingId());
            if (status != null) {
                recordEvent(parcel, status.toParcelEventType(), status.getLocation());
            }
        } catch (CarrierApiException e) {
            log.warn("Failed to poll carrier for parcel {}", parcel.getId());
            // Escalate after 24 hours with no update
            if (parcel.getLastUpdateAt().isBefore(LocalDateTime.now().minusHours(24))) {
                alertOpsTeam(parcel, "No tracking update in 24 hours");
            }
        }
    }
}

PART C — CODING (20 min)

👩‍💻 Lan: *"Given a list of parcels with their current status

and last-updated timestamp, write a method that identifies 'at-risk'

parcels: parcels IN_TRANSIT for more than 48 hours without an update,

grouped by carrier. Sort each carrier's list by how long they've

been stale (most stale first)."*

👨 Khanh:

public record Parcel(
    String id,
    String carrierId,
    ParcelStatus status,
    Instant lastUpdatedAt
) {}

public Map<String, List<Parcel>> findAtRiskParcels(
        List<Parcel> parcels,
        Instant asOf) {

    Instant staleThreshold = asOf.minus(48, ChronoUnit.HOURS);

    return parcels.stream()
        .filter(p -> p.status() == ParcelStatus.IN_TRANSIT)
        .filter(p -> p.lastUpdatedAt().isBefore(staleThreshold))
        .sorted(Comparator.comparing(Parcel::lastUpdatedAt)) // most stale first
        .collect(Collectors.groupingBy(
            Parcel::carrierId,
            LinkedHashMap::new,  // preserve insertion order (most-stale first per group)
            Collectors.toList()
        ));
}

Trace:

asOf = Jan 15 10:00
staleThreshold = Jan 13 10:00

P1: IN_TRANSIT, carrier=GHN, lastUpdate=Jan 12 08:00 → stale (50h ago) ✓
P2: IN_TRANSIT, carrier=GHN, lastUpdate=Jan 13 15:00 → not stale (19h ago) ✗
P3: DELIVERED,  carrier=VTP, lastUpdate=Jan 12 06:00 → wrong status ✗
P4: IN_TRANSIT, carrier=VTP, lastUpdate=Jan 12 06:00 → stale (52h ago) ✓
P5: IN_TRANSIT, carrier=GHN, lastUpdate=Jan 10 08:00 → stale (72h ago) ✓

Sorted by lastUpdatedAt ascending (most stale first):
  P5 (72h), P4 (52h), P1 (50h)

Grouped:
  GHN → [P5, P1]  (P5 more stale within GHN group)
  VTP → [P4]

Tests:

@Test
void groupsAtRiskParcelsByCarrier() {
    Instant now = Instant.parse("2024-01-15T10:00:00Z");
    List<Parcel> parcels = List.of(
        new Parcel("P1", "GHN", IN_TRANSIT, now.minus(50, HOURS)),
        new Parcel("P2", "GHN", IN_TRANSIT, now.minus(10, HOURS)), // not stale
        new Parcel("P3", "VTP", IN_TRANSIT, now.minus(72, HOURS)),
        new Parcel("P4", "GHN", DELIVERED,  now.minus(60, HOURS))  // wrong status
    );

    var result = service.findAtRiskParcels(parcels, now);

    assertThat(result).containsOnlyKeys("GHN", "VTP");
    assertThat(result.get("GHN")).hasSize(1); // P2 excluded (not stale), P4 excluded (DELIVERED)
    assertThat(result.get("GHN").get(0).id()).isEqualTo("P1");
    assertThat(result.get("VTP").get(0).id()).isEqualTo("P3");
}

@Test
void sortsWithinCarrierMostStaleFirst() {
    Instant now = Instant.now();
    List<Parcel> parcels = List.of(
        new Parcel("P1", "GHN", IN_TRANSIT, now.minus(50, HOURS)),
        new Parcel("P2", "GHN", IN_TRANSIT, now.minus(72, HOURS)) // more stale
    );

    var result = service.findAtRiskParcels(parcels, now);

    assertThat(result.get("GHN").get(0).id()).isEqualTo("P2"); // most stale first
}

@Test
void returnsEmptyWhenNoAtRiskParcels() {
    Instant now = Instant.now();
    List<Parcel> parcels = List.of(
        new Parcel("P1", "GHN", IN_TRANSIT, now.minus(10, HOURS)) // not stale
    );

    var result = service.findAtRiskParcels(parcels, now);

    assertThat(result).isEmpty();
}

👨‍💻 Wei: LinkedHashMap::new to preserve sort order across

grouping is a subtle but important detail. Most candidates use

default HashMap and lose the ordering. Well done.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

RoundCategoryScoreNotes
HRStack honesty + incident response10/10Honest about RocketMQ gap, mitigate-first incident philosophy
JavaRocketMQ transaction messages10/10Half-message protocol, checkLocalTransaction for crash recovery
JavaCanal CDC10/10Decoupled cache invalidation, binlog reliability advantage
DesignParcel routing system10/10Routing score, event sourcing for tracking, multi-leg journey
CodingAt-risk parcel detection10/10LinkedHashMap order preservation, 3 comprehensive tests

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 Lazada Interview Tips

  1. Know the Alibaba stack. RocketMQ, Dubbo, Canal, Nacos, AHAS — at least conceptually
  2. RocketMQ transaction messages — they often ask this specifically
  3. Canal CDC — Alibaba's answer to Debezium. Know why it's better than dual-write
  4. Logistics domain. Learn: parcel lifecycle, carrier APIs, customs, failed delivery flows
  5. Multi-country complexity. Different carriers, currencies, and regulations per country
  6. LinkedHashMap in Collectors.groupingBy — preserves insertion order
  7. Honest about gaps. "I know Kafka not RocketMQ — here's why it transfers" > pretending expertise
  8. Lazada salary. 45-70M gross. Alibaba benefits (AliCloud credits, Lazada shopping credits)

🎭 Mock Interview #31 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: Sky Mavis (Axie Infinity, HCMC, Global Gaming/Web3)

Context: Sky Mavis built Axie Infinity — the world's largest

blockchain game with 2.7M daily active players at peak and $1.3B+ revenue.

Based in HCMC. Engineering team: 200+ globally. Backed by a16z, Binance.

After the $625M Ronin bridge hack (2022), they rebuilt with the strongest

security culture in SEA tech.

Role: Marketplace Backend team — the Axie NFT marketplace where

players buy/sell game assets. Millions of listings, real-time price

discovery, RON/ETH payment settlement.

Why this is unique: Sky Mavis pays in USD (salaries often $4K-$8K/month

for seniors). The tech challenges are conventional backend but at unusual

stakes — every marketplace bug could mean real money lost. Security

mindset is non-negotiable after the Ronin hack.

Note: This interview is almost entirely conventional backend Java.

You don't need to know blockchain deeply — the blockchain layer is handled

separately. Your job is building fast, reliable APIs on top of it.

📞 ROUND 1 — HR + CULTURE (25 min, English)

👩 Sarah (People Ops): Hi Khanh! Sky Mavis is different from

typical tech companies. We had a $625M hack in 2022 — the largest

in crypto history. It changed how we build everything. Security is

now a first-class engineering concern, not an afterthought.

Tell me about yourself and how you think about security.

👨 Khanh: Hi Sarah! I'm Khanh, Senior Java Engineer. On security —

at TMA Solutions I built healthcare software under HIPAA compliance,

which means security can't be bolted on after — it's in the design.

At Axon Active, banking systems required PCI-DSS awareness. Security

mindset to me means:

Defense in depth — never trust one layer. Input validation at

the API layer, authorization checks at the service layer, row-level

security at the database layer.

Least privilege — every service and user account has only the

permissions it needs. If a service only reads data, its DB user

has only SELECT.

Audit everything — in systems where money moves, I log every

write operation with who, what, when, and from where.

The Ronin hack was a multi-sig key compromise — not a Java bug.

But it reinforced that security failures happen at system boundaries

and key management, not just at the code level.

👩 Sarah: Good perspective. Sky Mavis moves like a startup —

small teams, high ownership. Tell me about a time you worked with

ambiguous requirements.

👨 Khanh: At Sony, I was asked to "improve the performance of

the data pipeline." No specific metric, no timeline.

First, I clarified: "What does success look like?" The answer was

vague. So I spent three days measuring the current state — throughput,

latency distribution, error rate — and wrote a one-page summary:

"Current: 800ms P99. I propose we target 500ms P99 within 4 weeks."

That gave everyone a concrete anchor. Once we agreed on the target,

the ambiguity was gone. I find that ambiguous requirements usually

mean the stakeholders haven't decided what they want yet. My job

is to help them decide by proposing something specific.

👩 Sarah: "Help stakeholders decide by proposing something specific"

— that's senior thinking. Technical rounds next.

💻 ROUND 2 — TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE (75 min, English)

Setting: Staff Engineer + Security-focused Senior Engineer.

👨‍💻 Alex (Staff Eng): Hi Khanh. The Axie marketplace has

specific challenges: preventing wash trading, handling concurrent

purchases of the same listing, and ensuring every price is always accurate.

PART A — Java Security & API Design (25 min)

👨‍💻 Alex: **In a marketplace where every listing has real

monetary value, how do you prevent the most common API security

vulnerabilities? Walk me through your approach.**

👨 Khanh: I'll cover the top threats relevant to a game marketplace:

1. IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference) — most common marketplace bug:

// ❌ VULNERABLE: any authenticated user can view/modify any listing
@GetMapping("/listings/{id}")
public Listing getListing(@PathVariable Long id) {
    return listingRepo.findById(id).orElseThrow(); // no ownership check!
}

// ✅ SECURE: always verify ownership for write operations
@DeleteMapping("/listings/{id}")
public void cancelListing(@PathVariable Long id,
                           @AuthenticationPrincipal UserPrincipal user) {
    Listing listing = listingRepo.findById(id)
        .orElseThrow(() -> new NotFoundException("Listing not found"));

    // Ownership check — ALWAYS
    if (!listing.getSellerId().equals(user.getId())) {
        throw new ForbiddenException("You can only cancel your own listings");
        // Don't say "listing belongs to someone else" — information leakage
    }
    listing.cancel();
    listingRepo.save(listing);
}

2. Rate limiting to prevent listing scraping and price manipulation:

@PostMapping("/listings")
@RateLimiter(name = "createListing") // max 10 listings/minute per user
public ResponseEntity<ListingResponse> createListing(
        @Valid @RequestBody CreateListingRequest req,
        @AuthenticationPrincipal UserPrincipal user) {
    // ...
}

// Resilience4j rate limiter config:
// resilience4j.ratelimiter.instances.createListing.limitForPeriod=10
// resilience4j.ratelimiter.instances.createListing.limitRefreshPeriod=1m

3. Input validation — especially for prices:

public record CreateListingRequest(
    @NotNull String axieId,

    @NotNull
    @DecimalMin(value = "0.001", message = "Minimum price is 0.001 RON")
    @DecimalMax(value = "1000000", message = "Maximum price is 1,000,000 RON")
    @Digits(integer = 9, fraction = 3)
    BigDecimal priceInRon,

    @NotNull
    @Future(message = "Expiry must be in the future")
    @Max(value = 30, message = "Listing can expire maximum 30 days from now")
    LocalDate expiresAt
) {}

4. Preventing double-spend (someone buys, listing already sold):

@Transactional(isolation = Isolation.SERIALIZABLE)
public PurchaseResult purchaseListing(Long listingId, String buyerId) {
    // Pessimistic lock — only one buyer can proceed at a time
    Listing listing = listingRepo.findByIdForUpdate(listingId)
        .orElseThrow(() -> new NotFoundException("Listing not found"));

    if (listing.getStatus() != ACTIVE) {
        throw new ListingUnavailableException("This listing is no longer available");
    }
    if (listing.getSellerId().equals(buyerId)) {
        throw new InvalidOperationException("Cannot buy your own listing");
    }

    listing.sell(buyerId);
    listingRepo.save(listing);
    // ... payment processing ...
    return PurchaseResult.success(listing);
}

5. Audit log for every financial operation:

@Aspect
@Component
public class FinancialAuditAspect {

    @AfterReturning(
        pointcut = "@annotation(FinancialOperation)",
        returning = "result"
    )
    public void auditFinancialOperation(JoinPoint jp, Object result) {
        FinancialAuditLog log = FinancialAuditLog.builder()
            .operationType(jp.getSignature().getName())
            .userId(SecurityContextHolder.getContext()
                .getAuthentication().getName())
            .requestIp(getCurrentRequestIp())
            .parameters(Arrays.toString(jp.getArgs()))
            .result(result.toString())
            .timestamp(Instant.now())
            .build();
        auditLogRepo.save(log);
    }
}

👩‍💻 Minh (Security Sr Eng): **How do you detect wash trading —

where the same person creates fake buy/sell transactions to inflate

prices or volume metrics?**

👨 Khanh: Wash trading detection requires pattern analysis at

multiple levels:

Rule-based detection (fast, catches obvious cases):

@Service
public class WashTradeDetector {

    public void analyzeTransaction(Transaction txn) {
        String buyerId = txn.getBuyerId();
        String sellerId = txn.getSellerId();
        String axieId = txn.getAxieId();

        // Rule 1: Same wallet buying and selling (via alt accounts)
        // Check if buyer and seller share a payment source
        boolean sharedFunding = fundingAnalyzer.shareCommonSource(buyerId, sellerId);

        // Rule 2: Round-trip trading — same asset changing hands repeatedly
        int recentTrades = txnRepo.countTradesBetweenUsersInPeriod(
            buyerId, sellerId, Duration.ofDays(7));

        // Rule 3: Price anomaly — sold for 10x the floor price to self
        BigDecimal floorPrice = marketDataService.getFloorPrice(txn.getAxieClass());
        boolean priceAnomaly = txn.getPrice()
            .compareTo(floorPrice.multiply(BigDecimal.TEN)) > 0;

        // Rule 4: Velocity — same wallet doing many trades in short time
        int tradesLastHour = txnRepo.countByUserIdInLastHour(buyerId);

        if (sharedFunding || recentTrades > 5 || (priceAnomaly && recentTrades > 2)
                || tradesLastHour > 20) {
            flagForReview(txn, "Potential wash trade");
            // Don't auto-cancel — flag for human review to avoid false positives
        }
    }
}

Graph analysis (catches sophisticated wash trading):

Track wallet-to-wallet transaction graphs. A closed cycle of:

A → B → C → A with the same assets = almost certainly wash trading.

Store transaction relationships in a graph DB or adjacency list

and run cycle detection periodically.

Why not auto-cancel? False positives hurt legitimate users.

Flag → human review → confirm → action. The cost of a false positive

(frustrated legitimate trader) is higher than the cost of a delayed

true positive.

PART B — SYSTEM DESIGN: NFT Marketplace (35 min)

👨‍💻 Alex: *"Design the Axie NFT Marketplace. Players list

their Axies for sale. Other players browse, filter (by class, genes,

stats), and buy. Requirement: listing price is always accurate —

a buyer should never see a price and find it's already sold.*

Scale: 500K active listings, 50K DAU, 10K transactions/day."

👨 Khanh:

Core entities:

Axie (NFT): id, class, genes, stats, ownerId
Listing: id, axieId, sellerId, priceRon, status (ACTIVE/SOLD/CANCELLED), expiresAt
Transaction: id, listingId, buyerId, sellerId, priceRon, settledAt

Architecture:

Frontend (React)
    │
    ├── Browse/Search → Elasticsearch (fast, filtered search)
    │
    ├── View Listing  → API → PostgreSQL (authoritative price + status)
    │
    └── Purchase      → API → PostgreSQL (SERIALIZABLE tx)
                               │
                               └── Blockchain Settlement (async)

Why both Elasticsearch and PostgreSQL?

  • Elasticsearch: fast full-text + attribute search (class=AQUATIC, genes contains "Harlequin")
  • PostgreSQL: source of truth for listing status and price. Before showing "Buy Now", always

read from PostgreSQL — not Elasticsearch which may be seconds stale.

Listing Data Flow:

// Create listing
@Transactional
public Listing createListing(CreateListingRequest req, String sellerId) {
    // 1. Verify seller owns the Axie
    Axie axie = axieRepo.findById(req.getAxieId()).orElseThrow();
    if (!axie.getOwnerId().equals(sellerId)) throw new ForbiddenException();

    // 2. Verify Axie not already listed
    if (listingRepo.existsByAxieIdAndStatus(req.getAxieId(), ACTIVE)) {
        throw new ConflictException("Axie already has an active listing");
    }

    // 3. Create listing
    Listing listing = Listing.create(req, sellerId);
    listingRepo.save(listing);

    // 4. Index in Elasticsearch async (slight delay OK for search)
    eventPublisher.publishEvent(new ListingCreatedEvent(listing));

    return listing;
}

// Purchase listing — the critical path
@Transactional(isolation = Isolation.SERIALIZABLE)
@FinancialOperation  // triggers audit log
public PurchaseResult purchase(Long listingId, String buyerId) {
    // Row-level lock on the listing
    Listing listing = listingRepo.findByIdForUpdate(listingId)
        .orElseThrow(() -> new NotFoundException("Listing not found"));

    // Status check AFTER acquiring lock
    if (listing.getStatus() != ACTIVE) {
        throw new ListingUnavailableException("Already sold or cancelled");
    }
    if (listing.isExpired()) {
        listing.expire();
        listingRepo.save(listing);
        throw new ListingUnavailableException("Listing has expired");
    }
    if (listing.getSellerId().equals(buyerId)) {
        throw new InvalidOperationException("Cannot buy your own listing");
    }

    // Deduct buyer's balance (in RON — game currency)
    walletService.deductBalance(buyerId, listing.getPriceRon());

    // Mark listing sold
    listing.sell(buyerId);
    listingRepo.save(listing);

    // Record transaction
    Transaction txn = txnRepo.save(Transaction.from(listing, buyerId));

    // Transfer Axie ownership async (blockchain operation — slow)
    kafkaTemplate.send("blockchain.transfers", new OwnershipTransfer(
        listing.getAxieId(), listing.getSellerId(), buyerId, txn.getId()
    ));

    return PurchaseResult.success(txn);
}

Price Accuracy Guarantee:

The key insight: **always re-read price and status from PostgreSQL

right before displaying "Buy Now" button.** Never trust cached price.

@GetMapping("/listings/{id}")
public ListingDetailResponse getListing(@PathVariable Long id) {
    // This goes to PostgreSQL — not Redis/ES cache
    // Slight latency (5-10ms extra) but price is always accurate
    Listing listing = listingRepo.findById(id)
        .orElseThrow(() -> new NotFoundException("Listing not found"));

    return ListingDetailResponse.from(listing);
    // If status=SOLD, response shows "Sold" not a price
}

For search results (500K listings), showing slightly stale price

(seconds delay from ES index) is acceptable. But the listing detail

page and checkout must always hit the primary DB.

Blockchain Settlement:

@KafkaListener(topics = "blockchain.transfers")
public void processOwnershipTransfer(OwnershipTransfer transfer) {
    try {
        // Call Ronin blockchain to transfer NFT ownership
        String txHash = roninClient.transferNFT(
            transfer.getAxieId(),
            transfer.getFromWallet(),
            transfer.getToWallet()
        );

        // Confirm on-chain settlement
        transferRepo.markSettled(transfer.getId(), txHash);
        walletService.creditSeller(transfer.getSellerId(),
            transfer.getPrice().multiply(new BigDecimal("0.975"))); // 2.5% marketplace fee

    } catch (BlockchainException e) {
        // Blockchain failed — rollback: refund buyer, re-activate listing
        handleBlockchainFailure(transfer, e);
    }
}

private void handleBlockchainFailure(OwnershipTransfer transfer, Exception e) {
    log.error("Blockchain transfer failed for txn {}", transfer.getTransactionId(), e);
    // Compensating transaction
    walletService.refundBuyer(transfer.getBuyerId(), transfer.getPrice());
    listingRepo.reactivate(transfer.getListingId()); // make available again
    notificationService.notifyBuyer(transfer.getBuyerId(),
        "Purchase failed — funds refunded. Please try again.");
}

👨‍💻 Alex: The pattern of: PostgreSQL for detail/checkout,

ES for search only — and re-reading from PostgreSQL before every

"Buy Now" — is exactly our architecture. Smart.

How do you handle floor price calculation efficiently?

👨 Khanh: Pre-compute and cache:

// Run every 5 minutes — update floor price per Axie class
@Scheduled(fixedDelay = 300_000)
public void updateFloorPrices() {
    for (AxieClass cls : AxieClass.values()) {
        BigDecimal floor = listingRepo.findMinPriceByClass(cls);
        redisTemplate.opsForValue().set(
            "floor:" + cls.name(),
            floor.toPlainString(),
            Duration.ofMinutes(10)
        );
    }
}
// Floor price read from Redis — O(1), never hits DB on search

PART C — CODING (15 min)

👨‍💻 Alex: *"Implement a method that returns top-N most expensive

active listings per Axie class. Input: list of listings, N per class."*

👨 Khanh:

public Map<AxieClass, List<Listing>> topNByClass(
        List<Listing> listings, int n) {

    return listings.stream()
        .filter(l -> l.getStatus() == ListingStatus.ACTIVE)
        .collect(Collectors.groupingBy(
            Listing::getAxieClass,
            Collectors.collectingAndThen(
                Collectors.toList(),
                list -> list.stream()
                    .sorted(Comparator.comparing(Listing::getPriceRon).reversed())
                    .limit(n)
                    .collect(Collectors.toList())
            )
        ));
}

// Tests
@Test
void returnsTopNPerClass() {
    List<Listing> listings = List.of(
        listing("L1", AQUATIC, bd(100), ACTIVE),
        listing("L2", AQUATIC, bd(300), ACTIVE),
        listing("L3", AQUATIC, bd(200), ACTIVE),
        listing("L4", BEAST,   bd(150), ACTIVE),
        listing("L5", AQUATIC, bd(400), SOLD)    // excluded — not ACTIVE
    );

    var result = service.topNByClass(listings, 2);

    assertThat(result.get(AQUATIC)).hasSize(2);
    assertThat(result.get(AQUATIC).get(0).getId()).isEqualTo("L2"); // 300 first
    assertThat(result.get(AQUATIC).get(1).getId()).isEqualTo("L3"); // 200 second
    assertThat(result.get(BEAST)).hasSize(1);
}

@Test
void handlesFewerThanNListings() {
    var listings = List.of(listing("L1", AQUATIC, bd(100), ACTIVE));
    var result = service.topNByClass(listings, 5);
    assertThat(result.get(AQUATIC)).hasSize(1); // only 1 exists, not 5
}

📊 FULL SCORECARD

RoundCategoryScoreNotes
