JavaAPI security (IDOR, rate limiting)10/10Ownership check, pessimistic lock for purchase
JavaWash trading detection10/10Multi-rule + graph cycle detection, no auto-cancel
DesignNFT Marketplace10/10PostgreSQL for checkout, ES for search, blockchain async
CodingTop-N listings per class10/10collectingAndThen pattern, 2 tests

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 Sky Mavis Interview Tips

  1. Security is #1 after the hack. Every answer should mention defense-in-depth
  2. IDOR — the most common marketplace vulnerability. Always verify ownership
  3. Pessimistic locking for NFT purchase — only one buyer can win a listing
  4. PostgreSQL for price accuracy — never trust cache for financial data
  5. Blockchain is async — don't block HTTP response waiting for chain confirmation
  6. Compensating transactions — if blockchain fails, rollback: refund buyer, re-list
  7. Sky Mavis pays in USD — $4K-$8K/month for senior engineers
  8. English-first — international team, Bangkok/Singapore collaborators

🎭 Mock Interview #32 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: Binance (World's Largest Crypto Exchange, Remote/Global)

Context: Binance processes $76B+ in daily trading volume — more than

the next 10 crypto exchanges combined. 120M+ registered users. Engineering

team: 5000+ globally, fully remote. They hire heavily from Vietnam (many

Vietnamese senior engineers work for Binance remotely).

Role: Spot Trading Engine team — the system that matches buy and sell

orders in real-time. This is arguably the highest-stakes matching engine

in the world — microseconds of latency matter, and a single bug can

cause multi-million dollar losses.

Tech stack: Java 17, Spring Boot, Disruptor (LMAX), Redis, MySQL, Kafka,

Apache Flink (real-time risk), custom low-latency messaging.

Compensation: USD-denominated, highly competitive. Senior engineers:

$6K-$15K/month + BNB token bonuses. Fully remote — work from HCMC.

Bar: This is the hardest interview in crypto. Expect deep Java

concurrency, ultra-low-latency design, and financial system precision.

Higher bar than most FAANG roles for backend.

📞 ROUND 1 — RECRUITER SCREENING (20 min, English)

👩 Jessica (Tech Recruiter): Hi Khanh! Binance is remote-first

and moves extremely fast. Two questions before we discuss the role:

One — are you comfortable handling systems where a bug could cost

the company $10M+? What does that responsibility mean to you?

👨 Khanh: I've worked on banking systems at Axon Active and

payment systems at Sony — where bugs cause real financial losses.

The responsibility means three things to me:

First, test coverage is not optional. I don't ship financial

logic without unit tests for every edge case — including the ones

that seem impossible. The "impossible" cases are exactly what attackers probe.

Second, production changes follow a strict protocol. Deploy to staging,

shadow test against production traffic, canary to 1% with intensive

monitoring, then promote. Every step is a checkpoint.

Third, humility. I assume my code has bugs. I write it to fail

safely — circuit breakers, idempotency, compensating transactions.

A system that fails gracefully is safer than one that seems perfect.

Two — yes, that weight makes me more careful, not less confident.

The stakes clarify priorities. Everything else is secondary to

correctness and reliability.

👩 Jessica: Tell me about your highest-throughput system.

👨 Khanh: At Sony, I own a user profile service at 3K RPS.

The most challenging optimization I did: reducing P99 from 800ms

to 120ms through systematic profiling — parallelizing independent

queries, fixing N+1 from Jackson serializing Hibernate proxies,

and GC tuning.

I know 3K RPS is modest compared to Binance's matching engine.

But the methodology — profiling before optimizing, measuring

each change in isolation, understanding the JVM deeply — is what

scales. At Binance's scale, I'd be learning new performance

dimensions, but applying the same systematic approach.

👩 Jessica: Good self-awareness. Five technical rounds. This

process is intensive — typically 3-4 weeks. Still in?

👨 Khanh: Absolutely. The rigor reflects the stakes.

💻 ROUND 2 — JAVA CONCURRENCY & LOW-LATENCY (60 min, English)

Setting: Principal Engineer, Trading Engine team.

👨‍💻 David (Principal Eng): Hi Khanh. Binance's matching engine

processes 1.4M orders/second at peak. Every nanosecond wasted

in the JVM is money lost. I'll test your JVM internals depth.

Java Concurrency Deep-Dive (30 min)

👨‍💻 David: **Explain the LMAX Disruptor. How is it fundamentally

different from a LinkedBlockingQueue? Why does Binance use it?**

👨 Khanh: LMAX Disruptor is a lock-free, cache-friendly ring

buffer for inter-thread communication. Let me contrast with LinkedBlockingQueue:

LinkedBlockingQueue problems:

1. Linked nodes → heap allocations → GC pressure
   Every message = new LinkedBlockingQueue.Node object → eventual GC pause

2. Lock contention:
   - Producer acquires putLock
   - Consumer acquires takeLock
   - Under high throughput, threads spin waiting for locks → CPU wasted

3. Cache miss:
   Linked nodes are scattered in heap memory
   CPU cache line loads node → next node is elsewhere → cache miss
   At 1M+ messages/second: millions of cache misses/second → latency spikes

LMAX Disruptor design:

// Ring buffer — fixed array, pre-allocated at startup
// No allocations during operation = no GC during trading!

int BUFFER_SIZE = 1024; // must be power of 2 (for bitwise AND trick)
long[] ringBuffer = new long[BUFFER_SIZE]; // pre-allocated

// Producer: claim next sequence number (atomic, no lock)
// Consumer: follow producer's sequence, read when available

// Key insight: sequences instead of pointers
// Producer writes to sequence % BUFFER_SIZE
// Consumer reads when its sequence < producer's sequence
// NO LOCKS — uses memory barriers (volatile/Unsafe) instead
// Disruptor setup for order processing
Disruptor<OrderEvent> disruptor = new Disruptor<>(
    OrderEvent::new,       // event factory — pre-allocate all events
    1024,                  // ring buffer size (power of 2)
    DaemonThreadFactory.INSTANCE,
    ProducerType.SINGLE,   // single producer = no CAS needed, fastest
    new YieldingWaitStrategy() // consumer yields CPU instead of sleeping
);

disruptor.handleEventsWith(new OrderMatchingHandler());
RingBuffer<OrderEvent> ringBuffer = disruptor.start();

// Publish order (lock-free)
long sequence = ringBuffer.next(); // atomic sequence claim
try {
    OrderEvent event = ringBuffer.get(sequence);
    event.setOrder(incomingOrder); // populate pre-allocated object
} finally {
    ringBuffer.publish(sequence); // memory barrier → consumer sees it
}

Why Disruptor is 25x faster than LinkedBlockingQueue:

FactorLinkedBlockingQueueDisruptor
AllocationEvery messagePre-allocated ring buffer
GCYes — frequent young GCZero during operation
LockingReentrantLockLock-free (CAS + memory barriers)
Cache localityScattered (linked nodes)Sequential array — CPU prefetch works!
Wait strategySleep (wake-up latency)Spin/yield (microsecond response)

At Binance: The matching engine uses Disruptor internally.

Orders → Disruptor ring buffer → matching handler → fills/rejects.

Zero allocations on the hot path = zero GC pauses during matching.

A GC pause during a volatile market move could cost millions.

👨‍💻 David: **What is false sharing? How do you prevent it in

a high-performance Java system?**

👨 Khanh: False sharing is a CPU cache coherency problem that

destroys performance in multi-threaded code.

The problem:

CPU cache lines are 64 bytes.
If two variables fit in the same cache line, and two threads
on different CPU cores access them:

Thread 1 (Core 1) writes to variableA
→ Invalidates the ENTIRE cache line on Core 2
→ Core 2 must re-fetch the cache line from RAM
→ Even though Core 2 only uses variableB!

This happens thousands of times per second → massive latency

Example of false sharing in a trading system:

// ❌ BAD — sequence and producerIndex share a cache line
class SequenceTracker {
    volatile long consumerSequence = 0;  // written by consumer thread
    volatile long producerSequence = 0;  // written by producer thread
    // These two longs are 16 bytes — share the same 64-byte cache line!
    // → False sharing between producer and consumer threads
}

// ✅ FIX — padding to separate them into different cache lines
class PaddedSequence {
    // 7 longs = 56 bytes padding
    long p1, p2, p3, p4, p5, p6, p7;
    volatile long value = 0;  // now in its own 64-byte cache line
    long p8, p9, p10, p11, p12, p13, p14;
}

Java 8+ solution — @Contended annotation:

// Tell JVM to add padding around this field automatically
@sun.misc.Contended
class SequenceTracker {
    volatile long consumerSequence = 0;
    volatile long producerSequence = 0;
    // JVM adds 128 bytes padding around each @Contended field
}
// Run with: -XX:-RestrictContended

Where Disruptor uses this:

Disruptor's Sequence class is explicitly padded — the sequence

counter for each consumer gets its own 64-byte cache line. This

is one reason Disruptor is so fast: zero false sharing between

producer and each consumer.

How to detect false sharing:

  • Linux perf c2c tool shows cache-to-cache transfers
  • Java Mission Control → memory access patterns
  • Symptom: adding cores makes performance WORSE (more false sharing)

Low-Latency System Design (30 min)

👨‍💻 David: **What are the top 5 JVM optimizations you'd apply

to a trading system where P99 latency must be under 1 millisecond?**

👨 Khanh: In order of impact:

1. Eliminate GC pauses — use off-heap memory:

// Store price data in off-heap ByteBuffer (not managed by GC)
ByteBuffer orderBook = ByteBuffer.allocateDirect(1024 * 1024 * 100); // 100MB
// or use Chronicle Map / Agrona for structured off-heap storage

// JVM flags to minimize GC impact:
// -Xms8g -Xmx8g                  (fixed heap — no resize)
// -XX:+UseZGC                    (sub-1ms GC pauses, Java 15+)
// -XX:+ZGenerational             (generational ZGC, Java 21)
// -XX:AllocatePrefetchLines=4    (prefetch allocations)

2. Pre-allocate everything — zero allocation on hot path:

// Object pool for order objects
class OrderPool {
    private final Queue<Order> pool = new ArrayDeque<>(10_000);

    OrderPool() {
        for (int i = 0; i < 10_000; i++) pool.offer(new Order());
    }

    Order borrow() { return pool.poll(); }
    void returnToPool(Order o) { o.reset(); pool.offer(o); }
}
// Reuse Order objects → no allocation → no GC

3. JIT compilation warm-up:

// Cold JVM: JIT hasn't compiled hot methods yet → slow first 30 seconds
// Solution: warm up before accepting live traffic

@PostConstruct
public void warmUp() {
    log.info("Warming up matching engine...");
    for (int i = 0; i < 100_000; i++) {
        matchingEngine.processOrder(syntheticOrder(i));
    }
    log.info("Warm-up complete. JIT should have compiled hot paths.");
    // Now ready to accept live orders
}

4. CPU affinity — pin threads to cores:

# Prevent OS from moving threads between cores (cache eviction)
# Use taskset on Linux to pin JVM to specific CPU cores
taskset -c 2-7 java -jar trading-engine.jar

# Or use Affinity library in Java:
AffinityLock lock = AffinityLock.acquireCore();
// This thread is now pinned to one CPU core

5. Use Unsafe for direct memory access in truly critical paths:

// sun.misc.Unsafe — bypasses bounds checking, null checks
// 20-30% faster than normal array access
// ONLY use in well-tested, isolated hot-path code
private static final Unsafe UNSAFE = getUnsafe();
private static final long BASE_OFFSET = UNSAFE.arrayBaseOffset(long[].class);
private static final long SCALE = UNSAFE.arrayIndexScale(long[].class);

long readPrice(long[] buffer, int index) {
    return UNSAFE.getLong(buffer, BASE_OFFSET + index * SCALE);
}

JVM flags for sub-millisecond latency:

java \
  -server \
  -Xms8g -Xmx8g \
  -XX:+UseZGC \
  -XX:+ZGenerational \
  -XX:+AlwaysPreTouch \
  -XX:+DisableExplicitGC \
  -XX:+UseNUMA \
  -XX:+PerfDisableSharedMem \
  -XX:-OmitStackTraceInFastThrow \
  -Djava.lang.invoke.MethodHandle.CUSTOMIZE_THRESHOLD=0 \
  -jar trading-engine.jar

💻 ROUND 3 — SYSTEM DESIGN (60 min, English)

Setting: Staff Engineer. Order matching system design.

👨‍💻 Lin (Staff Eng): *"Design Binance's spot order matching

engine for BTC/USDT trading. Support: limit orders, market orders.

Match buy and sell orders by price-time priority. Publish real-time

order book updates and trade fills. Scale: 1.4M orders/second."*

👨 Khanh:

Core Data Structure — Order Book:

Price-Time Priority (best price first, earliest time wins ties):

BUY side (bids) — highest price first:    SELL side (asks) — lowest price first:
  $43,500 — 2.5 BTC (placed 10:00:01)      $43,600 — 1.0 BTC (placed 10:00:02)
  $43,490 — 1.0 BTC (placed 09:59:55)      $43,650 — 5.0 BTC (placed 09:58:00)
  $43,480 — 3.0 BTC (placed 09:59:58)      $43,700 — 2.0 BTC (placed 10:00:00)

Spread = $43,600 - $43,500 = $100

A market BUY order: fill at best ask ($43,600)
A limit BUY at $43,500: add to bid side, wait for matching ask

Order Book Implementation:

public class OrderBook {
    private final String symbol; // "BTCUSDT"

    // TreeMap maintains price order automatically
    // Each price level has a queue of orders (time priority within level)
    private final TreeMap<BigDecimal, Deque<Order>> bids =
        new TreeMap<>(Comparator.reverseOrder()); // highest first

    private final TreeMap<BigDecimal, Deque<Order>> asks =
        new TreeMap<>(); // lowest first

    public List<Trade> matchOrder(Order incoming) {
        List<Trade> trades = new ArrayList<>();

        if (incoming.getSide() == BUY) {
            matchBuyOrder(incoming, trades);
        } else {
            matchSellOrder(incoming, trades);
        }

        // If limit order not fully filled, add remaining to book
        if (incoming.getRemainingQty().compareTo(BigDecimal.ZERO) > 0
                && incoming.getType() == LIMIT) {
            addToBook(incoming);
        }

        return trades;
    }

    private void matchBuyOrder(Order buy, List<Trade> trades) {
        while (buy.getRemainingQty().compareTo(BigDecimal.ZERO) > 0
               && !asks.isEmpty()) {

            Map.Entry<BigDecimal, Deque<Order>> bestAsk = asks.firstEntry();

            // For limit orders: only match if ask price <= buy price
            if (buy.getType() == LIMIT
                    && bestAsk.getKey().compareTo(buy.getPrice()) > 0) {
                break; // no match possible at this price
            }

            // Match against best ask
            Deque<Order> askQueue = bestAsk.getValue();
            Order sell = askQueue.peekFirst();
            BigDecimal matchQty = buy.getRemainingQty().min(sell.getRemainingQty());
            BigDecimal matchPrice = sell.getPrice(); // price of resting order

            trades.add(new Trade(buy.getId(), sell.getId(),
                matchPrice, matchQty, Instant.now()));

            buy.fill(matchQty);
            sell.fill(matchQty);

            if (sell.getRemainingQty().compareTo(BigDecimal.ZERO) == 0) {
                askQueue.pollFirst(); // fully filled — remove from book
                if (askQueue.isEmpty()) asks.remove(bestAsk.getKey());
            }
        }
    }

    private void addToBook(Order order) {
        if (order.getSide() == BUY) {
            bids.computeIfAbsent(order.getPrice(), k -> new ArrayDeque<>())
                .addLast(order);
        } else {
            asks.computeIfAbsent(order.getPrice(), k -> new ArrayDeque<>())
                .addLast(order);
        }
    }
}

Architecture for 1.4M orders/second:

Clients (WebSocket / FIX API)
    │
    ▼
┌────────────────────────────┐
│    Order Gateway           │  Validates, deduplicates, assigns sequence#
│    (Disruptor in-process)  │
└────────────┬───────────────┘
             │ SINGLE THREAD — no concurrency in matching!
             ▼
┌────────────────────────────┐
│   Matching Engine          │  Pure in-memory OrderBook per symbol
│   (One thread per symbol)  │  BTC/USDT runs on Core 2
│                            │  ETH/USDT runs on Core 3
└────────────┬───────────────┘
             │
    ┌────────┴─────────┐
    │                  │
┌───▼──────┐    ┌──────▼──────┐
│ Trade    │    │  Order Book  │
│ Publiser │    │  Publisher   │
│ (Kafka)  │    │  (WebSocket) │
└──────────┘    └─────────────┘

Key design decisions:

1. Single thread per symbol — the matching engine processes orders

for BTC/USDT on one thread. No locks needed. The entire order book

is accessed by exactly one thread. This is the fundamental design

of all high-performance matching engines (NYSE, Binance, etc.).

Throughput = orders/second per symbol. Scale = more symbols on more cores.

2. Disruptor for inbound order queue:

Gateway publishes to Disruptor ring buffer.

Matching thread consumes. Lock-free, zero-allocation hot path.

3. Deterministic processing:

Orders processed in strict sequence number order.

Same sequence = same result, always. Critical for reproducibility.

4. Publishing fills and book updates:

After each match, fills published to Kafka (for account balance update,

notification). Order book delta published via WebSocket to all subscribers.

Order Book Update WebSocket:

// After each match, publish delta (not full book — too large)
public class OrderBookPublisher {
    private final SimpMessagingTemplate websocket;

    public void publishDelta(String symbol, BookDelta delta) {
        websocket.convertAndSend(
            "/topic/orderbook/" + symbol,
            delta // {bids: [[price, qty]], asks: [[price, qty]], sequence: 12345}
        );
    }
}

Clients reconstruct the full order book locally by applying deltas.

Much more efficient than sending the full order book (could be 100K levels).

👩‍💻 Lin: How do you handle a matching engine crash mid-match?

👨 Khanh: Event sourcing + sequence numbers:

Every order received is persisted to Kafka with a sequence number

BEFORE being processed by the matching engine. The matching engine

is a pure in-memory state machine driven by the Kafka log.

On crash/restart:

  1. Engine restarts from the last committed sequence number in its

local snapshot (taken every N orders)

  1. Replays Kafka events from that sequence to rebuild current order book state
  2. Resumes matching from current state

The Kafka log IS the source of truth. The in-memory order book is

a derived state that can always be rebuilt. This is the exchange

industry standard (LMAX Exchange uses this exact pattern).

PART D — CODING (25 min)

👩‍💻 Lin: *"Implement a simplified order book. Support:

addOrder(side, price, qty) and getTopOfBook() returning

best bid and best ask with their total quantities."*

👨 Khanh:

public class SimpleOrderBook {

    // Price → total quantity at that price level
    private final TreeMap<BigDecimal, BigDecimal> bids =
        new TreeMap<>(Comparator.reverseOrder()); // highest bid first

    private final TreeMap<BigDecimal, BigDecimal> asks =
        new TreeMap<>(); // lowest ask first

    public void addOrder(Side side, BigDecimal price, BigDecimal qty) {
        if (price.compareTo(BigDecimal.ZERO) <= 0)
            throw new IllegalArgumentException("Price must be positive");
        if (qty.compareTo(BigDecimal.ZERO) <= 0)
            throw new IllegalArgumentException("Qty must be positive");

        Map<BigDecimal, BigDecimal> book = (side == Side.BUY) ? bids : asks;
        book.merge(price, qty, BigDecimal::add); // add qty to existing level
    }

    public TopOfBook getTopOfBook() {
        PriceLevel bestBid = bids.isEmpty() ? null
            : new PriceLevel(bids.firstKey(), bids.firstEntry().getValue());

        PriceLevel bestAsk = asks.isEmpty() ? null
            : new PriceLevel(asks.firstKey(), asks.firstEntry().getValue());

        return new TopOfBook(bestBid, bestAsk);
    }

    public record PriceLevel(BigDecimal price, BigDecimal qty) {}
    public record TopOfBook(PriceLevel bid, PriceLevel ask) {}
}

// Tests
@Test
void bestBidIsHighestBuyPrice() {
    var book = new SimpleOrderBook();
    book.addOrder(BUY, bd("43500"), bd("1.0"));
    book.addOrder(BUY, bd("43490"), bd("2.0"));
    book.addOrder(BUY, bd("43510"), bd("0.5")); // highest

    var top = book.getTopOfBook();
    assertThat(top.bid().price()).isEqualByComparingTo("43510");
    assertThat(top.bid().qty()).isEqualByComparingTo("0.5");
}

@Test
void bestAskIsLowestSellPrice() {
    var book = new SimpleOrderBook();
    book.addOrder(SELL, bd("43600"), bd("1.0")); // lowest
    book.addOrder(SELL, bd("43650"), bd("2.0"));

    var top = book.getTopOfBook();
    assertThat(top.ask().price()).isEqualByComparingTo("43600");
}

@Test
void aggregatesQtyAtSamePrice() {
    var book = new SimpleOrderBook();
    book.addOrder(BUY, bd("43500"), bd("1.0"));
    book.addOrder(BUY, bd("43500"), bd("2.5")); // same price

    var top = book.getTopOfBook();
    assertThat(top.bid().qty()).isEqualByComparingTo("3.5"); // aggregated
}

@Test
void emptyBookReturnsNullLevels() {
    var top = new SimpleOrderBook().getTopOfBook();
    assertThat(top.bid()).isNull();
    assertThat(top.ask()).isNull();
}

👩‍💻 Lin: TreeMap.merge() with BigDecimal::add for aggregation

— elegant and concise. All 4 edge cases covered. This is production-quality.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

RoundCategoryScoreNotes
HRStakes + responsibility10/10"Fail safely, not perfectly" — mature engineering philosophy
JavaLMAX Disruptor10/10Ring buffer, cache locality, vs LinkedBlockingQueue — deep knowledge
JavaFalse sharing + @Contended10/10Cache line explanation, padding solution, perf c2c detection
JavaJVM low-latency optimization10/10ZGC, pre-allocation, warm-up, CPU affinity, off-heap
DesignMatching engine10/10Single thread per symbol, event sourcing crash recovery, delta WebSocket
CodingSimple order book10/10TreeMap for ordering, merge() for aggregation, 4 tests

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 Binance Interview Tips

  1. LMAX Disruptor — know it deeply. Binance explicitly uses it
  2. False sharing — common Binance question. Cache line = 64 bytes
  3. Single thread per symbol — fundamental matching engine design
  4. Event sourcing for crash recovery — Kafka log = source of truth
  5. ZGC not G1 — sub-1ms pauses required. GC pauses = trading losses
  6. Pre-allocation = zero allocation on hot path — no objects = no GC
  7. Binance pays USD — $6K-$15K/month for senior. Fully remote from VN
  8. 5 rounds — prepare for a 3-4 week process. Highest bar in crypto

🎭 Mock Interview #33 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: ByteDance / TikTok (HCMC, TikTok Shop Platform Engineering)

Context: ByteDance is the world's most valuable private tech company

($220B+ valuation). Products: TikTok (1B+ MAU), Douyin, Toutiao.

TikTok Shop (social commerce) is the fastest-growing e-commerce in SEA.

HCMC engineering hub: 800+ engineers, focused on TikTok Shop for SEA market.

Role: TikTok Shop Platform team — the backend services powering

TikTok Shop's seller platform, order management, and live commerce.

(Live commerce = sellers live-stream while viewers buy in real-time)

Tech stack: Java 17, Spring Boot, Kafka, Redis, MySQL, ByteDance's internal

frameworks (similar to open-source but proprietary: RPC framework similar

to Dubbo, config center similar to Apollo, monitoring similar to Prometheus).

Compensation: $6K-$12K/month (USD-denominated) + RSUs. Fully competitive

with Binance. One of top 3 employers in Vietnam by compensation.

Interview bar: ByteDance interviews are notoriously hard — similar to

Google. Strong emphasis on algorithms, system design at global scale, and

behavioral "ownership" questions.

📞 ROUND 1 — HR (25 min, English)

👩 Amy (HR, Singapore): Hi Khanh! ByteDance is data-driven and

fast-moving. Tell me about yourself and why ByteDance/TikTok Shop.

👨 Khanh: Hi Amy! I'm Khanh, Senior Java Engineer with 5 years

building high-throughput backend systems — payments at Sony,

banking at Axon Active, healthcare at TMA Solutions.

Why TikTok Shop specifically: live commerce is the most technically

demanding form of e-commerce. A seller is live-streaming to 50K

concurrent viewers. A viewer taps "Buy" — that's an order. 10,000

viewers tap "Buy" simultaneously for the same 100-unit flash sale.

That's: real-time inventory reservation, WebSocket/SSE for live

product state, and a payment flow — all under massive concurrency, all

in under 2 seconds. I haven't found a more interesting backend problem

in e-commerce.

My background in concurrent systems (payments) and event-driven

architecture (Kafka) maps directly here.

👩 Amy: ByteDance moves at extreme speed. Tell me about the

fastest you've ever shipped something significant.

👨 Khanh: At Sony, a critical production issue came in on a

Friday afternoon — our payment processing service was rejecting

valid transactions due to a regex bug in card number validation.

Revenue impact was immediate.

Within 45 minutes: reproduced locally, wrote a fix, wrote regression

tests for all card formats, deployed to staging, verified, then got

emergency approval to skip the normal 48-hour wait and deploy direct

to production with on-call monitoring.

The fix was live in 90 minutes total. Zero customer complaints

escalated after the fix.

What made it fast: I didn't guess — I used logs to pinpoint the

exact failing case in 10 minutes. Then I tested thoroughly before

deploying, even under pressure, because shipping a bad fix would

be worse than the 30-minute delay.

👩 Amy: "Tested thoroughly even under pressure" — that's the

discipline we need. Four technical rounds. Are you ready?

💻 ROUND 2 — ALGORITHMS & DATA STRUCTURES (50 min, English)

Setting: Senior Engineer. ByteDance-style algorithm round.

👨‍💻 Jason (Sr Eng): Hi Khanh. ByteDance takes algorithms

seriously — we use them in production. Two problems today.

Problem 1: Live Commerce Flash Sale — Top K Products (25 min)

👨‍💻 Jason: *"During a TikTok Shop live stream, products flash

on screen and users buy them. Given a stream of purchase events,

find the top K best-selling products at any point in real-time.*

void recordPurchase(String productId, int quantity) and

List<String> getTopK(int k).

Optimize for frequent recordPurchase calls and occasional getTopK."

👨 Khanh: Frequent writes, occasional reads → optimize writes.

Use a HashMap for O(1) sales tracking + a sorted structure for top-K.

But a full sort on every getTopK is O(N log N) — too slow.

Better: HashMap + MinHeap of size K:

public class LiveCommerceSalesTracker {

    private final Map<String, Integer> salesCount = new HashMap<>();

    // O(1) amortized for recordPurchase
    public void recordPurchase(String productId, int quantity) {
        salesCount.merge(productId, quantity, Integer::sum);
    }

    // O(N log K) — N products, heap of size K
    public List<String> getTopK(int k) {
        if (k <= 0) return Collections.emptyList();

        // Min-heap: keeps the K largest elements seen so far
        // Min at top — easy to evict the smallest of the "top K"
        PriorityQueue<Map.Entry<String, Integer>> minHeap =
            new PriorityQueue<>(k, Map.Entry.comparingByValue());

        for (Map.Entry<String, Integer> entry : salesCount.entrySet()) {
            minHeap.offer(entry);
            if (minHeap.size() > k) {
                minHeap.poll(); // remove smallest — not in top K
            }
        }

        // Extract in descending order (top seller first)
        List<String> result = new ArrayList<>(minHeap.size());
        while (!minHeap.isEmpty()) {
            result.add(0, minHeap.poll().getKey()); // insert at front
        }
        return result;
    }
}

Trace (k=2):

recordPurchase("A", 100), ("B", 200), ("C", 50), ("D", 150)
salesCount = {A:100, B:200, C:50, D:150}

getTopK(2):
  Process A(100): heap=[A:100], size=1
  Process B(200): heap=[A:100, B:200], size=2
  Process C(50):  heap full → add C(50) → size=3 → poll min(50)=C → heap=[A:100, B:200]
  Process D(150): add D(150) → size=3 → poll min(100)=A → heap=[B:200, D:150]

Extract in descending: [B, D] ✓

Complexity:

  • recordPurchase: O(1) — HashMap
  • getTopK(k): O(N log K) — N = number of distinct products

Follow-up: if getTopK is also frequent — use a TreeMap:

// TreeMap<sales, Set<productId>> — maintains sorted order at all times
// recordPurchase: O(log N) — update position in TreeMap
// getTopK: O(K) — just take first K from TreeMap
// Trade-off: slower writes, faster reads

Tests:

@Test
void basicTopK() {
    var tracker = new LiveCommerceSalesTracker();
    tracker.recordPurchase("A", 100);
    tracker.recordPurchase("B", 200);
    tracker.recordPurchase("C", 50);
    assertThat(tracker.getTopK(2)).containsExactly("B", "A");
}

@Test
void multipleRecordsForSameProduct() {
    var tracker = new LiveCommerceSalesTracker();
    tracker.recordPurchase("A", 50);
    tracker.recordPurchase("A", 75); // total 125
    tracker.recordPurchase("B", 100);
    assertThat(tracker.getTopK(1)).containsExactly("A");
}

@Test
void kLargerThanProducts() {
    var tracker = new LiveCommerceSalesTracker();
    tracker.recordPurchase("A", 100);
    assertThat(tracker.getTopK(5)).hasSize(1); // only 1 product exists
}

Problem 2: Live Stream Flash Sale — Inventory Reservation (25 min)

👨‍💻 Jason: *"In a live stream, 10,000 users simultaneously

try to buy the last 100 units of a product. Design and implement a

thread-safe inventory reservation system that:*

  • boolean reserve(String userId, int quantity) — returns true if reserved
  • void release(String userId) — cancels a reservation (user abandoned cart)
  • int getRemainingInventory() — current available inventory"

👨 Khanh: Classic concurrent reservation problem. Key insight:

use AtomicInteger for lock-free inventory — no synchronized needed.

public class InventoryReservationSystem {

    private final AtomicInteger availableInventory;
    private final ConcurrentHashMap<String, Integer> reservations =
        new ConcurrentHashMap<>();

    public InventoryReservationSystem(int totalInventory) {
        this.availableInventory = new AtomicInteger(totalInventory);
    }

    public boolean reserve(String userId, int quantity) {
        if (quantity <= 0) throw new IllegalArgumentException("Quantity must be positive");

        // Idempotency: if user already has a reservation, return true
        if (reservations.containsKey(userId)) return true;

        // Atomic decrement — only if enough inventory remains
        while (true) {
            int current = availableInventory.get();
            if (current < quantity) return false; // not enough inventory

            // CAS: set to current-quantity only if still == current
            if (availableInventory.compareAndSet(current, current - quantity)) {
                reservations.put(userId, quantity);
                return true;
            }
            // Another thread changed it — retry
        }
    }

    public void release(String userId) {
        Integer reserved = reservations.remove(userId);
        if (reserved != null) {
            availableInventory.addAndGet(reserved); // restore inventory
        }
    }

    public int getRemainingInventory() {
        return availableInventory.get();
    }
}

Why CAS loop instead of synchronized?

synchronized: all 10,000 threads queue at a monitor lock
→ at peak, 9,999 threads blocked → context switching → latency spikes

AtomicInteger.compareAndSet (CAS):
→ Threads don't block — they retry if CAS fails
→ CAS is a single CPU instruction (CMPXCHG on x86)
→ At 10K concurrent: most threads succeed or fail fast
→ No context switching overhead

Tests:

@Test
void reservesWhenInventoryAvailable() {
    var sys = new InventoryReservationSystem(100);
    assertThat(sys.reserve("user1", 10)).isTrue();
    assertThat(sys.getRemainingInventory()).isEqualTo(90);
}

@Test
void rejectsWhenInventoryInsufficient() {
    var sys = new InventoryReservationSystem(5);
    assertThat(sys.reserve("user1", 10)).isFalse(); // can't reserve 10 from 5
    assertThat(sys.getRemainingInventory()).isEqualTo(5); // unchanged
}

@Test
void releasesRestoresInventory() {
    var sys = new InventoryReservationSystem(100);
    sys.reserve("user1", 20);
    sys.release("user1");
    assertThat(sys.getRemainingInventory()).isEqualTo(100);
}

@Test
void concurrentReservationsNeverOversell() throws InterruptedException {
    var sys = new InventoryReservationSystem(100);
    int threadCount = 10_000;
    AtomicInteger successCount = new AtomicInteger(0);

    ExecutorService pool = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(200);
    CountDownLatch latch = new CountDownLatch(threadCount);

    for (int i = 0; i < threadCount; i++) {
        final String userId = "user-" + i;
        pool.submit(() -> {
            if (sys.reserve(userId, 1)) successCount.incrementAndGet();
            latch.countDown();
        });
    }

    latch.await(10, TimeUnit.SECONDS);
    // Exactly 100 reservations should succeed
    assertThat(successCount.get()).isEqualTo(100);
    assertThat(sys.getRemainingInventory()).isEqualTo(0);
    pool.shutdown();
}

👨‍💻 Jason: The CAS loop + concurrency stress test — exactly

the level of thinking we expect at ByteDance. Most candidates only

write the happy path test. You wrote the concurrent correctness test.

💻 ROUND 3 — SYSTEM DESIGN (60 min, English)

Setting: Staff Engineer. TikTok Shop live commerce.

👨‍💻 Wei (Staff Eng): *"Design TikTok Shop's live commerce

system. A seller starts a live stream. During the stream, they pin

products for sale. Viewers watch and buy products — potentially

100K concurrent viewers per stream buying during flash moments.

Show real-time viewer count, product inventory, and purchase feed."*

👨 Khanh:

Core real-time requirements:

1. Viewer count — updates as viewers join/leave (every 5s is fine)
2. Product inventory — MUST be accurate (oversell = bad)  
3. Purchase feed — "Lisa just bought Lip Tint!" (slight delay OK)
4. Live stream video — handled by CDN/media team (out of scope)

Architecture:

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  VIEWERS (up to 100K per stream)                      │
│  Connect via WebSocket to receive real-time updates   │
└──────────┬─────────────────────────────────┬──────────┘
           │ WebSocket (receive)             │ HTTP POST (buy)
           ▼                                 ▼
┌─────────────────────┐           ┌──────────────────────┐
│  Live Stream        │           │  Purchase API         │
│  Gateway            │           │  (reserve inventory)  │
│  (WebSocket server) │           └──────────┬────────────┘
└─────────┬───────────┘                      │
          │                          ┌───────▼────────┐
          │                          │  Redis          │
          │ Push updates             │  (inventory,    │
          │                          │  viewer count)  │
┌─────────▼───────────┐              └───────┬─────────┘
│  Event Bus          │◄─────────────────────┘
│  (Kafka)            │  publish purchase events
│  - viewer.joined    │
│  - viewer.left      │
│  - product.purchase │
│  - inventory.update │
└─────────┬───────────┘
          │
┌─────────▼──────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Real-time Aggregator (Kafka Streams / Flink)       │
│  - Count viewers per stream                         │
│  - Aggregate purchase feed (latest 20 purchases)    │
│  - Compute inventory alerts                         │
└─────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────┘
          │ Computed state
          ▼
┌─────────────────────┐
│  Redis Pub/Sub      │──► WebSocket Gateway pushes to viewers
│  (broadcast state)  │
└─────────────────────┘

Key Components:

1. Inventory Reservation (most critical):

@PostMapping("/live/{streamId}/buy")
public ResponseEntity<PurchaseResponse> buyProduct(
        @PathVariable String streamId,
        @RequestBody BuyRequest req,
        @AuthenticationPrincipal UserPrincipal user) {

    String inventoryKey = "inventory:" + streamId + ":" + req.getProductId();

    // Atomic inventory decrement via Lua script
    Long result = redisTemplate.execute(DEDUCT_INVENTORY_SCRIPT,
        List.of(inventoryKey),
        String.valueOf(req.getQuantity())
    );

    if (result == 0L) {
        return ResponseEntity.ok(PurchaseResponse.soldOut());
    }

    // Publish purchase event (async — don't block response)
    kafkaTemplate.send("product.purchases", new PurchaseEvent(
        user.getId(), streamId, req.getProductId(), req.getQuantity()
    ));

    return ResponseEntity.ok(PurchaseResponse.success(
        "Purchase confirmed! Processing payment..."));
}

2. Viewer Count via Redis HyperLogLog:

// HyperLogLog: probabilistic cardinality — uses ~12KB regardless of viewer count
// Accuracy: ~0.81% error — showing "100,023 viewers" vs exact 100,000 is fine

public void viewerJoined(String streamId, String userId) {
    redisTemplate.opsForHyperLogLog()
        .add("viewers:" + streamId, userId);
}

public long getViewerCount(String streamId) {
    return redisTemplate.opsForHyperLogLog().size("viewers:" + streamId);
}

3. Purchase Feed (last 20 purchases):

// Redis List capped at 20 items — the "purchase feed"
@KafkaListener(topics = "product.purchases")
public void updatePurchaseFeed(PurchaseEvent event) {
    String feedKey = "feed:" + event.getStreamId();
    String entry = event.getUsername() + " bought " + event.getProductName();

    redisTemplate.opsForList().leftPush(feedKey, entry);
    redisTemplate.opsForList().trim(feedKey, 0, 19); // keep only last 20
    redisTemplate.expire(feedKey, Duration.ofHours(2));

    // Broadcast to all viewers via WebSocket
    String payload = redisTemplate.opsForList().range(feedKey, 0, 19).toString();
    redisTemplate.convertAndSend("stream:" + event.getStreamId(), payload);
}

4. Scaling 100K concurrent WebSocket connections:

100K WebSocket connections cannot all connect to one server

(too much memory). Use sticky sessions + Redis Pub/Sub:

Viewer 1 → Pod A (WebSocket connection)
Viewer 2 → Pod B (WebSocket connection)

When purchase event fires:
  Any pod → publishes to Redis channel "stream:12345"
  Pod A → subscribed to "stream:12345" → pushes to Viewer 1
  Pod B → subscribed to "stream:12345" → pushes to Viewer 2
@Component
public class StreamEventBroadcaster {
    private final SimpMessagingTemplate wsTemplate;
    private final RedisMessageListenerContainer listenerContainer;

    public void subscribeToStream(String streamId) {
        listenerContainer.addMessageListener(
            (message, pattern) -> {
                wsTemplate.convertAndSend(
                    "/topic/stream/" + streamId,
                    new String(message.getBody())
                );
            },
            new PatternTopic("stream:" + streamId)
        );
    }
}

Each pod handles ~5K WebSocket connections. 20 pods = 100K total.

👨‍💻 Wei: HyperLogLog for viewer count — not many candidates

know this. Why not a simple Redis SET with viewer IDs?

👨 Khanh: At 100K viewers, a Redis SET storing all 100K user IDs

= 100K × ~50 bytes = ~5MB per stream. With 10K simultaneous streams:

5MB × 10K = 50GB just for viewer tracking.

HyperLogLog uses ~12KB regardless of cardinality. For 10K streams:

12KB × 10K = 120MB. And accuracy is ~0.81% error — showing "100,081"

instead of "100,000" is completely acceptable for a live viewer count.

The SET approach would also be slower: adding/removing a SET element

at 100K concurrent viewers is much more write-intensive than

HyperLogLog's single ADD operation.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

RoundCategoryScoreNotes
HRSpeed + responsibility10/1090-min incident fix story with methodology
Algo 1Top-K with MinHeap10/10O(N log K), TreeMap alternative for frequent reads
Algo 2CAS inventory reservation10/10CAS loop, concurrency stress test with CountDownLatch
DesignLive commerce system10/10Redis Lua deduct, HyperLogLog viewers, Redis Pub/Sub fan-out
Design depthHyperLogLog justification10/10Memory math: SET=50GB vs HLL=120MB for 10K streams

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 ByteDance/TikTok Interview Tips

  1. Algorithm round is real — ByteDance interviews like Google. MinHeap, CAS, BFS — know them
  2. Concurrency stress tests — write CountDownLatch tests for concurrent systems
  3. HyperLogLog — know when to use it over Redis SET (cardinality at scale)
  4. Redis Pub/Sub for WebSocket fan-out — essential pattern for live systems
  5. Lua scripts for atomic inventory — same pattern as MoMo/ZaloPay
  6. Live commerce is their crown jewel — show you understand the real-time challenges
  7. ByteDance pays USD — $6K-$12K/month + RSUs, fully remote from VN
  8. 4 technical rounds — algo, system design, behavioral, hiring manager

🎭 Mock Interview #34 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: Shopee (Sea Group E-Commerce, HCMC Product Engineering)

Context: Mock #26 covered Sea Group Platform Engineering (observability,

consistent hashing). This mock covers Shopee Product Engineering —

building the actual e-commerce features used by 300M+ buyers in SEA.

Role: Shopee Flash Sale & Promotions team — the system behind 9.9,

10.10, 11.11 mega sales. These events process 50M+ orders in a single day

(11.11), with peaks of 50K orders/second in the first 10 minutes.

Tech stack: Java 17, Spring Boot, Kafka, Redis, MySQL, Elasticsearch, AWS EKS.

Why this is different from Platform Engineering: You build features

that directly impact 300M users. Flash sale engineering is one of the

hardest e-commerce problems — the entire internet equivalent of a

"stampede" happens at midnight on November 11th every year.

📞 ROUND 1 — HIRING MANAGER (25 min, English)

👨‍💼 Kevin (Eng Manager): Hi Khanh! I lead the Promotions

Engineering team. This isn't an HR round — I want to understand

how you think about engineering trade-offs at scale.

Quick context: 11.11 last year, our flash sale started at midnight.

In the first 60 seconds, we received 3 million add-to-cart events.

How would you architect for that moment?

👨 Khanh: Three principles I'd apply:

First: Separate the read path from the write path completely.

Before midnight, 100M users are browsing flash sale products —

that's a pure read workload. At midnight, they all click "Buy."

These two workloads have completely different characteristics.

I'd serve product listings from a CDN-cached, pre-computed snapshot.

No DB read at midnight for browsing — everything cached.

Second: The inventory reservation must be atomic and fast.

At 3M add-to-carts in 60 seconds, the database cannot handle the

write volume. Redis with Lua scripts for atomic inventory decrement

— same pattern used by MoMo, TikTok Shop. Redis can do 100K+ atomic

operations/second. PostgreSQL peaks at ~10K writes/second.

Third: Accept eventual consistency for non-critical data.

The purchase feed, view counts, recommendation updates — all of these

can be eventual. Only inventory deduction must be strongly consistent.

By relaxing consistency for secondary systems, I reduce the blast radius

of the midnight spike.

👨‍💼 Kevin: Smart. You separated read/write paths immediately.

Most engineers jump to "use more servers." What's your experience

with Shopee's scale specifically?

👨 Khanh: I haven't worked at Shopee's scale before — 3K RPS

at Sony is much smaller. But I study scale problems systematically:

I understand Little's Law, queuing theory, why connection pools

saturate under spike traffic, and how to identify bottlenecks using

profiling rather than guessing.

What I bring is the right mental model: measure first, optimize

the bottleneck, don't optimize what isn't slow. The methodology

scales even when the numbers are new to me.

👨‍💼 Kevin: "Methodology scales" — good perspective. Four more

technical rounds.

💻 ROUND 2 — SPRING BOOT & DATABASE (50 min, English)

Setting: Staff Engineer.

👨‍💻 Ryan (Staff Eng): Hi Khanh. At Shopee, 99% of our Java

code is Spring Boot. I want to test depth, not breadth.

Spring Boot Internals (25 min)

👨‍💻 Ryan: **Walk me through what happens when a Spring Boot

application starts. From main() to first request handled.**

👨 Khanh: Spring Boot startup has 8 distinct phases:

// 1. main() is called
SpringApplication.run(ShopeeApplication.class, args);

// 2. SpringApplication created
//    - Detects application type (SERVLET/REACTIVE/NONE)
//    - Loads ApplicationListeners from spring.factories
//    - Determines main application class

// 3. Bootstrap context (Spring Boot 2.4+)
//    - Loads bootstrap configuration
//    - Sets up Config Data (application.yml)

// 4. ApplicationContext created
//    - AnnotationConfigServletWebServerApplicationContext (for web apps)

// 5. BeanDefinitions loaded
//    - @ComponentScan finds @Component, @Service, @Repository, @Controller
//    - @Import, @Configuration, @Bean methods registered
//    - Auto-configuration (spring.factories / AutoConfiguration.imports)
//    - BeanDefinition = "recipe" for creating a bean, not the bean itself

// 6. BeanFactory post-processing
//    - @Value resolved from environment
//    - @Conditional evaluated (e.g., @ConditionalOnProperty)
//    - PropertySourcesPlaceholderConfigurer runs

// 7. Bean instantiation
//    - Constructor called (DI via constructor injection)
//    - @Autowired field/setter injection
//    - @PostConstruct methods called
//    - BeanPostProcessors run (AOP proxies created here!)

// 8. Web server starts
//    - Embedded Tomcat started on port 8080 (default)
//    - DispatcherServlet registered
//    - Application ready to handle requests

Where bugs hide during startup:

// Problem 1: Circular dependency
@Service class A { @Autowired B b; }
@Service class B { @Autowired A a; }
// Spring 6 (Boot 3): circular dependency forbidden by default → startup fails
// Fix: use constructor injection + @Lazy on one side

// Problem 2: @PostConstruct too early
@Component
class CacheWarmer {
    @Autowired DataSource ds;

    @PostConstruct
    void warmUp() {
        // ⚠️ DataSource available but DB might not be ready yet
        // Use SmartLifecycle instead for post-startup initialization
    }
}

// Problem 3: Auto-configuration conflict
// Two libraries both auto-configure DataSource
// Fix: @SpringBootApplication(exclude = {DataSourceAutoConfiguration.class})

Monitoring startup time:

# Add to JVM args — shows which beans took the longest to initialize
java -Dspring.main.lazy-initialization=true -jar shopee.jar
# Lazy init: beans created on first use, not at startup → faster startup
# Trade-off: first request is slow (initializes beans on demand)

👨‍💻 Ryan: **At 11.11, our MySQL receives 50K writes/second

from the order service. How do you protect it?**

👨 Khanh: Five layers, from fastest to slowest:

Layer 1: Redis absorbs the spike (pre-decrement inventory):

// Before 11.11 starts: pre-load flash sale inventory into Redis
// Redis handles inventory decrement at 100K+ ops/sec
// MySQL only updated in batch after the spike subsides

redisTemplate.opsForValue().set("flash:inventory:" + productId,
    String.valueOf(stock), Duration.ofHours(24));

// Atomic decrement during flash sale
Long remaining = redisTemplate.execute(DEDUCT_SCRIPT,
    List.of("flash:inventory:" + productId), "1");
// MySQL not touched for inventory during peak

Layer 2: Write-behind cache (async MySQL writes):

// Don't write to MySQL synchronously during peak
// Write to Redis, then batch-sync to MySQL every 500ms
@Scheduled(fixedDelay = 500)
public void syncOrdersToDb() {
    List<Order> pending = redisQueue.drain(1000); // max 1000 per batch
    if (!pending.isEmpty()) {
        orderRepo.saveAll(pending); // JDBC batch insert — 1000 rows in 1 query
    }
}

Layer 3: JDBC batch insert:

spring:
  datasource:
    hikari:
      maximum-pool-size: 30
  jpa:
    properties:
      hibernate:
        jdbc:
          batch_size: 100         # insert 100 rows per SQL statement
          order_inserts: true     # group inserts of same table type
          order_updates: true

Layer 4: Vertical write sharding (separate DB for flash orders):

Flash sale orders → flash_db (dedicated MySQL, higher write capacity)
Regular orders    → main_db  (normal operations)

This gives flash sales dedicated DB resources during the event.

Layer 5: Circuit breaker — shed load gracefully:

@CircuitBreaker(name = "orderDb", fallbackMethod = "queueOrder")
public Order saveOrder(Order order) {
    return orderRepo.save(order);
}

public Order queueOrder(Order order, Exception ex) {
    // DB at limit → queue in Kafka, process when load drops
    kafkaTemplate.send("orders.overflow", order);
    return Order.queued(order.getId());
    // User sees: "Order received — confirming shortly"
}

Design: Flash Sale Pre-warm Strategy (25 min)

👨‍💻 Ryan: Shopee's biggest risk at 11.11 midnight is the

cold start problem. JVMs are cold, caches are empty, CDN

TTLs have expired. How do you pre-warm the system?

👨 Khanh: Pre-warming is a checklist, not a single technique:

1. JVM warm-up (1 hour before):

// Canary pod receives synthetic traffic 1 hour before 11.11
// This JIT-compiles the hot code paths
// When real traffic hits, JIT is already warm

@Scheduled(cron = "0 0 23 10 11 ?") // 11 PM Nov 10th = 1 hour before
public void warmUpJVM() {
    for (int i = 0; i < 100_000; i++) {
        flashSaleService.simulatePurchase(SYNTHETIC_PRODUCT_ID, SYNTHETIC_USER);
    }
    log.info("JVM warm-up complete");
}

2. Cache pre-population (30 min before):

@Scheduled(cron = "0 30 23 10 11 ?")
public void prePopulateFlashSaleCache() {
    List<FlashProduct> products = flashSaleRepo.findAll11_11Products();

    products.parallelStream().forEach(p -> {
        // Pre-load product details into Redis
        redisTemplate.opsForValue().set(
            "product:" + p.getId(), p, Duration.ofHours(2));

        // Pre-load inventory into Redis
        redisTemplate.opsForValue().set(
            "flash:inventory:" + p.getId(),
            String.valueOf(p.getStock()), Duration.ofHours(2));
    });

    log.info("Pre-populated {} flash products into cache", products.size());
}

3. Connection pool pre-warm:

// HikariCP: minIdle connections = maxPoolSize (all connections open before event)
// Prevents "connection establishment latency" during spike
spring.datasource.hikari.minimum-idle: 30    # same as maximum-pool-size
spring.datasource.hikari.initialization-fail-timeout: 60000

4. K8s pre-scaling (2 hours before):

# Scale up pods before traffic hits — avoid cold-start during spike
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
spec:
  minReplicas: 50    # normally 10, set to 50 before 11.11
  maxReplicas: 200

5. Readiness gate — don't serve until warm:

@Component
public class WarmUpReadinessCheck implements ReadinessHealthIndicator {

    private volatile boolean isWarm = false;

    @Override
    public Health getHealth(boolean includeDetails) {
        return isWarm
            ? Health.up().build()
            : Health.down().withDetail("reason", "Still warming up").build();
    }

    @PostConstruct
    void warmUp() {
        // ... do warm-up work ...
        isWarm = true; // only mark ready after warm-up complete
    }
}
// K8s waits for readiness probe before routing traffic to this pod

💻 ROUND 3 — CODING (30 min, English)

Setting: Senior Engineer. Flash sale specific.

👨‍💻 Mark (Sr Eng): "Implement a flash sale scheduler.

*A flash sale has: productId, startTime, endTime, discountedPrice,

stock.*

Implement:

  • void scheduleSale(FlashSale sale) — register a future sale
  • Optional<FlashSale> getActiveSale(String productId, Instant now) — return active sale for a product if any
  • List<FlashSale> getUpcomingSales(Instant now, Duration within) — sales starting within the given duration"

👨 Khanh:

public record FlashSale(
    String saleId,
    String productId,
    Instant startTime,
    Instant endTime,
    BigDecimal discountedPrice,
    int stock
) {
    public boolean isActive(Instant now) {
        return !now.isBefore(startTime) && now.isBefore(endTime);
    }

    public boolean startsWithin(Instant now, Duration window) {
        return startTime.isAfter(now)
            && startTime.isBefore(now.plus(window));
    }
}

public class FlashSaleScheduler {

    // productId → list of sales (a product may have multiple sales at different times)
    private final Map<String, List<FlashSale>> salesByProduct =
        new ConcurrentHashMap<>();

    public void scheduleSale(FlashSale sale) {
        Objects.requireNonNull(sale, "Sale cannot be null");
        if (!sale.endTime().isAfter(sale.startTime())) {
            throw new IllegalArgumentException("endTime must be after startTime");
        }

        salesByProduct.computeIfAbsent(sale.productId(),
            k -> new CopyOnWriteArrayList<>()).add(sale);
    }

    public Optional<FlashSale> getActiveSale(String productId, Instant now) {
        List<FlashSale> sales = salesByProduct.getOrDefault(
            productId, Collections.emptyList());

        return sales.stream()
            .filter(s -> s.isActive(now))
            .findFirst(); // at most one sale active at a time per product
    }

    public List<FlashSale> getUpcomingSales(Instant now, Duration within) {
        return salesByProduct.values().stream()
            .flatMap(List::stream)
            .filter(s -> s.startsWithin(now, within))
            .sorted(Comparator.comparing(FlashSale::startTime))
            .collect(Collectors.toList());
    }
}

Tests:

static final Instant NOW = Instant.parse("2024-11-11T00:00:00Z");

FlashSaleScheduler scheduler = new FlashSaleScheduler();

@Test
void returnsActiveSaleForProduct() {
    scheduler.scheduleSale(new FlashSale("S1", "P1",
        NOW.minus(10, MINUTES), NOW.plus(50, MINUTES), bd("99"), 100));

    var result = scheduler.getActiveSale("P1", NOW);

    assertThat(result).isPresent();
    assertThat(result.get().saleId()).isEqualTo("S1");
}

@Test
void returnsEmptyWhenSaleNotStarted() {
    scheduler.scheduleSale(new FlashSale("S2", "P2",
        NOW.plus(1, HOURS), NOW.plus(2, HOURS), bd("50"), 200));

    assertThat(scheduler.getActiveSale("P2", NOW)).isEmpty();
}

@Test
void returnsEmptyWhenSaleExpired() {
    scheduler.scheduleSale(new FlashSale("S3", "P3",
        NOW.minus(2, HOURS), NOW.minus(1, HOURS), bd("30"), 50));

    assertThat(scheduler.getActiveSale("P3", NOW)).isEmpty();
}

@Test
void upcomingSalesSortedByStartTime() {
    scheduler.scheduleSale(new FlashSale("S4", "P4",
        NOW.plus(2, HOURS), NOW.plus(3, HOURS), bd("80"), 100));
    scheduler.scheduleSale(new FlashSale("S5", "P5",
        NOW.plus(30, MINUTES), NOW.plus(1, HOURS), bd("60"), 50));

    var upcoming = scheduler.getUpcomingSales(NOW, Duration.ofHours(3));

    assertThat(upcoming).hasSize(2);
    assertThat(upcoming.get(0).saleId()).isEqualTo("S5"); // starts sooner
    assertThat(upcoming.get(1).saleId()).isEqualTo("S4");
}

@Test
void invalidSaleRejected() {
    assertThatThrownBy(() -> scheduler.scheduleSale(
        new FlashSale("S6", "P6",
            NOW.plus(2, HOURS), NOW.plus(1, HOURS), bd("10"), 100) // end < start
    )).isInstanceOf(IllegalArgumentException.class);
}

📊 FULL SCORECARD

RoundCategoryScoreNotes
HMRead/write split thinking10/10CDN for reads, Redis for writes — separated immediately
SpringStartup lifecycle10/108 phases, circular dep, @Conditional, lazy init
DBMySQL protection at 50K WPS10/105 layers: Redis, write-behind, JDBC batch, sharding, circuit breaker
Prep11.11 pre-warm strategy10/10JVM warm-up, cache population, K8s pre-scale, readiness gate

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 Shopee Flash Sale Interview Tips

  1. Read/write split — say this immediately when asked about scale
  2. Redis for inventory — not MySQL at peak. Lua script = atomic
  3. Write-behind cache — async batch flush to MySQL
  4. 11.11 pre-warming — JVM, cache, connection pool, K8s pods, readiness gate
  5. Spring startup phases — know all 8. BeanDefinition vs Bean instance distinction
  6. JDBC batch insert — batch_size: 100 + order_inserts: true in Hibernate
  7. CopyOnWriteArrayList — thread-safe list for read-heavy + occasional write
  8. Shopee salary — 60-100M gross (same as Sea Group). HCMC is a top hub

🎭 Mock Interview #35 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: ZaloPay (VNG's Digital Wallet, HCMC, 15M+ users)

Note: Mock #22 covered VNG/ZaloPay broadly — Java memory model,

ConcurrentHashMap, QR payment. This mock (#35) goes deeper into

ZaloPay-specific fintech engineering: wallet architecture, KYC tiers,

P2P transfer limits, bank settlement, and transaction velocity control.

Role: Core Wallet team — the engine behind every ZaloPay balance

operation: top-up, P2P transfer, bill payment, withdrawal to bank.

Tech: Java 17, Spring Boot, PostgreSQL, Redis, Kafka, NAPAS APIs.

📞 ROUND 1 — HR (15 min, Vietnamese/English)

👩 Trang (HR): Chào anh Khanh! ZaloPay đang mở rộng rất nhanh

— đang cạnh tranh trực tiếp với MoMo. Anh thấy điểm khác biệt

của ZaloPay so với MoMo là gì từ góc nhìn engineering?

👨 Khanh: Từ góc nhìn engineering, ZaloPay có lợi thế lớn là

Zalo ecosystem — 70M user Zalo có thể onboard ZaloPay friction-free.

Điều đó tạo ra một bài toán rất thú vị: làm sao handle growth burst

khi một feature Zalo như "Lì xì" hay "Chia bill" viral — đột nhiên

có hàng triệu user mới active trong vài giờ.

MoMo build merchant network rộng hơn, nhưng ZaloPay có distribution

channel độc quyền qua Zalo. Về technical, cả hai đều phải giải bài

toán tương tự — atomic balance operations, fraud detection, NAPAS

settlement. Sự khác biệt là scale-up pattern của ZaloPay phụ thuộc

vào viral social feature hơn là merchant-driven.

👩 Trang: Anh hiểu domain rất rõ. Technical rounds sẽ focus

vào wallet core và payments. Good luck anh!

💻 ROUND 2 — WALLET ARCHITECTURE (55 min, English)

Setting: Staff Engineer, Core Wallet team.

👨‍💻 Hưng (Staff Eng): Hi Khanh! ZaloPay's wallet is the heart

of everything. Let's go deep.

PART A — KYC Tiers & Transaction Limits (20 min)

👨‍💻 Hưng: **ZaloPay has 3 KYC tiers. Tier 1 (phone only):

max 20M VND/day. Tier 2 (CCCD): max 100M VND/day. Tier 3 (full

KYC + face): unlimited. How do you enforce these limits in code

while handling concurrent transactions?**

👨 Khanh: Two-layer enforcement: Redis for real-time speed,

PostgreSQL for accuracy.

public enum KycTier {
    TIER_1(20_000_000L),   // 20M VND/day
    TIER_2(100_000_000L),  // 100M VND/day
    TIER_3(Long.MAX_VALUE); // unlimited

    private final long dailyLimitVnd;
}

@Service
public class TransactionLimitService {

    // Redis key: "limit:{userId}:{date}" → total spent today (VND)
    private static final String LIMIT_KEY = "limit:%s:%s";

    private static final String CHECK_AND_RESERVE_SCRIPT = """
        local current = tonumber(redis.call('GET', KEYS[1])) or 0
        local limit = tonumber(ARGV[1])
        local amount = tonumber(ARGV[2])
        if current + amount > limit then
            return 0  -- exceeds limit
        end
        redis.call('INCRBY', KEYS[1], amount)
        redis.call('EXPIRE', KEYS[1], 86400)  -- expires at midnight-ish
        return 1  -- approved
        """;

    public void checkAndReserveDailyLimit(String userId, long amountVnd) {
        User user = userRepo.findById(userId).orElseThrow();
        long dailyLimit = user.getKycTier().getDailyLimitVnd();

        if (dailyLimit == Long.MAX_VALUE) return; // Tier 3 — no limit check

        String today = LocalDate.now(ZoneId.of("Asia/Ho_Chi_Minh")).toString();
        String key = String.format(LIMIT_KEY, userId, today);

        Long result = redisTemplate.execute(
            new DefaultRedisScript<>(CHECK_AND_RESERVE_SCRIPT, Long.class),
            List.of(key),
            String.valueOf(dailyLimit),
            String.valueOf(amountVnd)
        );

        if (!Long.valueOf(1L).equals(result)) {
            long used = getCurrentDailyUsage(userId);
            throw new DailyLimitExceededException(
                String.format("Daily limit exceeded. Used: %,d VND, Limit: %,d VND",
                    used, dailyLimit));
        }
    }

    // Reconciliation: nightly job verifies Redis vs PostgreSQL
    @Scheduled(cron = "0 55 23 * * *")  // 11:55 PM daily
    public void reconcileDailyLimits() {
        LocalDate today = LocalDate.now(ZoneId.of("Asia/Ho_Chi_Minh"));
        List<String> activeUsers = txnRepo.findUsersWithTransactionsToday(today);

        for (String userId : activeUsers) {
            long redisUsage  = getCurrentDailyUsage(userId);
            long dbUsage     = txnRepo.sumSuccessfulToday(userId, today);
            if (redisUsage != dbUsage) {
                log.error("Limit mismatch for user {}: Redis={}, DB={}", userId, redisUsage, dbUsage);
                alertOpsTeam(userId, redisUsage, dbUsage);
                // Correct Redis from DB (DB is source of truth)
                String key = String.format(LIMIT_KEY, userId, today.toString());
                redisTemplate.opsForValue().set(key, String.valueOf(dbUsage));
            }
        }
    }
}

Why Redis Lua script? Atomic check-and-increment in one operation.

Without it, two concurrent transfers could both pass the check

before either updates the counter — a classic TOCTOU race condition.

👨‍💻 Hưng: **ZaloPay's Lì xì (Red Envelope) feature goes viral

every Tết — 5M users send red envelopes in the first hour. The

feature requires: sender locks a total amount, splits it randomly

among N recipients, each recipient can claim once. Design the

claim mechanism under this load.**

👨 Khanh: Classic "grab red envelope" problem — used by WeChat,

ZaloPay, MoMo every Tết.

Pre-split approach (recommended for ZaloPay scale):

When sender creates envelope:
  1. Total: 500,000 VND, Recipients: 10
  2. Pre-generate 10 random amounts that sum to 500,000
  3. Store as a Redis List: [45000, 62000, 38000, 71000, ...]
  4. Deduct 500,000 from sender immediately (atomic)

When recipient claims:
  1. LPOP from Redis List (atomic, O(1))
  2. If nil → envelope exhausted → show "all claimed"
  3. If value → credit that amount to recipient wallet
@Service
public class RedEnvelopeService {

    public RedEnvelope create(String senderId, long totalVnd, int recipients) {
        // 1. Deduct from sender atomically
        walletService.debit(senderId, totalVnd, "Red envelope created");

        // 2. Pre-split into random amounts
        List<Long> amounts = randomSplit(totalVnd, recipients);

        // 3. Store in Redis List (LIFO stack — each claim pops one)
        String envelopeId = UUID.randomUUID().toString();
        String key = "envelope:" + envelopeId;
        redisTemplate.opsForList().rightPushAll(key,
            amounts.stream().map(String::valueOf).toArray(String[]::new));
        redisTemplate.expire(key, Duration.ofDays(1)); // expires after 1 day

        // 4. Persist metadata to DB
        envelopeRepo.save(RedEnvelope.of(envelopeId, senderId, totalVnd, recipients));
        return envelopeRepo.findById(envelopeId).orElseThrow();
    }

    @Transactional
    public ClaimResult claim(String envelopeId, String recipientId) {
        // Idempotency: already claimed?
        if (claimRepo.existsByEnvelopeAndRecipient(envelopeId, recipientId)) {
            return ClaimResult.alreadyClaimed();
        }

        // Atomic pop from Redis List
        String key = "envelope:" + envelopeId;
        String amountStr = redisTemplate.opsForList().leftPop(key);

        if (amountStr == null) {
            return ClaimResult.exhausted(); // all claimed
        }

        long amount = Long.parseLong(amountStr);

        // Credit recipient
        walletService.credit(recipientId, amount, "Red envelope from " + envelopeId);

        // Record claim
        claimRepo.save(new EnvelopeClaim(envelopeId, recipientId, amount));

        return ClaimResult.success(amount);
    }

    // Random split: generates N amounts summing to total
    private List<Long> randomSplit(long total, int n) {
        if (n == 1) return List.of(total);
        Random rng = new Random();
        List<Long> amounts = new ArrayList<>();
        long remaining = total;
        for (int i = 0; i < n - 1; i++) {
            // Each amount: between 1 VND and (remaining / (n-i) * 2)
            long max = Math.min(remaining - (n - i - 1), remaining * 2L / (n - i));
            long amt = 1 + (long)(rng.nextDouble() * (max - 1));
            amounts.add(amt);
            remaining -= amt;
        }
        amounts.add(remaining); // last gets the remainder
        Collections.shuffle(amounts); // so last recipient isn't always largest
        return amounts;
    }
}

Why LPOP is safe at 5M concurrent claims?

Redis LPOP is atomic — only one caller gets each element.

No two users claim the same amount. No oversplit possible.

PART B — NAPAS Bank Settlement (20 min)

👨‍💻 Hưng: **When a user withdraws from ZaloPay to their bank

account, we send the request to NAPAS (Vietnam's interbank network).

NAPAS can take 1-30 seconds to respond and sometimes doesn't respond

at all. Design the withdrawal flow.**

👨 Khanh: Three-phase design: Reserve → Submit → Confirm/Rollback.

@Service
public class WithdrawalService {

    @Transactional
    public WithdrawalResponse initiateWithdrawal(WithdrawalRequest req) {
        String withdrawalId = UUID.randomUUID().toString();

        // Phase 1: Reserve funds (debit ZaloPay wallet)
        walletService.debit(req.getUserId(), req.getAmountVnd(), withdrawalId);

        // Persist withdrawal in PENDING state
        Withdrawal withdrawal = withdrawalRepo.save(Withdrawal.builder()
            .id(withdrawalId)
            .userId(req.getUserId())
            .amountVnd(req.getAmountVnd())
            .bankCode(req.getBankCode())
            .bankAccountNo(req.getAccountNumber())
            .status(WithdrawalStatus.PENDING)
            .build());

        // Phase 2: Submit to NAPAS async (don't block user)
        kafkaTemplate.send("withdrawals.pending",
            withdrawalId, new NapasSubmitMessage(withdrawal));

        return WithdrawalResponse.accepted(withdrawalId,
            "Withdrawal processing — funds will arrive within 30 seconds");
    }
}

@KafkaListener(topics = "withdrawals.pending", groupId = "napas-submitter")
public void submitToNapas(NapasSubmitMessage msg) {
    Withdrawal w = withdrawalRepo.findById(msg.getWithdrawalId()).orElseThrow();

    if (w.getStatus() != PENDING) return; // idempotent

    try {
        NapasResponse response = napasClient.submitTransfer(
            NapasTransferRequest.from(w),
            Duration.ofSeconds(30)  // 30s timeout
        );

        if (response.isSuccess()) {
            // Phase 3a: Confirm
            w.setStatus(COMPLETED);
            w.setNapasRef(response.getReferenceNo());
            notificationService.notifySuccess(w);
        } else {
            // Phase 3b: Permanent failure (invalid account, etc.)
            w.setStatus(FAILED_PERMANENT);
            walletService.credit(w.getUserId(), w.getAmountVnd(), "Refund: " + w.getId());
            notificationService.notifyFailure(w, response.getErrorMessage());
        }

    } catch (TimeoutException | NapasUnavailableException ex) {
        // Timeout — NAPAS didn't respond in 30s
        w.setStatus(NAPAS_TIMEOUT);
        w.setRetryCount(w.getRetryCount() + 1);
        log.warn("NAPAS timeout for withdrawal {}, retry #{}", w.getId(), w.getRetryCount());
    }

    withdrawalRepo.save(w);
}

// Recovery job: poll NAPAS for timed-out withdrawals
@Scheduled(fixedDelay = 60_000)
public void recoverTimedOutWithdrawals() {
    List<Withdrawal> stuck = withdrawalRepo.findByStatusAndRetryCountLessThan(
        NAPAS_TIMEOUT, 5
    );

    for (Withdrawal w : stuck) {
        // Query NAPAS for status using our reference
        try {
            NapasStatusResponse status = napasClient.queryStatus(w.getId());
            if (status.isCompleted()) {
                w.setStatus(COMPLETED);
                notificationService.notifySuccess(w);
            } else if (status.isFailed()) {
                w.setStatus(FAILED_PERMANENT);
                walletService.credit(w.getUserId(), w.getAmountVnd(), "Refund timeout: " + w.getId());
            }
            // Still pending in NAPAS → leave for next check
        } catch (Exception e) {
            log.error("Recovery check failed for {}", w.getId(), e);
        }
        withdrawalRepo.save(w);
    }

    // After 5 retries → escalate to ops team
    withdrawalRepo.findByStatusAndRetryCountGreaterThanEqual(NAPAS_TIMEOUT, 5)
        .forEach(w -> alertOpsTeam(w, "Withdrawal stuck after 5 NAPAS retries"));
}

State machine:

PENDING → [submit to NAPAS]
    ├── SUCCESS  → COMPLETED
    ├── PERM ERR → FAILED_PERMANENT → refund user
    └── TIMEOUT  → NAPAS_TIMEOUT → retry up to 5x
                                 → after 5x → manual ops review

PART C — CODING: Velocity Check (15 min)

👨‍💻 Hưng: *"Implement a velocity checker. A user is suspicious

if they make more than 3 transactions above 5,000,000 VND within

any 1-hour sliding window. Return true if the user's latest

transaction triggers the alert."*

👨 Khanh:

public class VelocityChecker {

    private static final long THRESHOLD_VND    = 5_000_000L;
    private static final int  MAX_HIGH_VALUE   = 3;
    private static final long WINDOW_MS        = 60 * 60 * 1000L; // 1 hour

    // userId → deque of timestamps of high-value transactions
    private final ConcurrentHashMap<String, Deque<Long>> windows =
        new ConcurrentHashMap<>();

    public boolean checkVelocity(String userId, long amountVnd, long timestampMs) {
        if (amountVnd < THRESHOLD_VND) return false; // low-value — no alert

        Deque<Long> window = windows.computeIfAbsent(userId,
            k -> new ArrayDeque<>());

        synchronized (window) {
            long cutoff = timestampMs - WINDOW_MS;

            // Remove transactions outside 1-hour window
            while (!window.isEmpty() && window.peekFirst() <= cutoff) {
                window.pollFirst();
            }

            // Add current transaction
            window.addLast(timestampMs);

            // Alert if more than 3 high-value txns in window
            return window.size() > MAX_HIGH_VALUE;
        }
    }
}

// Tests
@Test
void noAlertForLowValueTransaction() {
    var vc = new VelocityChecker();
    assertThat(vc.checkVelocity("U1", 1_000_000L, 0L)).isFalse();
}

@Test
void alertOnFourthHighValueTransactionInWindow() {
    var vc = new VelocityChecker();
    long base = System.currentTimeMillis();
    vc.checkVelocity("U1", 10_000_000L, base);
    vc.checkVelocity("U1", 10_000_000L, base + 10_000);
    vc.checkVelocity("U1", 10_000_000L, base + 20_000);
    // 4th → alert
    assertThat(vc.checkVelocity("U1", 10_000_000L, base + 30_000)).isTrue();
}

@Test
void windowSlidesCorrectly() {
    var vc = new VelocityChecker();
    long base = 0L;
    // 3 high-value transactions
    vc.checkVelocity("U1", 10_000_000L, base);
    vc.checkVelocity("U1", 10_000_000L, base + 10_000);
    vc.checkVelocity("U1", 10_000_000L, base + 20_000);

    // 4th transaction at base + 2 hours → first 3 have expired
    long twoHoursLater = base + 2 * 60 * 60 * 1000L;
    assertThat(vc.checkVelocity("U1", 10_000_000L, twoHoursLater)).isFalse();
}

@Test
void isolatesUsersIndependently() {
    var vc = new VelocityChecker();
    long base = System.currentTimeMillis();
    for (int i = 0; i < 4; i++) vc.checkVelocity("U1", 10_000_000L, base + i * 1000);
    // U2 has zero history — should not alert
    assertThat(vc.checkVelocity("U2", 10_000_000L, base)).isFalse();
}

📊 FULL SCORECARD

RoundCategoryScoreNotes
HR (VN)ZaloPay vs MoMo insight10/10Zalo distribution advantage + viral feature burst analysis
WalletKYC tiers + Redis Lua limit10/10Atomic check-and-reserve, nightly reconciliation
FeatureLì xì red envelope at Tết scale10/10Pre-split + Redis LPOP — atomic, O(1) per claim
PaymentsNAPAS withdrawal state machine10/103-phase flow, timeout recovery job, 5-retry escalation
CodingVelocity checker10/10Sliding window + synchronized deque + 4 edge case tests

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 ZaloPay Interview Tips

  1. Zalo integration advantage — viral features cause burst traffic, not steady growth
  2. Redis Lua for limit enforcement — atomic check-and-increment. TOCTOU without it
  3. Pre-split red envelope — don't calculate amounts on claim (race condition). Pre-split at creation
  4. NAPAS timeout is normal — design recovery polling job, never assume NAPAS responds
  5. KYC tiers = limit tiers — 3 SBV-mandated levels. Know: 20M / 100M / unlimited
  6. Velocity check uses sliding window — same pattern as fraud detection
  7. ZaloPay salary — 45-70M+ gross. Part of VNG Group benefits
  8. Vietnamese HR round — be a ZaloPay user, reference real features (Lì xì, chia bill, nạp điện)

🎭 Mock Interview #36 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: Stripe (Global Payments Infrastructure, San Francisco / Remote)

Role: Backend Engineer — Payments Core team.

Focus: charge lifecycle, idempotency, webhook delivery, and

fraud-resistant payment flows.

Tech: Java 17, gRPC, PostgreSQL, Redis, Kafka, Stripe internal frameworks.

📞 ROUND 1 — HR / CULTURE FIT (15 min, English)

👩 Sarah (Recruiter): Hi Khanh! Stripe's mission is to grow the GDP

of the internet. What excites you most about building payment

infrastructure at this scale?

👨 Khanh: What excites me is the reliability contract Stripe holds

with its merchants. When a charge fails — even once — a merchant loses

a sale, possibly a customer. That responsibility pushes engineering

to a different standard: idempotency-by-default, exhaustive retry

semantics, and observable failure modes. I've been building similar

systems in fintech — handling atomic wallet debits, NAPAS settlements,

and Kafka-based async reconciliation — but Stripe's scale (hundreds of

millions of API calls per day) makes every design decision much

higher-stakes. I want to work on infrastructure where correctness

is non-negotiable.

👩 Sarah: Great answer. Stripe cares a lot about written

communication. How do you handle technical disagreement with peers?

👨 Khanh: I write a short doc — problem, options, tradeoffs, my

recommendation. Then I invite async feedback before any meeting.

Written discussion surfaces edge cases faster than verbal debate and

leaves a decision trail. If we're still split, I prefer a time-boxed

experiment over a long debate — let data decide.

👩 Sarah: Love it. Three technical rounds follow. Good luck!

💻 ROUND 2 — PAYMENTS CORE (55 min, English)

Setting: Staff Engineer, Payments Core team.

PART A — Idempotency Keys (20 min)

👨‍💻 Alex (Staff Eng): Stripe's API requires clients to pass an

Idempotency-Key header so that retrying a failed request doesn't

double-charge. Walk me through how you'd implement this server-side.

👨 Khanh: Three-layer approach: Redis for in-flight dedup, PostgreSQL

for durable result storage, and a middleware interceptor.

// --- Domain model ---
@Entity
@Table(name = "idempotency_keys")
public class IdempotencyRecord {
    @Id private String key;            // client-supplied UUID
    private String requestFingerprint; // SHA-256(method + path + body)
    private String responseBody;       // serialised JSON
    private int    httpStatus;
    private IdempotencyStatus status;  // IN_FLIGHT | COMPLETED | FAILED
    private Instant createdAt;
    private Instant expiresAt;         // 24h per Stripe spec
}

// --- Interceptor (runs before every mutating endpoint) ---
@Component
public class IdempotencyInterceptor implements HandlerInterceptor {

    private static final String LOCK_PREFIX = "idempotency:lock:";
    private static final Duration LOCK_TTL  = Duration.ofSeconds(30);

    @Override
    public boolean preHandle(HttpServletRequest req,
                             HttpServletResponse res,
                             Object handler) throws Exception {
        String idempKey = req.getHeader("Idempotency-Key");
        if (idempKey == null) return true; // GET/DELETE — skip

        String fingerprint = fingerprintOf(req);

        // 1. Check durable store first
        Optional<IdempotencyRecord> existing =
            idempotencyRepo.findById(idempKey);

        if (existing.isPresent()) {
            IdempotencyRecord rec = existing.get();

            // Fingerprint mismatch → same key, different request body
            if (!rec.getRequestFingerprint().equals(fingerprint)) {
                res.sendError(422,
                    "Idempotency-Key reused with different request body");
                return false;
            }

            if (rec.getStatus() == COMPLETED) {
                // Return cached response — no re-processing
                res.setStatus(rec.getHttpStatus());
                res.getWriter().write(rec.getResponseBody());
                return false; // short-circuit
            }

            if (rec.getStatus() == IN_FLIGHT) {
                // Another thread is processing — ask client to retry
                res.sendError(409,
                    "A request with this Idempotency-Key is already in flight");
                return false;
            }
        }

        // 2. Acquire distributed lock (prevents concurrent duplicate)
        boolean locked = redisTemplate.opsForValue()
            .setIfAbsent(LOCK_PREFIX + idempKey, "1", LOCK_TTL);

        if (!locked) {
            res.sendError(409, "Concurrent request with same Idempotency-Key");
            return false;
        }

        // 3. Insert IN_FLIGHT record
        idempotencyRepo.save(IdempotencyRecord.builder()
            .key(idempKey)
            .requestFingerprint(fingerprint)
            .status(IN_FLIGHT)
            .createdAt(Instant.now())
            .expiresAt(Instant.now().plus(Duration.ofHours(24)))
            .build());

        req.setAttribute("idempotency_key", idempKey);
        return true; // proceed to controller
    }

    private String fingerprintOf(HttpServletRequest req) throws Exception {
        String raw = req.getMethod() + req.getRequestURI()
                   + IOUtils.toString(req.getReader());
        return Hashing.sha256().hashString(raw, UTF_8).toString();
    }
}

// --- After controller returns, mark COMPLETED ---
@Component
public class IdempotencyResponseAdvice
    implements ResponseBodyAdvice<Object> {

    @Override
    public Object beforeBodyWrite(Object body, ...,
            ServerHttpRequest req, ServerHttpResponse res) {
        String key = /* retrieve from request attribute */ ...;
        if (key != null) {
            idempotencyRepo.markCompleted(key,
                serializeToJson(body), res.getHeaders().getStatusCode().value());
            redisTemplate.delete("idempotency:lock:" + key);
        }
        return body;
    }
}

Key decisions:

  • Redis lock prevents two simultaneous requests with the same key

from both entering the charge flow — only one wins.

  • Fingerprint check catches client bugs (same key, different amount).
  • IN_FLIGHT state handles crash-recovery: if the server dies mid-flight,

the record stays IN_FLIGHT. A sweep job can unlock it after TTL.

  • 24h expiry matches Stripe's public spec.

👨‍💻 Alex: What happens if the server crashes after debiting the

card but before writing COMPLETED? The client retries — what do you do?

👨 Khanh: This is the core challenge. The charge already happened

at the payment processor level. On retry:

1. Idempotency record is still IN_FLIGHT (crash = no COMPLETED write)
2. Lock has expired (30s TTL)
3. Retry request acquires lock, sees IN_FLIGHT

Strategy:
