  - Check the downstream charge table by (idempotency_key) index.
  - If a charge row exists → processor already charged → reconstruct
    response from charge row → mark COMPLETED → return cached response.
  - If no charge row → safe to retry the charge attempt.
// In charge service, always write charge row FIRST (with idempotency_key)
// before calling payment processor. Use outbox pattern.

@Transactional
public ChargeResult initiateCharge(ChargeRequest req, String idempKey) {
    // 1. Write PENDING charge to DB atomically with business transaction
    Charge charge = chargeRepo.save(Charge.builder()
        .id(UUID.randomUUID().toString())
        .idempotencyKey(idempKey)
        .amount(req.getAmount())
        .currency(req.getCurrency())
        .status(ChargeStatus.PENDING)
        .build());

    // 2. Publish to outbox (processed by Kafka relay)
    outboxRepo.save(OutboxEvent.of("charge.initiate", charge.getId()));

    return ChargeResult.pending(charge.getId());
    // Stripe/processor called asynchronously — never in this transaction
}

Now crash safety is guaranteed: DB transaction is atomic, outbox

relay retries until processor confirms. Idempotency key lookup

always finds the charge row on retry.

PART B — Webhook Delivery (20 min)

👨‍💻 Alex: Stripe delivers webhooks to merchant endpoints. A merchant

endpoint can be slow, return 5xx, or time out. Design the delivery

system with at-least-once semantics and exponential backoff.

👨 Khanh: Event-driven delivery pipeline with a retry scheduler.

@Entity
public class WebhookDelivery {
    @Id private String id;
    private String   eventId;
    private String   merchantEndpointUrl;
    private String   payload;           // JSON event body
    private int      attemptCount;      // starts at 0
    private Instant  nextAttemptAt;
    private DeliveryStatus status;      // PENDING | SUCCEEDED | FAILED | EXHAUSTED
}

@Service
public class WebhookScheduler {

    // Exponential backoff: 5s, 30s, 5m, 30m, 2h, 5h, 10h (max 7 retries)
    private static final long[] BACKOFF_SECONDS =
        {5, 30, 300, 1800, 7200, 18000, 36000};

    @Scheduled(fixedDelay = 5000)
    @Transactional
    public void dispatchDue() {
        List<WebhookDelivery> due = deliveryRepo
            .findDueForDelivery(Instant.now(), 100); // batch of 100

        for (WebhookDelivery d : due) {
            dispatch(d);
        }
    }

    private void dispatch(WebhookDelivery delivery) {
        try {
            HttpResponse<String> response = httpClient.send(
                HttpRequest.newBuilder()
                    .uri(URI.create(delivery.getMerchantEndpointUrl()))
                    .POST(HttpRequest.BodyPublishers.ofString(delivery.getPayload()))
                    .header("Stripe-Signature", sign(delivery.getPayload()))
                    .timeout(Duration.ofSeconds(30))
                    .build(),
                HttpResponse.BodyHandlers.ofString()
            );

            if (response.statusCode() >= 200 && response.statusCode() < 300) {
                delivery.setStatus(SUCCEEDED);
            } else {
                scheduleRetry(delivery);
            }

        } catch (IOException | InterruptedException e) {
            scheduleRetry(delivery);
        }

        deliveryRepo.save(delivery);
    }

    private void scheduleRetry(WebhookDelivery d) {
        int attempt = d.getAttemptCount();
        if (attempt >= BACKOFF_SECONDS.length) {
            d.setStatus(EXHAUSTED);
            alertMerchantDashboard(d);
            return;
        }
        d.setAttemptCount(attempt + 1);
        d.setNextAttemptAt(Instant.now().plusSeconds(BACKOFF_SECONDS[attempt]));
        d.setStatus(PENDING);
    }
}

Merchant-side idempotency: Each event has a unique event_id.

Merchants must deduplicate by event_id — Stripe's contract is

at-least-once, not exactly-once.

Signature (Stripe-Signature):

private String sign(String payload) {
    long ts = Instant.now().getEpochSecond();
    String signed = ts + "." + payload;
    String hmac = computeHmacSha256(webhookSecret, signed);
    return "t=" + ts + ",v1=" + hmac;
}

Merchant verifies timestamp is within 5 minutes to prevent replay attacks.

PART C — CODING: Charge Rate Limiter (15 min)

👨‍💻 Alex: *"Implement a per-merchant rate limiter: max 100 charges

per second per merchant. Use a token bucket algorithm. Thread-safe."*

👨 Khanh:

public class TokenBucketRateLimiter {

    private final int    capacity;       // max tokens (burst limit)
    private final double refillRatePerMs; // tokens added per millisecond

    private final ConcurrentHashMap<String, BucketState> buckets =
        new ConcurrentHashMap<>();

    public TokenBucketRateLimiter(int requestsPerSecond) {
        this.capacity       = requestsPerSecond;
        this.refillRatePerMs = requestsPerSecond / 1000.0;
    }

    public boolean allowRequest(String merchantId) {
        BucketState bucket = buckets.computeIfAbsent(merchantId,
            k -> new BucketState(capacity, System.currentTimeMillis()));

        synchronized (bucket) {
            long now      = System.currentTimeMillis();
            long elapsedMs = now - bucket.lastRefillTime;

            // Refill tokens based on elapsed time
            double newTokens = elapsedMs * refillRatePerMs;
            bucket.tokens    = Math.min(capacity, bucket.tokens + newTokens);
            bucket.lastRefillTime = now;

            if (bucket.tokens >= 1.0) {
                bucket.tokens -= 1.0;
                return true;   // request allowed
            }
            return false;      // throttled
        }
    }

    private static class BucketState {
        double tokens;
        long   lastRefillTime;

        BucketState(double initialTokens, long now) {
            this.tokens        = initialTokens;
            this.lastRefillTime = now;
        }
    }
}

// Tests
@Test
void allowsUpToLimit() {
    var limiter = new TokenBucketRateLimiter(100);
    for (int i = 0; i < 100; i++) {
        assertThat(limiter.allowRequest("merchant_A")).isTrue();
    }
    assertThat(limiter.allowRequest("merchant_A")).isFalse(); // 101st denied
}

@Test
void refillsOverTime() throws InterruptedException {
    var limiter = new TokenBucketRateLimiter(100);
    for (int i = 0; i < 100; i++) limiter.allowRequest("merchant_B");
    // Bucket empty — wait 10ms → ~1 token refilled
    Thread.sleep(10);
    assertThat(limiter.allowRequest("merchant_B")).isTrue();
}

@Test
void isolatesMerchants() {
    var limiter = new TokenBucketRateLimiter(100);
    for (int i = 0; i < 100; i++) limiter.allowRequest("merchant_C");
    // merchant_D is untouched — full bucket
    assertThat(limiter.allowRequest("merchant_D")).isTrue();
}

💻 ROUND 3 — SYSTEM DESIGN (45 min, English)

Setting: Principal Engineer, Platform.

👨‍💻 Maya (Principal Eng): Design Stripe's charge status notification

system. When a charge transitions (pending → succeeded, pending → failed),

merchants need to know in near-real-time. They have registered webhook

endpoints, but also poll our API. Scale: 500K charge events/minute.

👨 Khanh: Three-layer architecture: event capture → fan-out → delivery.

[Charge Service]
      │ publishes domain event to Kafka topic: charge.events
      ▼
[Event Processor] (Kafka consumer group)
      │
      ├─→ [Webhook Dispatcher] → merchant HTTPS endpoint (async, retry)
      │
      ├─→ [SSE/Polling Cache] → Redis — stores last N events per merchant
      │                          (merchant polls GET /v1/events?after=...)
      │
      └─→ [Analytics Sink] → BigQuery / data warehouse

Kafka topic design:

Topic: charge.events
Partitions: 100
Partition key: merchant_id
  → all events for merchant_X go to same partition
  → preserves ordering per merchant
Retention: 7 days (replay window)

Webhook fan-out:

@KafkaListener(topics = "charge.events", groupId = "webhook-fanout")
public void onChargeEvent(ChargeEvent event) {
    List<WebhookEndpoint> endpoints =
        endpointRepo.findActiveByMerchantId(event.getMerchantId());

    for (WebhookEndpoint ep : endpoints) {
        // Insert delivery record — scheduler picks it up
        deliveryRepo.save(WebhookDelivery.builder()
            .eventId(event.getId())
            .merchantEndpointUrl(ep.getUrl())
            .payload(serialize(event))
            .status(PENDING)
            .nextAttemptAt(Instant.now())
            .build());
    }
}

Polling (GET /v1/events):

Redis sorted set per merchant:
  Key:   events:{merchantId}
  Score: event_unix_timestamp
  Value: serialized event JSON

Query: ZRANGEBYSCORE events:{merchantId} {after_ts} +inf LIMIT 100

TTL: 72h (events expire from Redis, merchant must use webhooks for older)

Handling 500K events/minute:

  • 100 Kafka partitions → 100 parallel consumer threads
  • Webhook delivery is async (DB-backed queue) → fan-out doesn't block Kafka consumer
  • Redis sorted set reads are O(log N) — scales to millions of stored events
  • Webhook dispatcher horizontally scalable — add pods, same Postgres queue

Failure modes:

FailureMitigation
Kafka consumer lagAlert at 30s lag; scale consumer group
Merchant endpoint downExponential backoff, 7 retries, then EXHAUSTED
Redis evictionEvents still in Kafka (7d) + Postgres; Redis is cache only
Duplicate deliveryMerchant deduplicates on event_id

💻 ROUND 4 — BEHAVIOURAL (30 min, English)

Setting: Engineering Manager.

👩 Priya (EM): Tell me about a time you had to make a high-stakes

architectural decision under time pressure.

👨 Khanh: (STAR)

Situation: At my previous company, we had a payment processing

service that used synchronous HTTP calls to our banking partner for

every transaction. During a promotion event, traffic spiked 10x and

our banking partner's API started timing out at 8 seconds — our own

API timeout was 5 seconds, causing cascading failures. We had 48

hours before a major campaign.

Task: I needed to redesign the integration to be resilient to

partner slowness without a full rewrite.

Action: I proposed an async outbox pattern: write the payment

intent to our DB within the user's transaction, publish to Kafka,

and have a separate consumer call the banking API. The user gets an

immediate "processing" response. I wrote a design doc in 3 hours,

got async approval from 2 senior engineers, and we implemented it

in a feature branch over 36 hours with two other engineers. We added

a status-polling endpoint for the frontend so users could see

real-time updates.

Result: Campaign launched successfully. Throughput increased 8x.

Banking partner slowness became invisible to users. We later

generalised the outbox pattern to all third-party integrations.

👩 Priya: How do you keep quality high when moving fast?

👨 Khanh: Three things: mandatory integration tests for every

new happy path and top-2 failure paths — written before the PR is

reviewed, not after. Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) for any

decision that's hard to reverse. And blameless postmortems —

I write them even for near-misses, not just outages. Speed

without quality debt is a myth; these three habits keep the debt

manageable.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

RoundCategoryScoreNotes
HRCulture + written communication10/10GDP of the internet framing; data-over-debate
IdempotencyKey design + crash safety10/10Redis lock + fingerprint + outbox crash recovery
WebhooksAt-least-once delivery + backoff10/10Exponential backoff, HMAC signature, replay safety
CodingToken bucket rate limiter10/10Thread-safe, lazy refill, 3 edge case tests
System DesignCharge notification at 500K/min10/10Kafka fan-out, Redis polling cache, failure table
BehaviouralHigh-stakes decision + quality habits10/10STAR with outbox, ADRs, postmortems

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 Stripe Interview Tips

  1. Idempotency is Stripe's religion — know: key + fingerprint + IN_FLIGHT state + crash recovery via outbox
  2. Webhook at-least-once — merchant must deduplicate on event_id; Stripe guarantees delivery, not exactly-once
  3. HMAC-SHA256 signatures — timestamp + payload, verify within 5 minutes to block replay attacks
  4. Token bucket > fixed window — fixed window allows 2x burst at boundary; token bucket is smooth
  5. Outbox pattern — write intent to DB atomically, relay to external system via Kafka; crash-safe
  6. Kafka partition key = merchant_id — preserves per-merchant event ordering at scale
  7. Stripe values written communication — every design decision should be a short doc, not a verbal chat
  8. Salary range (Remote/APAC): USD 180K–250K base + equity; highly competitive even outside SF

🎭 Mock Interview #37 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: Atlassian (Sydney / Remote-first, Jira & Confluence platform)

Role: Senior Software Engineer — Jira Platform team.

This mock focuses heavily on **English communication, articulation,

and behavioural depth**. Technical questions are present but the

scoring emphasis is on how you explain, not just what you know.

Atlassian is known for: remote-first culture, written-first

communication, and a values-driven interview process ("TEAM Anywhere").

📞 ROUND 1 — RECRUITER SCREEN (20 min, English)

👩 Emma (Recruiter): Hi Khanh! Thanks for taking the time today.

Before we dive in, I'd love to hear — in your own words, not from

your CV — what has your engineering journey looked like so far?

👨 Khanh: Sure! So I started my career building backend services

for a logistics company — fairly standard CRUD work, Spring Boot,

MySQL. But the turning point for me was when I joined a fintech

startup. Suddenly, correctness wasn't just a nice-to-have. If a

wallet balance was wrong by even one cent, someone's money was

missing. That shifted how I think about software entirely.

From there, I got really invested in things like distributed

transactions, idempotency, and observability — not because they're

fashionable, but because I'd seen what happens without them.

Most recently I've been working on high-throughput payment systems

and I've started leading design reviews for my team's critical

services.

So in short: I grew from writing code that works, to writing code

that I can defend at 2am when something breaks in production.

👩 Emma: I love that framing — "code you can defend at 2am."

Atlassian is very much a remote-first company. How do you

communicate with your team when you're not in the same room?

👨 Khanh: Honestly, going remote made me a better communicator

because I couldn't rely on tapping someone on the shoulder anymore.

My main habits are: I default to writing over talking. If I'm

making a non-trivial decision, I write a short doc — context,

options, recommendation — and share it async before scheduling

any meeting. That way, people can engage on their own time and

the discussion is already productive by the time we meet.

For day-to-day work, I'm very deliberate about status visibility.

I update tickets with short, factual notes — not to prove I'm

working, but so teammates in different time zones aren't blocked

waiting for me to wake up. And I try to over-communicate blockers

early rather than struggling quietly.

I'll be honest — I still sometimes default to a quick verbal chat

when async feels slower. But I've learned that written context

almost always saves more time later than it costs upfront.

👩 Emma: That's a very self-aware answer. One more: why Atlassian

specifically, rather than another big tech company?

👨 Khanh: A few honest reasons.

First, Atlassian's products are in the workflow itself — Jira and

Confluence are the tools engineers use to build everything else.

Working on platform infrastructure that other engineers depend on

every day is a different kind of responsibility, and I find that

genuinely motivating.

Second, I've read about the TEAM Anywhere model and the emphasis

on written communication. That aligns with how I already work.

I'm not looking for a company I have to adapt to — I'm looking

for one where my habits are an asset, not a liability.

And third — the engineering challenges. Jira serves millions of

projects. Issues like multi-tenancy, search at scale, and

notification fan-out are exactly the class of problems I want

to work on next.

👩 Emma: Perfect. I'll pass you through to the technical rounds.

You'll have two: a values interview and a system discussion.

Good luck, Khanh!

💬 ROUND 2 — VALUES & BEHAVIOURAL (45 min, English)

Setting: Senior Engineer + Engineering Manager panel.

Note: This round is 100% communication. No code. Answers are

scored on: clarity, structure (STAR), honesty, and self-awareness.

SECTION A — Handling Complexity

👨‍💻 James (Sr Eng): Tell me about the most technically complex

problem you've solved. I'm less interested in the technology and

more interested in how you thought through it.

👨 Khanh: Happy to. The problem was a distributed payment

idempotency bug — one of those bugs that only appeared under

specific race conditions and was completely invisible in testing.

Here's how I thought through it.

First, I resisted the urge to guess. The symptom was occasional

duplicate charges — maybe one in ten thousand transactions. My

instinct was to jump to the obvious cause: a retry without an

idempotency check. But I'd already seen that check in the code,

so I knew it wasn't that simple.

**I started by writing down what I knew for certain versus what

I was assuming.** This sounds basic, but it immediately surfaced

a gap: I was assuming our Redis lock was always acquired before

the DB write. But what if Redis was briefly unavailable and we

silently fell through? I traced the code path and found exactly

that — a fallback that bypassed the lock under network partition.

**Then I framed the fix as a tradeoff conversation, not a

unilateral decision.** I wrote a doc: here's the bug, here are

three options — fail-closed, fail-open, or circuit breaker — with

the implications of each. We discussed it async, chose fail-closed

with an alert, and shipped it behind a feature flag.

The key lesson for me: complex problems usually have a simple root

cause, but you only find it by being disciplined about what you

know versus what you're assuming.

👨‍💻 James: Good. How did you explain the bug and the fix to

non-technical stakeholders — product managers, for example?

👨 Khanh: I try to lead with the user impact, not the mechanism.

So instead of saying "our Redis distributed lock had a silent

fallback under network partition," I said: "Occasionally, a very

small number of customers were charged twice when they retried

a failed payment. Here's what it looks like to them, here's how

often it was happening, and here's what we're doing to prevent it."

I find that once stakeholders understand the impact, they're

genuinely curious about the cause — and then the technical

explanation lands much better because they're invested in it.

I also make sure to separate "what happened" from "what we're

doing about it" — those are two different conversations, and

mixing them creates confusion. Stakeholders usually care more

about the second.

SECTION B — Disagreement & Influence

👩 Priya (EM): Tell me about a time you disagreed with a

technical decision made by a more senior engineer. What did

you do?

👨 Khanh: Yes, this happened about a year ago.

Situation: A senior architect proposed migrating our main

service database from PostgreSQL to a NoSQL solution — the

stated reason was scalability. I had real concerns about this.

Our data had complex relational integrity requirements, and

the team's NoSQL experience was limited.

My reaction: I didn't raise it in the meeting. I knew

disagreeing publicly without preparation would come across

as obstructive. Instead, I said: "I'd like to think about

this more before we commit — can I send some notes by

end of day?"

What I did: I wrote a two-page doc. Not "why NoSQL is

wrong" — that framing would be defensive and unhelpful.

Instead: "Here are the assumptions behind this proposal.

Here are the scenarios where it works well. Here are the

scenarios where I think we'd have problems — specifically

around our multi-step transaction flows and our team's

current skill set. And here's an alternative I'd like us

to consider."

Outcome: The architect read it, agreed with two of my

three concerns, and we ended up going with a PostgreSQL

sharding approach instead. The decision took two more weeks,

but the team was aligned and confident. No one felt overruled.

What I learned: The goal of disagreement isn't to win —

it's to get to the best decision. Writing things down forces

you to stress-test your own argument first. If you can't

write it clearly, you probably don't understand it clearly.

👩 Priya: What if the senior engineer had read your doc

and still disagreed? What then?

👨 Khanh: Honestly — it depends on the stakes.

If it's a reversible decision, I'd disagree and commit.

I'd say explicitly: "I still have reservations about X,

but I understand we're moving forward. Here's what I'd like

us to agree on as early warning signs that this is going

wrong, so we have a clear trigger to revisit." That keeps

the disagreement on record without being obstructive.

If it's truly irreversible — architectural choices that

would be very expensive to undo — I'd escalate once,

clearly and calmly. Not to my manager's manager as a power

move, but to bring in another technical perspective. And

I'd be explicit about why: "I want a third opinion because

I may be wrong, and this decision is hard to reverse."

What I wouldn't do is silently comply and then say "I told

you so" if things go wrong. That's the worst outcome for

the team.

SECTION C — Growth & Self-Awareness

👩 Priya: What's a skill you've actively worked on improving

in the last year that isn't a technical skill?

👨 Khanh: Running effective meetings — specifically, async-first

communication.

For most of my career, my default was to schedule a meeting

when I needed input on something. It felt faster. But I noticed

that after the meeting, people often needed more time to think

anyway — and those who were introverted or in different time

zones were structurally disadvantaged.

So I started doing a simple thing: before requesting a meeting,

I ask myself, "Can I send a doc and collect written feedback

instead?" If yes, I do that. If a meeting is still needed,

the doc becomes the pre-read, and the meeting becomes a

decision session rather than an information session.

It's made my work more inclusive and, counterintuitively, faster.

Decisions that used to take three back-and-forth meetings now

often take one async thread and one short call.

I'm still improving at it — I sometimes slip back into meeting-first

habits when I'm stressed or moving fast. But I'm much more

conscious of the cost of synchronous time than I used to be.

👨‍💻 James: Last question. At Atlassian, engineers are expected

to give and receive candid feedback. How do you give feedback

to a peer whose code quality has been slipping?

👨 Khanh: I try to be direct, but I always lead with curiosity

rather than judgment — because slipping quality almost always

has a reason, and I want to understand it before I react to it.

So my first move is a private, informal conversation. Something

like: "Hey, I've noticed a few things in your recent PRs that

I'd like to talk through. Is now a good time?" — not over Slack

message, because tone is too easy to misread.

In the conversation, I'm specific. Not "your code quality has

slipped" — that's vague and hard to act on. Instead: "In the

last three PRs I've seen missing error handling in the async

paths and a few test cases skipped. I wanted to flag it early

because I know you usually catch these things."

Then I listen. Maybe they're overloaded. Maybe there's a context

I'm missing. Maybe they disagree with my assessment. Any of those

is possible and important to hear.

If it continues after the conversation, then it becomes a

manager conversation — but with a clear record of what I

observed and when I raised it, so it's not a surprise.

I don't think feedback is a comfortable thing. But done privately,

specifically, and early, it's much more useful than a PR comment

that feels like a public correction.

💻 ROUND 3 — LIGHT TECHNICAL DISCUSSION (30 min, English)

Setting: Principal Engineer. No whiteboard, no code — discussion only.

Note: This round tests how you reason and communicate about

technical topics, not whether you can produce perfect code on demand.

👨‍💻 David (Principal Eng): Jira has a notification system — when

an issue is updated, watchers get notified by email, Slack, and

in-app. We have tens of millions of issues updated daily. Talk me

through how you'd think about designing this.

👨 Khanh: Sure. I'll think out loud and you can steer me if I

go too deep or too shallow anywhere.

The first thing I'd clarify: what's the latency expectation?

In-app notifications probably need to be near-real-time — within

seconds. Email can tolerate more delay — a minute is fine. Slack

sits somewhere in between.

That difference actually suggests separate delivery pipelines,

because they have different throughput and failure characteristics.

The core flow I'd propose:

An issue update emits a domain event — say, IssueUpdatedEvent

— onto a Kafka topic, partitioned by project ID to preserve

ordering within a project. A fan-out service reads from Kafka,

looks up the watchers for that issue, and routes a delivery

task per-channel per-user into three separate queues: email,

Slack, and in-app.

Each queue has its own consumer with its own retry logic. Email

retries are slow and expensive — maybe 3 retries over 10 minutes.

Slack has a rate limit API — we'd respect that. In-app can be

delivered via WebSocket or long-polling.

Things I'd want to talk through with the team:

  • User notification preferences. If someone has disabled email

for Jira, we shouldn't even enqueue an email delivery.

That filtering should happen early — ideally at fan-out time —

to avoid wasted work downstream.

  • Deduplication. If the same issue is updated five times in

ten seconds, should watchers get five emails? Probably not.

A short debounce window — say, 30 seconds — would batch rapid

updates into one digest.

  • At-least-once vs exactly-once. Email at-least-once is fine;

getting two emails for one update is annoying but not harmful.

Billing notifications would be a different story.

Where I'd start: The fan-out service and the in-app delivery path,

because those are the highest visibility and the ones where

latency matters most. Email can be added later with less risk.

👨‍💻 David: You mentioned debouncing rapid updates. How would

you implement that without losing any notification?

👨 Khanh: Good question — the key is that debouncing at the

notification level doesn't mean losing events at the data level.

I'd store every IssueUpdatedEvent in Kafka with full retention.

The debounce only applies to outgoing notifications. Practically:

when the fan-out service sees an event, instead of immediately

enqueuing a delivery, it writes a pending notification record

with a send_after timestamp of "now + 30 seconds." If a second

event for the same issue arrives within that window, it updates

the record — extends the timestamp, maybe appends the change —

rather than creating a new delivery.

A scheduler then picks up records whose send_after has passed

and actually enqueues them.

The tradeoff: you're adding 30 seconds of latency to the

notification. For most issue updates, that's completely fine.

For high-priority things like mentions or blocking status changes,

you'd want to bypass the debounce — which means the event needs

a priority flag, and the fan-out logic needs to branch on it.

That's a more complex design, so I'd want to validate the

30-second debounce solves 90% of the spam problem first

before adding priority routing.

👨‍💻 David: Nice. Last one — how do you decide when not

to build something yourself and to use an off-the-shelf solution?

👨 Khanh: My default is: use the off-the-shelf solution and

be intentional about when you override it.

The bar I set for building custom is: does the existing solution

have a fundamental mismatch with our requirements — not just

an inconvenient limitation, but something that would cause

correctness or reliability problems at our scale? Or does

building custom give us a meaningful competitive advantage?

For something like email delivery, I'd use SendGrid or SES

without hesitation. The failure modes are well-understood,

the SDKs are mature, and the alternative — managing our own

SMTP infrastructure — would be months of work that doesn't

differentiate us.

But for something like our idempotency system or our

transaction state machine — those encode business rules

that are core to what we do. Off-the-shelf solutions would

require us to adapt our business logic to fit their model,

which is backwards. That's where custom code is worth it.

The danger I try to avoid is "not invented here" syndrome —

building something custom because it's more interesting,

not because it's more appropriate. I've seen teams spend

months building a message queue when Kafka would have been

fine. I try to name that impulse when I see it, including

in myself.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

RoundCategoryScoreNotes
RecruiterSelf-introduction + remote work mindset10/10"Code I can defend at 2am" — memorable, authentic
Values AComplexity + stakeholder communication10/10Know vs assume framing; impact-first explanation
Values BDisagreement + influence without authority10/10Written doc approach; disagree-and-commit protocol
Values CGrowth mindset + peer feedback10/10Async-first habit; specific, private, early feedback model
TechnicalNotification system reasoning10/10Fan-out design; debounce tradeoff; build-vs-buy judgment

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 Key Communication Patterns to Practise

1. The "Know vs Assume" Frame

*"I started by writing down what I knew for certain versus

what I was assuming."*

Use this when describing how you debug or investigate.

It signals rigour and intellectual honesty.

2. Impact-First Explanation

*"Instead of explaining the technical mechanism first,

I led with what it meant for the user."*

Always translate technical problems into user or business impact

before going into the mechanism.

3. Disagree in Writing, Not in the Room

"I didn't raise it in the meeting — I sent a doc by end of day."

This is Atlassian's written-first culture in practice.

Positions you as someone who separates emotion from analysis.

4. Disagree-and-Commit Protocol

*"I'd say: I still have reservations, but here are the early

warning signs I'd want us to watch for."*

This is the most professional version of losing an argument —

you leave a record without being obstructive.

5. Build-vs-Buy Framing

*"My default is off-the-shelf. The bar for custom is fundamental

mismatch or meaningful competitive advantage."*

This signals senior engineering judgment — knowing when NOT to

build is as important as knowing how.

6. Feedback Delivery Formula

Private → Specific → Curious → Documented

Never vague, never public, always give the person a chance

to explain before escalating.

💰 Atlassian Salary Reference (Vietnam / Remote)

  • Senior SWE (L5): USD 80–120K base + equity (RSU)
  • Hiring model: Remote-first, timezone-flexible
  • Interview language: English only — all rounds

📝 Phrases Worth Memorising

SituationPhrase
Opening self-intro"I grew from writing code that works, to writing code I can defend at 2am."
Explaining bugs to PM"Let me start with what this meant for the user, then I'll explain why it happened."
Disagreeing"I'd like to send some notes before we commit — can I share a doc by end of day?"
Losing an argument"I still have a reservation about X, but I'm committed to making this work. Can we agree on a signal to revisit it?"
Build vs buy"My default is to use the existing solution. The bar for building custom is fundamental mismatch, not inconvenience."
Receiving critique"That's a fair point. Let me think about that — I might be anchored on my initial approach."

🎭 Mock Interview #38 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: Shopify (Ottawa / Remote-first, Global Commerce Platform)

Role: Senior Backend Engineer — Checkout & Orders Platform.

Shopify powers 10% of US e-commerce. Flash sales (Black Friday,

product drops) drive extreme, unpredictable traffic bursts.

Tech: Java 17, Spring Boot, MySQL (Vitess), Redis, Kafka, gRPC.

Balance: 50% English communication · 50% technical depth.

📞 ROUND 1 — RECRUITER SCREEN (20 min, English)

👩 Lena (Recruiter): Hi Khanh! Great to meet you. Let's skip

the formalities — tell me something about your engineering

background that isn't on your CV.

👨 Khanh: Ha — I like that question. Something not on the CV...

Honestly, the thing that shaped me most as an engineer is a failure.

Early in my career I shipped a payment feature that had a race

condition under concurrent load. It passed all our tests. It passed

code review. It went to production and worked fine for two weeks.

Then during a promotional campaign, we had a 10x traffic spike

and the bug surfaced — some users were double-charged. The

financial impact was small, but the trust impact wasn't.

I stayed up all night writing the postmortem — not because my

manager asked me to, but because I needed to understand exactly

why our process didn't catch it. That night changed how I think

about concurrency, testing, and system design. I became somewhat

obsessed with "what would have to be true for this to fail?" as

a design question.

That's not on my CV, but it's probably the most important thing

that happened in my career.

👩 Lena: That's a great answer — and very honest. Shopify's

Black Friday handles millions of checkouts per hour. Why do

you want to work on that kind of scale?

👨 Khanh: Because scale forces honesty about your architecture.

You can hide a lot of bad decisions behind low traffic. Cache

invalidation bugs, lock contention, N+1 queries — they're

invisible until you multiply them by a million. When your

system has to handle Black Friday, you can't paper over those

things. You have to actually solve them.

I find that genuinely exciting, not stressful. Problems that are

hard to reproduce in staging but obvious in production are the

most interesting engineering challenges there are. Shopify is

one of the few companies in the world where you can work on

that class of problem every day.

👩 Lena: Love it. Two more rounds — technical and leadership.

Good luck!

💻 ROUND 2 — TECHNICAL (55 min, English)

Setting: Staff Engineer, Checkout Platform.

PART A — Inventory Oversell Prevention (20 min)

👨‍💻 Tom (Staff Eng): Black Friday. A limited-edition sneaker

drops. 50,000 people hit "Add to Cart" simultaneously for

500 units. Walk me through how you'd prevent overselling.

👨 Khanh: Classic inventory problem — I've seen variants of

this in flash sale systems. There are three layers of defence.

Layer 1 — Redis atomic decrement (fast path):

@Service
public class InventoryService {

    private static final String STOCK_KEY = "stock:%s"; // variantId

    // Returns false if stock is exhausted
    public boolean reserve(String variantId, int qty) {
        String key = String.format(STOCK_KEY, variantId);

        // Lua script: atomic check-and-decrement
        String script = """
            local stock = tonumber(redis.call('GET', KEYS[1]))
            if stock == nil or stock < tonumber(ARGV[1]) then
                return 0
            end
            redis.call('DECRBY', KEYS[1], ARGV[1])
            return 1
            """;

        Long result = redisTemplate.execute(
            new DefaultRedisScript<>(script, Long.class),
            List.of(key),
            String.valueOf(qty)
        );

        return Long.valueOf(1L).equals(result);
    }
}

Layer 2 — Pessimistic DB lock (source of truth):

@Transactional
public Order confirmCheckout(String cartId, String userId) {
    Cart cart = cartRepo.findById(cartId).orElseThrow();

    // SELECT ... FOR UPDATE — blocks concurrent confirmations
    // for the same variant
    InventoryRow inv = inventoryRepo
        .findByVariantIdWithLock(cart.getVariantId());

    if (inv.getAvailable() < cart.getQuantity()) {
        // Redis was optimistic — DB is the final arbiter
        throw new OutOfStockException(cart.getVariantId());
    }

    inv.setAvailable(inv.getAvailable() - cart.getQuantity());
    inventoryRepo.save(inv);

    return orderRepo.save(Order.from(cart, userId));
}

Layer 3 — Soft reservation with TTL:

Cart added → Redis stock decremented (reservation held 15 min)
If user checks out → DB lock confirms, reservation consumed
If user abandons → TTL expires, Redis stock restored automatically
If payment fails → compensating event restores reservation

Why three layers?

Redis alone: fast but can lose data on crash.

DB lock alone: correct but can't handle 50,000 concurrent requests.

Together: Redis filters 99% of excess requests cheaply; DB handles

the small concurrent window that Redis allows through.

👨‍💻 Tom: Good. What happens if Redis crashes between the

reservation and the DB confirmation?

👨 Khanh: That's the gap I was about to mention.

If Redis crashes, two things can happen:

Scenario A — crash before DB confirm: The reservation in

Redis is lost. The DB hasn't been updated yet. The cart is

in an inconsistent "reserved but stock not decremented" state.

On recovery, we need a reconciliation job:

@Scheduled(fixedDelay = 60_000)
public void reconcilePendingCarts() {
    // Find carts in RESERVED state older than 5 min
    List<Cart> stale = cartRepo.findStaleReservations(
        Instant.now().minusSeconds(300)
    );
    for (Cart cart : stale) {
        // Re-check DB inventory — if still available, re-reserve in Redis
        // If not, mark cart EXPIRED and notify user
        inventoryReconciler.reconcile(cart);
    }
}

Scenario B — Redis crash after DB confirm: Order is committed.

Redis just lost the updated stock count. On Redis restart, we

reload stock from DB:

@EventListener(RedisReadyEvent.class)
public void onRedisReconnect() {
    List<Inventory> all = inventoryRepo.findAll();
    for (Inventory inv : all) {
        redisTemplate.opsForValue().set(
            "stock:" + inv.getVariantId(),
            String.valueOf(inv.getAvailable())
        );
    }
    log.info("Redis inventory cache warmed: {} variants", all.size());
}

Key principle: DB is always the source of truth.

Redis is a fast filter, never a store.

PART B — COMMUNICATION: Explaining a Production Incident (15 min)

👨‍💻 Tom: Imagine last Black Friday our checkout service had

a 4-minute outage at 10 AM. Walk me through how you'd run

the incident and then communicate it afterward.

👨 Khanh: There are two very different jobs here — managing

the incident live, and communicating after. I treat them

completely separately.

During the incident:

First, I'd establish an incident channel immediately —

not scattered DMs, one place. Second, I'd assign roles:

one person on comms, one on investigation, one on mitigation.

Everyone else stays out unless called. Noise in an incident

is dangerous.

My job in the first five minutes is to answer: what's the

blast radius? Is checkout fully down or partially degraded?

Are orders still being persisted even if the API is returning

errors? That determines urgency.

For mitigation, I work backwards from the simplest fix.

Before restarting services or rolling back, I check: did

anything deploy in the last hour? Did traffic spike

unusually? Is a dependency — Redis, MySQL — showing

elevated latency? Usually the cause is one of three:

a bad deploy, a traffic pattern we didn't handle, or a

downstream dependency degrading.

Once mitigated, I'd post a brief all-clear: "Checkout is

recovered as of 10:04 AM. Root cause under investigation.

Postmortem by 5 PM today."

After the incident — the postmortem:

I'd send it to the team by end of day, merchants the

following morning. The structure I always use:

1. Timeline — what happened, when (UTC timestamps)
2. Impact — how many users affected, estimated revenue impact
3. Root cause — the actual technical reason, one sentence
4. Why it wasn't caught before production — process gap
5. Action items — owner + due date for each, no orphans

The most important rule: postmortems are blameless.

The goal is to find the system gap, not the guilty person.

If a person made a mistake, the question isn't "why did

they do that" — it's "what condition made that mistake

easy to make, and how do we fix the condition?"

That's the document that actually changes how we work.

PART C — CODING: Flash Sale Queue (20 min)

👨‍💻 Tom: *"Implement a fair checkout queue for a flash sale.

Max 500 concurrent checkouts. Users beyond that get a queue

position. Thread-safe. No external dependencies."*

👨 Khanh:

public class CheckoutQueue {

    private final int maxConcurrent;
    private final AtomicInteger activeCheckouts = new AtomicInteger(0);
    private final AtomicInteger queuePosition   = new AtomicInteger(0);
    private final AtomicInteger nextServed      = new AtomicInteger(0);

    // userId → their queue ticket number
    private final ConcurrentHashMap<String, Integer> tickets =
        new ConcurrentHashMap<>();

    public CheckoutQueue(int maxConcurrent) {
        this.maxConcurrent = maxConcurrent;
    }

    public CheckoutEntry enter(String userId) {
        // Assign a ticket (FIFO fairness)
        int ticket = tickets.computeIfAbsent(userId,
            k -> queuePosition.getAndIncrement());

        if (activeCheckouts.get() < maxConcurrent
                && ticket == nextServed.get()) {
            // Slot available AND it's this user's turn
            activeCheckouts.incrementAndGet();
            nextServed.incrementAndGet();
            return CheckoutEntry.admitted(ticket);
        }

        // Queue position (1-based for display)
        int ahead = ticket - nextServed.get();
        return CheckoutEntry.queued(ticket, ahead + 1);
    }

    public void complete(String userId) {
        tickets.remove(userId);
        activeCheckouts.decrementAndGet();
    }

    public record CheckoutEntry(
        int ticket,
        boolean admitted,
        int queuePosition // 0 if admitted
    ) {
        static CheckoutEntry admitted(int ticket) {
            return new CheckoutEntry(ticket, true, 0);
        }
        static CheckoutEntry queued(int ticket, int position) {
            return new CheckoutEntry(ticket, false, position);
        }
    }
}

// Tests
@Test
void admitsUpToLimit() {
    var q = new CheckoutQueue(3);
    assertThat(q.enter("u1").admitted()).isTrue();
    assertThat(q.enter("u2").admitted()).isTrue();
    assertThat(q.enter("u3").admitted()).isTrue();
    assertThat(q.enter("u4").admitted()).isFalse(); // 4th queued
}

@Test
void releasingSlotAdmitsNext() {
    var q = new CheckoutQueue(1);
    q.enter("u1"); // admitted
    var u2 = q.enter("u2"); // queued
    assertThat(u2.admitted()).isFalse();

    q.complete("u1"); // release slot

    var u2retry = q.enter("u2");
    assertThat(u2retry.admitted()).isTrue();
}

@Test
void queuePositionIsAccurate() {
    var q = new CheckoutQueue(1);
    q.enter("u1"); // admitted
    var p2 = q.enter("u2");
    var p3 = q.enter("u3");
    assertThat(p2.queuePosition()).isEqualTo(1);
    assertThat(p3.queuePosition()).isEqualTo(2);
}

@Test
void idempotentEntry() {
    var q = new CheckoutQueue(2);
    var first  = q.enter("u1");
    var second = q.enter("u1"); // same user — same ticket
    assertThat(first.ticket()).isEqualTo(second.ticket());
}

💬 ROUND 3 — LEADERSHIP & CRAFT (35 min, English)

Setting: Engineering Manager + Principal Engineer panel.

SECTION A — Leading Without Title

👩 Rachel (EM): You're a senior engineer, not a manager.

Tell me about a time you drove a significant improvement

in your team that wasn't part of your job description.

👨 Khanh: About eighteen months ago, I noticed that our

team's deployment process had become a real source of anxiety.

Deploys happened at most twice a week, required three people

to be online, and had no automated rollback. Engineers were

nervous before every release.

That nervousness was a signal — it meant we didn't trust

our own process. And teams that don't trust their deploy

process ship less, move slower, and take fewer reasonable

risks.

I raised it in a retro, got a lukewarm response — it wasn't

a burning fire, just a slow drain. So I decided to just start

fixing it, one piece at a time.

I added automated smoke tests post-deploy. Then a one-command

rollback script. Then shifted from manual deploy checklists

to a GitHub Actions pipeline. Each piece took a day or two

of my own time, alongside my regular work.

Three months later, we were deploying daily. The conversation

in the team shifted from "are you sure it's safe to deploy?"

to "let's just ship it and watch the metrics."

The only moment it became a "project" was when I wrote it up

as a case study for the engineering blog. The work itself was

just small improvements, stacked deliberately.

👩 Rachel: What would you have done differently if the team

had actively resisted the changes?

👨 Khanh: That's an important question. Resistance usually

means one of two things: people don't see the problem the

same way I do, or they see a risk in the solution I don't.

If it was the first — I'd invest more in making the problem

visible. Not "deploys are bad" as an opinion, but data: here

are the deploy incidents we've had in the last six months,

here's the average time to recover, here's what other teams

with daily deploys look like. Data is harder to argue with

than feelings.

If it was the second — I'd genuinely want to understand the

concern. Maybe there's a class of change where daily deploys

really are risky for us and I hadn't thought it through.

In that case, the right answer is to design around the

concern, not steamroll it.

The one thing I wouldn't do is build the thing in isolation

and present it as a fait accompli. That builds resentment

even when the outcome is good. Buy-in matters more than

speed when you're changing how a team works.

SECTION B — On Technical Craft

👨‍💻 Daniel (Principal Eng): What does "good code" mean to

you? I'm not looking for a textbook answer.

👨 Khanh: My honest answer has changed over the years.

When I was junior, good code meant clever code — concise,

elegant, using every language feature available. I was proud

of one-liners and lambdas-in-lambdas.

Then I had to maintain my own "clever" code six months later

and couldn't understand it. That was humbling.

Now, good code to me means: the next engineer who reads this

— who might be me on a bad day, or a new teammate at midnight

during an incident — can understand what this does, why it

does it this way, and what would break if they changed it.

Practically, that means: method names that say why, not

just what. Error paths that are as readable as happy paths.

Comments that explain decisions, not descriptions. Tests that

serve as documentation. And honest complexity — if something

is inherently hard, don't hide it behind an abstraction that

lies about how simple it is.

The bar I use: would I be comfortable explaining every line

of this in a postmortem? If yes, it's good code.

👨‍💻 Daniel: How do you think about technical debt?

When do you pay it down versus live with it?

👨 Khanh: I think of technical debt in three categories,

because "technical debt" is overloaded to the point of

being useless as a term.

Category 1 — Deliberate shortcuts. We knew it was wrong

when we wrote it, did it anyway to meet a deadline, and left

a clear comment or ticket. This is fine. It's a conscious

tradeoff. Pay it down when the feature stabilises and you

have breathing room.

Category 2 — Discovered complexity. The system grew into

a shape we didn't anticipate. The abstraction that made sense

for three use cases breaks down at ten. This is the most

common type. Pay it down incrementally — don't wait for a

"refactoring sprint" that never comes. The rule I follow:

leave the code slightly better than you found it. Boy Scout

Rule. Not a rewrite, just a nudge.

Category 3 — Foundational rot. Core decisions that are

now actively causing production incidents, slowing every

feature, or creating security risk. This is the kind of debt

that needs to be treated like a project — scoped, prioritised,

and resourced properly. Living with it has a compounding cost.

The mistake I see most often is treating Category 1 like

Category 3 — panicking about a known shortcut and demanding

a rewrite when incremental improvement would do. The second

mistake is treating Category 3 like Category 1 — saying

"we'll deal with it later" when "later" has already arrived.

SECTION C — Communication Under Pressure

👩 Rachel: You're in the middle of a complex feature

implementation when your manager tells you a high-priority

bug just landed and needs you specifically. How do you respond?

👨 Khanh: First — I don't just drop what I'm doing.

Context switching has a real cost, and the cost isn't just

mine — it's the feature that loses momentum.

So my first question is: what's the actual urgency? "High

priority" can mean "production is down right now" or it can

mean "a customer complained and a VP noticed." Those require

very different responses.

If it's a live production issue, I hand off clearly. I'd

spend five minutes writing a brief note in the feature branch:

where I am, what's been tested, what the next step is.

Then I switch. The five minutes costs almost nothing

compared to the hours it saves the next person who picks it up.

If it's urgent-but-not-on-fire, I'd negotiate. "I can start

on this in two hours when I hit a natural stopping point.

Does that work, or is there something I'm not understanding

about the urgency?" That's not resistance — it's responsible

prioritisation. Managers generally appreciate it when you

ask rather than assume.

Either way, I'd close the loop after: "I'm on the bug now.

Here's my current status on the feature. Let me know if

the priority changes again." Keeping visibility high prevents

surprises in both directions.

👩 Rachel: Last question. Where do you want to be in

three years, and how does this role get you there?

👨 Khanh: Three years from now, I want to be the kind

of engineer other engineers come to not just for technical

answers, but for help thinking through hard problems —

architectural decisions, tradeoffs, incident post-mortems.

That's what I associate with genuine seniority: not knowing

more, but being able to help others think more clearly.

This role gets me there because of the scale. Working on

systems that handle Black Friday traffic isn't glamorous

every day, but it's the kind of environment where your

assumptions get stress-tested constantly. You can't fake

good architectural judgment at Shopify's scale — either

the system holds or it doesn't.

I also want to improve at cross-functional communication —

working with product managers and merchant partners, not

just engineers. Shopify's model, where engineers are close

to merchants and business outcomes, is exactly the context

I want to grow in.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

RoundCategoryScoreNotes
RecruiterAuthenticity + motivation10/10Failure story as opener — memorable and honest
Tech AInventory oversell — 3-layer defence10/10Redis Lua + DB lock + soft reservation + crash recovery
Tech BIncident communication10/10Live incident roles; blameless postmortem structure
Tech CCoding: flash sale queue10/10FIFO ticket, idempotent entry, 4 tests
Leadership ALeading without title10/10Bottom-up improvement with data, without steamrolling
CraftGood code + tech debt taxonomy10/103-category debt model; postmortem readability bar
PressureContext switching under urgency10/10Negotiate > assume; hand-off note practice

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 Shopify Interview Tips

  1. Black Friday = your design constraint — always qualify answers with "at flash sale scale"
  2. Redis is a filter, DB is the truth — never let Redis be your inventory source of truth
  3. Lua scripts for atomicity — check-and-decrement in one round trip, no TOCTOU
  4. Soft reservation with TTL — cart reservation expires automatically; no manual cleanup needed
  5. Incident comms = two jobs — live management (roles, blast radius) vs. postmortem (blameless, structured)
  6. Technical debt taxonomy — deliberate shortcut / discovered complexity / foundational rot
  7. Leadership without title — small improvements stacked deliberately beat one big "project"
  8. Shopify salaries (Remote/APAC): USD 120–160K base + RSU; Shopify is generous with equity

📝 Strong English Phrases from This Interview

ContextPhrase
Opener"The thing that shaped me most as an engineer is a failure."
Scale motivation"Scale forces honesty about your architecture."
Incident"Noise in an incident is dangerous."
Postmortem"What condition made that mistake easy to make?"
Resistance"Buy-in matters more than speed when you're changing how a team works."
Good code"Would I be comfortable explaining every line of this in a postmortem?"
Priority conflict"That's not resistance — it's responsible prioritisation."
Career goal"Not knowing more, but helping others think more clearly."

🎭 Mock Interview #39 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: Airbnb (San Francisco / Remote, Global Travel Platform)

Role: Senior Software Engineer — Payments & Trust team.

Airbnb processes $10B+ in payments annually across 190+ countries,

multiple currencies, and complex host-guest payout flows.

Tech: Java 17, Spring Boot, gRPC, MySQL, Kafka, Redis, Temporal.

Balance: 50% English communication · 50% technical depth.

📞 ROUND 1 — RECRUITER SCREEN (20 min, English)

👩 Claire (Recruiter): Hi Khanh! Tell me about yourself —

but specifically, what kind of engineering problems get you

genuinely excited?

👨 Khanh: The problems that excite me most are the ones

where correctness and scale are in tension.

Most engineering problems are either hard to get right

or hard to scale. The interesting ones are both. Payment

flows are a perfect example — a single payout to a host

involves currency conversion, fraud scoring, bank routing,

tax withholding, and a reconciliation trail. Every step

can fail. The system has to be correct across all of them,

and it has to do it for millions of transactions per day.

I love that class of problem because it forces you to think

carefully about failure modes, state machines, and eventual

consistency — not as academic topics but as real design

constraints with real consequences.

👩 Claire: Airbnb is very mission-driven — "belong anywhere."

How does that kind of mission influence how you think about

the engineering work?

👨 Khanh: It shapes the error handling, honestly.

When Airbnb's payout to a host fails silently, it's not just

a bug — it's someone waiting for money they've already earned.

That's a very different emotional weight than a 500 error on

a dashboard widget. Mission-driven context makes me more

serious about: surfacing failures clearly to users instead of

hiding them behind generic error messages, designing retry

and recovery paths so failures are temporary not permanent,

and monitoring the user experience of failure, not just

the system metrics.

Engineers who are connected to the mission tend to care about

those things. Engineers who aren't, tend to mark the ticket

"done" when the happy path works.

👩 Claire: Well said. Moving to technical rounds — good luck!

💻 ROUND 2 — TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE (60 min, English)

Setting: Staff Engineer, Payments Core.

PART A — Host Payout State Machine (25 min)

👨‍💻 Raj (Staff Eng): When a guest checks out, Airbnb

releases the host payout 24 hours later. The flow involves:

check-out confirmation, fraud hold (optional), currency

conversion, bank transfer, and reconciliation. Design the

state machine and make it resilient to partial failures.

👨 Khanh: This is a classic long-running transaction problem.

I'd model it as a saga — a sequence of compensable steps —

orchestrated by a state machine persisted in the database.

State machine:

INITIATED
  → FRAUD_CHECK_PENDING
      → FRAUD_HOLD (manual review required)
      → FRAUD_CLEARED
          → CURRENCY_CONVERTING
              → BANK_TRANSFER_PENDING
                  → COMPLETED
                  → BANK_TRANSFER_FAILED → RETRY or MANUAL_REVIEW
          → FRAUD_REJECTED → CANCELLED (payout blocked)
  → CANCELLED

Implementation — persisted state machine:

@Entity
public class HostPayout {
    @Id private String id;
    private String reservationId;
    private String hostId;
    private BigDecimal amount;
    private String currency;
    private PayoutStatus status;
    private int retryCount;
    private String failureReason;
    private Instant scheduledAt;    // 24h after checkout
    private Instant lastUpdatedAt;
}

public enum PayoutStatus {
    INITIATED, FRAUD_CHECK_PENDING, FRAUD_HOLD,
    FRAUD_CLEARED, FRAUD_REJECTED,
    CURRENCY_CONVERTING, BANK_TRANSFER_PENDING,
    COMPLETED, BANK_TRANSFER_FAILED,
    CANCELLED, MANUAL_REVIEW
}

Saga orchestrator — each step is idempotent:

@Service
public class PayoutOrchestrator {

    @Transactional
    public void advance(String payoutId) {
        HostPayout payout = payoutRepo.findById(payoutId)
            .orElseThrow();

        switch (payout.getStatus()) {
            case INITIATED -> submitFraudCheck(payout);
            case FRAUD_CLEARED -> startCurrencyConversion(payout);
            case CURRENCY_CONVERTING -> submitBankTransfer(payout);
            case BANK_TRANSFER_FAILED -> handleTransferFailure(payout);
            default -> log.warn("No transition for status: {}",
                payout.getStatus());
        }
    }

    private void submitFraudCheck(HostPayout payout) {
        transition(payout, FRAUD_CHECK_PENDING);
        kafkaTemplate.send("payout.fraud-check",
            payout.getId(), new FraudCheckRequest(payout));
    }

    private void startCurrencyConversion(HostPayout payout) {
        transition(payout, CURRENCY_CONVERTING);
        BigDecimal converted = fxService.convert(
            payout.getAmount(), payout.getCurrency(), "USD");
        payout.setAmountUsd(converted);
        transition(payout, BANK_TRANSFER_PENDING); // sync in this case
        kafkaTemplate.send("payout.bank-transfer",
            payout.getId(), new BankTransferRequest(payout));
    }

    private void handleTransferFailure(HostPayout payout) {
        if (payout.getRetryCount() < 3) {
            payout.setRetryCount(payout.getRetryCount() + 1);
            // Schedule retry with exponential backoff
            Instant retryAt = Instant.now().plusSeconds(
                (long) Math.pow(2, payout.getRetryCount()) * 60);
            payout.setScheduledAt(retryAt);
            transition(payout, BANK_TRANSFER_PENDING);
        } else {
            transition(payout, MANUAL_REVIEW);
            alertOpsTeam(payout, "Bank transfer failed after 3 retries");
        }
    }

    private void transition(HostPayout payout, PayoutStatus next) {
        payout.setStatus(next);
        payout.setLastUpdatedAt(Instant.now());
        payoutRepo.save(payout);
        // Audit log every transition
        auditLog.record(payout.getId(), next);
    }
}

Resilience mechanisms:

  • Every transition is persisted before the async action —

crash-safe by design.

  • Kafka consumers are idempotent: they check current status

before processing to avoid double-advancing.

  • A recovery job polls for payouts stuck in *_PENDING

states for more than 30 minutes and re-advances them.

👨‍💻 Raj: What if the fraud check service is down for

an hour? How does the host payout flow behave?

👨 Khanh: The payout stays in FRAUD_CHECK_PENDING state —

which is exactly what we want. The state machine doesn't

advance until the fraud service responds.

The Kafka message for the fraud check has already been sent.

When the fraud service recovers, it will process the message

and publish a result event — FraudCheckResult — which our

consumer picks up and advances the state machine.

Two safety nets:

// Safety net 1: Recovery sweep for stuck payouts
@Scheduled(fixedDelay = 900_000) // every 15 min
public void recoverStuckPayouts() {
    Instant cutoff = Instant.now().minusSeconds(3600); // 1 hour
    List<HostPayout> stuck = payoutRepo
        .findByStatusAndLastUpdatedAtBefore(FRAUD_CHECK_PENDING, cutoff);

    for (HostPayout p : stuck) {
        log.warn("Payout {} stuck in FRAUD_CHECK_PENDING for >1h", p.getId());
        // Re-publish fraud check request (idempotent — fraud service deduplicates)
        kafkaTemplate.send("payout.fraud-check",
            p.getId(), new FraudCheckRequest(p));
    }
}

// Safety net 2: Dead Letter Queue monitoring
// If fraud-check messages land in DLQ → alert ops

The host experience: they see "Payout processing" — a deliberately

vague status that doesn't mention fraud. If the hold exceeds

24 additional hours, we send a support ticket automatically.

PART B — COMMUNICATION: Delivering Bad News (15 min)

👨‍💻 Raj: Imagine a bug in the payout system caused 2,000

hosts to receive the wrong payout amount — some overpaid,

some underpaid. You discovered it. Walk me through what

you do next.

👨 Khanh: This is a financial correctness incident — I treat

it with the same urgency as a production outage, maybe more.

Step 1 — Stop the bleeding first.

Before I tell anyone, I need to know: is this bug still

running? If yes, I'd coordinate an immediate disable of the

payout batch job — not a rollback yet, just a pause — while

we understand the blast radius.

Step 2 — Quantify accurately before communicating.

The worst thing I can do is send a message saying "some hosts

were affected" and then need to correct the numbers twice.

I'd spend 20–30 minutes running exact queries:

  • How many hosts?
  • Total amount overpaid vs underpaid?
  • Date range affected?
  • Is the error consistent (formula bug) or random?

Step 3 — Tell the right people, in the right order.

First my manager and the on-call lead — before Slack,

before email. Then a short, factual incident brief:

"We have a confirmed payout calculation error affecting

2,000 hosts across [date range]. ~1,200 were underpaid

(total: $X), ~800 were overpaid (total: $Y). The batch

job is paused. Root cause is [one sentence]. Fix ETA: [time].

We will reconcile all affected payouts by [date]."

Step 4 — The hardest part: communication to hosts.

I'd push for proactive outreach over waiting for complaints.

Hosts who discover the error themselves before we tell them

lose trust permanently. A message that says "We found an

error and we're correcting it" is much better than one that

says "We noticed you contacted support."

The message should be: specific amount, clear correction

timeline, and a direct apology — no corporate language,

no passive voice. Hosts are small business owners.

They deserve a straight answer.

Step 5 — Postmortem.

Blameless, within 48 hours, with reconciliation as a tracked

action item until every cent is corrected.

PART C — CODING: Currency Conversion Cache (20 min)

👨‍💻 Raj: *"FX rates are fetched from an external API.

Implement a simple in-memory cache with a 5-minute TTL per

currency pair. Thread-safe. Handle expired entries correctly."*

👨 Khanh:

public class FxRateCache {

    private record CachedRate(BigDecimal rate, Instant expiresAt) {
        boolean isExpired() {
            return Instant.now().isAfter(expiresAt);
        }
    }

    private final Duration ttl;
    private final ConcurrentHashMap<String, CachedRate> cache =
        new ConcurrentHashMap<>();

    public FxRateCache(Duration ttl) {
        this.ttl = ttl;
    }

    // Returns cached rate if valid, else fetches fresh and caches it
    public BigDecimal getRate(String from, String to,
                              Supplier<BigDecimal> fetcher) {
        String key = from + "_" + to;

        CachedRate cached = cache.get(key);
        if (cached != null && !cached.isExpired()) {
            return cached.rate(); // cache hit
        }

        // Expired or absent — fetch fresh rate
        // Use compute to avoid duplicate fetches under concurrent access
        CachedRate[] result = new CachedRate[1];
        cache.compute(key, (k, existing) -> {
            // Double-check inside compute — another thread may have refreshed
            if (existing != null && !existing.isExpired()) {
                result[0] = existing;
                return existing; // no-op
            }
            BigDecimal fresh = fetcher.get();
            CachedRate newEntry = new CachedRate(
                fresh, Instant.now().plus(ttl));
            result[0] = newEntry;
            return newEntry;
        });

        return result[0].rate();
    }

    // Explicit eviction (e.g., on rate-update webhook)
    public void invalidate(String from, String to) {
        cache.remove(from + "_" + to);
    }

    public int size() { return cache.size(); }
}

// Tests
@Test
void returnsCachedRateOnHit() {
    var cache = new FxRateCache(Duration.ofMinutes(5));
    AtomicInteger fetchCount = new AtomicInteger(0);

    Supplier<BigDecimal> fetcher = () -> {
        fetchCount.incrementAndGet();
        return new BigDecimal("1.08");
    };

    cache.getRate("USD", "EUR", fetcher);
    cache.getRate("USD", "EUR", fetcher); // should hit cache

    assertThat(fetchCount.get()).isEqualTo(1);
}

@Test
void fetchesFreshRateAfterExpiry() throws InterruptedException {
    var cache = new FxRateCache(Duration.ofMillis(50)); // 50ms TTL
    AtomicInteger fetchCount = new AtomicInteger(0);

    cache.getRate("USD", "EUR", () -> {
        fetchCount.incrementAndGet();
        return BigDecimal.ONE;
    });
    Thread.sleep(60); // expire the entry

    cache.getRate("USD", "EUR", () -> {
        fetchCount.incrementAndGet();
        return BigDecimal.ONE;
    });

    assertThat(fetchCount.get()).isEqualTo(2);
}

@Test
void invalidationForcesRefetch() {
    var cache = new FxRateCache(Duration.ofMinutes(5));
    AtomicInteger fetchCount = new AtomicInteger(0);

    Supplier<BigDecimal> f = () -> {
        fetchCount.incrementAndGet();
        return BigDecimal.TEN;
    };

    cache.getRate("GBP", "USD", f);
    cache.invalidate("GBP", "USD");
    cache.getRate("GBP", "USD", f);

    assertThat(fetchCount.get()).isEqualTo(2);
}

@Test
void isolatesCurrencyPairs() {
    var cache = new FxRateCache(Duration.ofMinutes(5));
    AtomicInteger calls = new AtomicInteger(0);

    cache.getRate("USD", "EUR", () -> { calls.incrementAndGet(); return BigDecimal.ONE; });
    cache.getRate("USD", "GBP", () -> { calls.incrementAndGet(); return BigDecimal.TWO; });

    assertThat(calls.get()).isEqualTo(2); // different pairs — both fetched
    assertThat(cache.size()).isEqualTo(2);
}

💬 ROUND 3 — CULTURE & GROWTH (35 min, English)

Setting: Engineering Manager, Trust & Payments.

👩 Maya (EM): Airbnb had a rough period during COVID —

the company had to lay off 25% of its workforce and then

rebuild. Engineering culture shifted a lot. How do you

stay motivated when the environment around you is turbulent?

👨 Khanh: Honestly, I think about this one carefully because

I've seen two failure modes in turbulent environments.

The first is people who completely disconnect — they show up,

do the minimum, and wait for stability to return before

re-engaging. The second is people who try to fix everything

and burn out because they can't.

The version I try to practise is: find what I can control

and do that exceptionally well. In uncertain periods,

the most stabilising thing a senior engineer can do is be

the person their immediate team can rely on. Not heroics —

just consistency. Showing up to reviews, keeping documentation

current, being patient with people who are more anxious than

you. That kind of reliability is disproportionately valuable

when everything else feels unstable.

The thing that keeps me personally motivated is focusing

on the craft, not the company's quarterly metrics. The

code I write today either handles failure gracefully or

it doesn't — and that's true regardless of stock price

or headcount. I find that focusing on that level keeps

me grounded.

👩 Maya: Tell me about a time you had to earn trust

on a new team. What did you do?

👨 Khanh: When I joined my most recent team, I made a

deliberate decision not to have strong opinions for the

first four weeks.

That sounds passive, but it wasn't. I was intensely

listening and reading — code, Slack history, postmortems,

ADRs. I was building a map of what the team valued,

what frustrated them, and where they felt stuck. I'd

ask questions in one-on-ones rather than in group

discussions, so people could be honest without performing.

At the four-week mark, I'd identified one thing I could

contribute that was clearly useful and clearly within

my ability: improving the test coverage on the payment

reconciliation service, which everyone knew was fragile

but no one had time to fix. I did it quietly, put up

a PR, and asked for a review.

The PR itself was less important than the signal it sent:

I understand what matters to you, I do what I say I'll do,

and I'm not here to redesign everything in my first month.

Trust, in my experience, comes from predictability more

than brilliance. I'd rather be the person who ships what

they commit to, every time, than the person who occasionally

produces something brilliant and is otherwise a variable.

👩 Maya: What do you think is the hardest part of

being a senior engineer — not the technical part?

👨 Khanh: Knowing when to let someone struggle.

When you're a junior engineer, the most helpful thing a

senior can do is often just give you the answer. It saves

time, unblocks you, and you learn something.

But as a senior engineer, always giving answers is actually

harmful to the team. People stop thinking through problems

because they know the shortcut is available. And you become

a bottleneck — nothing moves without your input.

The harder skill is staying quiet long enough to let someone

work through something, and then asking a question that

helps them find the answer themselves. That's not satisfying

in the moment — it's slower, and it requires real restraint

when you can see the solution clearly. But it's the only

way a team actually develops.

I'm still learning this. I catch myself jumping to answers

more often than I'd like. But I've gotten better at pausing

and asking "what have you tried so far?" instead.

👩 Maya: Final question. What question do you wish

interviewers would ask you that they never do?

👨 Khanh: What have you changed your mind about recently?

I think that question tells you more about an engineer

than almost any technical question. It shows whether they're

still learning or whether they've calcified into a set of

positions they just defend.

My honest answer right now: I used to believe microservices

were almost always the right architectural choice for

scalable systems. I've changed my mind. The operational

overhead — distributed tracing, network latency, deployment

complexity — is significant, and I've seen too many teams

treat microservices as a scalability solution when their

actual bottleneck was the database or the algorithm. A

well-structured monolith with clear module boundaries

is often the better starting point. Migrate to services

when you have a specific reason, not as a default.

That's a position I would not have held five years ago.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

RoundCategoryScoreNotes
RecruiterMission alignment + motivation10/10"Error handling shaped by mission" — original and sharp
Tech APayout state machine + saga10/10Full state enum, idempotent transitions, recovery sweep
Tech BIncident: financial error comms10/10Stop → quantify → escalate → proactive host outreach
Tech CCoding: FX rate cache10/10compute() for double-check, 4 edge-case tests
Culture AResilience in turbulence10/10Craft over metrics; reliability as stability signal
Culture BEarning trust on a new team10/104-week listening period; predictability over brilliance
CraftSenior engineer's hardest skill10/10Knowing when to let someone struggle

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 Airbnb Interview Tips

  1. Mission shapes error handling — connect engineering decisions to host/guest experience, not just metrics
  2. Saga pattern for long-running flows — payout spans hours; each step must be compensable and idempotent
  3. Persist state before async action — crash-safety requires writing the transition before sending the Kafka message
  4. Financial incident = highest urgency — stop → quantify exactly → communicate proactively
  5. Trust = predictability — Airbnb values engineers who ship what they commit to, consistently
  6. FX cache: use compute() — prevents duplicate external calls under concurrent access (vs putIfAbsent)
  7. "What have you changed your mind about?" — signals intellectual honesty; prepare a genuine answer
  8. Airbnb salaries (Remote/APAC): USD 150–200K base + RSU; top-tier benefits globally

📝 Strong English Phrases from This Interview

ContextPhrase
Motivation"Correctness and scale are in tension — that's where the interesting problems are."
Mission"Mission-driven context makes me more serious about the user experience of failure."
Incident"The worst thing I can do is communicate before I have accurate numbers."
Proactive comms"Hosts who discover the error themselves before we tell them lose trust permanently."
Turbulence"The most stabilising thing a senior engineer can do is be consistent."
Trust"Trust comes from predictability more than brilliance."
Senior skill"Knowing when to let someone struggle — and staying quiet long enough for them to do it."
Growth"I'm still learning this. I catch myself jumping to answers more than I'd like."

🎭 Mock Interview #40 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: Uber (San Francisco / Remote, Global Mobility & Delivery Platform)

Role: Senior Software Engineer — Marketplace & Pricing team.

Uber processes 25 million trips per day across 70+ countries.

Real-time surge pricing, driver matching, and trip lifecycle

are the core engineering challenges.

Tech: Java 17, gRPC, Kafka, Redis, Cassandra, MySQL, Cadence/Temporal.

Balance: 50% English communication · 50% technical depth.

📞 ROUND 1 — RECRUITER SCREEN (15 min, English)

👩 Nina (Recruiter): Hi Khanh! Quick question to start —

if you had to describe your engineering style in three words,

what would they be and why?

👨 Khanh: Deliberate, honest, and recoverable.

Deliberate — I try not to write code on autopilot.

Every design choice is a tradeoff, and I want to be

conscious of what I'm trading.

Honest — I don't hide complexity behind clean-looking

abstractions. If something is hard, the code should

communicate that, not pretend it isn't.

Recoverable — I design for failure. Not because I'm

pessimistic, but because in distributed systems failure

is not the exception, it's the schedule. Every system

I build, I ask: when this breaks, can we recover

without losing data and without waking someone up at 3am?

👩 Nina: I love "recoverable" — very Uber. We deal with

real-time systems where even 200ms latency matters.

What's your experience with that kind of constraint?

👨 Khanh: My most direct experience is from payment

systems where users abandon flows after about 3 seconds —

so every hop in the chain matters. I learned to treat

latency as a first-class feature, not an afterthought.

Practically that meant: profiling before optimising —

never guess where the slowness is. Pushing slow work

async wherever the business logic allowed it. Being

very deliberate about what had to be synchronous

versus what could be fire-and-forget. And monitoring

p99 latency, not just average — because averages hide

the tail where users actually churn.

Real-time matching is a different scale of that

problem, but the thinking is the same.

👩 Nina: Great. Technical rounds are next. Good luck!

💻 ROUND 2 — TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE (60 min, English)

Setting: Staff Engineer, Marketplace Platform.

PART A — Surge Pricing (20 min)

👨‍💻 Wei (Staff Eng): Surge pricing kicks in when driver

supply is low relative to rider demand in a geographic

zone. Walk me through how you'd design the surge

calculation and apply it in real time.

👨 Khanh: Two parts: computing the surge multiplier,

and applying it safely at fare calculation time.

Supply/demand ratio per hex zone (H3 grid):

@Service
public class SurgeCalculator {

    // H3 hex index → current surge multiplier
    private final ConcurrentHashMap<String, Double> surgeCache =
        new ConcurrentHashMap<>();

    // Triggered every 60 seconds by Kafka consumer
    // consuming driver-location and ride-request events
    public void recalculate(String hexId,
                            int activeDrivers,
                            int pendingRequests) {
        double ratio = (pendingRequests == 0 || activeDrivers == 0)
            ? (pendingRequests > 0 ? 3.0 : 1.0)
            : (double) pendingRequests / activeDrivers;

        double multiplier = computeMultiplier(ratio);
        surgeCache.put(hexId, multiplier);

        // Publish for downstream services (fare calc, driver app)
        kafkaTemplate.send("surge.updates",
            hexId, new SurgeUpdate(hexId, multiplier, Instant.now()));
    }

    private double computeMultiplier(double demandSupplyRatio) {
        // Piecewise linear scaling, capped at 5x
        if (demandSupplyRatio <= 1.0) return 1.0;
        if (demandSupplyRatio <= 2.0) return 1.0 + (demandSupplyRatio - 1.0) * 0.5;
        if (demandSupplyRatio <= 4.0) return 1.5 + (demandSupplyRatio - 2.0) * 0.5;
        return Math.min(5.0, 2.5 + (demandSupplyRatio - 4.0) * 0.25);
    }

    public double getSurge(String hexId) {
        return surgeCache.getOrDefault(hexId, 1.0);
    }
}

Applying surge at fare estimation (before rider confirms):

@Service
public class FareCalculator {

    public FareEstimate estimate(RideRequest request) {
        String hexId = h3Service.latLngToHex(
            request.getPickupLat(), request.getPickupLng());

        double surge      = surgeCalculator.getSurge(hexId);
        double baseFare   = calculateBaseFare(request);
        double totalFare  = baseFare * surge;

        // Lock the surge for this request for 2 minutes
        // So fare doesn't change between estimate and confirmation
        String lockKey = "fare-lock:" + request.getRequestId();
        redisTemplate.opsForValue().set(lockKey,
            String.valueOf(surge), Duration.ofMinutes(2));

        return FareEstimate.builder()
            .baseFare(baseFare)
            .surgeMultiplier(surge)
            .totalFare(totalFare)
            .surgeHexId(hexId)
            .validUntil(Instant.now().plusSeconds(120))
            .build();
    }

    public ConfirmedFare confirmFare(String requestId) {
        String lockKey = "fare-lock:" + requestId;
        String lockedSurge = redisTemplate.opsForValue().get(lockKey);

        if (lockedSurge == null) {
            // Lock expired — recalculate with current surge
            throw new FareLockExpiredException(
                "Fare lock expired. Please request a new estimate.");
        }

        // Use the locked surge — not the current one
        double surge = Double.parseDouble(lockedSurge);
        return buildConfirmedFare(requestId, surge);
    }
}
