⚠️ Amazon-specific: Every round includes Leadership Principle

(LP) questions. Amazon interviewers score LP answers separately

from technical answers. Each LP story must be STAR format

and data-driven. Having 8–10 unique LP stories prepared is essential.

📞 ROUND 1 — PHONE SCREEN (30 min, English)

Setting: Senior SDE screens for LP fit and basic technical depth.

👨‍💻 Mark (Sr SDE): Hi Khanh! Amazon has 16 Leadership Principles.

Let's start with Customer Obsession. Tell me about a time

you went significantly beyond what was asked to deliver a better

outcome for a customer — internal or external.

👨 Khanh: (STAR — Customer Obsession)

Situation: I was building a batch reconciliation feature

for our payment operations team — an internal customer.

The requirement was simple: generate a daily CSV report

of mismatched transactions. I built it, it worked, ticket closed.

Task: But when I shared it with the operations team for UAT,

I noticed something. They had to download the CSV, open Excel,

apply three manual filters, and cross-reference with another

tool before they could act on anything. The report technically

met the spec. But the actual workflow had five manual steps

we'd never discussed.

Action: I wasn't asked to do anything more. But I spent

one extra day adding: a server-side filter API so they could

query by mismatch type and date range directly, a summary

row at the top of the CSV with total counts and total VND

in dispute, and an optional email delivery so they didn't

have to log in to download it each morning.

I presented the additions to my manager as a fast-follow

and got them approved. Total extra effort: one day.

Result: The operations team's daily reconciliation workflow

went from 40 minutes to 8 minutes. They later told me it

was the most useful internal tool shipped that quarter.

And they trusted our team enough to bring us into the

design of their next tool — which became a much bigger

project. One extra day of effort opened a year-long relationship.

👨‍💻 Mark: Strong. Now a quick technical one to calibrate.

*"DynamoDB stores items in partitions. If 80% of your reads

hit the same partition key, what happens and how do you fix it?"*

👨 Khanh: That's a hot partition problem — one of the most

common DynamoDB anti-patterns.

What happens: DynamoDB allocates read/write capacity

uniformly across partitions. If 80% of traffic hits one

partition, that partition gets throttled while others sit

idle. You see ProvisionedThroughputExceededException

even though your overall table capacity looks fine.

Three mitigation strategies, in order of preference:

1 — Redesign the partition key (best long-term):

The root cause is almost always a poor key choice.

If you're partitioning by countryCode and 80% of users

are from Vietnam, that's the problem. Add a shard suffix:

instead of VN, use VN_0 through VN_9, and select

the shard by userId % 10. Reads need to fan out across

10 shards, but writes distribute evenly.

public String shardedKey(String country, String userId) {
    int shard = Math.abs(userId.hashCode() % 10);
    return country + "_" + shard;
}

// Read: query all 10 shards in parallel, merge results
public List<Item> readAll(String country) {
    return IntStream.range(0, 10).parallel()
        .mapToObj(i -> dynamodb.query(country + "_" + i))
        .flatMap(Collection::stream)
        .toList();
}

2 — DAX (DynamoDB Accelerator) for hot-read items:

A fully managed in-memory cache in front of DynamoDB.

Microsecond reads for the hottest items. No code change

to the read path — just change the endpoint.

3 — Write sharding with read aggregation:

For counters or leaderboards, write to userId_0..userId_N,

aggregate on read. Same pattern as #1 but specifically

for high-write scenarios.

💻 ROUND 2 — TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE (60 min, English)

Setting: Principal Engineer + Staff Engineer panel.

PART A — Distributed Key-Value Store Design (25 min)

👨‍💻 Priya (Principal Eng): Let's go deeper on distributed

storage. Design a simplified key-value store — like DynamoDB —

that supports: put(key, value), get(key), eventual

consistency, and horizontal scaling. Walk through your design.

👨 Khanh: I'll clarify one thing first: eventual consistency

implies we accept that reads might return stale data for a brief

window after a write. That's the tradeoff I'll design around.

Core architecture — consistent hashing for distribution:

[Client]
    ↓
[Coordinator Node] — receives all reads/writes
    ↓ hashes key → determines responsible nodes
[Replica Nodes] — N=3 replicas per key (configurable)
    ↓
[Storage Engine] — per-node LSM tree (like LevelDB)

Consistent hashing — assigning keys to nodes:

public class ConsistentHashRing {

    private final TreeMap<Long, String> ring = new TreeMap<>();
    private final int virtualNodes; // e.g., 150 per physical node

    public void addNode(String nodeId) {
        for (int i = 0; i < virtualNodes; i++) {
            long hash = hash(nodeId + "#" + i);
            ring.put(hash, nodeId);
        }
    }

    // Returns N distinct nodes responsible for a key
    public List<String> getNodes(String key, int replicationFactor) {
        long keyHash = hash(key);
        Set<String> nodes = new LinkedHashSet<>();

        // Walk clockwise from keyHash position
        NavigableMap<Long, String> tail = ring.tailMap(keyHash);
        for (String node : Iterables.concat(
                tail.values(), ring.values())) {
            nodes.add(node);
            if (nodes.size() == replicationFactor) break;
        }
        return new ArrayList<>(nodes);
    }

    private long hash(String input) {
        return Hashing.murmur3_128().hashString(
            input, UTF_8).asLong();
    }
}

Write path — quorum writes (W=2 of N=3):

@Service
public class KvCoordinator {

    private static final int N = 3; // replicas
    private static final int W = 2; // write quorum
    private static final int R = 2; // read quorum

    public void put(String key, String value) {
        List<String> nodes = hashRing.getNodes(key, N);
        String versionedValue = vectorClock.stamp(value);

        // Send to all N replicas in parallel
        List<CompletableFuture<Boolean>> writes = nodes.stream()
            .map(node -> CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(
                () -> replicaClient.put(node, key, versionedValue)))
            .toList();

        // Wait for W acknowledgements
        long successes = writes.stream()
            .map(f -> f.orTimeout(500, TimeUnit.MILLISECONDS))
            .filter(f -> {
                try { return f.get(); }
                catch (Exception e) { return false; }
            })
            .count();

        if (successes < W) {
            throw new WriteQuorumException(
                "Only " + successes + "/" + W + " replicas acked");
        }
        // W-1 lagging replicas will catch up via anti-entropy
    }

    // Read path — quorum reads (R=2 of N=3)
    public String get(String key) {
        List<String> nodes = hashRing.getNodes(key, N);

        List<CompletableFuture<VersionedValue>> reads = nodes.stream()
            .map(node -> CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(
                () -> replicaClient.get(node, key)))
            .toList();

        // Collect R responses, return highest vector clock version
        return reads.stream()
            .map(f -> {
                try { return f.orTimeout(300, TimeUnit.MILLISECONDS).get(); }
                catch (Exception e) { return null; }
            })
            .filter(Objects::nonNull)
            .limit(R)
            .max(Comparator.comparing(VersionedValue::clockVersion))
            .map(VersionedValue::value)
            .orElseThrow(() -> new ReadQuorumException("Not enough replicas responded"));
    }
}

Anti-entropy — background repair for lagging replicas:

@Scheduled(fixedDelay = 30_000)
public void antiEntropy() {
    // Merkle tree comparison between replica pairs
    // Identifies divergent key ranges, syncs them
    // This is how eventual consistency "eventual" resolves
    merkleRepairService.syncAll();
}

Why W + R > N for strong consistency?

With N=3, W=2, R=2: any read quorum of 2 must overlap

with any write quorum of 2 by at least 1 node. That node

has the latest write. So even eventual consistency can

be tuned toward strong consistency by raising W and R.

PART B — LP ROUND: Dive Deep + Disagree & Commit (20 min)

👨‍💻 Priya: Dive Deep LP. Tell me about a time you

discovered a problem that nobody else on your team had noticed.

How did you find it and what did you do?

👨 Khanh: (STAR — Dive Deep)

Situation: About 18 months ago, our team had a Java

microservice that was experiencing periodic GC pauses —

roughly every 6 hours, latency spiked for 3–4 seconds.

The on-call team had been restarting the service every

time it happened. Nobody had investigated the root cause

because "restart fixes it" had become the accepted response.

Task: I wasn't on-call during most of these incidents,

but I noticed the pattern in our metrics dashboard during

a routine review. The interval was too regular — exactly

every 5.5 to 6.5 hours — which suggested a periodic

accumulation, not random load.

Action: I added heap profiling with async-profiler

during the next expected window and let it run for two hours

before the pause. What I found: a static Map being used

as a cache with no eviction policy. Every API request added

an entry. After ~6 hours of traffic the map hit 2GB,

triggered a full GC, and paused the world.

The fix was one afternoon: replace the unbounded map with

a Caffeine cache with a 10,000 entry limit and 1-hour TTL.

Zero GC pauses in the 4 months since.

Result: Before: restart every 6 hours, 3–4 second user

impact each time, 2 on-call engineer-hours per week.

After: zero restarts, zero pauses, zero pages.

What struck me was how long this had been accepted as normal.

The culture of "restart fixes it" had made an invisible

problem invisible. Diving into the metrics with fresh eyes

surfaced it in two hours.

👨‍💻 Priya: Disagree & Commit LP. Tell me about a time

you strongly disagreed with a decision, but committed to it

anyway. What was the outcome?

👨 Khanh: (STAR — Disagree & Commit)

Situation: My team decided to migrate our primary database

from MySQL to a NoSQL solution to handle growing write volume.

I had serious reservations — our data model had complex

relational integrity requirements and our team had limited

NoSQL operational experience. I believed the migration risk

outweighed the scaling benefit, at least at our current volume.

Task: I had one opportunity to influence the decision

before it was finalised: the architecture review meeting.

Action: I came prepared with a written doc — not a

rebuttal, but a structured risk analysis. I presented:

the specific relational constraints I was concerned about,

the estimated migration effort, an alternative PostgreSQL

sharding approach with projected capacity headroom, and

the key question I wanted us to answer before deciding —

"At what write volume does NoSQL become unavoidable?"

The team listened. The tech lead acknowledged two of my

three concerns. But the decision held — the organisation

had strategic reasons for the migration beyond our team's

scope that I hadn't been fully briefed on.

How I committed: I said explicitly in the meeting:

*"I still have reservations about the migration timeline

and our NoSQL operational readiness. I want those concerns

on record. And I am fully committed to making this succeed."*

Then I volunteered to lead the migration runbook and own

the rollback plan — because if I believed the risk was

real, I should be the one managing it, not hoping someone

else would.

Result: The migration completed successfully. My rollback

plan was used once during a staging rehearsal — which

validated the concern about operational readiness —

but not in production. The tech lead later said my

concern about the timeline was correct, and we should

have taken an extra three weeks. That conversation

opened the door to more influence in future architectural

decisions.

PART C — CODING: LRU Cache (15 min)

👨‍💻 Priya: *"Implement an LRU (Least Recently Used) cache

with get and put in O(1) time. Thread-safe."*

👨 Khanh: Classic — doubly linked list + HashMap.

public class LruCache<K, V> {

    private final int capacity;

    // Node in doubly-linked list
    private static class Node<K, V> {
        K key; V value;
        Node<K, V> prev, next;
        Node(K k, V v) { this.key = k; this.value = v; }
    }

    // Sentinel head (MRU end) and tail (LRU end)
    private final Node<K, V> head = new Node<>(null, null);
    private final Node<K, V> tail = new Node<>(null, null);

    private final Map<K, Node<K, V>> map = new HashMap<>();

    private final ReadWriteLock lock = new ReentrantReadWriteLock();

    public LruCache(int capacity) {
        this.capacity = capacity;
        head.next = tail;
        tail.prev = head;
    }

    public V get(K key) {
        lock.readLock().lock();
        try {
            Node<K, V> node = map.get(key);
            if (node == null) return null;
            // Promote to MRU under write lock
            lock.readLock().unlock();
            lock.writeLock().lock();
            try {
                moveToFront(node);
                return node.value;
            } finally {
                lock.readLock().lock();
                lock.writeLock().unlock();
            }
        } finally {
            lock.readLock().unlock();
        }
    }

    public void put(K key, V value) {
        lock.writeLock().lock();
        try {
            Node<K, V> existing = map.get(key);
            if (existing != null) {
                existing.value = value;
                moveToFront(existing);
                return;
            }

            Node<K, V> node = new Node<>(key, value);
            map.put(key, node);
            addToFront(node);

            if (map.size() > capacity) {
                Node<K, V> lru = removeTail();
                map.remove(lru.key);
            }
        } finally {
            lock.writeLock().unlock();
        }
    }

    private void addToFront(Node<K, V> node) {
        node.next = head.next;
        node.prev = head;
        head.next.prev = node;
        head.next = node;
    }

    private void moveToFront(Node<K, V> node) {
        removeNode(node);
        addToFront(node);
    }

    private void removeNode(Node<K, V> node) {
        node.prev.next = node.next;
        node.next.prev = node.prev;
    }

    private Node<K, V> removeTail() {
        Node<K, V> lru = tail.prev;
        removeNode(lru);
        return lru;
    }

    public int size() {
        lock.readLock().lock();
        try { return map.size(); }
        finally { lock.readLock().unlock(); }
    }
}

// Tests
@Test
void returnsNullForMissingKey() {
    var cache = new LruCache<String, Integer>(3);
    assertThat(cache.get("missing")).isNull();
}

@Test
void evictsLeastRecentlyUsed() {
    var cache = new LruCache<String, Integer>(2);
    cache.put("a", 1);
    cache.put("b", 2);
    cache.put("c", 3); // evicts "a" (LRU)
    assertThat(cache.get("a")).isNull();
    assertThat(cache.get("b")).isEqualTo(2);
    assertThat(cache.get("c")).isEqualTo(3);
}

@Test
void accessPromotesToMru() {
    var cache = new LruCache<String, Integer>(2);
    cache.put("a", 1);
    cache.put("b", 2);
    cache.get("a"); // promote "a"
    cache.put("c", 3); // should evict "b", not "a"
    assertThat(cache.get("a")).isEqualTo(1);
    assertThat(cache.get("b")).isNull();
}

@Test
void updatesExistingKey() {
    var cache = new LruCache<String, Integer>(2);
    cache.put("a", 1);
    cache.put("a", 99); // update
    assertThat(cache.get("a")).isEqualTo(99);
    assertThat(cache.size()).isEqualTo(1);
}

💬 ROUND 3 — BAR RAISER (40 min, English)

Setting: Bar Raiser — a senior engineer from a different

team who has veto power regardless of team preference.

Their job is to evaluate against the full SDE bar, not fit.

👨‍💻 Omar (Bar Raiser): Earn Trust LP. Tell me about

a time you made a mistake that affected others. What did you do?

👨 Khanh: (STAR — Earn Trust)

Situation: About two years ago, I merged a configuration

change to our production Kafka consumer — adjusting the

max.poll.records setting — during a low-traffic window

on a Friday afternoon. I'd tested it in staging and

believed the change was safe.

What went wrong: I hadn't accounted for the interaction

between max.poll.records and our session timeout config.

With the new setting, our consumers were polling more records

per batch than they could process within the session timeout

window. Kafka started rebalancing consumers every few minutes.

The result: message processing throughput dropped by 60%

for 3 hours before I was paged. Several downstream services

backed up significantly.

Task: Fix the immediate problem and make sure the team

understood what happened and why.

Action: I reverted the config change immediately — 5 minutes

from being paged to recovery. Then I wrote a thorough

postmortem that evening, even though it wasn't a required

process for configuration changes at the time.

The postmortem was honest about two things I got wrong:

the technical gap — I hadn't read the Kafka documentation

carefully enough about the interaction between these two

settings — and the process gap — I had merged and deployed

without asking anyone to review the change, even informally.

I sent the postmortem to the whole team, including my manager,

the next morning with a message that said: *"I made this

mistake. Here's exactly what happened, here's why I didn't

catch it, and here are the two changes I'm making to my

own process to ensure it doesn't happen again."*

Result: No one on the team was surprised or angry —

the postmortem had already named everything. What they

were was appreciative. Two engineers told me privately

that they'd made similar config changes without review

and were going to start doing peer review too.

My manager later cited the postmortem as an example of

how to handle mistakes on the team. The trust I built

by being direct about it was worth more than the trust

I would have lost by hiding it.

What I changed: I now always get a second pair of eyes

on any config change that touches consumer or producer

settings, even in staging. Thirty minutes of review has

saved us from several near-misses since.

👨‍💻 Omar: Think Big LP. Most engineers optimise

within the existing system. Have you ever proposed something

that fundamentally changed the direction of a project?

👨 Khanh: (STAR — Think Big)

Situation: My team was planning a major feature: a rule

engine that operations staff could configure to auto-flag

suspicious transactions. The plan was a traditional

if-then rule builder — a UI, a rule DSL, a rule evaluation

engine. The project was scoped at four months.

My observation: I looked at how the operations team

actually worked. They didn't think in rules — they thought

in examples. They'd say: *"Flag transactions like this

one"* and point to a specific suspicious transaction.

They weren't going to write rules in a DSL; they'd tried

something similar before and abandoned it.

What I proposed: Instead of a rule engine, build a

similarity-based flagging system. Operations staff mark

known-suspicious transactions. The system finds new

transactions that are similar — same amount range, same

merchant category, same device fingerprint cluster —

and flags them automatically. No rules to write. The

UI is: "Mark this as suspicious" or "This is fine."

This was a fundamentally different direction. It

reduced the engineering scope (no DSL, no rule UI),

but required us to build a lightweight feature-similarity

pipeline — something the team had never done.

Action: I wrote a two-page proposal with a prototype

I'd built in two days using a simple cosine similarity

approach over a feature vector. I presented it to the

PM and tech lead, showed it finding real suspicious

patterns in synthetic data, and quantified the comparison:

*"Rule engine: 4 months, requires operations training,

adoption risk. Similarity approach: 6 weeks, zero learning

curve, self-improving as they mark more transactions."*

Result: The PM and tech lead adopted the proposal.

We built it in 7 weeks. Operations adoption was immediate —

no training required. It caught 34 suspicious transaction

patterns in the first month that the old manual process

had missed. The project became a reference case for

how to scope features by starting from user behaviour

rather than the engineering solution.

👨‍💻 Omar: Last question. Why Amazon specifically —

and why DynamoDB?

👨 Khanh: Amazon because of the scale and the standards.

AWS infrastructure underpins a significant fraction of

the internet. When DynamoDB has a bad day, thousands

of applications have a bad day. That level of responsibility

requires engineering discipline that I find genuinely

motivating — not stressful, motivating. You can't get

away with weak failure handling or vague monitoring

at that scale. The bar is set externally by the stakes,

not internally by culture alone.

DynamoDB specifically because it's a system I've used

extensively as a consumer and have deep respect for.

The design decisions — eventual consistency as a first-class

choice, partition-key-based sharding, the consistency

model tradeoffs — are things I've thought about from

the outside. I want to understand them from the inside.

And the Leadership Principles honestly resonate with me —

particularly Customer Obsession and Dive Deep. Those aren't

cultural slogans I'm saying because they're on the website.

They describe habits I already have. This role would let

me practise them at a scale I haven't had access to before.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

RoundCategoryScoreNotes
Phone ScreenCustomer Obsession LP10/1040 min → 8 min ops workflow; trust opened a year-long relationship
Phone ScreenHot partition problem10/10Sharded key design + DAX + write aggregation — all three options
Tech AKV store design10/10Consistent hashing, quorum W+R>N, anti-entropy repair, vector clocks
LP — Dive DeepGC pause root cause10/10Profiling → unbounded map → Caffeine fix; before/after metrics
LP — Disagree & CommitNoSQL migration10/10Written risk analysis, named concerns publicly, owned rollback plan
CodingLRU Cache O(1)10/10DLL + HashMap, ReadWriteLock, 4 tests
LP — Earn TrustKafka config mistake10/10Immediate revert, honest postmortem, personal process change
LP — Think BigSimilarity engine proposal10/10Rethought feature from user behaviour; 4 months → 7 weeks

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 Amazon / AWS Interview Tips

  1. Prepare 8–10 LP stories — each unique, each with data. You'll need them across 5 rounds
  2. STAR is mandatory — Situation, Task, Action, Result. Interviewers take notes on each section
  3. "I" not "We" — Amazon wants YOUR specific contribution. "We built..." tells them nothing
  4. Hot partition = sharded key — add _0 to _N suffix, fan-out reads in parallel
  5. LRU cache is a classic Amazon coding question — O(1) get/put with DLL + HashMap
  6. Disagree & Commit — must show genuine disagreement, written escalation, then full commitment
  7. Think Big — don't describe an optimisation; describe a directional change with evidence
  8. Amazon/AWS salaries (SDE II/SDE III): USD 150–220K base; significant RSU (vest over 4 years, back-heavy)

📝 Leadership Principle Quick Reference

LPOne-line signal
Customer Obsession"I went beyond the spec because the workflow wasn't right for the customer."
Dive Deep"I noticed the pattern no one else had. I profiled, not guessed."
Disagree & Commit"I raised it in writing, named my concern publicly, then committed fully."
Earn Trust"I wrote the postmortem the same evening, sent it to the whole team."
Think Big"I proposed a different direction, not an optimisation of the existing one."
Deliver Results"Before: 6 hours. After: 22 minutes. Zero pages since."

📝 Strong English Phrases from This Interview

ContextPhrase
Fast decision"Good enough and undoable beats perfect and slow when you're losing transactions every second."
Ownership"I only claim something if I have a realistic plan to move it forward."
Postmortem"I made this mistake. Here's exactly what happened, and here's what I'm changing."
Think Big"I started from user behaviour, not from the engineering solution."
Why Amazon"The bar is set externally by the stakes, not internally by culture alone."
LPs genuine"Those aren't cultural slogans I'm saying because they're on the website."

🎭 Mock Interview #45 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: Revolut (London / Remote-first, Global Neobank)

Role: Senior Backend Engineer — Core Banking team.

Revolut serves 45M+ customers across 35+ countries, offering

multi-currency accounts, instant transfers, stock trading, and crypto.

It processes 500M+ transactions per year. Revolut is known for

an intense, high-ownership engineering culture — move fast,

break nothing, own your service end to end.

Tech: Java 17, Spring Boot, PostgreSQL, Redis, Kafka, gRPC,

internal ledger framework.

Balance: 50% English communication · 50% technical depth.

What makes Revolut interviews distinct:

They test real production thinking — messy problems, incomplete

specs, and a strong preference for engineers who ask "what could

go wrong?" before "what should I build?"

📞 ROUND 1 — RECRUITER / VALUES SCREEN (20 min, English)

👩 Elena (Recruiter): Hi Khanh! Revolut moves very fast —

sometimes too fast, honestly. How do you maintain quality

when the pace around you is high?

👨 Khanh: That's a question I've had to think about practically,

not theoretically — because I've worked in environments where

"move fast" became a reason to skip things that shouldn't be skipped.

My answer is: I protect a small set of non-negotiables

and stay flexible on everything else.

The non-negotiables for me are: error paths must be handled

explicitly — not swallowed or logged and forgotten; any

data mutation that can fail halfway needs a recovery story;

and anything going to production needs at least one test

that would have caught the last real bug in that component.

Everything else — naming conventions, architecture elegance,

refactoring opportunities — I'll defer, document, and pick

up when the pace allows.

The discipline is knowing which category something falls into.

When I'm moving fast, I still ask: *"If this fails in the

worst possible way, what happens to the user's money or data?"*

If the answer is alarming, I slow down. If it's recoverable

and observable, I ship it.

👩 Elena: Revolut engineers own their services fully —

deployment, monitoring, on-call. No handoffs. Does that

appeal to you or concern you?

👨 Khanh: It appeals to me, but I want to be honest about why —

because I think the appeal can be naive if you haven't

actually done it.

Full ownership is motivating because the feedback loop is

direct. When something I built breaks at 2am, I'm the one

who fixes it — which means I'm also the one most motivated

to make sure it doesn't break at 2am. That alignment of

incentives produces better software than any process I've seen.

The concern — and it's real — is that full ownership without

adequate tooling is just full exhaustion. The question I'd

want to answer in the first month is: what does the on-call

experience actually look like? How mature is the observability

stack? Can I diagnose an issue in minutes from dashboards, or

do I need to SSH into servers and read raw logs?

If the tooling is good, full ownership is one of the best

engineering environments you can be in. If it isn't, it's

burnout with extra steps. I've learned to ask that question

early.

👩 Elena: Very self-aware. Moving to three technical rounds.

Good luck!

💻 ROUND 2 — TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE (60 min, English)

Setting: Staff Engineer, Core Banking.

PART A — Multi-Currency Ledger (25 min)

👨‍💻 Ivan (Staff Eng): Revolut's core product is a

multi-currency wallet. A user can hold GBP, EUR, USD, and

VND simultaneously. They can transfer between currencies

instantly. Design the ledger for this.

👨 Khanh: Before I design, one clarification: when a user

converts GBP to EUR, do they get a live FX rate locked at

the moment of the transfer, or do they select a rate and

confirm separately? This affects whether the FX conversion

is part of the same atomic ledger transaction.

Assuming: rate is locked at transfer time, single atomic operation.

Core principle — double-entry bookkeeping:

Every money movement has two sides. A debit from one account

must equal a credit to another. The ledger never creates or

destroys money — it only moves it.

@Entity
public class LedgerEntry {
    @Id     private String entryId;
    private String accountId;       // which account (user+currency)
    private String transactionId;   // groups debit + credit entries
    private EntryType type;         // DEBIT | CREDIT
    private BigDecimal amount;      // always positive
    private String currency;        // ISO 4217: GBP, EUR, USD, VND
    private String description;
    private Instant createdAt;
}

// Each user has one account per currency
@Entity
public class Account {
    @Id     private String accountId;    // userId + currency
    private String userId;
    private String currency;
    private BigDecimal balance;          // current balance
    private BigDecimal reservedBalance;  // held for pending ops
}

Atomic transfer — same currency (e.g., GBP → GBP):

@Transactional(isolation = Isolation.SERIALIZABLE)
public TransferResult transfer(TransferRequest req) {
    // Lock both accounts in consistent order to prevent deadlock
    String fromId = accountId(req.getSenderId(), req.getCurrency());
    String toId   = accountId(req.getRecipientId(), req.getCurrency());

    // Always lock lower ID first
    List<String> lockOrder = Stream.of(fromId, toId)
        .sorted().toList();

    Account a1 = accountRepo.findByIdWithLock(lockOrder.get(0));
    Account a2 = accountRepo.findByIdWithLock(lockOrder.get(1));

    Account from = a1.getId().equals(fromId) ? a1 : a2;
    Account to   = a1.getId().equals(toId)   ? a1 : a2;

    if (from.getBalance().compareTo(req.getAmount()) < 0) {
        throw new InsufficientFundsException(from.getId());
    }

    String txnId = UUID.randomUUID().toString();

    // Debit sender
    from.setBalance(from.getBalance().subtract(req.getAmount()));
    ledgerRepo.save(LedgerEntry.debit(txnId, from, req.getAmount()));

    // Credit recipient
    to.setBalance(to.getBalance().add(req.getAmount()));
    ledgerRepo.save(LedgerEntry.credit(txnId, to, req.getAmount()));

    accountRepo.saveAll(List.of(from, to));

    return TransferResult.success(txnId, req.getAmount(), req.getCurrency());
}

Cross-currency transfer (GBP → EUR):

@Transactional(isolation = Isolation.SERIALIZABLE)
public TransferResult crossCurrencyTransfer(CrossCurrencyRequest req) {
    // 1. Lock FX rate (provided by FX service at request time)
    BigDecimal rate       = req.getLockedRate();       // e.g., 1.18
    BigDecimal eurAmount  = req.getGbpAmount().multiply(rate)
        .setScale(2, RoundingMode.HALF_UP);

    String txnId = UUID.randomUUID().toString();

    // 2. Debit GBP from sender
    Account gbpAccount = accountRepo.findByIdWithLock(
        accountId(req.getSenderId(), "GBP"));
    if (gbpAccount.getBalance().compareTo(req.getGbpAmount()) < 0) {
        throw new InsufficientFundsException(gbpAccount.getId());
    }
    gbpAccount.setBalance(gbpAccount.getBalance()
        .subtract(req.getGbpAmount()));
    ledgerRepo.save(LedgerEntry.debit(txnId, gbpAccount,
        req.getGbpAmount(), "GBP→EUR FX debit"));

    // 3. Credit EUR to recipient
    Account eurAccount = accountRepo.findByIdWithLock(
        accountId(req.getRecipientId(), "EUR"));
    eurAccount.setBalance(eurAccount.getBalance().add(eurAmount));
    ledgerRepo.save(LedgerEntry.credit(txnId, eurAccount,
        eurAmount, "GBP→EUR FX credit"));

    // 4. Record FX spread to Revolut's revenue account
    BigDecimal spread   = req.getGbpAmount()
        .multiply(req.getSpreadRate()); // e.g., 0.005 = 0.5%
    Account    fxRevenue = accountRepo.findByIdWithLock("revolut_fx_revenue_GBP");
    fxRevenue.setBalance(fxRevenue.getBalance().add(spread));
    ledgerRepo.save(LedgerEntry.credit(txnId, fxRevenue, spread, "FX spread"));

    accountRepo.saveAll(List.of(gbpAccount, eurAccount, fxRevenue));

    return TransferResult.success(txnId, eurAmount, "EUR");
}

Why SERIALIZABLE isolation?

Lower isolation levels allow phantom reads — two concurrent

transfers from the same account could both pass the balance

check before either deducts, causing the balance to go negative.

Serializable prevents this at the DB level. For very high-volume

accounts, we'd layer Redis pre-checks on top to avoid hitting

the DB for obviously insufficient-funds cases.

👨‍💻 Ivan: What happens if the transaction commits the

GBP debit but then crashes before writing the EUR credit?

👨 Khanh: That's the partial write problem — the most

dangerous failure mode in a ledger.

With SERIALIZABLE and a single DB, this can't happen —

the entire transaction either commits or rolls back atomically.

If the server crashes mid-transaction, PostgreSQL rolls back

on restart.

The harder case is cross-service or cross-database:

If GBP and EUR accounts live in different database shards —

which they might at Revolut's scale — then a single DB

transaction doesn't span both. Here I'd use the outbox + saga pattern:

Phase 1: Debit GBP → write debit entry + "pending EUR credit" outbox event
         → commit to GBP shard
Phase 2: Outbox relay publishes event to Kafka
Phase 3: EUR shard consumer processes → writes credit entry
         → publishes "EUR credit confirmed" event
Phase 4: GBP shard marks the transaction COMPLETED

On failure between phases:
  → If Phase 1 committed but Phase 3 never completes →
    compensation job detects pending outbox events older
    than 5 minutes → alerts ops or auto-reverses the GBP debit

Invariant we maintain: every txnId must have exactly

one DEBIT and one CREDIT entry at the end. A sweep job

runs every minute checking for orphaned single-entry

transactions and flags them for manual review.

PART B — COMMUNICATION: Explaining a Compliance Risk (15 min)

👨‍💻 Ivan: A PM wants to ship a new peer-to-peer transfer

feature by end of the month. You realise it doesn't have

any AML (Anti-Money Laundering) transaction monitoring.

How do you handle this conversation?

👨 Khanh: I want to handle this carefully because the

natural instinct — just saying "we can't ship without AML" —

often triggers a negotiation I shouldn't be having.

AML monitoring isn't an engineering best practice we're trading

off against speed. It's a legal requirement in every jurisdiction

Revolut operates in. Shipping a P2P transfer feature without it

isn't a calculated risk — it's a regulatory violation.

So my first move is to understand whether this is a knowledge

gap or a deliberate decision. I'd go to the PM privately

and say: *"Before we finalise the timeline, I want to make

sure compliance has signed off on the AML monitoring plan

for this feature. Have we looped them in?"*

If the answer is no — they didn't know it was required —

that's a knowledge gap I can help close. I'd offer to

set up a 30-minute call with the PM, the compliance team,

and our engineering lead to align on what's needed and

whether there's a phased approach: for example, launch

with basic rule-based transaction screening and add

ML-based monitoring in a follow-up sprint.

If the answer is "we know, but we're going to ship anyway

and handle it later" — that's a different conversation.

I'd be direct: *"I can't support shipping this without

AML monitoring in place. That's not a quality bar I'm

setting — it's a legal requirement. I need us to escalate

this to our Head of Compliance before any timeline is

confirmed."*

What I wouldn't do is write a ticket, add a comment,

and let it become someone else's problem. If this ships

incorrectly and Revolut gets fined, "I flagged it in Jira"

is not an adequate defence — for the company or for me

professionally.

PART C — CODING: Balance Snapshot (20 min)

👨‍💻 Ivan: *"Given a list of ledger entries (debits and credits)

in no particular order, implement a function that returns the

current balance per currency for a given user. Handle multiple

currencies. Efficient and clean."*

👨 Khanh:

public record LedgerEntry(
    String userId,
    String currency,     // "GBP", "EUR", "USD"
    EntryType type,      // DEBIT | CREDIT
    BigDecimal amount    // always positive
) {}

public enum EntryType { DEBIT, CREDIT }

public class BalanceCalculator {

    /**
     * Returns current balance per currency for a user.
     * Balance = sum(CREDIT) - sum(DEBIT) per currency.
     * Result may be negative — caller decides if that's valid.
     */
    public Map<String, BigDecimal> calculateBalances(
            String userId,
            List<LedgerEntry> entries) {

        return entries.stream()
            .filter(e -> e.userId().equals(userId))
            .collect(Collectors.groupingBy(
                LedgerEntry::currency,
                Collectors.reducing(
                    BigDecimal.ZERO,
                    e -> e.type() == EntryType.CREDIT
                        ? e.amount()
                        : e.amount().negate(),
                    BigDecimal::add
                )
            ));
    }

    /**
     * Balance at a specific point in time — same as above
     * but entries pre-filtered by timestamp externally.
     * (Clean separation of concerns — this function doesn't
     *  know about time; the caller filters the entry list.)
     */
    public BigDecimal balanceForCurrency(
            String userId,
            String currency,
            List<LedgerEntry> entries) {

        return calculateBalances(userId, entries)
            .getOrDefault(currency, BigDecimal.ZERO);
    }
}

// Tests
@Test
void singleCurrencyBalance() {
    var calc = new BalanceCalculator();
    var entries = List.of(
        new LedgerEntry("u1", "GBP", CREDIT, new BigDecimal("100.00")),
        new LedgerEntry("u1", "GBP", DEBIT,  new BigDecimal("30.00")),
        new LedgerEntry("u1", "GBP", CREDIT, new BigDecimal("50.00"))
    );
    assertThat(calc.balanceForCurrency("u1", "GBP", entries))
        .isEqualByComparingTo("120.00");
}

@Test
void multiCurrencyBalance() {
    var calc = new BalanceCalculator();
    var entries = List.of(
        new LedgerEntry("u1", "GBP", CREDIT, new BigDecimal("100.00")),
        new LedgerEntry("u1", "EUR", CREDIT, new BigDecimal("200.00")),
        new LedgerEntry("u1", "EUR", DEBIT,  new BigDecimal("50.00"))
    );
    var balances = calc.calculateBalances("u1", entries);
    assertThat(balances.get("GBP")).isEqualByComparingTo("100.00");
    assertThat(balances.get("EUR")).isEqualByComparingTo("150.00");
}

@Test
void ignoresOtherUsers() {
    var calc = new BalanceCalculator();
    var entries = List.of(
        new LedgerEntry("u1", "USD", CREDIT, new BigDecimal("500.00")),
        new LedgerEntry("u2", "USD", CREDIT, new BigDecimal("999.00"))
    );
    assertThat(calc.balanceForCurrency("u1", "USD", entries))
        .isEqualByComparingTo("500.00");
}

@Test
void returnsZeroForUnknownCurrency() {
    var calc = new BalanceCalculator();
    assertThat(calc.balanceForCurrency("u1", "JPY", List.of()))
        .isEqualByComparingTo("0.00");
}

@Test
void handlesNegativeBalance() {
    var calc = new BalanceCalculator();
    var entries = List.of(
        new LedgerEntry("u1", "GBP", DEBIT, new BigDecimal("200.00"))
        // No credit — negative balance possible during ledger repair
    );
    assertThat(calc.balanceForCurrency("u1", "GBP", entries))
        .isEqualByComparingTo("-200.00");
}

💬 ROUND 3 — ENGINEERING CULTURE & JUDGMENT (35 min, English)

Setting: Engineering Manager + Senior Engineer.

👩 Anya (EM): Revolut has had public incidents in the past —

briefly blocking transactions due to a fraud system bug in 2018.

If you were on the team that shipped that bug, what would you

want the culture to look like in the aftermath?

👨 Khanh: I'd want three things from the culture — and they

have to exist together, because each one is hollow without the others.

First: a fast, honest postmortem with no blame.

Within 24 hours. Every step of the failure timeline documented

with facts, not narratives. The goal of that document is

to identify the system condition that made the bug easy to

ship — not to find who wrote the line of code. If the culture

punishes the person, people start hiding problems instead of

surfacing them. That's how small incidents become large ones.

Second: engineering changes, not promises.

The postmortem must produce specific, assignable action items

with owners and due dates. Not "we will be more careful."

That's meaningless. Specific: *"We will add integration tests

that simulate this fraud rule input. Owner: X. Due: two weeks."*

I've seen postmortems that were beautifully written and led

to zero change. That's worse than no postmortem.

Third: executive visibility without witch-hunting.

Leadership needs to know what happened — that's legitimate.

But the communication to leadership should come from the

team, in writing, and should focus on the systemic fix,

not on individual accountability. If leadership uses the

incident as an opportunity to find someone to blame,

you will lose your best engineers, because the best engineers

take risks. Risk-taking and blameless culture have to coexist.

Revolut's 2018 incident — from what's publicly known —

was handled reasonably well. They communicated quickly

to users, restored service fast, and were transparent.

That's the bar. The internal culture in the aftermath

determines whether the lesson sticks or the same incident

happens again in 18 months.

👩 Anya: Revolut moves into new markets quickly — a new

country every few weeks at peak expansion. Engineers need

to build features that work globally by default. How do you

think about internationalisation in backend engineering?

👨 Khanh: Internationalisation in backend is mostly about

resisting assumptions — and the assumptions are sneaky

because they feel like facts.

The ones I've been burned by personally, and now check explicitly:

Currency and arithmetic: never use double or float

for money. Always BigDecimal, always explicit rounding mode,

always store amounts as integers in the smallest unit

(cents, pence, đồng) and convert for display only.

Time zones: UTC everywhere in the system — storage,

logs, Kafka messages, API payloads. Convert to local time

only at the presentation layer. One timezone assumption

buried in a scheduled job caused a reconciliation bug

for our Vietnamese customers that took two days to find.

Phone numbers: E.164 format from day one. Never store

phone numbers as int or without country code. I've seen

a system that stored Vietnamese numbers without the country

code break the moment they added international SMS.

Names and addresses: don't assume first name / last name

order, don't assume single-line addresses, don't assume

ASCII characters. Vietnam has Nguyễn Thị Thanh — accented

characters, family name first. A form that strips diacritics

or reverses name order breaks KYC for an entire country.

Regulatory per-market: transaction limits, KYC tiers,

and reporting thresholds differ by country. These shouldn't

be hardcoded — they should be configuration, ideally per-market

config in a database that compliance can update without a code deploy.

The discipline I try to install in the team: before shipping

any feature, ask — *"What assumption in this code is specific

to one country?"* If you can find one, you've found your next

internationalisation bug.

👨‍💻 Dmitri (Sr Eng): Last one. You discover during a code

review that a colleague has implemented a financial calculation

using double arithmetic. There's a subtle precision bug that

won't show up in normal usage but will cause rounding errors

on large amounts. How do you handle the review?

👨 Khanh: This is a case where being direct in the review

comment is both kind and necessary.

My comment would be specific and educational, not just corrective:

*"This calculation uses double, which has binary floating-point

precision issues. For example, 0.1 + 0.2 in Java returns

0.30000000000000004, not 0.3. For financial calculations,

this causes rounding errors that accumulate over large volumes

or large amounts. Please replace with BigDecimal and specify

an explicit RoundingMode — HALF_UP is standard for financial

rounding. I can share a reference or pair on this if helpful."*

I flag it as a blocking comment — not because I want to be

difficult, but because a precision bug in a financial calculation

is a correctness issue, not a style issue. It will cause real

money to be wrong. That's the line.

What I wouldn't do is fix it myself without telling them —

that removes their learning opportunity. And I wouldn't be

vague about why it matters — "use BigDecimal instead of double"

without explanation is dismissive. I want them to understand

the reason so they make the right choice next time without

needing the review to catch it.

If this is a pattern — double arithmetic appearing in

multiple PRs from multiple engineers — I'd raise it in a

team session and add a BigDecimal standard to our code

review checklist. One-on-one feedback for one PR;

systemic change for a systemic pattern.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

RoundCategoryScoreNotes
RecruiterQuality under speed + ownership10/10Non-negotiable filter; tooling question before accepting on-call
Tech AMulti-currency ledger + double-entry10/10Lock ordering for deadlock prevention, SERIALIZABLE, FX spread entry
Tech A+Crash between debit/credit10/10Outbox + saga for cross-shard; orphan sweep job
Tech BAML compliance conversation10/10Knowledge gap vs wilful non-compliance — handled differently
Tech CCoding: balance from ledger entries10/10Stream + groupingBy + reducing, 5 edge-case tests
Culture APost-incident culture10/10Postmortem + engineering changes + exec visibility without witch-hunt
Culture BInternationalisation10/10BigDecimal, UTC, E.164, diacritics, per-market config — specific and practical
Culture CBlocking code review10/10Educational comment, blocking flag, systemic vs individual response

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 Revolut Interview Tips

  1. Double-entry bookkeeping — every transfer has a debit and a credit; total must always balance
  2. BigDecimal not double — the most important financial rule; bring it up proactively
  3. Lock ordering to prevent deadlock — always lock accounts in sorted ID order in DB transactions
  4. SERIALIZABLE isolation — required for concurrent balance checks; know the tradeoff (performance)
  5. AML = legal, not optional — Revolut operates under FCA, NBB, and other regulators; this is binary
  6. UTC everywhere — convert to local time only in the presentation layer
  7. E.164 for phone numbers — Revolut is in 35+ countries; phone number assumptions break at border
  8. Revolut salaries (Senior/Staff): GBP 90–140K base + competitive equity; remote-first since 2020

📝 Strong English Phrases from This Interview

ContextPhrase
Quality vs speed"I protect a small set of non-negotiables and stay flexible on everything else."
On-call ownership"Full ownership without adequate tooling is just full exhaustion."
AML concern"AML monitoring isn't a best practice we trade off against speed — it's a legal requirement."
Compliance"'I flagged it in Jira' is not an adequate defence — for the company or for me professionally."
Post-incident"If leadership uses the incident to find someone to blame, you will lose your best engineers."
i18n"Before shipping any feature, ask: what assumption in this code is specific to one country?"
Code review"A precision bug in a financial calculation is a correctness issue, not a style issue."
Pattern vs instance"One-on-one feedback for one PR. Systemic change for a systemic pattern."

🎭 Mock Interview #46 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: LinkedIn (Sunnyvale / Remote, Professional Network)

Role: Senior Software Engineer — Feed & Notifications team.

LinkedIn has 1 billion members. The feed processes billions of

events daily — posts, likes, comments, job changes, endorsements.

Feed ranking, notification fan-out, and relevance at scale are

the core engineering challenges.

Tech: Java 17, Spring Boot, Kafka, Venice (internal feature store),

Espresso (distributed NoSQL), MySQL, Voldemort, Pinot (OLAP).

Balance: 50% English communication · 50% technical depth.

What makes LinkedIn interviews distinct:

Strong emphasis on large-scale distributed systems, data pipelines,

and — uniquely — how engineering decisions affect member experience

and professional trust. Culture is collaborative and values

engineers who balance speed with member safety.

📞 ROUND 1 — RECRUITER SCREEN (15 min, English)

👩 Priya (Recruiter): Hi Khanh! LinkedIn's mission is to

create economic opportunity for every member of the global

workforce. How does engineering connect to that mission

for you personally?

👨 Khanh: It connects more directly than most products.

LinkedIn is one of the few platforms where the quality

of the engineering directly affects someone's ability

to find a job, grow their career, or connect with the

right person at the right time. A job recommendation

algorithm that surfaces irrelevant roles wastes a

candidate's time. A notification system that's too

noisy trains people to ignore it — so the one

notification that matters gets missed.

That's a different kind of engineering responsibility

than making a feed slightly more engaging. It's about

whether the product actually fulfills its purpose for

the people who need it most. I find that motivating in

a way that's hard to manufacture — it's either there

or it isn't.

👩 Priya: Well said. One culture question: LinkedIn

values "members first." Have you ever pushed back on a

business or product requirement because you believed

it wasn't in users' best interest? What happened?

👨 Khanh: Yes — and I want to give you the honest version,

not the sanitised one.

A product manager wanted to increase the frequency of

notification emails to drive engagement metrics. The

proposed change would triple the number of emails a

user received per week. The metrics rationale was

reasonable — open rates on the first email of the week

were high, so "more emails = more engagement."

I raised a concern: open rate on email one is high

because it's the first. Email three of the week is

where users hit unsubscribe. We'd be optimising for

a short-term metric that would erode long-term trust.

I didn't just say no. I proposed an alternative:

personalised notification frequency based on observed

behaviour — users who open daily get more; users who

open weekly get fewer. Same engineering effort,

aligned incentives.

The PM was initially resistant because it was more

complex than "just increase the frequency." I wrote

a short doc with the expected churn curve based on

benchmark data from similar products, and shared it.

That moved the conversation.

We shipped the personalised approach. Unsubscribe rates

dropped 18%. Engagement among high-intent users improved.

The metric the PM cared about ended up being better,

not worse. But it took me being willing to be temporarily

unpopular to get there.

👩 Priya: Perfect. Technical rounds are next. Good luck!

💻 ROUND 2 — TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE (60 min, English)

Setting: Staff Engineer, Feed & Notifications.

PART A — Notification Fan-Out at Scale (25 min)

👨‍💻 Wei (Staff Eng): When someone with 500,000 followers

posts on LinkedIn, all 500,000 followers potentially need

a notification. We call this the "celebrity problem."

Design the notification fan-out system.

👨 Khanh: The celebrity problem is a classic fan-out

challenge. Let me start with a clarifying question:

do all 500,000 followers need real-time notification,

or is near-real-time (within a few minutes) acceptable

for most users? And does notification relevance filter

reduce the actual fan-out — e.g., only followers who

have engaged with this person recently get notified?

*Assuming: near-real-time acceptable, relevance filter

reduces effective fan-out to ~20% of followers.*

Two fan-out strategies — push vs pull:

Push fan-out (write to each follower on post):

Pre-compute and store a notification for each follower.

Fast reads. But 500K writes per celebrity post is expensive.

Pull fan-out (read following list on load):

No pre-computation. On feed load, merge posts from

all followed accounts. Scales read side cheaply.

But complex at read time for large follow graphs.

Hybrid (LinkedIn's actual approach):

  • Normal users (< 5K followers): push fan-out
  • Celebrities (> 5K followers): pull fan-out at read time
  • Notification specifically: push to an async queue,

batch and filter before delivery

@Service
public class NotificationFanOutService {

    private static final int CELEBRITY_THRESHOLD = 5_000;

    public void fanOut(Post post) {
        int followerCount = socialGraphService
            .getFollowerCount(post.getAuthorId());

        if (followerCount <= CELEBRITY_THRESHOLD) {
            pushFanOut(post);    // write to each follower's queue
        } else {
            deferredFanOut(post); // log event; pull at read time
        }
    }

    private void pushFanOut(Post post) {
        // Paginate followers to avoid memory explosion
        int pageSize = 500;
        int page     = 0;
        List<String> followers;

        do {
            followers = socialGraphService.getFollowers(
                post.getAuthorId(), page++, pageSize);

            List<NotificationTask> tasks = followers.stream()
                .filter(uid -> relevanceFilter.shouldNotify(
                    uid, post)) // engagement-based filter
                .map(uid -> NotificationTask.of(uid, post))
                .toList();

            // Bulk publish to Kafka — consumers handle delivery
            kafkaTemplate.send("notifications.pending",
                new BulkNotificationEvent(tasks));

        } while (followers.size() == pageSize);
    }

    private void deferredFanOut(Post post) {
        // Just record the post event — no pre-fan-out
        // Feed service pulls celebrity posts at read time
        kafkaTemplate.send("posts.celebrity",
            post.getAuthorId(), new CelebrityPostEvent(post));
    }
}

Notification delivery pipeline (Kafka consumers):

@KafkaListener(topics = "notifications.pending",
               groupId = "notification-delivery",
               concurrency = "20")
public void deliver(BulkNotificationEvent event) {
    for (NotificationTask task : event.getTasks()) {
        // Check user's notification preferences
        NotificationPreferences prefs = prefsCache.get(task.getUserId());
        if (!prefs.isEnabled(task.getType())) continue;

        // Batch: in-app notification (immediate)
        inAppStore.upsert(task.getUserId(), task.toInAppNotification());

        // Email: only if user hasn't received one in last 24h for this type
        if (prefs.isEmailEnabled() &&
                !recentEmailTracker.sentRecently(task.getUserId(),
                    task.getType(), Duration.ofHours(24))) {
            emailQueue.enqueue(task.toEmailNotification());
        }
    }
}

Deduplication: If the same event triggers multiple

notifications (e.g., 5 people like your post within

a minute), batch into one digest notification rather

than sending 5. Controlled by a 5-minute dedup window

per (userId, notificationType, targetId) in Redis.

👨‍💻 Wei: The relevance filter decides whether to notify

a follower. How would you design that filter without making

it a black-box ML model that nobody can explain?

👨 Khanh: I'd design it as a transparent rule engine

with observable inputs and explainable outputs — not

because ML is wrong, but because trust requires explainability

for something that affects whether members see important

career content.

Tiered rule engine:

public class RelevanceFilter {

    public boolean shouldNotify(String followerId, Post post) {
        double score = computeRelevanceScore(followerId, post);
        // Threshold tuned by experimentation (e.g., 0.3)
        return score >= NOTIFY_THRESHOLD;
    }

    private double computeRelevanceScore(String uid, Post post) {
        // Each signal is observable and logged separately
        double engagementSignal = recentEngagement(uid,
            post.getAuthorId()); // 0.0–1.0: did they interact recently?
        double recencySignal    = recencyScore(post.getAuthorId(),
            uid);               // 0.0–1.0: how fresh is their connection?
        double topicSignal      = topicOverlap(uid,
            post.getTopics());  // 0.0–1.0: do their interests match?
        double activitySignal   = memberActivity(uid); // are they active?

        // Weighted sum — weights are tunable config, not hardcoded
        return weights.engagement() * engagementSignal
             + weights.recency()    * recencySignal
             + weights.topic()      * topicSignal
             + weights.activity()   * activitySignal;
    }
}

Why this approach over a pure ML model:

  • Every notification decision can be audited: *"User X

wasn't notified because their engagement score was 0.1

and topic overlap was 0.0."*

  • Product and trust teams can tune weights without an

ML deployment.

  • A/B testing is clean — swap weights, measure outcomes.
  • If a member complains "why didn't I see this?" — we

can answer specifically.

ML can be layered on top later to learn the optimal weights.

But the structure remains explainable.

PART B — COMMUNICATION: Presenting a System Design Decision (15 min)

👨‍💻 Wei: Imagine you've designed the notification

fan-out system and you need to present it to a group

of four stakeholders: a product manager, a data scientist,

a mobile engineer, and a senior backend engineer.

How do you structure that presentation?

👨 Khanh: Four different people with four different

contexts. A presentation that works for one will lose

another. So I structure it in layers.

Layer 1 — the shared opening (2 minutes):

"Here's the problem we're solving and why it matters."

Everyone needs this, regardless of role. I frame it

in member terms: *"When someone with 500K followers posts,

how do we make sure the right followers see it promptly

without crashing our systems?"* That's relatable to

everyone in the room.

Layer 2 — the key decision and rationale (5 minutes):

The hybrid push/pull model. I explain this in plain

language — no code, no jargon. For the PM: what the

user experience looks like. For the data scientist:

where the relevance filter plugs in. For the mobile

engineer: what the notification payload looks like and

when it arrives. For the backend engineer: the high-level

architecture diagram.

This is the core. Everyone needs to understand the

decision even if they don't understand every implementation detail.

Layer 3 — the deep dives (optional, per audience):

After the shared layer, I ask: *"Does anyone want to

go deeper on a specific area?"* The backend engineer

will want to talk about Kafka partition design and

failure modes. The data scientist will want to talk

about the relevance filter signals. The mobile engineer

will want to talk about the push notification contract.

The PM probably doesn't need either.

What I never do:

Start with the technical architecture diagram and expect

everyone to follow. That's presenting to the room you

wish you had, not the room you're in.

PART C — CODING: Feed Merge (20 min)

👨‍💻 Wei: *"Given N sorted streams of posts (sorted by

timestamp, newest first), merge them into a single sorted

stream. This is the celebrity pull fan-out read path —

merging posts from multiple followed accounts.

Handle it efficiently."*

👨 Khanh: Classic K-way merge — priority queue, O(N log K)

where K is the number of streams.

public record Post(String postId, String authorId, Instant publishedAt,
                   String content) {}

public class FeedMerger {

    /**
     * Merges K sorted (newest-first) post streams into one sorted stream.
     * Uses a max-heap on publishedAt — newest post wins each poll.
     * Time: O(N log K), Space: O(K) where K = number of streams.
     */
    public List<Post> merge(List<Iterator<Post>> streams, int limit) {
        // Max-heap: compare by publishedAt descending (newest first)
        PriorityQueue<HeapEntry> heap = new PriorityQueue<>(
            Comparator.comparing((HeapEntry e) -> e.post().publishedAt())
                      .reversed()
        );

        // Seed the heap with the first post from each stream
        for (int i = 0; i < streams.size(); i++) {
            Iterator<Post> stream = streams.get(i);
            if (stream.hasNext()) {
                heap.offer(new HeapEntry(stream.next(), stream, i));
            }
        }

        List<Post> result = new ArrayList<>();

        while (!heap.isEmpty() && result.size() < limit) {
            HeapEntry entry = heap.poll();
            result.add(entry.post());

            // Advance that stream and push its next post into the heap
            if (entry.stream().hasNext()) {
                heap.offer(new HeapEntry(
                    entry.stream().next(), entry.stream(), entry.streamIndex()));
            }
        }

        return result;
    }

    private record HeapEntry(Post post, Iterator<Post> stream, int streamIndex) {}
}

// Tests
@Test
void mergesTwoStreams() {
    var merger = new FeedMerger();
    Instant now = Instant.now();

    List<Post> streamA = List.of(
        new Post("a1", "userA", now.minusSeconds(1), "A newest"),
        new Post("a2", "userA", now.minusSeconds(10), "A older")
    );
    List<Post> streamB = List.of(
        new Post("b1", "userB", now.minusSeconds(5), "B middle"),
        new Post("b2", "userB", now.minusSeconds(20), "B oldest")
    );

    var result = merger.merge(
        List.of(streamA.iterator(), streamB.iterator()), 10);

    assertThat(result).extracting(Post::postId)
        .containsExactly("a1", "b1", "a2", "b2");
}

@Test
void respectsLimit() {
    var merger = new FeedMerger();
    Instant now = Instant.now();

    List<Post> stream = IntStream.range(0, 100)
        .mapToObj(i -> new Post("p" + i, "u1",
            now.minusSeconds(i), "post " + i))
        .toList();

    var result = merger.merge(List.of(stream.iterator()), 10);
    assertThat(result).hasSize(10);
}

@Test
void handlesEmptyStreams() {
    var merger = new FeedMerger();
    Instant now = Instant.now();

    List<Post> nonEmpty = List.of(
        new Post("p1", "u1", now, "only post"));

    var result = merger.merge(
        List.of(Collections.<Post>emptyList().iterator(),
                nonEmpty.iterator()), 10);

    assertThat(result).extracting(Post::postId).containsExactly("p1");
}

@Test
void handlesAllEmptyStreams() {
    var merger = new FeedMerger();
    var result = merger.merge(
        List.of(Collections.<Post>emptyList().iterator()), 10);
    assertThat(result).isEmpty();
}

💬 ROUND 3 — LEADERSHIP & MEMBER TRUST (35 min, English)

Setting: Engineering Manager + Staff Engineer.

👩 Lisa (EM): LinkedIn is unusual in that a bad engineering

decision can affect someone's career. Has that kind of

responsibility ever made you slow down and think differently

about a technical choice?

👨 Khanh: Yes — and there's a specific instance I think about.

I was working on a job recommendation feature. We were

optimising the ranking model for click-through rate —

a standard engagement metric. During a review, a data

scientist on the team pointed out something I wouldn't

have thought to check: our model was systematically

ranking senior roles lower for profiles with career gaps,

and lower-paying roles higher for certain geographic regions.

The model wasn't wrong by its own metric. It was learning

patterns in historical data that reflected real-world bias.

But "accurate to historical patterns" and "fair" are not

the same thing. A model that learns that people with

career gaps don't get senior roles will ensure that

people with career gaps don't get senior roles.

We paused the rollout. That was an uncomfortable decision —

we had a timeline and the click-through numbers were good.

But shipping a model that systematically disadvantaged

certain groups of job seekers wasn't a risk I could

rationalise as an acceptable tradeoff.

We spent three weeks auditing the training data for

demographic proxies, added fairness constraints to the

model objective, and retrained. The final model had

slightly lower click-through but significantly better

fairness across career gap and geography segments.

The lesson I carry from it: when you're building something

that affects people's economic opportunities, the

responsibility check isn't "does the metric look good?"

It's "who does this work for, and who does it not work for?"

👩 Lisa: How do you handle it when your engineering

team is moving fast and the quality of collaboration

starts to drop — people skip reviews, documentation

gets sparse, on-call gets noisy?

👨 Khanh: I've been in that situation, and the instinct

I've learned to resist is addressing it with more process.

The temptation is: people are skipping reviews, so mandate

reviews. Documentation is sparse, so require documentation.

On-call is noisy, so add an alert review meeting.

That almost never works. Engineers skip reviews because

they're overloaded or because the reviews aren't adding

value. Adding a rule that says "you must review" doesn't

change the underlying condition. It just adds friction

and resentment.

What I do instead is start with a conversation —

not a retrospective where everyone has to say something,

but one-on-one conversations with three or four people

to understand what's actually happening. Is it overload?

Is it unclear expectations? Is it that reviews are slow

and blocking their work?

Then I address the underlying thing. If it's overload,

the question is: can we reduce scope? If it's reviews

being slow, can we establish a 24-hour SLA? If it's

on-call noise, can we dedicate one sprint to silence

the top five flapping alerts?

The principle is: treat process problems the same way

you treat technical problems — find the root cause,

don't just add more monitoring to the symptom.

👨‍💻 Ryan (Staff Eng): Last question. LinkedIn has been

around for 20+ years and has significant technical debt

in its core systems. How do you stay motivated working

on old systems that need modernisation, rather than

greenfield projects?

👨 Khanh: Honestly, I find old systems more interesting

than greenfield, not less.

A greenfield project starts with a blank slate and a set

of assumptions. An old system tells you what actually

happened — which assumptions held up and which didn't,

where the original design hit limits, what the real

usage patterns turned out to be. That's information

you can't manufacture.

When I work on an old system, I try to understand it

before I change it. Not superficially — I mean reading

the original design docs if they exist, talking to

engineers who built it if they're still around, reading

the incident history to understand what has broken

and why. That context makes every improvement more

targeted and less likely to introduce new problems.

The other thing that motivates me is the compounding

value. Improving a system that processes billions of

requests per day by 10% is worth more than building

a new system that processes a million. The impact is

a function of scale, not novelty.

I also think greenfield envy — the feeling that the

interesting work is always the new thing — is a sign

of underestimating the complexity of what exists.

The LinkedIn feed has been running for 20 years

under production load. Understanding it well enough

to change it safely is genuinely hard. I find hard

things interesting.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

RoundCategoryScoreNotes
RecruiterMission alignment + members first10/10Job recommendations affecting career; pushed back on notification frequency
Tech ANotification fan-out + celebrity problem10/10Hybrid push/pull, relevance filter, dedup window, paginated fan-out
Tech A+Explainable relevance filter10/10Weighted rule engine with observable inputs; ML-ready but auditable
Tech BMulti-audience presentation10/10Layered structure: shared opening → key decision → optional deep dives
Tech CCoding: K-way merge10/10Max-heap, O(N log K), 4 edge-case tests
Culture AFairness in job recommendation10/10Paused rollout; fairness constraint added; "accurate ≠ fair" framing
Culture BCollaboration quality drop10/10Find root cause, not add more process; one-on-one before retrospective
Culture CWorking on legacy systems10/10Old systems reveal what actually happened; compounding scale value

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 LinkedIn Interview Tips

  1. Celebrity problem = hybrid push/pull — push for normal users, pull fan-out for high-follower accounts
  2. Notification deduplication — dedup window per (userId, notificationType, targetId) in Redis
  3. Explainability matters at LinkedIn — member trust requires auditable, not black-box, ranking decisions
  4. K-way merge = priority queue — O(N log K); know it cold for feed, search result, timeline merging
  5. Fairness is a first-class engineering concern — LinkedIn interview will test if you think about who a model disadvantages
  6. Multi-audience presentations — layer: shared context → key decision → optional deep dives per role
  7. Legacy > greenfield — LinkedIn values engineers who can modernise complex existing systems
  8. LinkedIn salaries (Senior/Staff, SWE): USD 160–230K base + RSU; total comp USD 250K–400K+

📝 Strong English Phrases from This Interview

ContextPhrase
Mission"Whether the product actually fulfills its purpose for the people who need it most."
Pushback"It took me being willing to be temporarily unpopular to get there."
Fairness"'Accurate to historical patterns' and 'fair' are not the same thing."
Fairness"Who does this work for — and who does it not work for?"
Process"Treat process problems the same way you treat technical problems — find the root cause."
Presentation"Presenting to the room you wish you had, not the room you're in."
Legacy"Old systems tell you what actually happened — which assumptions held and which didn't."
Scale"Improving a system that processes billions of requests by 10% is worth more than building a new one."

🎭 Mock Interview #47 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: Coinbase (San Francisco / Remote, Crypto Exchange)

Role: Senior Software Engineer — Trading & Order Management team.

Coinbase is the largest US crypto exchange, processing billions of

dollars in trades daily across 250+ crypto assets. Engineers

face challenges around order book management, blockchain confirmation

latency, wallet security, and regulatory compliance in 100+ countries.

Tech: Java 17, gRPC, PostgreSQL, Redis, Kafka, Go (some services),

internal blockchain integration layer.

Balance: 50% English communication · 50% technical depth.

What makes Coinbase interviews distinct:

They test deep understanding of financial systems with crypto-specific

nuance — blockchain finality, wallet custody, trade settlement.

Communication emphasis is on clarity under complexity and

explaining highly technical concepts to non-technical audiences.

📞 ROUND 1 — RECRUITER SCREEN (15 min, English)

👩 Rachel (Recruiter): Hi Khanh! Crypto is a polarising

space — some engineers love it, some are sceptical.

Where do you stand, and why does that bring you to Coinbase?

👨 Khanh: Honest answer: I'm interested in the engineering

problems more than the asset class itself.

Crypto creates a genuinely novel class of technical challenges

that don't exist in traditional finance. Blockchain confirmation

latency — where a transaction is "pending" for minutes or hours

before it's final — creates state management problems that have

no clean precedent in bank-to-bank transfers. Wallet custody

at scale, where losing a private key means losing money

permanently with no recourse, sets a different reliability

bar than any system I've worked on before.

I also think financial inclusion is a real use case —

sending money across borders cheaply and quickly matters

to hundreds of millions of people, and crypto infrastructure

is part of that story whether or not you believe in any

particular token's long-term value.

What brings me to Coinbase specifically: you operate in

highly regulated markets, which means you're building

crypto infrastructure with the same compliance rigour as

traditional finance. That's a harder problem and a more

interesting one than unregulated exchanges.

👩 Rachel: Coinbase has had some well-publicised incidents —

outages during market spikes, briefly incorrect prices.

How do you think about building reliable systems in a

domain where the asset price can move 30% in an hour?

👨 Khanh: Price volatility and system reliability are

actually correlated in the worst possible way — the moments

when your system is under the most load are exactly the

moments when it must be most reliable, because that's when

your users need it most urgently.

The design principle that follows from this: never let

traffic volume determine your error rate. A system that

works at 1,000 requests per second but falls over at

10,000 isn't reliable — it's just lightly loaded.

Load shedding, circuit breakers, and graceful degradation

need to be designed in, not bolted on when the first spike hits.

The second principle: during a market spike, users can

tolerate slightly stale prices or a 500ms order submission

latency. What they cannot tolerate is submitting an order

and not knowing whether it was accepted. The worst UX

in a volatile market is uncertainty. So your first

reliability investment should be in clear, accurate order

status communication — even if the underlying system

is under stress.

👩 Rachel: Perfect framing. Three technical rounds next.

Good luck, Khanh!

💻 ROUND 2 — TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE (60 min, English)

Setting: Staff Engineer, Trading Platform.

PART A — Order Book & Trade Matching (25 min)

👨‍💻 Sanjay (Staff Eng): An order book holds buy orders

(bids) and sell orders (asks) for a trading pair like

BTC/USD. When a new order arrives, it tries to match

against existing orders at a suitable price. Design

the data structure and matching engine.

👨 Khanh: Let me clarify the order types first —

this shapes the matching logic significantly.

*Assuming: limit orders (price specified) and market orders

(fill at best available price). No stop orders for now.*

Order book data structure:

// Bid side: sorted by price DESC (highest bid gets matched first)
// Ask side: sorted by price ASC  (lowest ask gets matched first)
// Within same price: FIFO by arrival time (time priority)

public class OrderBook {

    // Key: price, Value: deque of orders at that price (FIFO)
    private final TreeMap<BigDecimal, Deque<Order>> bids =
        new TreeMap<>(Comparator.reverseOrder()); // highest first

    private final TreeMap<BigDecimal, Deque<Order>> asks =
        new TreeMap<>(); // lowest first

    private final Map<String, Order> ordersById = new HashMap<>();

    public void addOrder(Order order) {
        ordersById.put(order.getId(), order);
        TreeMap<BigDecimal, Deque<Order>> side =
            order.getSide() == Side.BUY ? bids : asks;
        side.computeIfAbsent(order.getPrice(),
            p -> new ArrayDeque<>()).addLast(order);
    }

    public BigDecimal bestBid() {
        return bids.isEmpty() ? null : bids.firstKey();
    }

    public BigDecimal bestAsk() {
        return asks.isEmpty() ? null : asks.firstKey();
    }

    public void removeOrder(String orderId) {
        Order order = ordersById.remove(orderId);
        if (order == null) return;
        TreeMap<BigDecimal, Deque<Order>> side =
            order.getSide() == Side.BUY ? bids : asks;
        Deque<Order> q = side.get(order.getPrice());
        if (q != null) {
            q.remove(order);
            if (q.isEmpty()) side.remove(order.getPrice());
        }
    }
}

Matching engine — price-time priority:

@Service
public class MatchingEngine {

    @Transactional
    public List<Trade> match(Order incoming) {
        List<Trade> trades = new ArrayList<>();
        TreeMap<BigDecimal, Deque<Order>> opposingSide =
            incoming.getSide() == Side.BUY
                ? orderBook.getAsks()
                : orderBook.getBids();

        BigDecimal remaining = incoming.getQuantity();

        while (remaining.compareTo(BigDecimal.ZERO) > 0
                && !opposingSide.isEmpty()) {

            BigDecimal bestPrice = opposingSide.firstKey();

            // Check price compatibility
            boolean priceMatches = incoming.getSide() == Side.BUY
                ? incoming.getPrice().compareTo(bestPrice) >= 0  // bid >= ask
                : incoming.getPrice().compareTo(bestPrice) <= 0; // ask <= bid

            // Market orders always match (no price check)
            if (!incoming.isMarketOrder() && !priceMatches) break;

            Deque<Order> ordersAtPrice = opposingSide.get(bestPrice);
            Order resting = ordersAtPrice.peekFirst();

            BigDecimal fillQty = remaining.min(resting.getRemainingQty());
            BigDecimal fillPrice = resting.getPrice(); // resting order's price

            // Record trade
            trades.add(Trade.of(incoming.getId(), resting.getId(),
                fillQty, fillPrice));

            // Update quantities
            remaining = remaining.subtract(fillQty);
            resting.fill(fillQty);

            if (resting.isFullyFilled()) {
                ordersAtPrice.pollFirst();
                if (ordersAtPrice.isEmpty()) opposingSide.remove(bestPrice);
            }
        }

        // Any unfilled remainder: add to book (limit) or reject (market)
        if (remaining.compareTo(BigDecimal.ZERO) > 0) {
            if (incoming.isMarketOrder()) {
                incoming.setStatus(OrderStatus.PARTIALLY_FILLED_CANCELLED);
            } else {
                incoming.setRemainingQty(remaining);
                orderBook.addOrder(incoming);
            }
        } else {
            incoming.setStatus(OrderStatus.FULLY_FILLED);
        }

        return trades;
    }
}

Persistence after matching:

// Every match produces an immutable audit trail
// Never update trades — only append
@Entity
public class Trade {
    @Id     private String tradeId;
    private String makerOrderId;  // resting order
    private String takerOrderId;  // incoming order
    private BigDecimal quantity;
    private BigDecimal price;
    private String tradingPair;   // "BTC/USD"
    private Instant executedAt;
}

Why TreeMap for order book?

O(log n) for insert, remove, and find-best-price.

The FIFO deque at each price level gives time priority

within the same price — ensuring fairness.

👨‍💻 Sanjay: When a user buys Bitcoin, they expect to

receive it in their wallet. But Bitcoin confirmation takes

10–60 minutes on-chain. How do you design the settlement flow?

👨 Khanh: This is where crypto differs fundamentally

from traditional equity trading.
