channels without resolution — I'd leave. That's a real

option, not a theoretical one. But I'd exercise it for

a specific, concrete reason, not for a general discomfort

with the mission.

The engineers who resigned made a choice I respect.

What I'd push back on is the framing that discomfort

with complexity equals having principles. Working in

grey areas with clear personal standards is also a

principled position.

👩 Diane: Palantir's culture values direct, blunt

feedback. How do you give difficult feedback to a

colleague without damaging the relationship?

👨 Khanh: Blunt and respectful aren't opposites —

they only feel that way when blunt is mistaken for rude.

The formula I use: be specific about the behaviour,

honest about the impact, and curious about the cause —

in that order.

Specific: *"In the last three design reviews, you presented

conclusions without walking through the tradeoffs."*

Not: "You're not rigorous."

Honest about impact: *"It makes it hard for the team

to trust the recommendation, because we don't know

what alternatives were considered."*

Curious about cause: *"Is that intentional — do you

prefer to share conclusions and discuss tradeoffs live?

Or is something else going on?"*

The curious question matters because it leaves space

for me to be wrong about my interpretation. Maybe they're

under time pressure. Maybe they think the tradeoffs are

obvious. Maybe there's context I don't have.

Blunt without that curiosity is just criticism.

Blunt with it is a conversation that has a chance

of actually changing something.

👩 Diane: Last question. You've been debugging a

production issue for four hours. You've narrowed it

down to two possible causes but can't definitively

confirm which one. Your manager needs an update.

What do you say?

👨 Khanh: I give an honest state-of-play, not a

false certainty or a vague "still investigating."

*"I've been on this for four hours. The issue is

definitely in the data ingestion pipeline — I've

ruled out the API layer and the storage layer.

I have two hypotheses left: either the source connector

is silently dropping records with malformed timestamps,

or the entity resolver is creating duplicate IDs under

concurrent load. I believe it's more likely to be

the first — I have one log entry that supports it —

but I haven't been able to reproduce it reliably yet.*

*I need roughly two more hours to confirm. If I'm

right about hypothesis one, the fix is a 30-minute

patch. If I'm wrong and it's hypothesis two, that's

a deeper concurrency bug — probably a day's work.*

*Do you want me to continue to confirmation, or would

you prefer I implement a workaround now and investigate

the root cause separately?"*

That update does three things: it shows progress,

it gives a time estimate, and it hands the manager

a decision — which is appropriate when the timeline

matters for other stakeholders.

What I wouldn't say: "I'm still looking." That's

technically true and practically useless.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

RoundCategoryScoreNotes
RecruiterMission sensitivity10/10Nuanced view; asks about governance; not reflexive rejection or acceptance
RecruiterCustomer-facing engineering10/10Immediate feedback loop; knowing when quick fix vs proper fix
Tech AData integration pipeline10/10Entity resolution, conflict merge, DQ monitoring, source priority
Tech A+ID migration without disruption10/10Stable internal UUID + mapping table; downstream sees zero change
Tech BCIO confidence conversation10/10Four DQ dimensions with specific numbers; recurring review offer
Tech CCoding: graph reachability + cycle detection10/10BFS reachable + 3-colour DFS, 4 tests
Culture AControversial company mission10/10Principled nuance; specific trigger for leaving vs general discomfort
Culture BDebugging update to manager10/10Progress + two hypotheses + time estimate + decision handed back

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 Palantir Interview Tips

  1. Entity resolution — deterministic (exact ID match) then probabilistic (name + DOB similarity)
  2. Stable internal IDs — never use source IDs as canonical; mapping table absorbs external changes
  3. Data quality = 4 dimensions — completeness, consistency, timeliness, accuracy
  4. 3-colour DFS for cycle detection — WHITE/GRAY/BLACK; back edge to GRAY = cycle
  5. Mission sensitivity — Palantir expects you to have thought about this; vague answers fail
  6. Debugging updates — progress + hypotheses + time estimate + decision for manager
  7. "Blunt without curiosity is just criticism" — the Palantir feedback philosophy in one line
  8. Palantir salaries (Senior/SWE-3): USD 180–260K base + significant equity; GovCloud roles may vary

📝 Strong English Phrases from This Interview

ContextPhrase
Mission"Powerful tools mean the governance around how they're used matters enormously."
Customer"The feedback loop when you're in a room with a user is immediate in a way that dashboards never are."
Data quality"Trust requires evidence, not reassurance."
DQ presentation"A recurring review turns an anxiety into a process."
Controversy"Working in grey areas with clear personal standards is also a principled position."
Feedback"Blunt without curiosity is just criticism. Blunt with it is a conversation."
Debug update"'I'm still looking' is technically true and practically useless."
Debug update"I hand the manager a decision — which is appropriate when the timeline matters."

🎭 Mock Interview #59 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: Figma (San Francisco / Remote, Collaborative Design Platform)

Role: Senior Software Engineer — Real-Time Collaboration team.

Figma is the design tool used by 4M+ teams. Its core differentiator:

true multiplayer — multiple designers editing the same file

simultaneously, like Google Docs but for design.

The backend handles real-time sync, version history, and rendering

data for complex vector graphics.

Tech: Java 17 (backend services), TypeScript (frontend), Rust (engine),

WebSockets, CRDTs, PostgreSQL, AWS, Redis.

Balance: 50% English communication · 50% technical depth.

What makes Figma distinct:

Real-time collaboration is the product. Latency, consistency, and

conflict resolution are not edge cases — they're the entire problem.

Engineers must deeply understand distributed state synchronisation.

The culture is collaborative, design-forward, and strongly values

engineers who can communicate with designers and PMs clearly.

📞 ROUND 1 — RECRUITER SCREEN (15 min, English)

👩 Maya (Recruiter): Hi Khanh! Figma's core feature is

real-time multiplayer — multiple designers editing the

same canvas at once. Why is that technically hard?

👨 Khanh: It's hard because of the consistency problem.

When two designers edit the same canvas simultaneously —

one moves a rectangle, one changes its colour — their

edits are generated independently on their own devices.

The server receives them out of order due to network latency.

The question is: what does each designer see?

If we just apply edits in arrival order, the two designers

might see different final states — one sees rectangle moved

then colour changed, the other sees colour changed then

moved. That violates the expectation that they're in the

same shared workspace.

The solution space involves either locking (only one person

edits at a time — defeats the purpose) or conflict

resolution algorithms like Operational Transformation

(Google Docs' approach) or CRDTs — Conflict-free

Replicated Data Types — which are designed so that any

order of applying the same operations produces the

same result. The catch is that designing a CRDT for

hierarchical design data — nested layers, styles,

components — is significantly harder than for plain text.

👩 Maya: Figma was acquired by Adobe for $20B, then

the deal collapsed. How does that kind of external uncertainty

affect how you'd think about joining?

👨 Khanh: Honestly, I'd treat it as a useful signal

rather than a deterrent.

The deal collapsing means Figma remains an independent

company with full control over its product direction.

For engineers, independence often means faster decisions,

clearer mission, and fewer integration politics.

The alternative — being absorbed into a large incumbent —

often introduces the exact constraints that slow down

product development.

What I'd want to understand: did the Adobe acquisition

attempt change anything about Figma's internal culture

or hiring strategy? Companies that went through that

level of external pressure and came out independent

sometimes emerge with stronger clarity about what they're

building and why. That's actually attractive.

The risk I'd assess isn't the Adobe situation — it's

the standard early-IPO question: what does the growth

trajectory look like, and is the current product still

the one that justifies continued investment? Based on

what I've read, Figma's market position looks strong.

💻 ROUND 2 — TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE (60 min, English)

Setting: Staff Engineer, Real-Time Platform.

PART A — Real-Time Sync Architecture (25 min)

👨‍💻 Sam (Staff Eng): Two designers are editing the same

Figma file. Designer A moves a rectangle. Simultaneously,

Designer B changes the same rectangle's fill colour.

Both edits are sent to the server 50ms apart. Design the

sync system so both designers see the correct final state.

👨 Khanh: This is a classic concurrent edit problem.

The target state is: rectangle is both moved AND colour-changed,

and both designers converge to that same final state.

Core model — operation-based CRDT:

Each edit is an immutable, timestamped operation with

a vector clock for causal ordering:

public record Operation(
    String  operationId,    // UUID
    String  fileId,
    String  nodeId,         // which design element
    String  userId,
    OperationType type,     // MOVE | FILL | RESIZE | DELETE | etc.
    Map<String, Object> payload,
    VectorClock vectorClock,  // logical timestamp per user
    Instant wallClock
) {}

public record VectorClock(Map<String, Long> counters) {
    // A happens-before B if A.counters[u] <= B.counters[u] for all u
    public boolean happensBefore(VectorClock other) {
        return counters.entrySet().stream().allMatch(e ->
            e.getValue() <= other.counters().getOrDefault(e.getKey(), 0L));
    }

    public VectorClock increment(String userId) {
        Map<String, Long> next = new HashMap<>(counters);
        next.merge(userId, 1L, Long::sum);
        return new VectorClock(next);
    }
}

Server sync service:

@Service
public class SyncService {

    // In-memory operation log per file (or Redis for multi-instance)
    private final Map<String, List<Operation>> opLog =
        new ConcurrentHashMap<>();

    @MessageMapping("/file/{fileId}/ops")
    public void receiveOperation(@DestinationVariable String fileId,
                                  Operation op,
                                  SimpMessageHeaderAccessor headers) {

        // 1. Persist operation
        opLog.computeIfAbsent(fileId,
            k -> Collections.synchronizedList(new ArrayList<>())).add(op);
        persistenceService.save(op);

        // 2. Check for concurrent ops on same node (conflict candidates)
        List<Operation> concurrent = findConcurrent(fileId, op);

        // 3. Apply CRDT merge: for independent properties (move vs fill),
        //    both apply — no conflict. For same property from two users,
        //    last-write-wins by wall clock as tiebreaker.
        List<Operation> resolved = crdt.merge(op, concurrent);

        // 4. Broadcast resolved state to ALL clients in this file session
        messagingTemplate.convertAndSend(
            "/topic/file/" + fileId + "/ops",
            new BroadcastMessage(resolved, computeDocumentState(fileId))
        );
    }

    private List<Operation> findConcurrent(String fileId, Operation op) {
        return opLog.getOrDefault(fileId, List.of()).stream()
            .filter(o -> !o.operationId().equals(op.operationId()))
            .filter(o -> isConcurrent(o.vectorClock(), op.vectorClock()))
            .filter(o -> o.nodeId().equals(op.nodeId()))
            .toList();
    }

    // Concurrent: neither happens-before the other
    private boolean isConcurrent(VectorClock a, VectorClock b) {
        return !a.happensBefore(b) && !b.happensBefore(a);
    }
}

CRDT merge rules for design properties:

public class DesignCRDT {

    // Different properties on the same node = always composable
    // e.g., MOVE + FILL_COLOUR = apply both, no conflict
    public List<Operation> merge(Operation incoming,
                                  List<Operation> concurrent) {
        List<Operation> conflicts = concurrent.stream()
            .filter(c -> c.type() == incoming.type()) // same property
            .toList();

        if (conflicts.isEmpty()) {
            return List.of(incoming); // no conflict, apply as-is
        }

        // Same property conflict: last-write-wins by wall clock
        // Pick the operation with the latest timestamp
        Operation winner = Stream.concat(Stream.of(incoming),
                conflicts.stream())
            .max(Comparator.comparing(Operation::wallClock))
            .orElse(incoming);

        return List.of(winner);
    }
}

What the two designers see:

Designer A: MOVE rectangle to (200, 300)       @ t=0ms
Designer B: FILL colour = #FF0000               @ t=50ms
Server receives A first, then B.
Both are on different properties (position vs fill) → composable.
Final state: rectangle at (200,300) with fill #FF0000.
Both designers receive this broadcast → converge to same state. ✅

👨‍💻 Sam: A designer closes their laptop mid-edit for

2 hours, then reopens it. How does their client reconcile

with 2 hours of operations they missed?

👨 Khanh: Reconnection sync — the client needs to

catch up on operations that happened while it was offline.

// Client sends its last known operation ID on reconnect
// Server sends all operations since that point

@MessageMapping("/file/{fileId}/sync")
public void syncOnReconnect(@DestinationVariable String fileId,
                             SyncRequest req,
                             SimpMessageHeaderAccessor headers) {
    String userId = getUserId(headers);

    // Find all ops since client's last known state
    List<Operation> missed = opLog.getOrDefault(fileId, List.of())
        .stream()
        .filter(op -> op.wallClock().isAfter(req.getLastSeenAt()))
        .sorted(Comparator.comparing(Operation::wallClock))
        .toList();

    // Also check if client has pending local ops (offline edits)
    List<Operation> pending = req.getPendingOps();

    // Merge: apply missed server ops, then re-base client's pending ops
    List<Operation> rebaseResult = rebase(pending, missed);

    // Send full current document state + rebase instructions
    messagingTemplate.convertAndSendToUser(userId,
        "/queue/file/" + fileId + "/reconnect",
        new ReconnectResponse(
            computeDocumentState(fileId),
            rebaseResult
        )
    );
}

For 2 hours of missed ops — we don't replay 2 hours

of individual operations. Instead: send the current

canonical document state (snapshot), and the client

replaces its local state entirely. Local pending edits

are rebased on top of the snapshot.

This is the same pattern Git uses: git pull --rebase

rather than replaying all commits since divergence.

PART B — COMMUNICATION: Explaining a Sync Bug to a Designer (15 min)

👨‍💻 Sam: A designer files a bug:

*"I was editing with my colleague. I typed new text

into a text layer. Then suddenly my text disappeared

and my colleague's older text appeared instead.

Figma lost my work."*

How do you investigate and respond?

👨 Khanh: This is a conflict resolution bug — a case

where our last-write-wins logic picked the wrong winner.

I'd investigate before responding to confirm the cause.

Internal investigation:

Pull the operation log for their file session and

find the two concurrent text-edit operations.

Check the wall clock timestamps — if the client clocks

were slightly out of sync, our server might have chosen

the colleague's op as "later" even though the designer

typed after. This is the classic problem with wall-clock

tie-breaking: it assumes client clocks are reliable,

which they aren't.

Response to the designer — honest and specific:

*"Thank you for the detailed report — this is really helpful.

I've reviewed the operation log for your session.*

*What happened: you and your colleague both edited the

same text layer within milliseconds of each other.

Our system uses a 'last edit wins' rule for this case —

but in your session, there was a small clock difference

between your devices that caused your colleague's edit

to be treated as 'later,' even though you typed after.*

*I want to be direct: this is a bug in our conflict

resolution. Your work was not lost due to a network

error — it was incorrectly overwritten by our own logic.*

*We're tracking this issue and working on a fix that

uses server-assigned logical timestamps instead of device

clocks, which eliminates the clock skew problem.*

*In the short term, Figma preserves your version history —

you can recover your text from the 'Show version history'

option in the File menu. I'm happy to walk you through

that if it would help."*

What I wouldn't do: blame network conditions or

tell them to "try again." This was our bug.

PART C — CODING: Last-Write-Wins Register (20 min)

👨‍💻 Sam: *"Implement a thread-safe LWW (Last Write Wins)

register for a design property. Store a value with its

timestamp. On write: only update if the new timestamp is

strictly greater. On read: return current value. Thread-safe."*

👨 Khanh:

import java.util.concurrent.atomic.AtomicReference;

public class LwwRegister<T> {

    public record Entry<T>(T value, long timestampMs) {}

    // AtomicReference for lock-free thread safety
    private final AtomicReference<Entry<T>> ref;

    public LwwRegister(T initialValue, long initialTimestamp) {
        this.ref = new AtomicReference<>(
            new Entry<>(initialValue, initialTimestamp));
    }

    /**
     * Write value only if timestampMs > current timestamp.
     * Returns true if the write succeeded.
     */
    public boolean write(T value, long timestampMs) {
        while (true) {
            Entry<T> current = ref.get();
            if (timestampMs <= current.timestampMs()) {
                return false; // stale write — reject
            }
            Entry<T> next = new Entry<>(value, timestampMs);
            if (ref.compareAndSet(current, next)) {
                return true; // CAS succeeded
            }
            // CAS failed = another thread updated concurrently → retry
        }
    }

    /**
     * Read current value.
     */
    public T read() {
        return ref.get().value();
    }

    public long timestamp() {
        return ref.get().timestampMs();
    }
}

// Tests
@Test
void acceptsNewerWrite() {
    var reg = new LwwRegister<>("red", 100L);
    assertThat(reg.write("blue", 200L)).isTrue();
    assertThat(reg.read()).isEqualTo("blue");
}

@Test
void rejectsOlderWrite() {
    var reg = new LwwRegister<>("red", 200L);
    assertThat(reg.write("blue", 100L)).isFalse();
    assertThat(reg.read()).isEqualTo("red");
}

@Test
void rejectsEqualTimestamp() {
    var reg = new LwwRegister<>("red", 100L);
    assertThat(reg.write("blue", 100L)).isFalse();
    assertThat(reg.read()).isEqualTo("red"); // original preserved
}

@Test
void threadSafeUnderConcurrency() throws InterruptedException {
    var reg = new LwwRegister<>(0, 0L);
    int threads = 100;
    var latch = new java.util.concurrent.CountDownLatch(threads);

    for (int i = 1; i <= threads; i++) {
        final long ts = i;
        Thread.ofVirtual().start(() -> {
            reg.write((int) ts, ts);
            latch.countDown();
        });
    }
    latch.await();
    assertThat(reg.read()).isEqualTo(100); // highest timestamp wins
    assertThat(reg.timestamp()).isEqualTo(100L);
}

💬 ROUND 3 — CULTURE & CRAFT (30 min, English)

Setting: Engineering Manager.

👩 Priya (EM): Figma's users are designers, not engineers.

How does that change how you build and communicate?

👨 Khanh: It changes two things fundamentally: the

vocabulary I use and the empathy I bring to the problem.

Designers experience software differently from engineers.

An engineer dealing with a bug thinks about root cause.

A designer dealing with the same bug thinks "is my

work safe? Can I still deliver?" Those are different

questions, and the response needs to address both.

When communicating with designers about a technical

limitation or bug: lead with what it means for their work,

not how the system works. "Your text might not sync

correctly if you're offline" matters more than

"our CRDT doesn't support causal consistency under

partition." Both are true; only one is useful to them.

The other change: I'd actively seek out designers

as the primary users of anything I build. Not just

product managers' interpretation of what designers need —

direct observation. What are they doing in Figma when

they're frustrated? Where do they lose work? Where does

the interface feel slow or unreliable? Those are the

engineering problems worth solving.

Engineers who build for users they've never observed

make different mistakes than engineers who have.

👩 Priya: Your team is split on a technical approach —

half want to use CRDTs for a new collaboration feature,

half want to use Operational Transformation.

You have a strong opinion. How do you facilitate

the decision without steamrolling?

👨 Khanh: I try to separate two things: helping the

team reach a well-informed decision, and advocating for

my own position.

First step: make sure both sides are understood clearly.

I'd write up both approaches — not as "my way vs wrong way,"

but as a fair comparison: what each approach is good at,

where it breaks down, what it would cost to implement,

and what our specific constraints are. That document should

be readable by someone who hasn't followed the debate.

Second: identify the deciding criteria. For a collaboration

feature, I'd push the team to agree on the questions first —

"which approach handles our specific data types better?",

"which is easier to debug in production?", "which has

more implementation examples we can learn from?" — before

arguing the answers. Agreeing on criteria often resolves

the disagreement faster than arguing from positions.

Third — my opinion: I'd advocate for CRDTs specifically

because our data model is hierarchical (nested layers,

groups, components), and OT is notoriously hard to

extend to tree structures. I'd make that case clearly

and with evidence.

Fourth — disagree and commit: if the team reaches

a different conclusion through the process, I'd commit

to it fully. A decision made with good process and

good information that I disagree with is still a decision

I'll build well. I'd save my "I told you so" for the

retrospective, if it ever comes to that — and even then,

I'd frame it as a learning, not a score.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

RoundCategoryScoreNotes
RecruiterReal-time sync fundamentals10/10CRDTs vs OT; vector clocks; composable vs conflicting ops
RecruiterAdobe acquisition context10/10Independent = faster decisions; assess growth, not external drama
Tech AReal-time sync architecture10/10Vector clock ops, concurrent detection, CRDT merge, broadcast
Tech A+Reconnection reconciliation10/10Snapshot + rebase; Git pull analogy; avoid replaying 2h of ops
Tech BSync bug for designer10/10Own the bug; explain clock skew clearly; version history recovery path
Tech CCoding: LWW register10/10AtomicReference CAS loop, 4 tests including concurrent thread safety
Culture ABuilding for designers10/10Lead with work impact not system mechanics; observe real users
Culture BCRDT vs OT team split10/10Fair write-up → decide criteria first → advocate clearly → commit

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 Figma Interview Tips

  1. Vector clocks for causality — A happens-before B if all of A's counters ≤ B's; concurrent if neither
  2. CRDTs for design — different properties on same node = composable; same property = LWW with server timestamp
  3. LWW register = AtomicReference + CAS loop — lock-free, thread-safe, retry on CAS failure
  4. Reconnection = snapshot + rebase — don't replay 2h of ops; send current state, rebase local pending
  5. Clock skew is a real bug — client wall clocks are unreliable; use server-assigned logical timestamps
  6. Designer communication — "is my work safe?" before "here's how the system works"
  7. Team disagreements — write up both sides fairly → agree on criteria → advocate → commit
  8. Figma salaries (Senior): USD 180–260K base + RSU; strong remote-first culture

📝 Strong English Phrases from This Interview

ContextPhrase
Real-time sync"CRDTs are designed so any order of applying the same operations produces the same result."
CRDTs"Different properties = composable. Same property = last-write-wins with server timestamp."
Reconnection"Send the snapshot; the client replaces its local state. Same pattern as 'git pull --rebase'."
Bug response"This is a bug in our conflict resolution. Your work was not lost — it was incorrectly overwritten by our own logic."
Designer comms"Lead with what it means for their work, not how the system works."
User empathy"Engineers who build for users they've never observed make different mistakes."
Team decision"Agree on the criteria before arguing the answers."
Commit"A decision made with good process that I disagree with is still a decision I'll build well."

Java / Spring / Hibernate / System Design — Interview Q&A

Based on: Grokking The Java Interview + Grokking The Spring Boot Interview + Pham Khanh Tuong's CV

Scope: Theory only (no coding problems)

PART 1 — CORE JAVA

OOP Fundamentals

Q1. What is method overloading vs method overriding?

  • Overloading: same method name, different parameter types/count; resolved at compile time (static polymorphism).
  • Overriding: subclass redefines a superclass method with the same signature; resolved at runtime (dynamic polymorphism).

Q2. Can we override a static method?

No. Static methods belong to the class and are resolved at compile time. You can hide it by declaring the same static method in the subclass, but it is not true overriding.

Q3. Can we override a private or final method?

No. Private methods are not visible to subclasses. Final methods are explicitly locked from overriding.

Q4. What is covariant return type in overriding?

Since Java 5, an overriding method may return a subtype of the return type declared in the parent. Useful with clone().

Q5. What is the difference between abstract class and interface?

Abstract ClassInterface
MethodsCan have concrete methodsJava 8+: can have default/static methods
FieldsAny fieldsOnly public static final constants
InheritanceSingleMultiple
Use caseShare default behaviourDefine contract/type

Q6. What are the SOLID principles?

  • S – Single Responsibility: one class, one reason to change.
  • O – Open/Closed: open for extension, closed for modification.
  • L – Liskov Substitution: subtypes must be replaceable for their base types.
  • I – Interface Segregation: prefer small, specific interfaces.
  • D – Dependency Inversion: depend on abstractions, not concretions.

Q7. Composition vs Inheritance?

Composition is more flexible — behaviour can be changed at runtime by swapping the composed object. Inheritance is static. Prefer composition over inheritance (GoF principle).

Q8. Association, Aggregation, Composition?

  • Association: general relationship between objects.
  • Aggregation: weak "has-a"; parts can exist independently (department → employee).
  • Composition: strong "has-a"; parts cannot exist without the whole (house → room).

Q9. Strategy vs State pattern?

  • Strategy: switch algorithms at runtime; the context delegates to the chosen strategy.
  • State: object changes behaviour based on internal state; the state itself drives transitions.

Q10. Decorator vs Proxy vs Adapter?

  • Decorator: adds behaviour without changing the class (wraps it).
  • Proxy: controls access to another object.
  • Adapter: makes incompatible interfaces work together.

Q11. Singleton pattern — when to use and thread-safe implementation?

Use when exactly one instance is needed globally (e.g., config, connection pool). Thread-safe approach: double-checked locking with volatile, or an enum-based singleton.

Q12. Factory vs Abstract Factory?

  • Factory: creates one family of products.
  • Abstract Factory: creates families of related products without specifying concrete classes.

Java Core — String, Collections, Exceptions

Q13. String vs StringBuffer vs StringBuilder?

  • String: immutable; every change creates a new object.
  • StringBuffer: mutable, synchronized (thread-safe, slower).
  • StringBuilder: mutable, not synchronized (faster, single-thread use).

Q14. How does HashMap work internally?

HashMap uses an array of buckets. Key's hashCode() determines the bucket index. On collision, entries are chained (linked list, or red-black tree from Java 8 when size > 8). equals() is used to locate the exact key within a bucket.

Q15. equals() and hashCode() contract?

If a.equals(b) then a.hashCode() == b.hashCode(). The reverse is not required. You must override both together; breaking the contract corrupts HashMap/HashSet behaviour.

Q16. HashMap vs Hashtable vs ConcurrentHashMap?

HashMapHashtableConcurrentHashMap
Thread-safeNoYes (full lock)Yes (segment/bucket lock)
Null keys1 null keyNot allowedNot allowed
PerformanceBest single-threadPoor multi-threadBest multi-thread

Q17. ArrayList vs LinkedList?

  • ArrayList: backed by array; O(1) random access; O(n) insert/delete in middle.
  • LinkedList: doubly-linked list; O(n) random access; O(1) insert/delete at head/tail.

Q18. fail-fast vs fail-safe iterator?

  • fail-fast: throws ConcurrentModificationException if collection is modified during iteration (e.g., ArrayList, HashMap iterators).
  • fail-safe: works on a copy; no exception (e.g., CopyOnWriteArrayList, ConcurrentHashMap).

Q19. Checked vs Unchecked exceptions?

  • Checked: must be declared with throws or caught; represents recoverable conditions (e.g., IOException).
  • Unchecked (RuntimeException): programming errors; no forced handling (e.g., NullPointerException).

Q20. Comparable vs Comparator?

  • Comparable (natural order): implemented inside the class; compareTo().
  • Comparator (custom order): external; compare(); multiple comparators possible; does not require source access.

Q21. What is transient vs volatile?

  • transient: field excluded from serialization.
  • volatile: guarantees visibility of changes across threads; prevents caching in registers; establishes happens-before.

Q22. Serializable vs Externalizable?

  • Serializable: marker interface; default JVM serialization.
  • Externalizable: two methods readExternal()/writeExternal(); developer controls serialization fully; faster.

Q23. What is SerialVersionUID?

A static final long that acts as a version number for a Serializable class. JVM uses it to verify that a deserialized class matches the loaded class. If they differ, InvalidClassException is thrown.

Multithreading & Concurrency

Q24. Thread vs Process?

A process has its own memory space. Threads within the same process share heap memory but each has its own stack. Context-switching between threads is cheaper than between processes.

Q25. Runnable vs Callable?

  • Runnable.run(): no return value, cannot throw checked exception.
  • Callable.call(): returns a value, can throw checked exception; used with ExecutorService.submit() returning Future.

Q26. start() vs run()?

start() spawns a new OS thread and internally calls run(). Calling run() directly executes it on the current thread — no new thread is created.

Q27. synchronized vs ReentrantLock?

synchronizedReentrantLock
Try-lockNotryLock() yes
FairnessNoConfigurable
InterruptibleNoYes
Conditionwait/notifyCondition object
Auto-releaseYes (block exit)Must call unlock()

Q28. What is volatile?

Guarantees that reads/writes go directly to main memory (no CPU cache). Prevents instruction reordering. Suitable for single-writer flags; not sufficient for compound operations (use AtomicXxx instead).

Q29. What is deadlock and how to avoid it?

Deadlock: two or more threads wait for each other's locks forever.

Prevention: acquire locks in a fixed global order; use tryLock() with timeout; avoid nested locks; prefer higher-level concurrency utilities.

Q30. What is the Java Memory Model (JMM)?

JMM defines visibility and ordering guarantees across threads. The happens-before rule ensures that actions before a synchronized write or volatile write are visible to subsequent reads.

Q31. CyclicBarrier vs CountDownLatch?

  • CountDownLatch: one-time; threads wait until count reaches zero; cannot be reset.
  • CyclicBarrier: reusable; threads wait for each other at a barrier point; useful for phased computation.

Q32. ThreadLocal?

Provides a per-thread variable. Each thread has its own independent copy. Useful for per-request data (e.g., user session, DB connection) without synchronization. Always call remove() in thread-pool threads.

Q33. What is thread pool? Why use it?

A pool of pre-created threads that pick up tasks from a queue. Avoids the overhead of thread creation/destruction for every task; controls max concurrency; prevents resource exhaustion.

Q34. What is double-checked locking for Singleton?

private static volatile Singleton instance;
public static Singleton getInstance() {
    if (instance == null) {
        synchronized (Singleton.class) {
            if (instance == null) instance = new Singleton();
        }
    }
    return instance;
}

volatile prevents partially-constructed object visibility.

Q35. What is a race condition?

When the correctness of a program depends on the relative timing/interleaving of threads. Classic example: i++ (read-modify-write) without synchronization.

Java 8 / Functional Programming

Q36. What is a functional interface?

An interface with exactly one abstract method (SAM). Can be annotated with @FunctionalInterface. Enables lambda expressions.

Q37. Stream intermediate vs terminal operations?

  • Intermediate (lazy): filter(), map(), flatMap(), sorted(), peek() — return a new Stream.
  • Terminal (trigger execution): collect(), forEach(), reduce(), count(), findFirst().

Q38. map() vs flatMap()?

  • map(): transforms each element; 1-to-1.
  • flatMap(): transforms each element to a stream, then flattens; 1-to-many (useful for nested collections).

Q39. What does "Stream is lazy" mean?

Intermediate operations are not executed until a terminal operation is called. The pipeline is only traversed once at the terminal call.

Q40. Predicate, Supplier, Consumer, Function?

InterfaceSignatureUse
Predicate<T>boolean test(T t)filtering
Supplier<T>T get()factory/lazy init
Consumer<T>void accept(T t)side effects
Function<T,R>R apply(T t)transform

Garbage Collection

Q41. What is the structure of the Java Heap?

Heap is divided into Young Generation (Eden + Survivor spaces S0/S1) and Old Generation (Tenured). PermGen was replaced by Metaspace in Java 8.

Q42. Minor GC vs Major GC?

  • Minor GC: collects Young Generation; frequent, fast.
  • Major/Full GC: collects Old Generation (and Metaspace); infrequent, slow (stop-the-world pause).

Q43. When is an object eligible for GC?

When no live (reachable) reference points to it. Circular references between two unreachable objects are also collected (GC uses reachability, not reference counting).

Q44. Can we force GC?

System.gc() is a suggestion; JVM may ignore it. You cannot force GC.

PART 2 — SPRING / SPRING BOOT

Spring Core & IoC

Q45. What is the Spring Framework?

An open-source framework that simplifies Java development through Dependency Injection (IoC container), AOP, and a rich ecosystem (MVC, Data, Security, Cloud, etc.).

Q46. What is Inversion of Control (IoC)?

Control of object creation and wiring is inverted from the developer to the Spring container. The container reads configuration, creates beans, injects dependencies, and manages their lifecycle.

Q47. What is Dependency Injection? Types?

DI is when an object's dependencies are provided externally rather than created by the object itself. Spring supports:

  1. Constructor injection — mandatory deps; immutable; preferred.
  2. Setter injection — optional deps; allows circular dependency resolution.
  3. Field injection — @Autowired on field (not recommended for testability).

Q48. Constructor injection vs setter injection?

ConstructorSetter
Mandatory deps✅❌
Immutability✅❌
Circular deps❌ (fails)✅ (resolvable)
ReadabilityIndex-basedNamed setters
Spring recommendationPreferredOptional deps

Q49. What is BeanFactory vs ApplicationContext?

  • BeanFactory: basic IoC container; lazy initialization; minimal features.
  • ApplicationContext: extends BeanFactory; adds event publishing, i18n, AOP, eager initialization. Use ApplicationContext in production.

Q50. What is the default bean scope?

Singleton — one instance per Spring container.

Q51. What are the bean scopes?

ScopeLifecycle
singletonOne per container
prototypeNew instance per request
requestOne per HTTP request
sessionOne per HTTP session
applicationOne per ServletContext

Q52. Spring Bean Lifecycle?

  1. Instantiation → 2. Populate properties → 3. BeanNameAware, BeanFactoryAware → 4. BeanPostProcessor.postProcessBeforeInitialization → 5. @PostConstruct / InitializingBean.afterPropertiesSet() / init-method → 6. Bean ready → 7. @PreDestroy / DisposableBean.destroy() / destroy-method.

Q53. Are singleton beans thread-safe?

Not by default. Spring does not manage thread-safety. Singleton beans should be stateless or use synchronization.

Q54. What is component scanning?

Spring scans the classpath for classes annotated with @Component, @Service, @Repository, @Controller and registers them as beans automatically.

Q55. @Component vs @Service vs @Repository vs @Controller?

All are specializations of @Component. @Repository also adds persistence exception translation. @Service signals service layer. @Controller signals MVC controller. Semantically meaningful for readability and tooling.

Q56. What is @Autowired? How does it work with @Qualifier?

@Autowired tells Spring to inject a matching bean by type. When multiple beans of the same type exist, @Qualifier("beanName") selects the correct one.

Q57. What is @Value?

Injects property values from application.properties or environment variables. Supports SpEL expressions: @Value("${server.port}") or @Value("#{someBean.property}").

AOP

Q58. What is AOP and what problem does it solve?

Aspect-Oriented Programming separates cross-cutting concerns (logging, security, transactions, caching) from business logic, avoiding code scattering and tangling.

Q59. Key AOP terms?

TermMeaning
AspectModule encapsulating a cross-cutting concern
Join PointPoint in execution (Spring AOP: always a method call)
PointcutPredicate matching join points
AdviceAction at a matched join point
WeavingLinking aspects with application code

Q60. Types of advice?

  • @Before — before the method.
  • @After — after (regardless of outcome).
  • @AfterReturning — after successful return.
  • @AfterThrowing — after exception.
  • @Around — surrounds; can control invocation with ProceedingJoinPoint.proceed().

Q61. Spring AOP proxy types?

  • JDK Dynamic Proxy: used when bean implements an interface.
  • CGLIB Proxy: used when bean has no interface (subclasses the class). Methods must be non-final and public.

Spring MVC & REST

Q62. What is DispatcherServlet?

The front controller of Spring MVC. All HTTP requests pass through it. It delegates to HandlerMapping → Controller → ViewResolver → Response.

Q63. @RequestParam vs @PathVariable?

  • @PathVariable: extracts value from the URI path (/users/{id}).
  • @RequestParam: extracts value from query parameters (/users?id=1).

Q64. @Controller vs @RestController?

@RestController = @Controller + @ResponseBody on every method. Returns JSON/XML directly without a view resolver.

Q65. What HTTP methods does REST use?

  • GET — read (safe, idempotent)
  • POST — create (not idempotent)
  • PUT — full update (idempotent)
  • PATCH — partial update
  • DELETE — delete (idempotent)

Q66. What is idempotency?

Calling the same operation multiple times produces the same result. GET, PUT, DELETE are idempotent; POST is not.

Q67. Is REST stateless?

Yes. Each request contains all information needed to process it; no server-side session state between requests.

Q68. What is @Transactional?

Wraps a method in a transaction. Spring creates a proxy that begins a transaction before the method and commits/rolls back after. Default rollback on RuntimeException; checked exceptions do NOT trigger rollback by default.

Q69. Transaction propagation — what are the key types?

PropagationBehaviour
REQUIRED (default)Join existing or create new
REQUIRES_NEWAlways create new; suspend existing
SUPPORTSJoin if exists; otherwise no tx
NOT_SUPPORTEDSuspend existing; run without tx
MANDATORYMust have existing tx; else exception
NEVERMust not have tx; else exception

Q70. Self-invocation problem with @Transactional?

When a @Transactional method calls another @Transactional method in the same class, the AOP proxy is bypassed — the inner transaction annotation is ignored. Solution: inject the bean into itself, or use ApplicationContext to get the proxy.

Spring Boot

Q71. What is Spring Boot?

An opinionated, convention-over-configuration framework built on top of Spring. It provides auto-configuration, embedded servers (Tomcat/Jetty/Undertow), and starter POMs to get production-ready apps running with minimal setup.

Q72. What is auto-configuration?

Spring Boot reads META-INF/spring.factories (or AutoConfiguration.imports in Boot 3), detects JARs on the classpath, and conditionally configures beans using @Conditional* annotations. E.g., if DataSource driver is on classpath, a DataSource bean is auto-configured.

Q73. @SpringBootApplication = ?

Combines: @Configuration + @EnableAutoConfiguration + @ComponentScan.

Q74. What is Spring Boot Actuator?

Provides production-ready monitoring endpoints: /health, /metrics, /info, /env, /loggers, /threaddump, etc. Accessible over HTTP or JMX. Endpoints should be secured.

Q75. What is a Spring Boot Starter?

A curated dependency descriptor (POM) that pulls in all necessary libraries for a feature. E.g., spring-boot-starter-web includes Spring MVC, Jackson, and embedded Tomcat.

Q76. Where does Spring Boot look for properties?

Priority (highest to lowest): command-line args → SPRING_APPLICATION_JSON → OS env vars → application-{profile}.properties → application.properties (classpath root).

Q77. How to change embedded Tomcat port?

Set server.port=8081 in application.properties or --server.port=8081 as CLI arg.

Spring Data JPA & Hibernate

Q78. What is JPA?

Java Persistence API — a specification (not implementation) for ORM in Java. Hibernate is the most popular implementation.

Q79. What is Hibernate? Key advantages?

Hibernate is a JPA ORM provider that maps Java objects to relational tables. Advantages: reduced JDBC boilerplate, HQL/JPQL queries, caching, lazy loading, dirty checking, database portability.

Q80. What is the difference between get() and load() in Hibernate?

  • get(): hits DB immediately; returns null if not found.
  • load(): returns a proxy; hits DB only when properties are accessed; throws ObjectNotFoundException if not found when accessed.

(In Spring Data JPA: findById() vs getOne()/getReferenceById())

Q81. Hibernate caching levels?

  • L1 Cache (Session cache): enabled by default; per-session; flushed when session closes.
  • L2 Cache: optional; shared across sessions; configured per entity (e.g., EhCache, Redis).
  • Query Cache: caches query results (needs L2 enabled).

Q82. What is lazy vs eager loading?

  • Lazy: associated entities are loaded on first access (default for @OneToMany, @ManyToMany).
  • Eager: associated entities are loaded immediately with the parent (default for @ManyToOne, @OneToOne).

Q83. What is N+1 query problem?

When fetching N entities triggers N additional queries for each association. Solutions: JOIN FETCH in JPQL, @EntityGraph, @BatchSize, or @Fetch(FetchMode.SUBSELECT).

Q84. What is dirty checking in Hibernate?

At the end of a transaction, Hibernate compares the current state of managed entities with their snapshot taken at load time. If different, it auto-generates UPDATE SQL — no need to call save() explicitly.

Q85. @OneToMany mapping — best practices?

Use FetchType.LAZY, map the owning side with @JoinColumn, use Set over List for better performance, avoid bidirectional mappings unless necessary, and use @BatchSize or JOIN FETCH to avoid N+1.

Q86. What is PlatformTransactionManager?

The Spring abstraction for transaction management. Different implementations: DataSourceTransactionManager (JDBC), JpaTransactionManager (JPA/Hibernate), JtaTransactionManager (distributed).

Q87. Spring Data JPA repository naming convention?

Methods are parsed from their name: findByFirstNameAndLastName, findByAgeGreaterThan, findTop3ByOrderByCreatedAtDesc. Spring generates the JPQL automatically.

Spring Security

Q88. What is Spring Security?

A powerful, customizable authentication and authorization framework for Spring applications. Based on servlet filters.

Q89. Authentication vs Authorization?

  • Authentication: verifying who the user is (login).
  • Authorization: determining what the user is allowed to do (access control). Authentication must come first.

Q90. What is the Security Filter Chain?

An ordered chain of servlet filters (SecurityFilterChain) that intercepts every HTTP request. Key filters: UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter, BasicAuthenticationFilter, ExceptionTranslationFilter, FilterSecurityInterceptor.

Q91. What is SecurityContextHolder?

Stores the authenticated Principal in a ThreadLocal per request. Access the current user with SecurityContextHolder.getContext().getAuthentication().

Q92. What is password encoding?

Never store plain-text passwords. Spring Security's PasswordEncoder (e.g., BCryptPasswordEncoder) hashes passwords with a salt. BCrypt is the recommended default.

Q93. @Secured vs @PreAuthorize?

  • @Secured({"ROLE_ADMIN"}): simple role check.
  • @PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN') and #id == principal.id"): supports SpEL expressions for fine-grained control.

Q94. How to implement JWT authentication in Spring Boot?

  1. User logs in → /auth/login → server validates credentials → returns JWT.
  2. Client sends JWT in Authorization: Bearer <token> header.
  3. A custom OncePerRequestFilter validates JWT, sets Authentication in SecurityContextHolder.
  4. Stateless (SessionCreationPolicy.STATELESS).

PART 3 — SYSTEM DESIGN (RELEVANT TO CV)

Q95. What is microservices architecture? Pros/Cons?

  • Pros: independent deployment, technology diversity, fault isolation, scalability per service.
  • Cons: distributed system complexity, network latency, eventual consistency, operational overhead (many services to monitor).

Q96. How do microservices communicate?

  • Synchronous: REST (HTTP/HTTPS), gRPC.
  • Asynchronous: message brokers (Kafka, RabbitMQ, AWS SQS/SNS) — decoupled, resilient.

Q97. What is an API Gateway?

Single entry point for all client requests. Handles routing, rate limiting, authentication, SSL termination, logging, and request aggregation. Examples: AWS API Gateway, Kong, Nginx.

Q98. What is event-driven architecture?

Services communicate by publishing and consuming events via a message broker. Producers do not know consumers. Enables loose coupling, replay capability, and scalability. Kafka is the common choice.

Q99. What is Kafka? Key concepts?

Distributed, append-only log for high-throughput event streaming.

  • Topic: category of events.
  • Partition: unit of parallelism; ordered within partition.
  • Offset: position of a message in a partition.
  • Consumer Group: each partition consumed by one consumer in a group; enables parallel consumption.
  • Retention: messages kept for a configurable duration (not deleted on consume).

Q100. What is Redis? How do you use it?

In-memory key-value store supporting strings, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets, streams. Use cases: caching, session storage, rate limiting, pub/sub, leaderboards. Sub-millisecond latency.

Q101. Caching strategies?

StrategyHow
Cache-AsideApp checks cache; on miss, loads from DB and populates cache
Write-ThroughWrite to cache and DB simultaneously
Write-BehindWrite to cache; async write to DB
Read-ThroughCache sits in front; fetches from DB on miss

Cache invalidation: TTL expiry, event-driven invalidation, or version-based keys.

Q102. What is CAP theorem?

A distributed system can guarantee only 2 of 3: Consistency, Availability, Partition Tolerance. Since partitions are unavoidable in real networks, systems choose between CP (consistent) or AP (available) when partitions occur.

Q103. How do you design a high-availability system?

  • No single point of failure (active-active or active-passive redundancy).
  • Load balancing across multiple instances.
  • Health checks and auto-recovery.
  • Circuit breaker pattern (Resilience4j, Hystrix).
  • Multi-AZ / multi-region deployments.
  • Graceful degradation and fallback logic.

Q104. What is a circuit breaker pattern?

Monitors failures for a service call. When failure rate exceeds threshold → circuit OPENS → fallback is returned immediately (no waiting). After a timeout → HALF-OPEN → probes if service recovered → CLOSE if healthy. Prevents cascade failures.

Q105. What is database sharding?

Horizontal partitioning: data is distributed across multiple DB instances (shards) by a shard key (e.g., user_id % N). Enables horizontal scaling. Challenges: cross-shard queries, rebalancing, hotspots.

Q106. SQL vs NoSQL — when to choose?

SQL (PostgreSQL, Aurora)NoSQL (DynamoDB, MongoDB)
SchemaFixedFlexible
TransactionsACIDEventual consistency (varies)
ScaleVertical (+ read replicas)Horizontal
Use caseComplex relations, reportingHigh-scale, document/KV

Q107. What is CQRS?

Command Query Responsibility Segregation — separate read model and write model. Writes update the command side; reads use optimized projections (read replicas, caches, Elasticsearch). Often combined with Event Sourcing.

Q108. Observability: Metrics, Logging, Tracing?

  • Metrics: numeric measurements over time (Prometheus + Grafana, CloudWatch).
  • Logging: structured log events (ELK Stack, CloudWatch Logs).
  • Tracing: distributed trace of a request across services (Jaeger, AWS X-Ray, OpenTelemetry).

Q109. What is a distributed lock?

A mechanism to ensure only one node executes a critical section at a time. In Redis: SET key value NX PX <ttl> (Redlock algorithm for multi-node Redis). In databases: SELECT FOR UPDATE.

Q110. Rate limiting strategies?

  • Token Bucket: tokens replenish at fixed rate; allows bursts.
  • Leaky Bucket: requests processed at fixed rate; excess queued or dropped.
  • Fixed Window: count requests per window; edge case at window boundary.
  • Sliding Window: smoothed version; avoids boundary spikes.

PART 4 — AWS / CLOUD (FROM YOUR CV)

Q111. AWS Aurora vs RDS?

Aurora is AWS's cloud-native relational DB (MySQL/PostgreSQL compatible). Up to 5× faster than standard MySQL. Auto-scaling storage, up to 15 read replicas, automatic failover in < 30 seconds. Better HA than standard RDS.

Q112. DynamoDB — key concepts?

Managed NoSQL (key-value + document). Partition key (hash) determines shard. Optional sort key for range queries. Provisioned or on-demand capacity. Supports DAX for microsecond caching. Global Tables for multi-region replication.

Q113. What is AWS WAF?

Web Application Firewall. Protects against common web exploits (SQL injection, XSS, bad bots). Deployed in front of ALB, API Gateway, or CloudFront. Rules can be custom or managed (AWS Managed Rules).

Q114. What is an SLA and how do you maintain it?

Service Level Agreement defines uptime/latency commitments (e.g., 99.9% = 8.7h downtime/year). Maintained through: proactive monitoring (CloudWatch alarms), runbooks, auto-scaling, incident response, chaos engineering.

Q115. What is MTTR and MTBF?

  • MTTR (Mean Time To Recover): avg time to restore after failure. Reduce with good runbooks, automation, alerting.
  • MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures): avg time between failures. Improve with code quality, testing, redundancy.

Total: 115 Q&A covering Java Core, Spring/Boot, Hibernate/JPA, System Design, and AWS — tailored to Pham Khanh Tuong's CV.

Java / Spring / Hibernate / System Design — Interview Q&A

Based on: Grokking The Java Interview + Grokking The Spring Boot Interview + Pham Khanh Tuong's CV

Scope: Theory only (no coding problems)

PART 1 — CORE JAVA

OOP Fundamentals

Q1. What is method overloading vs method overriding?

  • Overloading: same method name, different parameter types/count; resolved at compile time (static polymorphism).
  • Overriding: subclass redefines a superclass method with the same signature; resolved at runtime (dynamic polymorphism).

Q2. Can we override a static method?

No. Static methods belong to the class and are resolved at compile time. You can hide it by declaring the same static method in the subclass, but it is not true overriding.

Q3. Can we override a private or final method?

No. Private methods are not visible to subclasses. Final methods are explicitly locked from overriding.

Q4. What is covariant return type in overriding?

Since Java 5, an overriding method may return a subtype of the return type declared in the parent. Useful with clone().

Q5. What is the difference between abstract class and interface?

Abstract ClassInterface
MethodsCan have concrete methodsJava 8+: can have default/static methods
FieldsAny fieldsOnly public static final constants
InheritanceSingleMultiple
Use caseShare default behaviourDefine contract/type

Q6. What are the SOLID principles?

  • S – Single Responsibility: one class, one reason to change.
  • O – Open/Closed: open for extension, closed for modification.
  • L – Liskov Substitution: subtypes must be replaceable for their base types.
  • I – Interface Segregation: prefer small, specific interfaces.
  • D – Dependency Inversion: depend on abstractions, not concretions.

Q7. Composition vs Inheritance?

Composition is more flexible — behaviour can be changed at runtime by swapping the composed object. Inheritance is static. Prefer composition over inheritance (GoF principle).

Q8. Association, Aggregation, Composition?

  • Association: general relationship between objects.
  • Aggregation: weak "has-a"; parts can exist independently (department → employee).
  • Composition: strong "has-a"; parts cannot exist without the whole (house → room).

Q9. Strategy vs State pattern?

  • Strategy: switch algorithms at runtime; the context delegates to the chosen strategy.
  • State: object changes behaviour based on internal state; the state itself drives transitions.

Q10. Decorator vs Proxy vs Adapter?

  • Decorator: adds behaviour without changing the class (wraps it).
  • Proxy: controls access to another object.
  • Adapter: makes incompatible interfaces work together.

Q11. Singleton pattern — when to use and thread-safe implementation?

Use when exactly one instance is needed globally (e.g., config, connection pool). Thread-safe approach: double-checked locking with volatile, or an enum-based singleton.

Q12. Factory vs Abstract Factory?

  • Factory: creates one family of products.
  • Abstract Factory: creates families of related products without specifying concrete classes.

Java Core — String, Collections, Exceptions

Q13. String vs StringBuffer vs StringBuilder?

  • String: immutable; every change creates a new object.
  • StringBuffer: mutable, synchronized (thread-safe, slower).
  • StringBuilder: mutable, not synchronized (faster, single-thread use).

Q14. How does HashMap work internally?

HashMap uses an array of buckets. Key's hashCode() determines the bucket index. On collision, entries are chained (linked list, or red-black tree from Java 8 when size > 8). equals() is used to locate the exact key within a bucket.

Q15. equals() and hashCode() contract?

If a.equals(b) then a.hashCode() == b.hashCode(). The reverse is not required. You must override both together; breaking the contract corrupts HashMap/HashSet behaviour.

Q16. HashMap vs Hashtable vs ConcurrentHashMap?

HashMapHashtableConcurrentHashMap
Thread-safeNoYes (full lock)Yes (segment/bucket lock)
Null keys1 null keyNot allowedNot allowed
PerformanceBest single-threadPoor multi-threadBest multi-thread

Q17. ArrayList vs LinkedList?

  • ArrayList: backed by array; O(1) random access; O(n) insert/delete in middle.
  • LinkedList: doubly-linked list; O(n) random access; O(1) insert/delete at head/tail.

Q18. fail-fast vs fail-safe iterator?

  • fail-fast: throws ConcurrentModificationException if collection is modified during iteration (e.g., ArrayList, HashMap iterators).
  • fail-safe: works on a copy; no exception (e.g., CopyOnWriteArrayList, ConcurrentHashMap).

Q19. Checked vs Unchecked exceptions?

  • Checked: must be declared with throws or caught; represents recoverable conditions (e.g., IOException).
  • Unchecked (RuntimeException): programming errors; no forced handling (e.g., NullPointerException).

Q20. Comparable vs Comparator?

  • Comparable (natural order): implemented inside the class; compareTo().
  • Comparator (custom order): external; compare(); multiple comparators possible; does not require source access.

Q21. What is transient vs volatile?

  • transient: field excluded from serialization.
  • volatile: guarantees visibility of changes across threads; prevents caching in registers; establishes happens-before.

Q22. Serializable vs Externalizable?

  • Serializable: marker interface; default JVM serialization.
  • Externalizable: two methods readExternal()/writeExternal(); developer controls serialization fully; faster.

Q23. What is SerialVersionUID?

A static final long that acts as a version number for a Serializable class. JVM uses it to verify that a deserialized class matches the loaded class. If they differ, InvalidClassException is thrown.

Multithreading & Concurrency

Q24. Thread vs Process?

A process has its own memory space. Threads within the same process share heap memory but each has its own stack. Context-switching between threads is cheaper than between processes.

Q25. Runnable vs Callable?

  • Runnable.run(): no return value, cannot throw checked exception.
  • Callable.call(): returns a value, can throw checked exception; used with ExecutorService.submit() returning Future.

Q26. start() vs run()?

start() spawns a new OS thread and internally calls run(). Calling run() directly executes it on the current thread — no new thread is created.

Q27. synchronized vs ReentrantLock?

synchronizedReentrantLock
Try-lockNotryLock() yes
FairnessNoConfigurable
InterruptibleNoYes
Conditionwait/notifyCondition object
Auto-releaseYes (block exit)Must call unlock()

Q28. What is volatile?

Guarantees that reads/writes go directly to main memory (no CPU cache). Prevents instruction reordering. Suitable for single-writer flags; not sufficient for compound operations (use AtomicXxx instead).

Q29. What is deadlock and how to avoid it?

Deadlock: two or more threads wait for each other's locks forever.

Prevention: acquire locks in a fixed global order; use tryLock() with timeout; avoid nested locks; prefer higher-level concurrency utilities.

Q30. What is the Java Memory Model (JMM)?

JMM defines visibility and ordering guarantees across threads. The happens-before rule ensures that actions before a synchronized write or volatile write are visible to subsequent reads.

Q31. CyclicBarrier vs CountDownLatch?

  • CountDownLatch: one-time; threads wait until count reaches zero; cannot be reset.
  • CyclicBarrier: reusable; threads wait for each other at a barrier point; useful for phased computation.

Q32. ThreadLocal?

Provides a per-thread variable. Each thread has its own independent copy. Useful for per-request data (e.g., user session, DB connection) without synchronization. Always call remove() in thread-pool threads.

Q33. What is thread pool? Why use it?

A pool of pre-created threads that pick up tasks from a queue. Avoids the overhead of thread creation/destruction for every task; controls max concurrency; prevents resource exhaustion.

Q34. What is double-checked locking for Singleton?

private static volatile Singleton instance;
public static Singleton getInstance() {
    if (instance == null) {
        synchronized (Singleton.class) {
            if (instance == null) instance = new Singleton();
        }
    }
    return instance;
}

volatile prevents partially-constructed object visibility.

Q35. What is a race condition?

When the correctness of a program depends on the relative timing/interleaving of threads. Classic example: i++ (read-modify-write) without synchronization.

Java 8 / Functional Programming

Q36. What is a functional interface?

An interface with exactly one abstract method (SAM). Can be annotated with @FunctionalInterface. Enables lambda expressions.

Q37. Stream intermediate vs terminal operations?

  • Intermediate (lazy): filter(), map(), flatMap(), sorted(), peek() — return a new Stream.
  • Terminal (trigger execution): collect(), forEach(), reduce(), count(), findFirst().

Q38. map() vs flatMap()?

  • map(): transforms each element; 1-to-1.
  • flatMap(): transforms each element to a stream, then flattens; 1-to-many (useful for nested collections).

Q39. What does "Stream is lazy" mean?

Intermediate operations are not executed until a terminal operation is called. The pipeline is only traversed once at the terminal call.

Q40. Predicate, Supplier, Consumer, Function?

InterfaceSignatureUse
Predicate<T>boolean test(T t)filtering
Supplier<T>T get()factory/lazy init
Consumer<T>void accept(T t)side effects
Function<T,R>R apply(T t)transform

Garbage Collection

Q41. What is the structure of the Java Heap?

Heap is divided into Young Generation (Eden + Survivor spaces S0/S1) and Old Generation (Tenured). PermGen was replaced by Metaspace in Java 8.

Q42. Minor GC vs Major GC?

  • Minor GC: collects Young Generation; frequent, fast.
  • Major/Full GC: collects Old Generation (and Metaspace); infrequent, slow (stop-the-world pause).

Q43. When is an object eligible for GC?

When no live (reachable) reference points to it. Circular references between two unreachable objects are also collected (GC uses reachability, not reference counting).

Q44. Can we force GC?

System.gc() is a suggestion; JVM may ignore it. You cannot force GC.

PART 2 — SPRING / SPRING BOOT

Spring Core & IoC

Q45. What is the Spring Framework?

An open-source framework that simplifies Java development through Dependency Injection (IoC container), AOP, and a rich ecosystem (MVC, Data, Security, Cloud, etc.).

Q46. What is Inversion of Control (IoC)?

Control of object creation and wiring is inverted from the developer to the Spring container. The container reads configuration, creates beans, injects dependencies, and manages their lifecycle.

Q47. What is Dependency Injection? Types?

DI is when an object's dependencies are provided externally rather than created by the object itself. Spring supports:

  1. Constructor injection — mandatory deps; immutable; preferred.
  2. Setter injection — optional deps; allows circular dependency resolution.
  3. Field injection — @Autowired on field (not recommended for testability).

Q48. Constructor injection vs setter injection?

ConstructorSetter
Mandatory deps✅❌
Immutability✅❌
Circular deps❌ (fails)✅ (resolvable)
ReadabilityIndex-basedNamed setters
Spring recommendationPreferredOptional deps

Q49. What is BeanFactory vs ApplicationContext?

  • BeanFactory: basic IoC container; lazy initialization; minimal features.
  • ApplicationContext: extends BeanFactory; adds event publishing, i18n, AOP, eager initialization. Use ApplicationContext in production.

Q50. What is the default bean scope?

Singleton — one instance per Spring container.

Q51. What are the bean scopes?

ScopeLifecycle
singletonOne per container
prototypeNew instance per request
requestOne per HTTP request
sessionOne per HTTP session
applicationOne per ServletContext

Q52. Spring Bean Lifecycle?

  1. Instantiation → 2. Populate properties → 3. BeanNameAware, BeanFactoryAware → 4. BeanPostProcessor.postProcessBeforeInitialization → 5. @PostConstruct / InitializingBean.afterPropertiesSet() / init-method → 6. Bean ready → 7. @PreDestroy / DisposableBean.destroy() / destroy-method.

Q53. Are singleton beans thread-safe?

Not by default. Spring does not manage thread-safety. Singleton beans should be stateless or use synchronization.

Q54. What is component scanning?

Spring scans the classpath for classes annotated with @Component, @Service, @Repository, @Controller and registers them as beans automatically.

Q55. @Component vs @Service vs @Repository vs @Controller?

All are specializations of @Component. @Repository also adds persistence exception translation. @Service signals service layer. @Controller signals MVC controller. Semantically meaningful for readability and tooling.

Q56. What is @Autowired? How does it work with @Qualifier?

@Autowired tells Spring to inject a matching bean by type. When multiple beans of the same type exist, @Qualifier("beanName") selects the correct one.

Q57. What is @Value?

Injects property values from application.properties or environment variables. Supports SpEL expressions: @Value("${server.port}") or @Value("#{someBean.property}").

AOP

Q58. What is AOP and what problem does it solve?

Aspect-Oriented Programming separates cross-cutting concerns (logging, security, transactions, caching) from business logic, avoiding code scattering and tangling.

Q59. Key AOP terms?

TermMeaning
AspectModule encapsulating a cross-cutting concern
Join PointPoint in execution (Spring AOP: always a method call)
PointcutPredicate matching join points
AdviceAction at a matched join point
WeavingLinking aspects with application code

Q60. Types of advice?

  • @Before — before the method.
  • @After — after (regardless of outcome).
  • @AfterReturning — after successful return.
  • @AfterThrowing — after exception.
  • @Around — surrounds; can control invocation with ProceedingJoinPoint.proceed().

Q61. Spring AOP proxy types?

  • JDK Dynamic Proxy: used when bean implements an interface.
  • CGLIB Proxy: used when bean has no interface (subclasses the class). Methods must be non-final and public.

Spring MVC & REST

Q62. What is DispatcherServlet?

The front controller of Spring MVC. All HTTP requests pass through it. It delegates to HandlerMapping → Controller → ViewResolver → Response.

Q63. @RequestParam vs @PathVariable?

  • @PathVariable: extracts value from the URI path (/users/{id}).
  • @RequestParam: extracts value from query parameters (/users?id=1).

Q64. @Controller vs @RestController?

@RestController = @Controller + @ResponseBody on every method. Returns JSON/XML directly without a view resolver.

Q65. What HTTP methods does REST use?

  • GET — read (safe, idempotent)
  • POST — create (not idempotent)
  • PUT — full update (idempotent)
  • PATCH — partial update
  • DELETE — delete (idempotent)

Q66. What is idempotency?

Calling the same operation multiple times produces the same result. GET, PUT, DELETE are idempotent; POST is not.

Q67. Is REST stateless?

Yes. Each request contains all information needed to process it; no server-side session state between requests.

Q68. What is @Transactional?

Wraps a method in a transaction. Spring creates a proxy that begins a transaction before the method and commits/rolls back after. Default rollback on RuntimeException; checked exceptions do NOT trigger rollback by default.

Q69. Transaction propagation — what are the key types?

PropagationBehaviour
REQUIRED (default)Join existing or create new
REQUIRES_NEWAlways create new; suspend existing
SUPPORTSJoin if exists; otherwise no tx
NOT_SUPPORTEDSuspend existing; run without tx
MANDATORYMust have existing tx; else exception
NEVERMust not have tx; else exception

Q70. Self-invocation problem with @Transactional?

When a @Transactional method calls another @Transactional method in the same class, the AOP proxy is bypassed — the inner transaction annotation is ignored. Solution: inject the bean into itself, or use ApplicationContext to get the proxy.

Spring Boot

Q71. What is Spring Boot?

An opinionated, convention-over-configuration framework built on top of Spring. It provides auto-configuration, embedded servers (Tomcat/Jetty/Undertow), and starter POMs to get production-ready apps running with minimal setup.

Q72. What is auto-configuration?

Spring Boot reads META-INF/spring.factories (or AutoConfiguration.imports in Boot 3), detects JARs on the classpath, and conditionally configures beans using @Conditional* annotations. E.g., if DataSource driver is on classpath, a DataSource bean is auto-configured.

Q73. @SpringBootApplication = ?

Combines: @Configuration + @EnableAutoConfiguration + @ComponentScan.

Q74. What is Spring Boot Actuator?

Provides production-ready monitoring endpoints: /health, /metrics, /info, /env, /loggers, /threaddump, etc. Accessible over HTTP or JMX. Endpoints should be secured.

Q75. What is a Spring Boot Starter?

A curated dependency descriptor (POM) that pulls in all necessary libraries for a feature. E.g., spring-boot-starter-web includes Spring MVC, Jackson, and embedded Tomcat.

Q76. Where does Spring Boot look for properties?

Priority (highest to lowest): command-line args → SPRING_APPLICATION_JSON → OS env vars → application-{profile}.properties → application.properties (classpath root).

Q77. How to change embedded Tomcat port?

Set server.port=8081 in application.properties or --server.port=8081 as CLI arg.

Spring Data JPA & Hibernate

Q78. What is JPA?

Java Persistence API — a specification (not implementation) for ORM in Java. Hibernate is the most popular implementation.

Q79. What is Hibernate? Key advantages?

Hibernate is a JPA ORM provider that maps Java objects to relational tables. Advantages: reduced JDBC boilerplate, HQL/JPQL queries, caching, lazy loading, dirty checking, database portability.

Q80. What is the difference between get() and load() in Hibernate?

  • get(): hits DB immediately; returns null if not found.
  • load(): returns a proxy; hits DB only when properties are accessed; throws ObjectNotFoundException if not found when accessed.

(In Spring Data JPA: findById() vs getOne()/getReferenceById())

Q81. Hibernate caching levels?

  • L1 Cache (Session cache): enabled by default; per-session; flushed when session closes.
  • L2 Cache: optional; shared across sessions; configured per entity (e.g., EhCache, Redis).
  • Query Cache: caches query results (needs L2 enabled).

Q82. What is lazy vs eager loading?

  • Lazy: associated entities are loaded on first access (default for @OneToMany, @ManyToMany).
  • Eager: associated entities are loaded immediately with the parent (default for @ManyToOne, @OneToOne).

Q83. What is N+1 query problem?

When fetching N entities triggers N additional queries for each association. Solutions: JOIN FETCH in JPQL, @EntityGraph, @BatchSize, or @Fetch(FetchMode.SUBSELECT).

Q84. What is dirty checking in Hibernate?

At the end of a transaction, Hibernate compares the current state of managed entities with their snapshot taken at load time. If different, it auto-generates UPDATE SQL — no need to call save() explicitly.

Q85. @OneToMany mapping — best practices?

Use FetchType.LAZY, map the owning side with @JoinColumn, use Set over List for better performance, avoid bidirectional mappings unless necessary, and use @BatchSize or JOIN FETCH to avoid N+1.

Q86. What is PlatformTransactionManager?

The Spring abstraction for transaction management. Different implementations: DataSourceTransactionManager (JDBC), JpaTransactionManager (JPA/Hibernate), JtaTransactionManager (distributed).

Q87. Spring Data JPA repository naming convention?

Methods are parsed from their name: findByFirstNameAndLastName, findByAgeGreaterThan, findTop3ByOrderByCreatedAtDesc. Spring generates the JPQL automatically.

Spring Security

Q88. What is Spring Security?

A powerful, customizable authentication and authorization framework for Spring applications. Based on servlet filters.

Q89. Authentication vs Authorization?

  • Authentication: verifying who the user is (login).
  • Authorization: determining what the user is allowed to do (access control). Authentication must come first.

Q90. What is the Security Filter Chain?

An ordered chain of servlet filters (SecurityFilterChain) that intercepts every HTTP request. Key filters: UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter, BasicAuthenticationFilter, ExceptionTranslationFilter, FilterSecurityInterceptor.

Q91. What is SecurityContextHolder?

Stores the authenticated Principal in a ThreadLocal per request. Access the current user with SecurityContextHolder.getContext().getAuthentication().

Q92. What is password encoding?

Never store plain-text passwords. Spring Security's PasswordEncoder (e.g., BCryptPasswordEncoder) hashes passwords with a salt. BCrypt is the recommended default.

Q93. @Secured vs @PreAuthorize?

  • @Secured({"ROLE_ADMIN"}): simple role check.
  • @PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN') and #id == principal.id"): supports SpEL expressions for fine-grained control.

Q94. How to implement JWT authentication in Spring Boot?

  1. User logs in → /auth/login → server validates credentials → returns JWT.
  2. Client sends JWT in Authorization: Bearer <token> header.
  3. A custom OncePerRequestFilter validates JWT, sets Authentication in SecurityContextHolder.
  4. Stateless (SessionCreationPolicy.STATELESS).

PART 3 — SYSTEM DESIGN (RELEVANT TO CV)

Q95. What is microservices architecture? Pros/Cons?

  • Pros: independent deployment, technology diversity, fault isolation, scalability per service.
  • Cons: distributed system complexity, network latency, eventual consistency, operational overhead (many services to monitor).

Q96. How do microservices communicate?

  • Synchronous: REST (HTTP/HTTPS), gRPC.
  • Asynchronous: message brokers (Kafka, RabbitMQ, AWS SQS/SNS) — decoupled, resilient.

Q97. What is an API Gateway?

Single entry point for all client requests. Handles routing, rate limiting, authentication, SSL termination, logging, and request aggregation. Examples: AWS API Gateway, Kong, Nginx.

Q98. What is event-driven architecture?

Services communicate by publishing and consuming events via a message broker. Producers do not know consumers. Enables loose coupling, replay capability, and scalability. Kafka is the common choice.

Q99. What is Kafka? Key concepts?

Distributed, append-only log for high-throughput event streaming.

  • Topic: category of events.
  • Partition: unit of parallelism; ordered within partition.
  • Offset: position of a message in a partition.
  • Consumer Group: each partition consumed by one consumer in a group; enables parallel consumption.
  • Retention: messages kept for a configurable duration (not deleted on consume).

Q100. What is Redis? How do you use it?

In-memory key-value store supporting strings, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets, streams. Use cases: caching, session storage, rate limiting, pub/sub, leaderboards. Sub-millisecond latency.

Q101. Caching strategies?

StrategyHow
Cache-AsideApp checks cache; on miss, loads from DB and populates cache
Write-ThroughWrite to cache and DB simultaneously
Write-BehindWrite to cache; async write to DB
Read-ThroughCache sits in front; fetches from DB on miss

Cache invalidation: TTL expiry, event-driven invalidation, or version-based keys.

Q102. What is CAP theorem?

A distributed system can guarantee only 2 of 3: Consistency, Availability, Partition Tolerance. Since partitions are unavoidable in real networks, systems choose between CP (consistent) or AP (available) when partitions occur.

Q103. How do you design a high-availability system?

  • No single point of failure (active-active or active-passive redundancy).
  • Load balancing across multiple instances.
  • Health checks and auto-recovery.
  • Circuit breaker pattern (Resilience4j, Hystrix).
  • Multi-AZ / multi-region deployments.
  • Graceful degradation and fallback logic.

Q104. What is a circuit breaker pattern?

Monitors failures for a service call. When failure rate exceeds threshold → circuit OPENS → fallback is returned immediately (no waiting). After a timeout → HALF-OPEN → probes if service recovered → CLOSE if healthy. Prevents cascade failures.

Q105. What is database sharding?

Horizontal partitioning: data is distributed across multiple DB instances (shards) by a shard key (e.g., user_id % N). Enables horizontal scaling. Challenges: cross-shard queries, rebalancing, hotspots.

Q106. SQL vs NoSQL — when to choose?

SQL (PostgreSQL, Aurora)NoSQL (DynamoDB, MongoDB)
SchemaFixedFlexible
TransactionsACIDEventual consistency (varies)
ScaleVertical (+ read replicas)Horizontal
Use caseComplex relations, reportingHigh-scale, document/KV

Q107. What is CQRS?

Command Query Responsibility Segregation — separate read model and write model. Writes update the command side; reads use optimized projections (read replicas, caches, Elasticsearch). Often combined with Event Sourcing.

Q108. Observability: Metrics, Logging, Tracing?

  • Metrics: numeric measurements over time (Prometheus + Grafana, CloudWatch).
  • Logging: structured log events (ELK Stack, CloudWatch Logs).
  • Tracing: distributed trace of a request across services (Jaeger, AWS X-Ray, OpenTelemetry).

Q109. What is a distributed lock?

A mechanism to ensure only one node executes a critical section at a time. In Redis: SET key value NX PX <ttl> (Redlock algorithm for multi-node Redis). In databases: SELECT FOR UPDATE.

Q110. Rate limiting strategies?

  • Token Bucket: tokens replenish at fixed rate; allows bursts.
  • Leaky Bucket: requests processed at fixed rate; excess queued or dropped.
  • Fixed Window: count requests per window; edge case at window boundary.
  • Sliding Window: smoothed version; avoids boundary spikes.

PART 4 — AWS / CLOUD (FROM YOUR CV)

Q111. AWS Aurora vs RDS?

Aurora is AWS's cloud-native relational DB (MySQL/PostgreSQL compatible). Up to 5× faster than standard MySQL. Auto-scaling storage, up to 15 read replicas, automatic failover in < 30 seconds. Better HA than standard RDS.

Q112. DynamoDB — key concepts?

Managed NoSQL (key-value + document). Partition key (hash) determines shard. Optional sort key for range queries. Provisioned or on-demand capacity. Supports DAX for microsecond caching. Global Tables for multi-region replication.

Q113. What is AWS WAF?

Web Application Firewall. Protects against common web exploits (SQL injection, XSS, bad bots). Deployed in front of ALB, API Gateway, or CloudFront. Rules can be custom or managed (AWS Managed Rules).

Q114. What is an SLA and how do you maintain it?

Service Level Agreement defines uptime/latency commitments (e.g., 99.9% = 8.7h downtime/year). Maintained through: proactive monitoring (CloudWatch alarms), runbooks, auto-scaling, incident response, chaos engineering.

Q115. What is MTTR and MTBF?

  • MTTR (Mean Time To Recover): avg time to restore after failure. Reduce with good runbooks, automation, alerting.
  • MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures): avg time between failures. Improve with code quality, testing, redundancy.

Total: 115 Q&A covering Java Core, Spring/Boot, Hibernate/JPA, System Design, and AWS — tailored to Pham Khanh Tuong's CV.
